topics.title: Overview of Network Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Network Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Security** is the discipline of engineering, implementing, and maintaining protective measures that ensure the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability** of data and resources transmitted across or residing within computer networks. It encompasses the policies, procedures, tools, and cryptographic mechanisms designed to defend against unauthorized access, misuse, modification, destruction, or improper disclosure of networked information assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The course *Intrusion Detection and Prevention* (PECPT753) treats Network Security as the foundational substrate upon which all detection/prevention logic operates. You cannot detect an intrusion if you do not first understand the *normal* secure state of the network.

According to the **ITU-T X.800 Recommendation** (the international standard endorsed by KTU for security architecture), network security provides a set of **security services** that are implemented by **security mechanisms** to counter **security attacks**, all of which threaten the **CIA Triad** of information security.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a corporate office building:

- **Confidentiality** = Locked filing cabinets and private offices. Only authorized employees can read sensitive files.
- **Integrity** = Tamper-evident seals on documents. You can tell if someone altered a contract after it was signed.
- **Availability** = Working elevators, lights, and HVAC. Even with perfect locks, the building is useless if a fire shuts it down.

**Network Security** is the *combination* of locks (encryption), seals (hashes), alarm systems (IDS), and sprinklers (firewalls/IPS) that protect a building whose "rooms" are servers, whose "hallways" are network links, and whose "windows and doors" are open TCP/UDP ports.

---

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — The Foundational Model

The **CIA Triad** is the bedrock model of information security and forms the evaluation rubric for *every* security control discussed in this course.

| Pillar | Definition | Network Security Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Preventing unauthorized disclosure of information | TLS/SSL encryption on HTTPS traffic |
| **Integrity** | Preventing unauthorized modification of data | SHA-256 hashes, digital signatures |
| **Availability** | Ensuring authorized access when needed | DDoS mitigation, redundant servers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Extended Models:** Some KTU-recommended texts extend the CIA Triad to include **Authentication, Authorization, Accountability (AAA)** and **Non-Repudiation**. These are *derived* services that *enforce* the three primary CIA properties.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definitions you MUST memorize verbatim for the ESE:**
> - **Threat:** A potential violation of security.
> - **Attack:** A deliberate, realized threat action.
> - **Vulnerability:** A weakness in the system that can be exploited.
> - **Risk:** The probability that a vulnerability will be exploited, multiplied by the impact.
> - **Countermeasure / Security Control:** Any mechanism that reduces risk.

---

## 1.5 Why Network Security Matters (Engineering Motivation)

Modern networks carry **financial transactions, medical records, industrial control signals, and personal communications**. A breach can cause:
- **Direct financial loss** (ransomware, fraud).
- **Regulatory penalties** (GDPR, India's IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023).
- **Reputational damage** (loss of customer trust).
- **Physical harm** (attacks on SCADA/ICS systems in power plants).

> [!NOTE]
> The global average cost of a data breach in 2024 was **USD 4.88 million** (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report) — a key industry metric often cited in KTU case-study questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSI Security Architecture (X.800 Framework)

The **ITU-T X.800** standard defines a systematic way to reason about network security. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to articulate the relationship between the three core concepts:

$$\text{SECURITY ATTACK} \xrightarrow{\text{threatens}} \text{CIA TRIAD} \xrightarrow{\text{protected by}} \text{SECURITY SERVICES} \xrightarrow{\text{realized via}} \text{SECURITY MECHANISMS}$$

---

## 2.2 Classification of Security Attacks

### A. Passive Attacks (Eavesdropping — harder to detect)

Passive attacks monitor or eavesdrop on transmissions *without altering* them. The attacker seeks to *break confidentiality*.

| Attack | Description |
|---|---|
| **Release of Message Contents** | Attacker reads the actual data (e.g., cleartext email) |
| **Traffic Analysis** | Attacker infers sensitive info from traffic patterns (timing, frequency, volume) even when content is encrypted |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Passive attacks are **virtually impossible to detect** because they do not alter data. The only defense is **prevention** via encryption.

### B. Active Attacks (Modification/Fabrication — easier to detect)

Active attacks attempt to alter system resources or affect operation.

| Attack | Description |
|---|---|
| **Masquerade** | One entity pretends to be another (e.g., IP spoofing) |
| **Replay** | Attacker retransmits a captured valid message |
| **Modification of Messages** | Altering portions of a legitimate message |
| **Denial of Service (DoS/DDoS)** | Disrupting service availability |

---

## 2.3 The Five Pillars of X.800 Security Services

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask: *"List and explain the X.800 security services."* Memorize these five:

1. **Data Confidentiality** — Protection against unauthorized disclosure.
2. **Data Integrity** — Assurance that data is unaltered.
3. **Authentication** — Assurance that a communicating entity is who it claims to be.
4. **Authorization / Access Control** — Prevention of unauthorized use of resources.
5. **Non-Repudiation** — Protection against denial by either party of having participated.

Optional extension services: **Availability** service (e.g., via replication, backups) and **Data Origin Authentication**.

---

## 2.4 X.800 Security Mechanisms

Mechanisms are the *building blocks* used to implement services:

- **Encipherment (Encryption)** — Mathematical transformation using keys.
- **Digital Signature** — Binds identity to data.
- **Access Control** — Enforces permission rules.
- **Data Integrity** — Hashes (MD5, SHA-256).
- **Authentication Exchange** — Challenge-response protocols.
- **Traffic Padding** — Inserting dummy traffic to defeat traffic analysis.
- **Routing Control** — Selecting secure routes.
- **Notarization** — Trusted third party validates communications.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While Network Security Overview is largely conceptual, the following metrics appear in numerical/analytical KTU questions.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | $T$ = Threat likelihood, $V$ = Vulnerability, $I$ = Impact | Dimensionless score (qualitative) |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | SLE = Single Loss Expectancy, ARO = Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Currency (INR/USD) |
| **Single Loss Expectancy** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | EF = fraction of asset lost in one breach | Currency |
| **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** | $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}$ | Measures cost-effectiveness of a control | Ratio or % |
| **Encryption Strength** | $K = 2^k$ | $k$ = key length in bits, $K$ = number of possible keys | Keyspace size |
| **Average Time to Exhaust Keyspace** | $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{K}{2 \times R}$ | $R$ = guesses per second (assumes brute-force at rate $R$) | Seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` notation for any absolute-value or conditional operators inside tables. KTU graders will deduct marks for malformed markdown that breaks table rendering.

---

## 2.6 Network Security Model — The Layered Defense Paradigm

A formal general model of network security interaction:

1. **Sender (Alice)** has a *message* $M$ she wishes to send to **Receiver (Bob)**.
2. A **security-related transformation** $f(M, K)$ is applied to $M$ using a **key** $K$ to produce a *ciphertext* $C$.
3. $C$ is transmitted over an **insecure channel** where a **third party (Trudy / adversary)** may attempt to:
   - **Intercept** $C$ (passive attack)
   - **Modify** $C$ (active attack)
   - **Inject** new traffic (active attack)
4. Bob receives $C'$ and applies the **inverse transformation** $f^{-1}(C', K)$ to recover $M$ (or detects tampering).
5. A **trusted third party** may mediate key distribution or notarization.

> [!NOTE]
> The letters **Alice, Bob, and Trudy** are the canonical cryptographic placeholders used in the seminal *Handbook of Applied Cryptography* and are universally expected in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since "Overview of Network Security" is foundational and conceptual, the analytical work here focuses on **quantitative risk analysis** and **encryption keyspace mathematics** — both of which are testable in the 14-mark KTU ESE.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) — A Standard KTU 14-Mark Problem

The ALE framework allows an organization to make a *rational economic decision* about which security controls to deploy.

### Step 1 — Define the Asset Value ($AV$)

The total monetary value of the resource being protected. Example: A customer database worth ₹1,00,00,000.

### Step 2 — Define the Exposure Factor ($EF$)

The fraction of the asset's value that is lost in a *single* breach event. Expressed as a decimal in $[0, 1]$. Example: A breach exposes 40% of records → $EF = 0.40$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy ($\text{SLE}$)

The expected monetary loss from a *single* occurrence of the risk.

$$\text{SLE} = AV \times EF$$

### Step 4 — Determine the Annualized Rate of Occurrence ($\text{ARO}$)

The estimated number of times per year the threat will materialize. Example: A phishing attack succeeds an average of 4 times per year → $\text{ARO} = 4$.

### Step 5 — Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy ($\text{ALE}$)

The expected annual monetary loss *without* a control.

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

### Step 6 — Compute ROSI of a Proposed Control

If a control costs $C$ per year and reduces the ALE to $\text{ALE}_{\text{new}}$, the Return on Security Investment is:

$$\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{(\text{ALE} - \text{ALE}_{\text{new}}) - C}{C}$$

If $\text{ROSI} > 0$, the control is economically justified.

---

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A KTU-affiliated campus hosts a student records database worth ₹50,00,000. A ransomware attack would encrypt 60% of records. Historical data shows such attacks occur twice per year. A proposed endpoint detection system (EDS) costs ₹8,00,000 annually and would reduce the exposure factor to 10% and the ARO to 0.5. Compute the SLE, ALE (before), ALE (after), and ROSI. Justify deployment.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Asset Value $AV = \text{₹}50,00,000$.

**Step 2:** $EF_{\text{before}} = 0.60$, $EF_{\text{after}} = 0.10$.

**Step 3:** Compute SLE before and after.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= AV \times EF_{\text{before}} \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
&= \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= AV \times EF_{\text{after}} \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 \\
&= \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** $\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = 2$, $\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = 0.5$.

**Step 5:** Compute ALE before and after.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}} \\
&= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 2 \\
&= \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{after}} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 \\
&= \text{₹}2{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Cost of control $C = \text{₹}8{,}00{,}000$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \frac{(\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - C}{C} \\
&= \frac{(60{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000) - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{57{,}50{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{49{,}50{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 6.1875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\text{ROSI} \approx 6.19$ (positive and substantial), deployment is **economically justified**. The control returns ₹6.19 for every ₹1 invested.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Encryption Keyspace and Brute-Force Resistance

### Step 1 — Keyspace Size

For a symmetric cipher with key length $k$ bits:

$$K = 2^k$$

This is the total number of possible keys.

### Step 2 — Average Brute-Force Time

A brute-force attacker on average tries half the keyspace before finding the key. At a rate of $R$ guesses/second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{2^k}{2 \times R} \\
&= \frac{2^{k-1}}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Worked Example

A 128-bit AES key attacked at $R = 10^9$ guesses/sec (1 billion/s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{2^{127}}{10^9} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx \frac{1.7 \times 10^{38}}{10^9} \\
&\approx 1.7 \times 10^{29} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 5.4 \times 10^{21} \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

The age of the universe is only $\approx 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years, so 128-bit AES is computationally infeasible to break by brute force.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. CIA Property Breached

| Attack Type | Confidentiality? | Integrity? | Availability? | Detection Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | ✓ Broken | — | — | High |
| Traffic Analysis | Partial | — | — | Very High |
| Masquerade | — | ✓ | — | Medium |
| Replay | — | ✓ | — | Medium |
| Message Modification | — | ✓ | — | Medium |
| DoS / DDoS | — | — | ✓ | Low–Medium |
| Malware Injection | Possible | ✓ | ✓ | Low–Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**: *"For each attack, identify which CIA property is violated and justify."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ALICE - SENDER"] -->|Message M| nodeB["SECURITY TRANSFORMATION f M K"]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    nodeB --> nodeC["NETWORK LAYER - IDS IPS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["HOST LAYER - ANTIVIRUS EDR"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["APPLICATION LAYER - WAF AUTHENTICATION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["DATA LAYER - ENCRYPTION ACCESS CONTROL"]

    nodeH -.->|Attempts bypass| nodeC

```

```mermaid

    nodeA --> nodeC["ACTIVE ATTACKS"]

```

## 4.5 The Risk Management Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["IDENTIFY ASSET"] --> nodeB["ASSIGN VALUE AV"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["IDENTIFY THREAT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["COMPUTE EXPOSURE FACTOR EF"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["COMPUTE SLE = AV x EF"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ESTIMATE ARO"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["COMPUTE ALE = SLE x ARO"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["COMPARE WITH CONTROL COST"]
    nodeH -->|ROSI greater than 0| nodeI["DEPLOY CONTROL"]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between passive and active network security attacks with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Monitors/eavesdrops on transmission *without altering* it | Modifies, fabricates, or disrupts data/system |
| **Goal** | Break **confidentiality** | Break **integrity, availability, or authentication** |
| **Detection** | Very difficult (no data change) | Easier (anomaly is visible) |
| **Example** | **Traffic analysis** of encrypted flows | **DoS attack** flooding a server |
| **Defense** | Prevention (encryption) | Detection + prevention (IDS/IPS, firewalls) |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for contrast, 1 for examples)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the CIA Triad and briefly explain each component in the context of network security.**

**Model Answer:**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security:
- **Confidentiality:** Protection of data from unauthorized disclosure. *Example:* TLS encryption of HTTPS traffic prevents eavesdropping.
- **Integrity:** Assurance that data has not been altered in transit. *Example:* SHA-256 hash comparison detects tampering.
- **Availability:** Ensures that authorized users can access resources when needed. *Example:* DDoS mitigation and server redundancy maintain uptime.

*(3 marks: 1 triad definition + 1 each for C, I, A)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **OSI Security Architecture (X.800 framework)** in detail. List and describe the **five major security services** defined by X.800. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **different types of active attacks** with suitable network-level examples. For each, identify which property of the CIA Triad is violated. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

The **X.800 Security Architecture** (ITU-T Recommendation, 1991) provides a systematic framework for security in OSI networks. It is structured around three core concepts:

1. **Security Attack** — Any action that compromises the security of information.
2. **Security Mechanism** — A process designed to detect, prevent, or recover from an attack.
3. **Security Service** — A service that enhances the security of data processing and information transfer. Services make use of mechanisms.

The architecture is layered above the existing OSI layers, providing security services to the application, transport, and network layers.

**The Five Security Services (X.800):**

1. **Data Confidentiality:** Protects against unauthorized disclosure of information. Includes connection confidentiality, connectionless confidentiality, selective-field confidentiality, and traffic-flow confidentiality.

2. **Data Integrity:** Assures that data is received exactly as sent, with no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay. Includes connection integrity with/without recovery and selective-field integrity.

3. **Authentication:** Provides assurance about the identity of the communicating entity — applies to both connection-oriented and connectionless transfers.

4. **Access Control (Authorization):** Prevents unauthorized use of resources. Includes permission granting, revocation, and enforcement.

5. **Non-Repudiation:** Protects against false denial by either the sender (non-repudiation of origin) or the receiver (non-repudiation of receipt) of having participated in a communication.

> **Marks Breakdown:** 2 marks for the three concepts, 1 mark for the X.800 origin, and 1 mark each for the five services (5 marks).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

Active attacks attempt to **alter system resources or affect their operation**. They fall into four canonical categories:

1. **Masquerade:** An entity pretends to be a different entity. *Network example:* **IP spoofing** — attacker sends packets with a forged source IP to bypass IP-based access controls on a server. *CIA violated:* **Authentication** (and potentially integrity).

2. **Replay:** An attacker captures a valid data unit and retransmits it to produce an unauthorized effect. *Network example:* **Session token replay** — capturing a valid authentication cookie and reusing it to gain access. *CIA violated:* **Integrity** and **Authentication**.

3. **Modification of Messages:** Some portion of a legitimate message is altered, or messages are delayed/reordered. *Network example:* **Man-in-the-middle (MITM)** altering bank transfer details in transit. *CIA violated:* **Integrity** (and confidentiality if TLS is absent).

4. **Denial of Service (DoS):** Prevents or impairs the normal use/management of communication facilities. *Network example:* **SYN flood attack** exhausting a server's connection table. *CIA violated:* **Availability**.

5. **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS):** Coordinated DoS from many sources. *Example:* Botnet-driven UDP amplification. *CIA violated:* **Availability**.

> **Marks Breakdown:** 1 mark each for 4 attack types + examples, 1 mark for DDoS extension, 2 marks for CIA mapping, 1 mark for diagram (network-level illustration).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define **Network Security**. With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **general network security model** involving Alice, Bob, and a trusted third party. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A fintech company has a customer transaction database valued at ₹2,00,00,000. A SQL injection attack would expose 50% of records, and historical data shows 3 incidents per year. A proposed Web Application Firewall (WAF) costs ₹5,00,000/year and is projected to reduce the exposure factor to 5% and the ARO to 0.2. Calculate the **SLE, ALE (before deployment), ALE (after deployment), and ROSI**. Recommend whether to deploy. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Definition:** Network Security is the set of policies, cryptographic mechanisms, and procedural controls designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data and resources transmitted across or stored within computer networks.

**General Model (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**

1. **Alice (Sender)** holds message $M$.
2. A **security transformation** $f(M, K)$ is applied with key $K$ to produce ciphertext $C$.
3. $C$ is transmitted over the **insecure channel**.
4. **Trudy (Adversary)** may attempt to intercept, modify, or inject traffic.
5. **Bob (Receiver)** receives $C'$ and applies the inverse transformation $f^{-1}(C', K)$ to recover $M$.
6. A **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** is responsible for secure **key distribution** to both Alice and Bob out-of-band.

> **Marks:** 2 definition, 3 diagram with labels, 2 explanation of components.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** $AV = \text{₹}2,00,00,000$; $EF_{\text{before}} = 0.50$; $EF_{\text{after}} = 0.05$; $\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = 3$; $\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = 0.2$; $C = \text{₹}5,00,000$.

**Step 1 — SLE before:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 \\
&= \text{₹}1{,}00{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — SLE after:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 \\
&= \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — ALE before:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 3 \\
&= \text{₹}3{,}00{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — ALE after:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2 \\
&= \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — ROSI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \frac{(3{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000) - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{2{,}98{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{2{,}93{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 58.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\text{ROSI} = 58.6$ → the WAF returns ₹58.6 for every ₹1 invested. **Deployment is strongly recommended.**

> **Marks:** 1 each for SLE before/after, ALE before/after (4 marks), 2 marks for ROSI computation, 1 mark for recommendation with justification.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Conflating passive and active attacks.** Do NOT say "eavesdropping modifies the data" — it does not. Passive attacks are *read-only*.
> 2. **Confusing "threat" with "attack."** A threat is *potential*; an attack is *realized*. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Skipping the X.800 diagram in 14-mark answers.** A labelled block diagram of the X.800 architecture or the Alice–Bob–Trudy model is worth **2–3 marks by itself**.
> 4. **Mathematical errors in ALE/ROSI problems.** Double-check: SLE is computed *per incident*, ALE is per *year*. Mixing these yields zero credit.
> 5. **Omitting CIA mapping for attacks.** KTU rubrics explicitly award 1–2 marks for stating which CIA property is violated by a given attack.
> 6. **Not justifying the recommendation** in risk-analysis questions. A numerical answer without a "Deploy / Do not deploy" conclusion loses the final mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the core evaluation framework.
- [ ] **X.800 Architecture** = Attack → threatens CIA → protected by Services → realized via Mechanisms.
- [ ] **Five X.800 Services** = Data Confidentiality, Data Integrity, Authentication, Access Control, Non-Repudiation.
- [ ] **Passive attacks** = eavesdropping, traffic analysis; hard to detect; defense = encryption.
- [ ] **Active attacks** = masquerade, replay, modification, DoS/DDoS; easier to detect; defense = IDS/IPS, firewalls.
- [ ] **Alice / Bob / Trudy** = the canonical cryptographic actors in the network security model.
- [ ] **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** = mediates key distribution and notarization.
- [ ] **Risk Equation** = $R = T \times V \times I$.
- [ ] **ALE Equation** = $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$.
- [ ] **SLE Equation** = $\text{SLE} = AV \times EF$.
- [ ] **ROSI Equation** = $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{(\text{ALE} - \text{ALE}_{\text{new}}) - C}{C}$.
- [ ] **Keyspace size** = $K = 2^k$; **Average brute-force time** = $T_{\text{avg}} = 2^{k-1} / R$.
- [ ] **Defense in Depth** = layered security (perimeter → network → host → application → data).
- [ ] **Vulnerability ≠ Threat ≠ Attack ≠ Risk** — these four terms are *not* synonyms.
- [ ] Always **draw a labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers — it is essentially free marks.
- [ ] Always **map each attack to a CIA violation** when answering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 — Overview of Network Security notes. You are now ready for Module 2, which typically covers the OSI Security Architecture and Cryptographic Foundations in deeper technical detail.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Threats and Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Threats and Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention (IDP)**, a **threat** is defined as any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system, organization, or individual. A threat is characterized by three primary attributes: the **source** (origin), the **target** (asset), and the **capability** (means). An **attack**, on the other hand, is the *realization* or *materialization* of a threat — it is the concrete sequence of actions taken to compromise the **confidentiality, integrity, or availability (CIA Triad)** of an information asset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Core Distinction:**
> A **Threat** is a *possibility* (latent danger). An **Attack** is an *event* (realized action).
> Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) are designed to detect attacks; Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) are designed to block attacks.

The threat landscape is formally classified by NIST SP 800-30 (the standard KTU examiners expect) into:
- **Threat Source** — the origin of the threat (human, natural, environmental).
- **Threat Event** — the actualization (e.g., phishing, DDoS, malware delivery).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your home (your computer system):

- A **threat** is like a burglar who *might* exist somewhere in your city. You do not know exactly where, but you know the risk is there.
- An **attack** is when that burglar *actually drives down your street and tries your door handle*.
- An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is the *motion-sensor alarm* on your porch — it screams when the burglar tries the handle.
- An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is the *reinforced door with a deadbolt* — it does not just scream, it physically stops the burglar from entering.

A **vulnerability** is the *unlocked window*. A threat exploits a vulnerability to launch an attack. This triad — *Threat + Vulnerability ⇒ Attack ⇒ Impact* — is the foundational risk equation every KTU paper will test.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA Triad (Must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Confidentiality** — keeping data secret from unauthorized users.
> - **Integrity** — ensuring data is not tampered with.
> - **Availability** — ensuring systems are accessible when needed.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Metric | Standard Value / Definition | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | **Minutes to Hours** | Time between attack initiation and IDS alert. |
| **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** | **Minutes to Days** | Time between alert and full containment. |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ | Legitimate traffic wrongly flagged as malicious. |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $$\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$$ | Malicious traffic missed by the IDS. |
| **Zero-Day** | **No known signature** | Attack exploiting an unpatched vulnerability. |

The **threat level** in any organization is calculated as:

$$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **risk equation** above. Memorize the three multiplicative components: **Threat × Vulnerability × Impact**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Taxonomy of Threats (Structured Logic)

Threats are classified along **four orthogonal axes** that KTU 2024 scheme explicitly mandates:

### Axis 1 — By Source (Origin)
- **Insider Threats:** Originate from within the trusted boundary (employees, contractors, partners). They have legitimate access, making them the most damaging.
- **Outsider Threats:** Originate from outside the organization (hackers, nation-states, script kiddies).
- **Third-Party / Supply Chain Threats:** Compromise of a trusted vendor, software update, or hardware component (e.g., SolarWinds attack 2020).

### Axis 2 — By Intent
- **Unstructured Threats:** Opportunistic, low-skill actors using readily available tools (e.g., off-the-shelf malware, Wi-Fi crackers).
- **Structured Threats:** Coordinated, planned, resourced campaigns (e.g., Advanced Persistent Threats — APTs).

### Axis 3 — By Opportunity
- **Intentional Threats:** Deliberate, goal-oriented (espionage, sabotage, fraud).
- **Unintentional Threats:** Accidental (misconfiguration, human error, fat-finger incidents).

### Axis 4 — By Capability
- **Passive Threats:** Eavesdropping without altering data (sniffing, traffic analysis).
- **Active Threats:** Modifying, fabricating, or destroying data (MITM, injection).

## 2.2 Operational Taxonomy of Attacks

According to the **STRIDE model** (Microsoft's threat-modeling standard) and KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Attack Class | CIA Property Violated | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Spoofing** | Authentication | IP spoofing, DNS spoofing, email spoofing |
| **Tampering** | Integrity | SQL injection, binary patching, log tampering |
| **Repudiation** | Non-repudiation | Audit log deletion, transaction denial |
| **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality | Shoulder surfing, sniffing, exfiltration |
| **Denial of Service (DoS)** | Availability | SYN flood, ping-of-death, botnet DDoS |
| **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization | Buffer overflow → root, UAC bypass |

## 2.3 The Attack Lifecycle (Cyber Kill Chain — Lockheed Martin)

The 7-stage kill chain is a KTU high-yield topic:

1. **Reconnaissance** — Passive/active info gathering (WHOIS, Shodan, social media).
2. **Weaponization** — Coupling malware with an exploit (e.g., PDF + CVE).
3. **Delivery** — Transmission vector (email, USB, watering hole).
4. **Exploitation** — Triggering the vulnerability in the target system.
5. **Installation** — Persistence mechanisms (registry keys, cron jobs, rootkits).
6. **Command & Control (C2)** — Establishing outbound channel (HTTP, DNS tunnel).
7. **Actions on Objectives (Exfiltration/Destruction)** — Data theft, ransomware detonation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for IDP:** An IDS should ideally detect an attack in *Stage 1 or 2* — early detection drastically reduces the MTTR and the cost of breach (IBM reports: early-stage containment is **~5× cheaper**).

## 2.4 Passive vs. Active Attacks — The Fundamental Distinction

A **passive attack** is a *violation of confidentiality only*. The system continues to function normally; the attacker merely *observes*. Detection is extremely difficult.

An **active attack** is a *violation of integrity and/or availability*. The attacker alters the system, its data, or its operations. Detection is comparatively easier through behavioral analysis.

**Kerkhoff's Principle (1883, still binding in 2024):**
$$\text{Security} \neq \text{Secrecy of Algorithm}$$
$$\text{Security} = \text{Secrecy of Key} + \text{Public Algorithm Auditability}$$

Modern cryptography assumes the attacker knows the algorithm — only the **key** is secret.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Application Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Magnitude** | $$R = T \times V \times I$$ | Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact |
| **False Positive Rate** | $$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ | IDS evaluation metric |
| **False Negative Rate** | $$\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$$ | IDS evaluation metric (most critical) |
| **Detection Rate (Recall)** | $$\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ | Also called *sensitivity* |
| **Precision** | $$\text{P} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ | Reliability of IDS alerts |
| **Accuracy** | $$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$ | Overall correctness |
| **F1-Score** | $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot \text{TPR}}{P + \text{TPR}}$$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| **Shannon Entropy (anomaly detection)** | $$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$ | Used in entropy-based IDSes to flag encrypted exfiltration |
| **Birthday Bound (collision)** | $$\sqrt{\pi \cdot 2^{n}/2}$$ | Probability of hash collision in n-bit space |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The risk equation is **multiplicative**, not additive. If any one of T, V, I is zero, the risk is zero. This is why complete elimination of vulnerabilities (e.g., patch management) is more effective than reducing threat probability.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Analysts** (Security Operations Center) use the kill chain to triage alerts.
- **Penetration Testers** (red teams) follow the same attack lifecycle to legally test defenses.
- **AI/ML-based IDSes** use the F1-score and entropy metrics to evaluate model performance.
- **Insurance Underwriters** (cyber-insurance) use the risk equation to compute premiums.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 emphasizes **threat modeling** as an engineering discipline — not just an abstract concept. STRIDE and the Kill Chain are *frameworks* an engineer *applies* to design a system.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Shannon Entropy for Anomaly Detection

In **entropy-based IDS**, network traffic is modeled as a stochastic process. Each source IP, destination port, or payload byte is treated as a symbol in a probability distribution. The Shannon entropy quantifies the *disorder* or *randomness* of that distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> An IDS monitors the **information-theoretic entropy** of network traffic. Encrypted exfiltration or DDoS traffic has abnormally *high* entropy (uniform distribution), while normal business traffic has *low* entropy (Zipfian distribution).

**Derivation of Shannon Entropy:**

Let $X$ be a discrete random variable representing a network attribute (e.g., a destination port). It takes values $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ with probabilities $p(x_1), p(x_2), \ldots, p(x_n)$.

The **self-information** of a single event $x_i$ is defined as:

$$I(x_i) = -\log_2 p(x_i)$$

The reasoning: an event with probability 1 (certainty) carries 0 information, while an event with probability approaching 0 carries infinite information (a huge surprise).

The **expected self-information** over the entire distribution is the **Shannon Entropy**:

$$H(X) = E[I(X)] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot I(x_i)$$

Substituting $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p(x_i)$:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

$$H(X) = 0 \iff \exists i : p(x_i) = 1 \text{ (deterministic)}$$

$$H(X) = \log_2 n \iff p(x_i) = \frac{1}{n} \quad \forall i \text{ (uniform distribution)}$$

**Engineering Application Step:**

1. Bin the destination ports observed in a time window $W$ (say, 60 seconds).
2. Count occurrences: $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n$ where $\sum c_i = N$.
3. Estimate probabilities: $\hat{p}(x_i) = \frac{c_i}{N}$.
4. Compute the empirical entropy:

$$\hat{H}(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{c_i}{N} \log_2 \frac{c_i}{N}$$

5. Compare $\hat{H}(X)$ against a baseline threshold $\tau$. If $\hat{H}(X) > \tau$, raise an **anomaly alert**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Examiner's note: students frequently confuse **Shannon entropy** (information theory) with **thermodynamic entropy**. They are mathematically analogous but conceptually distinct. In IDP, only Shannon's formulation is used.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — IDS Confusion Matrix Metrics

The detection performance of an IDS is evaluated using a **2×2 confusion matrix**. Let the model classify $N$ traffic events:

- **True Positive (TP):** Malicious traffic correctly classified as malicious.
- **True Negative (TN):** Legitimate traffic correctly classified as legitimate.
- **False Positive (FP):** Legitimate traffic wrongly flagged as malicious (Type I error).
- **False Negative (FN):** Malicious traffic wrongly classified as legitimate (Type II error).

The **detection rate** (also called *sensitivity* or *recall*) measures how many of the actual attacks the IDS catches:

$$\text{Detection Rate} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

The **false alarm rate** (or false positive rate) measures how many of the legitimate events are falsely alarmed:

$$\text{FAR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

The **precision** measures the reliability of an IDS alert — if it raises an alarm, how likely is it to be a real attack?

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

The **accuracy** is the overall fraction of correct classifications:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing both:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

Substituting the definitions:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The F1-Score is preferred over accuracy when the dataset is **imbalanced** (e.g., in network traffic, malicious packets are typically $< 1\%$ of total).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Threat vs. Attack Categorization

| Threat Source | Threat Type | Realized Attack | Detection Difficulty | KTU Difficulty Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Insider | Malicious | Privilege abuse, data theft | **High** (has credentials) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Insider | Accidental | Misconfiguration, data leak | Medium | ⭐⭐ |
| Outsider | Unstructured | Virus, Wi-Fi cracking, defacement | Low | ⭐ |
| Outsider | Structured (APT) | Spear-phishing, lateral movement | **Very High** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Supply Chain | Injected backdoor | SolarWinds-type compromise | **Very High** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Botnet | Distributed DoS | Mirai-style DDoS | Medium | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Nation-State | Cyber-espionage | Zero-day exploitation | **Extreme** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Risk Calculation

**Problem:** A KTU 2024 question states: "An organization rates its *Threat probability* as **0.6** (on a 0–1 scale), *Vulnerability severity* as **0.8**, and *Impact* as **0.9**. Calculate the risk score."

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.** The standard risk equation is multiplicative:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$R = 0.6 \times 0.8 \times 0.9$$

**Step 3 — Compute step by step.**

First, $0.6 \times 0.8 = 0.48$.

Then, $0.48 \times 0.9 = 0.432$.

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

$$R = 0.432 \quad \text{(on a 0–1 scale, this is "Moderate-High" risk)}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering recommendation.** Since $R > 0.4$, the organization should implement controls: an IDS at the perimeter, multi-factor authentication, and quarterly vulnerability scans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The risk score is **dimensionless** in this formulation. Real-world risk frameworks (FAIR, NIST 800-30) express Risk in monetary terms (Annual Loss Expectancy, ALE):

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$
$$\text{ALE} = (\text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}) \times \text{Annual Rate of Occurrence}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Threat-Attack Conceptual Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vulnerability in Target System] --> B[Threat Exists Latent]
    B --> C[Attacker Motivated and Capable]
    C --> D[Attack Launched]
    D --> E{Attack Succeeds?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Compromise of CIA Triad]
    E -- No --> G[IDS IPS Block Detected]
    F --> H[Impact Data Loss Reputation Damage]
    G --> I[Alert Logged SOC Triage]
    H --> J[Post Incident Forensics and Patching]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Vulnerability Mitigated System Hardened]
```

## 4.2 STRIDE Threat Model Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[S - Spoofing] --> CIA1[Authentication Violated]
    T[T - Tampering] --> CIA2[Integrity Violated]
    R[R - Repudiation] --> CIA3[Non Repudiation Violated]
    I1[I - Information Disclosure] --> CIA4[Confidentiality Violated]
    D[D - Denial of Service] --> CIA5[Availability Violated]
    E[E - Elevation of Privilege] --> CIA6[Authorization Violated]
    CIA1 --> Defense1[PKI MFA Certificates]
    CIA2 --> Defense2[Hashing Digital Signatures]
    CIA3 --> Defense3[Audit Logs Timestamping]
    CIA4 --> Defense4[Encryption Access Control]
    CIA5 --> Defense5[Rate Limiting Redundancy]
    CIA6 --> Defense6[Least Privilege RBAC]
```

## 4.3 Cyber Kill Chain — Attack Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Stage 1 Reconnaissance OSINT] --> K2[Stage 2 Weaponization Exploit plus Malware]
    K2 --> K3[Stage 3 Delivery Email USB Watering Hole]
    K3 --> K4[Stage 4 Exploitation Trigger Vulnerability]
    K4 --> K5[Stage 5 Installation Persistence]
    K5 --> K6[Stage 6 Command and Control Channel]
    K6 --> K7[Stage 7 Actions on Objectives Exfiltration Destruction]
    K1 -.-> IDS1[IDS should detect here EARLY]
    K4 -.-> IDS2[IDS may detect here MIDWAY]
    K7 -.-> IDS3[Detection is TOO LATE]
```

## 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Layered Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Internet Untrusted Network] --> L1[Layer 1 Firewall Perimeter]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 IDS IPS Signature plus Anomaly]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 DMZ plus Reverse Proxy]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Internal Firewall Segmentation]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Host based HIDS EDR]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Application Layer WAF plus Input Validation]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Data Layer Encryption plus DLP]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8 Audit Logs and SIEM Correlation]
```

## 4.5 Attack Classification Block Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AA[Cyber Attacks] --> AB{Modifies Data or System?}
    AB -- No --> AC[Passive Attacks]
    AC --> AD[Eavesdropping Traffic Analysis]
    AB -- Yes --> AE[Active Attacks]
    AE --> AF[Interruption DoS]
    AE --> AG[Interception Theft]
    AE --> AH[Modification Tampering]
    AE --> AI[Fabrication Spoofing]
    AC --> IDSDetect1[Hard to Detect]
    AE --> IDSDetect2[Easier to Detect via Anomaly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Key:**
> - **Solid arrows** = primary flow.
> - **Dashed arrows** = meta-annotation (where the IDS should/can act).
> - **Diamond nodes** = decision points.
> - **Rectangular nodes** = process/state.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a *Threat* and an *Attack* in the context of Intrusion Detection. Differentiate them with a suitable example. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Threat** is a potential cause of an unwanted incident that could harm a system or organization. It is a *latent* condition — the danger exists but has not yet materialized. A **Threat Source** may be human, natural, or environmental.

An **Attack** is the actual realization of a threat — the concrete sequence of malicious actions executed by an attacker to compromise confidentiality, integrity, or availability of a target system.

**Differentiation:**

| Aspect | Threat | Attack |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Latent (potential) | Realized (event) |
| Detection | Hard (predictive) | Easier (reactive) |
| Example | Existence of a software vulnerability (e.g., unpatched Apache server) | An attacker exploiting CVE-2021-41773 to execute arbitrary code |

A real-world analogy: A *threat* is a loaded gun in a public park; an *attack* is someone actually firing it.

---

### Question 2: List and briefly explain the **6 categories of the STRIDE threat model**. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The **STRIDE** model (Microsoft) classifies threats into six categories, each corresponding to a security property violation:

1. **S — Spoofing:** Falsifying identity (e.g., IP spoofing, email spoofing). Violates *Authentication*.
2. **T — Tampering:** Modifying data in transit or at rest (e.g., SQL injection). Violates *Integrity*.
3. **R — Repudiation:** Denying an action without proof (e.g., audit log deletion). Violates *Non-Repudiation*.
4. **I — Information Disclosure:** Unauthorized data exposure (e.g., sniffing). Violates *Confidentiality*.
5. **D — Denial of Service:** Disrupting system availability (e.g., SYN flood). Violates *Availability*.
6. **E — Elevation of Privilege:** Gaining higher access than authorized (e.g., buffer overflow → root). Violates *Authorization*.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Student attempts *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Cyber Kill Chain** model proposed by Lockheed Martin. Illustrate each stage with a real-world example and explain at which stage an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is most effective.

**Model Solution:**

The **Cyber Kill Chain** is a 7-stage framework describing the lifecycle of a sophisticated cyber attack. It is used by defenders to identify and disrupt attacks at the earliest possible stage.

**Stage 1 — Reconnaissance:** The attacker gathers information about the target using OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) tools such as WHOIS lookups, Shodan scans, and LinkedIn profiling. *Example:* The 2017 Equifax breach began with attackers scanning for the Apache Struts vulnerability (CVE-2017-5638) across Equifax's public-facing servers.

**Stage 2 — Weaponization:** The attacker couples a remote-access trojan (RAT) with an exploit payload into a deliverable vector, typically a weaponized document. *Example:* A malicious PDF that exploits Adobe Reader to install a backdoor.

**Stage 3 — Delivery:** The weaponized payload is transmitted to the victim. Common vectors include phishing emails, USB drops, and watering-hole attacks. *Example:* The 2013 Target breach was delivered via a phishing email to a third-party HVAC vendor (Fazio Mechanical Services).

**Stage 4 — Exploitation:** The vulnerability in the target system is triggered, allowing arbitrary code execution. *Example:* A stack-based buffer overflow in the target application granting shell access.

**Stage 5 — Installation:** The attacker installs persistence mechanisms — registry run keys, cron jobs, systemd services, or rootkits. *Example:* A Windows registry entry under `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` to restart the malware on reboot.

**Stage 6 — Command and Control (C2):** The compromised host establishes an outbound channel (often HTTPS or DNS tunneling) to the attacker's infrastructure. *Example:* Sunburst malware (SolarWinds, 2020) communicated via a subdomain masquerading as legitimate SolarWinds traffic.

**Stage 7 — Actions on Objectives:** The attacker achieves their goal — data exfiltration, ransomware detonation, or sabotage. *Example:* The 2017 NotPetya attack destroyed data on infected Maersk systems causing **\$300M in damages**.

**Where is the IDS most effective?**

The IDS is **most effective in Stages 1 and 3** (Reconnaissance and Delivery) because:
- Network-based IDS can detect port scans and anomalous DNS queries.
- Email security gateways can quarantine weaponized attachments.
- Detection at Stage 1 dramatically reduces breach cost (IBM: **\$1.2M saved per early detection**).
- Detection at Stage 7 is "too late" — the attacker has already executed their objective.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Passive** and **Active** attacks. Give two examples of each. Explain why passive attacks are harder to detect.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Attacker *observes* traffic without modification | Attacker *modifies* data, system, or operations |
| **CIA Property Violated** | Confidentiality only | Integrity and/or Availability |
| **System State** | Unchanged | Altered |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Very High (no artifacts) | Moderate (behavioral anomalies) |
| **Attacker Goal** | Information gathering | Direct harm or compromise |

**Examples of Passive Attacks:**

1. **Eavesdropping (Sniffing):** Using tools like Wireshark or tcpdump to capture unencrypted traffic on a LAN. The network functions normally; no traffic is altered.
2. **Traffic Analysis:** Even when payload is encrypted, an attacker can infer business activity by analyzing traffic volume, timing, and patterns (e.g., a spike in traffic to a competitor's domain at 3 AM).

**Examples of Active Attacks:**

1. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):** The attacker intercepts and *forwards* modified packets between two parties, e.g., SSL stripping on a public Wi-Fi.
2. **Denial of Service (DoS):** A SYN flood exhausts the target's connection table, making the service unavailable to legitimate users.

**Why are passive attacks harder to detect?**

1. **No data modification:** The system and its logs behave exactly as in normal operation — there is no integrity violation to trigger an alert.
2. **No resource consumption:** Unlike active attacks, passive observation does not consume CPU, bandwidth, or memory in a way that triggers anomaly detectors.
3. **No persistent artifact:** The attacker leaves no file, process, or registry entry — they only capture transient data.
4. **Encrypted channels:** Modern encryption (TLS 1.3) makes payload inspection impossible, leaving only metadata (timing, size, frequency) for analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A Type:**
> Students often write "passive attacks modify data" — this is **incorrect** and will attract **−1 to −2 marks deduction**. Passive attacks are read-only by definition. Also, students confuse "active" with "physical" — it is a logical property, not a physical one.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify threats based on **Source** and **Intent**. Explain the term **Advanced Persistent Threat (APT)** with a real-world example.

**Model Solution:**

**Classification by Source:**

1. **Insider Threats:** Originate from within the trusted boundary. Includes malicious employees, negligent contractors, and compromised third-party accounts with legitimate credentials.
2. **Outsider Threats:** Originate from outside the organization — external hackers, hacktivists, criminal gangs, nation-state actors.
3. **Third-Party / Supply Chain Threats:** Originate from a trusted upstream vendor whose product is compromised. *Example:* SolarWinds Orion (2020) where attackers injected SUNBURST malware into legitimate software updates.

**Classification by Intent:**

1. **Unstructured Threats:** Opportunistic attackers using off-the-shelf tools, lacking specific targeting. *Example:* Script kiddies running Mirai botnet to perform opportunistic DDoS.
2. **Structured Threats:** Highly organized, planned, and resourced campaigns. *Example:* A nation-state intelligence agency conducting multi-year espionage.

**Advanced Persistent Threat (APT):**

An **APT** is a prolonged, targeted, multi-stage cyber attack in which an intruder gains long-term access to a network and exfiltrates sensitive data while remaining undetected. The defining characteristics are:

- **Advanced:** Uses zero-day exploits, custom malware, and sophisticated evasion techniques.
- **Persistent:** Maintains long-term footholds (months to years).
- **Threat:** Coordinated, well-funded (often state-sponsored).

**Real-World Example — APT29 (Cozy Bear):**
APT29 is a Russian state-sponsored group that compromised the Democratic National Committee (DNC) networks in 2015–2016. They:
- Used spear-phishing emails to deliver malicious PowerShell payloads (Stage 3 — Delivery).
- Established persistence via scheduled tasks (Stage 5).
- Maintained C2 over HTTPS for over a year (Stage 6).
- Exfiltrated sensitive emails and documents (Stage 7).
- Remained undetected by traditional signature-based AV for over **12 months**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **Risk Equation**? A KTU-suggested scenario: An e-commerce website has a *Threat probability* of **0.7**, a *Vulnerability score* of **0.5**, and an *Impact* of **0.8**. Calculate the risk and recommend two engineering controls.

**Model Solution:**

The **Risk Equation** quantifies cybersecurity risk as the multiplicative product of three components:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

Where:
- $T$ = Threat probability (likelihood of a threat materializing)
- $V$ = Vulnerability score (likelihood of exploitation)
- $I$ = Impact (consequence severity)

**Calculation:**

Step 1 — Identify the values: $T = 0.7$, $V = 0.5$, $I = 0.8$.

Step 2 — Substitute into the equation:

$$R = 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.8$$

Step 3 — Compute $0.7 \times 0.5 = 0.35$.

Step 4 — Compute $0.35 \times 0.8 = 0.28$.

$$R = 0.28$$

Step 5 — **Interpretation:** On a 0–1 scale, a risk of 0.28 falls in the **"Moderate"** category (commonly: 0.0–0.2 Low, 0.2–0.5 Moderate, 0.5–0.8 High, 0.8–1.0 Critical).

**Two Engineering Controls:**

1. **Deploy a Web Application Firewall (WAF) with a Network-based IDS (NIDS):** Reduces the *Vulnerability* component by filtering SQL injection, XSS, and other OWASP Top-10 attacks before they reach the application layer. A signature-based IDS (e.g., Snort/Suricata) plus an anomaly-based IDS (e.g., Zeek) gives layered coverage.

2. **Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) and Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):** Reduces the *Threat* component (probability of successful credential-based attack) and limits the *Impact* by enforcing least privilege. Even if credentials are compromised, the blast radius is contained.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Type:**
> - **Do not write Risk as a sum** ($T + V + I$). It is **multiplicative** — losing 2 marks is common here.
> - **Do not omit units** when expressing risk in monetary terms (e.g., Annual Loss Expectancy in INR/USD).
> - **Do not forget to interpret** the numerical result (0.28 = Moderate) — examiners allocate 1 mark for interpretation alone.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Threat vs. Attack:** Threat = *latent*; Attack = *realized* event. Risk arises from Threat + Vulnerability + Impact.
- ✅ **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. Memorize which STRIDE letter maps to which.
- ✅ **STRIDE Model:** 6 letters — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege. KTU 2024 syllabus lists it as a *must-know* framework.
- ✅ **Cyber Kill Chain (7 stages):** Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions on Objectives. The IDS should fire **early** in the chain.
- ✅ **Passive vs. Active:** Passive = *observation only* (no modification, hard to detect). Active = *modification or disruption* (easier to detect via behavioral anomaly).
- ✅ **Risk Equation:** $R = T \times V \times I$ — *multiplicative, not additive*.
- ✅ **IDS Metrics:** Detection Rate (Recall) = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$; FPR = $\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$; F1-Score = harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- ✅ **Shannon Entropy:** $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$. Bounded between $0$ and $\log_2 n$. Used in entropy-based anomaly detection.
- ✅ **Insider Threats:** Most damaging — they have legitimate access. Detection requires User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA), not just perimeter IDS.
- ✅ **APT Characteristics:** Advanced, Persistent, Threat. Real-world examples: APT29 (Cozy Bear), APT28 (Fancy Bear), Lazarus Group.
- ✅ **Kerkhoff's Principle:** Assume the algorithm is public; security must rest solely on key secrecy.
- ✅ **Zero-Day:** No known patch or signature. Detected only by *anomaly-based* or *behavioral* IDSes, not signature-based ones.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** 8 layers (Perimeter → DMZ → IDS/IPS → Internal Firewall → HIDS → WAF → Data Encryption → SIEM Logs). No single layer is sufficient.
- ✅ **APT Lifecycle Difference:** An APT uses *multiple* kill chains over months/years, with re-entry at any stage — this is why detection rate > 90% is mandatory for high-assurance environments.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Types of Threats and Attacks**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Policies and Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Policies and Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Policy (ITU-T X.800 / RFC 4949 Standard Definition):**
> A **Security Policy** is a formal, documented set of rules, principles, and practices that govern how an organization manages, protects, and distributes sensitive information assets, and specifies the authorized states, actors, and operations permitted on a computing system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Mechanism (ITU-T X.800 Definition):**
> A **Security Mechanism** is a logical, software, or hardware-based control (process, device, or procedure) that is designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack, and is used to implement and enforce one or more security policies.

In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention (IDP)**, security policies define the *expected secure state*, while security mechanisms (firewalls, IDS/IPS, ACLs, encryption engines) are the *enforcement primitives* that observe the live system and compare its state against that policy.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **bank vault system**:

| Real-World Entity | Security Concept | Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Bank's written rulebook ("Only managers can open safe deposit boxes") | **Security Policy** | Defines *what* is allowed |
| The physical lock, biometric scanner, CCTV, alarm | **Security Mechanism** | Enforces *how* the rule is kept |
| A customer trying to pry open a box at 3 AM | **Intrusion / Attack** | The violation to be detected |
| The motion sensor that beeps and calls police | **IDS / IPS** | The detection + prevention engine |

Without a **policy**, mechanisms have no reference to decide whether an action is *good* or *bad*. Without **mechanisms**, a policy is just a piece of paper no one enforces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Triad Recap — The CIA Triad (Foundational Policy Goal)**
> - **C**onfidentiality — Prevent unauthorized disclosure
> - **I**ntegrity — Prevent unauthorized modification
> - **A**vailability — Ensure authorized access when needed
> Every security policy in an IDP context ultimately aims to protect one or more of these three properties, quantified using the standard metrics of **Confidentiality (C)**, **Integrity (I)**, and **Availability (A)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Security Policies

Security policies operate at three hierarchical layers:

1. **Organizational-Level (Master) Security Policy**
   - High-level, management-driven document.
   - Defines the organization's security goals, risk posture, and compliance scope (e.g., ISO 27001, NIST 800-53).
   - *Example:* "All customer PII must be encrypted at rest using AES-256."

2. **System-Level Security Policy**
   - Specific to a particular system, network, or application.
   - Translates the master policy into technical rules.
   - *Example:* "The IDS shall drop any outbound packet matching signature `SID 2024-0042`."

3. **Issue-Specific Security Policy**
   - Targeted at a specific technology, service, or threat vector.
   - *Examples:* Password Policy, Email Use Policy, Remote Access Policy, BYOD Policy, Incident Response Policy.

### 2.2 Access Control Policy Models (High-Yield for KTU)

| Model | Full Form | Decision Basis | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Owner's discretion (ACL on resource) | "D-ACL = Discretion + ACL" |
| **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | Labels (Clearance vs. Classification) | "M-AC = Military" |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Role assigned to user (e.g., Admin, User) | "R-BAC = Role-Based" |
| **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | Attributes of subject, object, action, environment | "A-BAC = Attribute" |

### 2.3 Security Mechanism Classes (Per ITU-T X.800)

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Security Mechanisms** (operate on specific OSI layers / services):
> - **Encipherment (Encryption / Decryption)** — mathematical transformation of data
> - **Digital Signature** — binds identity to a message
> - **Access Control** — enforces DAC / MAC / RBAC / ABAC
> - **Data Integrity** — checksum, hash, MAC
> - **Authentication Exchange** — proving identity (password, token, biometric)
> - **Traffic Padding** — adding dummy traffic to thwart traffic analysis
> - **Routing Control** — selecting secure routes for sensitive data
> - **Notarization** — trusted third party (TTP) certifies transactions

> [!NOTE]
> **Pervasive Security Mechanisms** (apply across multiple services):
> - **Trusted Functionality** — hardware/software trusted to enforce policy
> - **Security Labels** — markers bound to resources (used in MAC)
> - **Event Detection** — log security-relevant events (basis of IDP)
> - **Security Audit Trail** — chronological record for forensic analysis
> - **Security Recovery** — restoring a secure state post-incident

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` / `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol `|` inside table rows to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept / Formula | Mathematical Form | Use in IDP / Security |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shannon Entropy (information content) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_{2} p_i$ bits | Quantifies uncertainty / randomness of attack patterns |
| 2 | Redundancy in language | $R = 1 - \frac{H(X)}{H_{max}(X)}$ | Measures plaintext predictability for cryptanalysis |
| 3 | Encryption / Decryption (symmetric) | $C = E_{K}(M),\quad M = D_{K}(C)$ | Confidentiality of stored and transmitted data |
| 4 | Hash function output size | $h = H(M) ; \quad \vert h \vert = n \text{ bits}$ | Integrity verification (SHA-256 $\to$ $n = 256$ bits) |
| 5 | Digital signature | $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$ | Authentication + Non-repudiation |
| 6 | Birthday paradox (collision) | $P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-k^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{n})}$ | Strength of an $n$-bit hash against forgery |
| 7 | Access control tuple (Lampson model) | $A = (s, o, \pm)$ — subject, object, right | Foundation of authorization in IDP |
| 8 | Bell-LaPadula confidentiality | $\text{read} \le \text{level},\ \text{write} \ge \text{level}$ | No read-up, no write-down (MAC policy) |
| 9 | Biba integrity model | $\text{read} \ge \text{level},\ \text{write} \le \text{level}$ | No read-down, no write-up (integrity policy) |
| 10 | Risk quantification | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Basis of every policy decision |

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

Every deployed **IDS/IPS** (Snort, Suricata, Zeek, Cisco Firepower) operates on the principle of **policy-mechanism duality**: the **rule-set** is the policy; the **packet-processing engine** is the mechanism. A misconfigured policy on a perfect mechanism still causes breaches, and vice versa. KTU examiners frequently test this duality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shannon Entropy of an Attack Source

Consider an attacker $X$ choosing between $n$ attack vectors with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$. We want to quantify the **average uncertainty** (information gained by detecting one attack) in **bits**.

**Step 1 — Self-information of a single event.**
If event $i$ occurs with probability $p_i$, the information content (surprise) is inversely proportional to $p_i$:

$$I(x_i) = \log_{2}\left(\frac{1}{p_i}\right) \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Weighted average over all possible events.**
The expected self-information (entropy) is the sum of each event's surprise multiplied by its probability:

$$H(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot I(x_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot \log_{2}\left(\frac{1}{p_i}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Pull the negative sign out of the logarithm.**
Multiplying $\log_{2}(1/p_i)$ by $p_i$ and reversing the argument gives the canonical Shannon form:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot \log_{2}\left(\frac{1}{p_i}\right) \\
     &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot \left(-\log_{2}(p_i)\right) \\
     &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_{2}(p_i) \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.**
- $H(X) = 0$ when one $p_i = 1$ (no uncertainty — deterministic attacker).
- $H(X) = \log_{2}(n)$ when all $p_i = 1/n$ (uniform — maximum uncertainty).
- $0 \le H(X) \le \log_{2}(n)$.

**Engineering Utility:** A higher entropy attack pattern is *harder to predict*, so the IDS must use broader anomaly-detection techniques (statistical / ML) rather than strict signatures. This is precisely the policy threshold that decides whether to deploy signature-based vs. anomaly-based detection.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Collision Probability of an n-bit Hash (Birthday Bound)

The IDP engine uses hashes to verify packet/file integrity. We derive the probability that an attacker produces **two messages with the same hash** after $k$ trials.

**Step 1 — Probability of NO collision in a single trial.**
The second message must avoid the first hash: probability $= 1 - 1/2^n$.

**Step 2 — Probability of NO collision after $k$ trials (independence approximation).**

$$P(\text{no collision}) \approx \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{2^n}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Use the Taylor expansion $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$ for small $x$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{no collision}) &\approx \exp\left(-\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{i}{2^n}\right) \\
                       &= \exp\left(-\frac{k(k-1)}{2 \cdot 2^n}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore, collision probability.**

$$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-k^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{n})}$$

**Rule of thumb:** collision probability reaches **0.5** when $k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$. For SHA-256 ($n=256$), this is astronomically large ($\approx 2^{128}$ trials) — confirming why SHA-256 is the standard for IDS integrity checks.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Policy-to-Mechanism Mapping (Common KTU Question Type)

| Security Policy Requirement | Required Mechanism | Layer of Operation | Example Tool / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality of stored data | Encipherment (Encryption) | Application / Storage | AES-256, RSA |
| Confidentiality of transmitted data | Encipherment + Routing Control | Network / Transport | TLS 1.3, IPsec |
| User identity proof | Authentication Exchange | Session / Application | Kerberos, OAuth 2.0, SAML |
| Authorized access only | Access Control (DAC/MAC/RBAC) | OS / Middleware | SELinux (MAC), Windows ACL (DAC) |
| Detect ongoing attacks | Event Detection + Security Audit | Network / Host | Snort, Suricata, OSSEC |
| Block ongoing attacks | Security Recovery / IPS | Network / Host | Snort-inline, Cisco Firepower |
| Verify message untouched | Data Integrity (Hash / MAC) | Network / Application | SHA-256, HMAC-SHA256 |
| Prove message origin | Digital Signature | Application | RSA-PSS, ECDSA |
| Forensics after incident | Security Audit Trail | All layers | SIEM (Splunk, ELK) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Policy–Mechanism Duality Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ORGANIZATIONAL MASTER POLICY] --> B[SYSTEM LEVEL SECURITY POLICY]
    A --> C[ISSUE SPECIFIC POLICIES]
    B --> D[ACCESS CONTROL POLICY]
    B --> E[NETWORK SECURITY POLICY]
    B --> F[DATA CLASSIFICATION POLICY]
    C --> G[PASSWORD POLICY]
    C --> H[INCIDENT RESPONSE POLICY]
    C --> I[ACCEPTABLE USE POLICY]
    D --> J[DAC MAC RBAC ABAC]
    E --> K[FIREWALL POLICY]
    E --> L[IDS IPS POLICY]
    F --> M[ENCRYPTION STANDARD]
    J --> N[PERMISSIONS AND LABELS]
    K --> O[PACKET FILTER ENGINE]
    L --> P[SIGNATURE ENGINE]
    L --> Q[ANOMALY ENGINE]
    M --> R[AES ENGINE]
    N --> S[ENFORCEMENT LAYER]
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[SECURE SYSTEM STATE]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: ITU-T X.800 Security Mechanism Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SPECIFIC_MECHANISMS
        X1[ENCIPHERMENT]
        X2[DIGITAL SIGNATURE]
        X3[ACCESS CONTROL]
        X4[DATA INTEGRITY]
        X5[AUTHENTICATION EXCHANGE]
        X6[TRAFFIC PADDING]
        X7[ROUTING CONTROL]
        X8[NOTARIZATION]
    end
    subgraph PERVASIVE_MECHANISMS
        Y1[TRUSTED FUNCTIONALITY]
        Y2[SECURITY LABELS]
        Y3[EVENT DETECTION]
        Y4[SECURITY AUDIT TRAIL]
        Y5[SECURITY RECOVERY]
    end
    X3 --> Z1[IDS]
    X3 --> Z2[FIREWALL]
    X3 --> Z3[OS ACCESS CONTROL]
    X4 --> Z4[HASH ENGINE]
    X5 --> Z5[AUTH SERVER]
    Y3 --> Z1
    Y4 --> Z6[SIEM]
    Y5 --> Z7[IPS RESPONSE MODULE]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: IDP Workflow Showing Policy as Reference State

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[DEFINED SECURITY POLICY] --> R1[POLICY RULESET IN IDP]
    N1[NETWORK PACKET OR HOST EVENT] --> C1[COLLECTION SENSOR]
    C1 --> A1[PREPROCESSOR NORMALIZATION]
    A1 --> D1{DETECTION ENGINE}
    D1 -- MATCH --> V1[ALERT GENERATION]
    D1 -- NO MATCH --> E1[EVENT LOGGED]
    R1 --> D1
    V1 --> P2{INLINE PREVENTION}
    P2 -- YES IPS --> B1[DROP OR BLOCK PACKET]
    P2 -- NO IDS --> B2[FORWARD ALERT TO SIEM]
    B1 --> B3[SECURITY AUDIT TRAIL]
    B2 --> B3
    E1 --> B3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Security Policy. Differentiate between an Organizational Security Policy and a System-Level Security Policy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A Security Policy is a formal document that specifies the rules, practices, and authorized operations governing the protection of an organization's information assets (per ITU-T X.800).
- **Organizational Policy (1 mark):** Management-driven, high-level, scope-wide document that defines the overall security goals, risk tolerance, and compliance framework (e.g., ISO 27001, NIST 800-53). It is technology-agnostic.
- **System-Level Policy (1 mark):** Technology-specific document that operationalizes the organizational policy for a particular system or network, e.g., "The IDS sensor must drop any packet matching signature SID 1001234." It is technology-specific and implementation-oriented.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six security mechanisms defined in the ITU-T X.800 standard. Classify them as specific or pervasive.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Six mechanisms, each with classification (0.5 mark each):*

| # | Mechanism | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Encipherment (Encryption) | Specific |
| 2 | Digital Signature | Specific |
| 3 | Access Control | Specific |
| 4 | Data Integrity | Specific |
| 5 | Authentication Exchange | Specific |
| 6 | Event Detection | Pervasive |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module-1 questions carry 14 marks each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Solve either Option A or Option B.**

---

#### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **CIA Triad** as the foundational security policy model. How do confidentiality, integrity, and availability translate into specific mechanisms in an IDP deployment? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Bell-LaPadula** and **Biba** models. Show mathematically how they enforce confidentiality and integrity policies respectively. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The CIA Triad (7 marks):**
- **Confidentiality (2 marks):** Ensures information is disclosed only to authorized subjects. In IDP: encryption (AES, RSA), access control lists, traffic padding to prevent traffic analysis, and confidentiality-based MLS rules in MAC.
- **Integrity (2 marks):** Ensures data is not modified in unauthorized ways. In IDP: cryptographic hash (SHA-256), HMAC, digital signatures, and Biba-model MAC labels. IDP engines verify file integrity using AIDE or Tripwire mechanisms.
- **Availability (2 marks):** Ensures systems are accessible when needed. In IDP: rate-limiting, DDoS mitigation (e.g., scrubbing centers), redundant paths (routing control), and IPS drop rules to neutralize volumetric attacks.
- **Synthesis (1 mark):** The CIA triad is the *policy reference state* against which every IDP alert is evaluated.

**(b) Bell-LaPadula (BLP) and Biba Models (7 marks):**

*Bell-LaPadula — Confidentiality (3.5 marks):*

The BLP model uses clearance levels (Unclassified < Confidential < Secret < Top Secret) and the simple security and star properties:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{No Read-Up (NRU):} \quad & \text{subject clearance} \ge \text{object classification} \\
\text{No Write-Down (NWD):} \quad & \text{subject clearance} \le \text{object classification}
\end{aligned}$$

*Worked Example:* A "Secret"-cleared user may read "Confidential" or "Secret" files (NRU satisfied), but cannot write to a "Confidential" file because that would leak Secret data downward (NWD enforced).

*Biba — Integrity (3.5 marks):*

Biba inverts the axis to protect integrity levels (e.g., Untrusted < User < Trusted < Critical):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{No Read-Down (NRD):} \quad & \text{subject integrity} \le \text{object integrity} \\
\text{No Write-Up (NWU):} \quad & \text{subject integrity} \ge \text{object integrity}
\end{aligned}$$

*Worked Example:* A "Trusted" system process may not read from an "Untrusted" source (prevents contamination), and may not write to a "Critical" log (prevents unauthorized elevation). Both models are foundational MAC policies implemented in SELinux, Trusted Solaris, and military-grade IDP platforms.

---

#### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With neat architectural diagrams, explain the **ITU-T X.800 Security Architecture**. Differentiate between **specific** and **pervasive** security mechanisms with three examples each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An attacker chooses uniformly between 4 attack vectors: SQLi, XSS, Brute Force, and CSRF. Compute the **Shannon entropy** of this attack distribution. If the attacker shifts to a deterministic SQLi-only strategy, what happens to the entropy? Comment on the IDP policy implication. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ITU-T X.800 Architecture (7 marks):**
- **Brief context (1 mark):** X.800 (1989, ITU-T Recommendation) is the standard *Security Architecture for Open Systems Interconnection*. It defines security services, mechanisms, and their relationship.
- **Five security services it provides (2 marks):** Authentication, Access Control, Data Confidentiality, Data Integrity, Non-repudiation.
- **Specific vs. Pervasive mechanisms (3 marks):**
  - *Specific mechanisms (tied to a service):* Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization.
  - *Pervasive mechanisms (apply to multiple services):* Trusted Functionality, Security Labels, Event Detection, Security Audit Trail, Security Recovery.
- **Diagram relevance (1 mark):** Draw or describe the layered mapping: Application → Presentation → Session (security services) → Data Link/Physical (specific mechanisms); management plane supports pervasive mechanisms across all layers.

**(b) Shannon Entropy Computation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Define $n = 4$ and $p_i = 1/4$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Apply the Shannon formula:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i \log_{2}(p_i) = -4 \times \left(\frac{1}{4}\right) \log_{2}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Simplify $\log_{2}(1/4) = -2$:

$$H(X) = -4 \times \left(\frac{1}{4}\right) \times (-2) = 2 \text{ bits}$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Deterministic case — if all $p_1 = 1$ (SQLi) and others $= 0$:

$$H(X) = -1 \cdot \log_{2}(1) - 0 = 0 \text{ bits}$$

*Step 5 (2 marks):* **IDP Policy Implication:**
- $H = 2$ bits (uniform): High unpredictability. The IDP cannot rely on a single signature; it must deploy **anomaly-based or ML-based detection** to cover the wide attack surface.
- $H = 0$ bits (deterministic): Fully predictable. The IDP can use a **single, focused signature** for SQLi, with minimal false positives. A single rule achieves near-100% detection.
