- **Generalization (1 mark):** Entropy is a quantitative measure of attack-surface uncertainty; the IDP's *detection policy* should be tuned inversely — high entropy demands broader, more sophisticated detection, while low entropy permits lean rule sets.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "policy" with "mechanism":** Many students write "firewall is a policy" — wrong. Firewall is the **mechanism**; the *firewall rule set* is the **policy**. Lose **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Missing the formula in BLP:** Examiners want both the **No Read-Up** *and* the **No Write-Down** axioms stated explicitly. Writing only the verbal description loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\log$ base in Shannon entropy:** Always state **bits (base 2)**; using natural log will yield the wrong unit and lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Listing mechanisms without classifying them** in ITU-T X.800 questions: you must explicitly say "Specific" or "Pervasive" — partial credit is halved otherwise.
> 5. **Mixing up Biba and BLP axes:** Biba protects **integrity**, BLP protects **confidentiality**. Reversing them is a critical conceptual error and loses **3 marks**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Security Policy = WHAT to protect**; **Security Mechanism = HOW to protect it**.
- [x] Three policy layers: **Organizational → System-Level → Issue-Specific**.
- [x] CIA Triad is the **universal policy goal** — **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability**.
- [x] **ITU-T X.800** defines 5 services and 13 mechanisms (8 specific + 5 pervasive).
- [x] **DAC** = owner discretion (ACL); **MAC** = labels/clearance; **RBAC** = roles; **ABAC** = attributes.
- [x] **Bell-LaPadula** = confidentiality (No Read-Up, No Write-Down).
- [x] **Biba** = integrity (No Read-Down, No Write-Up).
- [x] **Shannon entropy**: $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, range $[0, \log_2 n]$.
- [x] **Birthday collision** in n-bit hash at $\approx 2^{n/2}$ trials — why SHA-256 is safe.
- [x] IDP engines are **mechanisms**; rule sets / signature databases / threshold values are the **policy** that drives them.
- [x] Always mention **CIA + ITU-T X.800 + BLP/Biba** in any policy-related answer — these are KTU examiners' favorite keywords.
- [x] Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) inside markdown tables to avoid breaking table syntax.
- [x] State the **log base** in any entropy calculation — default is **base 2 (bits)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism—either hardware or software-based—that monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts, and produces reports to a management station. An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is the proactive evolution of an IDS; it possesses the additional capability to automatically block, drop, or reject the offending traffic in real-time without human intervention.

According to the **RFC 4949 (Internet Security Glossary)** maintained by the IETF, an intrusion is defined as *a security event, or a combination of multiple security events, that constitutes a deliberate, unauthorized attempt to access, manipulate, or render a computing system unreliable or unusable.*

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module expects students to clearly distinguish between an **IDS (passive, detective control)** and an **IPS (active, preventive control)**. In KTU valuation, marks are specifically awarded for identifying which device sits *in-line* with the traffic flow and which only *taps* the traffic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your home has a **CCTV camera** installed at the front door.

- An **IDS** is like a CCTV camera. It silently *records* everything, including the suspicious person trying to pick the lock. The moment it detects a problem, it **alarms loudly** (sends an alert to the security guard), but the camera itself does not stop the thief. It is a **detective control**.
- An **IPS** is like the CCTV camera combined with a **robotic door lock and a security guard**. The moment the camera sees the suspicious activity, the door automatically slams shut, and the guard physically blocks the person. It is a **preventive control**.

In network terms, the IDS sits *off the live data path* (connected via a span port or tap), while the IPS sits *inline* with the firewall—every packet physically passes through it, and the IPS decides whether to forward or drop the packet in microseconds.

> [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics You Must Remember for KTU:**
> - **True Positive (TP):** Attack correctly identified as attack.
> - **True Negative (TN):** Legitimate traffic correctly identified as legitimate.
> - **False Positive (FP):** Legitimate traffic falsely flagged as attack (Type I Error).
> - **False Negative (FN):** Attack falsely allowed through as legitimate (Type II Error).
> - **Detection Rate (DR) = TP / (TP + FN)**
> - **False Alarm Rate (FAR) = FP / (FP + TN)**

## 1.3 Historical Evolution (For 3-Mark Sub-Questions)

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1980** | **James Anderson** published the seminal paper *"Computer Security Threat Monitoring and Surveillance"*, introducing the conceptual framework of intrusion detection. |
| **1984–1987** | **Dorothy Denning** and **Peter Neumann** developed the **IDES (Intrusion Detection Expert System)** at SRI International—the first mathematical model of an IDS. |
| **1990** | **L. Todd Heberlein** at UC Davis introduced the **Network Security Monitor (NSM)**—the first Network-based IDS (NIDS) analyzing live TCP/IP traffic. |
| **1998** | **Sourcefire** was founded, bringing open-source IDS **Snort** to the industry (still taught in KTU labs). |
| **2000s** | The concept of **Inline IDS / IPS** emerged with commercial products from Cisco, Juniper, and TippingPoint. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of an IDS / IPS (Bulleted Reasoning)

The core "Why and How" behind an IDS engine can be broken down into five logical stages:

- **Step 1 – Data Collection (Sensors):** The system gathers raw data from host logs (HIDS) or network packets (NIDS). *Why?* You cannot detect what you cannot see.
- **Step 2 – Preprocessing & Normalization:** The data is converted into a standardized format (e.g., ASCII logs, decoded TCP/IP packets). *Why?* Heterogeneous data sources need a unified mathematical representation.
- **Step 3 – Detection Engine Analysis:** The core algorithm compares the input against either a *signature database* (e.g., Snort rules) or a *behavioral profile* (statistical / machine learning model). *Why?* This is the "brain" that classifies events.
- **Step 4 – Decision/Response:** The system generates an alert (IDS) or executes a blocking action (IPS). Responses may be passive (log entry) or active (TCP reset, packet drop, quarantine).
- **Step 5 – Storage & Reporting:** Events are logged to a **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** system for forensic analysis and compliance audits (e.g., PCI-DSS, ISO 27001).

## 2.2 Primary Detection Methodologies

### A. Signature-Based (Misuse) Detection
- **How it works:** Maintains a database of known attack patterns (signatures or rules). Uses pattern-matching algorithms like the **Aho-Corasick algorithm** or **Boyer-Moore string search**.
- **Advantages:** Very low False Positive rate; highly accurate for *known* attacks.
- **Disadvantages:** **Zero-day attacks** go undetected (Zero-day vulnerability). Requires constant signature updates.

### B. Anomaly-Based Detection
- **How it works:** Builds a statistical or machine-learning model of "normal" behavior. Anything deviating beyond a defined threshold is flagged.
- **Mathematical Foundation:** Leverages **Bayes' Theorem**, **Hidden Markov Models (HMM)**, or clustering algorithms (k-means, SVM).
- **Advantages:** Capable of detecting *novel / zero-day* attacks.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher False Positive rate because legitimate-but-unusual behavior triggers alerts.

### C. Stateful Protocol Analysis
- **How it works:** Tracks the state of network protocols (e.g., TCP handshakes) and flags deviations such as out-of-order SYN packets, invalid flags, or DNS cache poisoning attempts.
- **Advantage:** Excellent for detecting protocol-level exploits and tunneling.

### D. Hybrid Detection
- **How it works:** Combines signature and anomaly engines, then uses a *correlation engine* to merge verdicts and reduce false positives.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use / Notes |
|------------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|
| Detection Rate (DR) / Recall | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Ability to catch real attacks. Higher is better. |
| False Alarm Rate (FAR) | $FAR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Likelihood of misclassifying normal traffic. Lower is better. |
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts, how many are real attacks? |
| Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness of the IDS. |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot DR}{P + DR}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. |
| Bayes Posterior Probability (Anomaly Detection) | $P(C \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$ | Used in statistical IDS to compute the probability that observation $X$ belongs to attack class $C$. |
| ROC Curve Metric – AUC | $AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve. |
| Shannon Entropy (Traffic Anomaly) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Used to detect DDoS/port-scan traffic by measuring randomness. |
| Throughput Constraint (Inline IPS) | $T_{max} = \dfrac{C}{L \cdot 8}$ | Maximum packets/sec where $C$ = link capacity (bps), $L$ = packet length (bytes). |

> [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` breaks markdown tables. In LaTeX inside tables, always use `\vert` or `\mid` to express absolute value or conditional probability bar. Example: $P(A \mid B)$ is correct; $P(A \mid B)$ inside a `| ... |` cell will break the layout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Networks:** Deployed as inline sensors behind the perimeter firewall to catch threats that bypass signature-based defenses.
- **SCADA / ICS / OT Systems:** Specialized IDS variants (e.g., **Snort with ICS rulesets**, **Dragos Platform**) protect power grids and manufacturing PLCs.
- **Cloud Environments:** Host-based IDS agents (e.g., **OSSEC, Wazuh, Falco**) monitor container and Kubernetes workloads.
- **Compliance:** Mandated under **PCI-DSS Requirement 11.4**, **NIST SP 800-94 (Guide to IDS/IPS)**, and **ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.12.4**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes' Theorem in Statistical Intrusion Detection

The detection engine of an anomaly-based IDS must decide whether an observed network event $X$ (e.g., a packet's feature vector containing duration, protocol, source/destination bytes, etc.) belongs to the **Normal class ($N$)** or the **Attack class ($A$)**.

We want to compute the posterior probability $P(A \mid X)$ — *the probability that event $X$ is an attack, given that we have observed $X$*.

**Step 1 — State the joint probability.**
Using the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(A \mid X) = \dfrac{P(A \cap X)}{P(X)}$$

*Explanation:* The probability of attack *given* observation is the joint probability of both occurring divided by the probability of the observation alone.

**Step 2 — Express the joint probability symmetrically.**
Since $P(A \cap X) = P(X \cap A)$, and $P(X \cap A) = P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A)$:

$$P(A \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(X)}$$

*Explanation:* We replace the numerator with the product of the likelihood $P(X \mid A)$ and the prior $P(A)$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Law of Total Probability to the denominator.**
Since an observation is either generated by the attack process or the normal process, $P(X) = P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(X \mid N) \cdot P(N)$:

$$P(A \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(X \mid N) \cdot P(N)}$$

*Explanation:* We split the denominator into mutually exclusive scenarios (Normal and Attack). This is the complete form of Bayes' theorem.

**Step 4 — Decision Rule.**
The IDS raises an alert if the posterior probability exceeds a threshold $\tau$:

$$\text{Alert} \iff P(A \mid X) \geq \tau$$

*Explanation:* The system operator tunes $\tau$ (typically $0.5$ to $0.9$) to trade off between catching more attacks (lower $\tau$) and reducing false positives (higher $\tau$).

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**
Suppose:
- $P(A) = 0.01$ (1 in 100 packets is malicious)
- $P(X \mid A) = 0.95$ (95% of attacks trigger this observation)
- $P(X \mid N) = 0.05$ (5% of normal packets look suspicious — false alarm source)

Substitute:

$$P(A \mid X) = \dfrac{0.95 \times 0.01}{0.95 \times 0.01 + 0.05 \times 0.99}$$

$$P(A \mid X) = \dfrac{0.0095}{0.0095 + 0.0495} = \dfrac{0.0095}{0.0590}$$

$$P(A \mid X) \approx 0.1610$$

*Interpretation:* Even with a strong signature, the low base rate of attacks keeps the posterior at only **16.1%**. This is the famous **"Base-Rate Fallacy"** in IDS design — why false positives dominate in real deployments.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Entropy for DDoS Detection

During a **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)** attack, the source IP distribution of incoming packets becomes either highly concentrated (a few bot IPs) or highly uniform (a botnet sweeping the IP space). Both scenarios alter the entropy of the source-IP distribution.

Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ be the set of source IP addresses observed in a window, and $p_i$ be the empirical probability of IP $x_i$ appearing in the window.

**Step 1 — Normalize the probability distribution.**
Sum of all probabilities must equal 1:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 1$$

**Step 2 — Compute Shannon Entropy.**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

**Step 3 — Compare with normal baseline.**
Let $H_{normal}$ be the entropy during peacetime. A deviation of $\Delta H = \vert H_{current} - H_{normal} \vert > \delta$ triggers an alert.

*Explanation:* $\delta$ is the detection threshold. In NSL-KDD benchmark datasets, $H_{normal} \approx 6.5$ bits for the source-IP distribution. DDoS attacks typically drop this to below $3.0$ bits.

## 3.3 Analytical Confusion Matrix for a 1000-Event Sample

Suppose an IDS evaluates **1000 network events** in one hour, of which **100 are actual attacks**. The detection engine produces the following results:

| Actual ↓ \ Predicted → | **Predicted Attack** | **Predicted Normal** | **Row Total** |
|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Actual Attack**      | 90 (TP)              | 10 (FN)              | 100           |
| **Actual Normal**      | 50 (FP)              | 850 (TN)             | 900           |
| **Column Total**       | 140                  | 860                  | 1000          |

Now compute the KTU-required metrics:

$$DR = \dfrac{90}{100} = 0.90 \quad (90\%)$$

$$FAR = \dfrac{50}{900} \approx 0.0556 \quad (5.56\%)$$

$$P = \dfrac{90}{140} \approx 0.6429 \quad (64.29\%)$$

$$Acc = \dfrac{90 + 850}{1000} = 0.940 \quad (94\%)$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.6429 \times 0.90}{0.6429 + 0.90} \approx 0.7512$$

*Interpretation:* The IDS catches 9 out of 10 attacks (good DR) but 1 in 18 legitimate events is wrongly flagged (5.56% FAR). The precision is mediocre because of the relatively large absolute number of false positives — a typical real-world tradeoff.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IDS / IPS Placement in a Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet[Internet] --> fw[Perimeter Firewall]
    fw --> ips1[Inline IPS]
    ips1 --> switchL2[Core L3 Switch]
    switchL2 --> server1[Web Server Farm]
    switchL2 --> server2[Database Cluster]
    switchL2 --> ids1[NIDS Sensor Span Port]
    ids1 --> siem1[SIEM Management Console]
    fw --> ids2[External NIDS Tap]
    ids2 --> siem1
    siem1 --> soc[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
    hids1[HIDS Agent on Server] --> siem1
```

*Explanation of Flow:*
- Traffic flows from the Internet → Firewall → **Inline IPS** → Switch → Servers.
- The IPS sits **inline** (every packet passes through it) and can drop malicious packets.
- The NIDS sensors are connected via **span/mirror ports**, passively monitoring copies of traffic.
- All events aggregate in the **SIEM** for unified dashboarding.

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of an IDS Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sensorA[Network Sensor Packet Capture] --> pre1[Protocol Decoder and Normalizer]
    sensorB[Host Log Collector syslog] --> pre2[Log Parser and Tagger]
    pre1 --> engine1[Signature Matching Engine Aho-Corasick]
    pre2 --> engine2[Anomaly Detection Engine Statistical Model]
    pre1 --> engine3[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]
    engine1 --> correl[Correlation and Fusion Engine]
    engine2 --> correl
    engine3 --> correl
    correl --> decision{Threshold Check}
    decision -- Alert --> out1[Alert Generator and SIEM Forwarder]
    decision -- Inline Block --> out2[IPS Drop and TCP Reset Action]
    correl --> storage[Event Database]
```

*Explanation:*
- **Sensors** capture raw data (packets or logs).
- **Preprocessors** decode the data into uniform feature vectors.
- **Three parallel detection engines** run signature, anomaly, and stateful analysis.
- The **Correlation Engine** merges verdicts to suppress noise and false positives.
- A **Decision Block** chooses between passive alerting (IDS mode) or active blocking (IPS mode).

## 4.3 Detection Methodology Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Incoming Event X] --> q1{Known Signature Match Available?}
    q1 -- Yes --> sig[Signature Based Detection]
    q1 -- No --> stat[Build Statistical Profile of Normal]
    stat --> dev[Compute Deviation Score d]
    dev --> q2{Deviation Exceeds Threshold tau?}
    q2 -- Yes --> alertAnom[Anomaly Alert]
    q2 -- No --> allow[Pass as Normal]
    sig --> q3{Pattern Matches Database}
    q3 -- Yes --> alertSig[Signature Alert]
    q3 -- No --> state[Check Protocol State Validity]
    state --> q4{State Consistent with RFC}
    q4 -- No --> alertSt[Stateful Violation Alert]
    q4 -- Yes --> allow
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) in terms of placement and operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An IDS is a **passive detective control** that monitors network traffic or system logs and generates alerts upon detecting malicious activity; it is typically connected via a span port or tap, *off* the live data path. An IPS is an **active preventive control** placed *inline* with the traffic flow; in addition to detection, it can automatically **block, drop, or reset** the offending packets in real-time. The IDS may use *any* detection method, while the IPS must operate at line speed to avoid becoming a bottleneck. *(3 marks: 1 for IDS, 1 for IPS, 1 for placement distinction.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2. List and briefly explain the three primary detection methodologies used in modern IDS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Signature-Based (Misuse) Detection** — Compares traffic against a database of known attack patterns. High accuracy, but ineffective against zero-day attacks. *(1 mark)*
2. **Anomaly-Based Detection** — Establishes a baseline of normal behavior and flags statistical deviations. Can detect novel attacks, but suffers from higher false positives. *(1 mark)*
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis** — Tracks the state of protocol sessions (e.g., TCP handshakes) and flags violations of the protocol specification. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Define an Intrusion Detection System. With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a generic IDS. List the various components and their functions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
An IDS is a software or hardware system that automates the process of monitoring events occurring in a computer system or network and analyzes them for signs of security policy violations. *(1 mark)*

**Architecture (refer to the diagram in Section 4.2):**
- **Data Source / Sensor:** Captures raw events from the network (packet capture via libpcap) or from the host (audit logs, syslog). *(1 mark)*
- **Preprocessor:** Decodes protocols (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → application), normalizes fields, reassembles fragmented packets. *(1 mark)*
- **Detection Engine:** The analytical core. It applies signature matching (e.g., Snort rules with Aho-Corasick), statistical anomaly detection, or stateful protocol analysis. *(1 mark)*
- **Knowledge Base:** Stores signatures, statistical profiles, and protocol state tables. *(1 mark)*
- **Decision / Response Module:** Generates alerts (e-mail, SNMP traps, SIEM forwarding) or, in IPS mode, executes blocking actions (TCP reset, packet drop). *(1 mark)*
- **Management Console:** Provides the GUI/dashboard for the security analyst to configure rules, view alerts, and generate reports. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the four possible outcomes of an IDS detection decision using a confusion matrix. Compute the Detection Rate, False Alarm Rate, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score for the following data: TP=80, FP=40, FN=20, TN=860. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
The four outcomes are: True Positive (attack correctly identified), False Positive (legitimate flagged as attack — Type I Error), True Negative (legitimate correctly allowed), False Negative (attack missed — Type II Error). *(2 marks)*

Given: TP=80, FP=40, FN=20, TN=860, total events = 1000.

**Detection Rate (DR):**

$$DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{80}{80 + 20} = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.80 \quad (80\%)$$

**False Alarm Rate (FAR):**

$$FAR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{40}{40 + 860} = \dfrac{40}{900} \approx 0.0444 \quad (4.44\%)$$

**Precision (P):**

$$P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{80}{80 + 40} = \dfrac{80}{120} = 0.6667 \quad (66.67\%)$$

**Accuracy:**

$$Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \dfrac{80 + 860}{1000} = \dfrac{940}{1000} = 0.940 \quad (94\%)$$

**F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot DR}{P + DR} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.6667 \times 0.80}{0.6667 + 0.80} \approx 0.7272$$

*(1 mark per metric calculation, 1 mark for the four-outcome description, 1 mark for correct substitution and final answers.)*

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the three main categories of IDS based on the data source: Network-based (NIDS), Host-based (HIDS), and Hybrid IDS. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Network-based IDS (NIDS):** Placed at strategic points in the network (e.g., behind the firewall, at network choke points). It captures and analyzes all traffic flowing on the segment using a span port or network tap. Example: **Snort, Suricata, Zeek**. *(1 mark)*

*Advantages:* (i) Monitors entire network segments with a single sensor; (ii) Detects scans and worms early; (iii) Non-intrusive. *(1 mark)*
*Disadvantages:* (i) Cannot inspect encrypted traffic payload; (ii) Cannot see host-local events (e.g., file integrity); (iii) Performance degrades on high-speed links (10G+). *(1 mark)*

**Host-based IDS (HIDS):** Installed as an agent on the individual host. It monitors OS-level events such as system calls, file integrity (using hash baselines), registry changes, and application logs. Example: **OSSEC, Wazuh, Tripwire**. *(1 mark)*

*Advantages:* (i) Can see decrypted traffic after TLS termination; (ii) Detects insider threats and host-specific malware; (iii) Works inside encrypted channels. *(1 mark)*
*Disadvantages:* (i) Requires installation on every host; (ii) Uses host resources (CPU, RAM, disk); (iii) Limited network-wide visibility. *(1 mark)*

**Hybrid IDS:** Combines NIDS and HIDS via a central correlation engine. Example: **Cisco Firepower + AMP for Endpoints**. *(1 mark)*
*Advantage:* Provides both network-level and host-level context, reducing false positives through cross-verification. *Disadvantage:* Higher cost and deployment complexity. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q. (b)** State and derive Bayes' Theorem as applied to statistical anomaly-based intrusion detection. Why is the Base-Rate Fallacy critical in IDS design? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** Bayes' theorem computes the posterior probability $P(A \mid X)$ that an event $X$ belongs to the attack class $A$ given prior knowledge and likelihoods. *(1 mark)*

**Derivation:**
Step 1 — Conditional probability definition: $P(A \mid X) = \dfrac{P(A \cap X)}{P(X)}$ *(1 mark)*
Step 2 — Joint probability expansion: $P(A \cap X) = P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A)$, giving $P(A \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(X)}$ *(1 mark)*
Step 3 — Total probability for denominator: $P(X) = P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(X \mid N) \cdot P(N)$ *(1 mark)*
Step 4 — Final form:

$$P(A \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(X \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(X \mid N) \cdot P(N)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Step 5 — Decision rule: Alert if $P(A \mid X) \geq \tau$ *(1 mark)*

**Base-Rate Fallacy:** Even if the attack likelihood $P(X \mid A)$ is high (e.g., 0.95), the *prior* probability $P(A)$ of an attack is extremely low (e.g., 0.01 in typical traffic). This causes the posterior $P(A \mid X)$ to remain small (e.g., 0.16 as shown in the numerical example). As a result, the IDS must process thousands of alerts to find a true positive, overwhelming SOC analysts and causing *alert fatigue*. This is why modern SIEMs use correlation, whitelisting, and machine learning to raise the effective base rate. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS placement:** Many students incorrectly state that an IDS sits inline. The IDS is *off-path*; only the IPS is inline. This is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing False Positive with False Negative:** FP = normal flagged as attack; FN = attack missed. Mixing these up invalidates all your FAR/DR calculations. A single wrong substitution will lose **2 marks** on a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Forgetting units in entropy:** Shannon Entropy is in **bits** (log base 2) or **nats** (log base e). State the base explicitly in derivation questions.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in architecture questions:** A 7-mark question on IDS architecture **requires** a labeled block diagram. Text-only answers are capped at 4 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of mutual exclusivity** in Bayes' theorem derivation — this is a favorite 1-mark trap in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **IDS = Detective, Off-path, Alerts** | **IPS = Preventive, Inline, Blocks.** *(High-priority KTU distinction.)*
- **Three detection methods:** Signature (known), Anomaly (novel), Stateful Protocol (RFC violation).
- **Three data sources:** Network (NIDS), Host (HIDS), Hybrid.
- **Key formula trio (must memorize):** $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $FAR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$, $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$.
- **Accuracy alone is misleading** when classes are imbalanced (rare attacks) — always quote DR and FAR.
- **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean — penalizes extreme imbalance between Precision and Recall.
- **Bayes' Theorem** is the mathematical backbone of statistical anomaly detection.
- **Base-Rate Fallacy:** Low attack priors make naive Bayesian IDS produce too many false positives.
- **Shannon Entropy** detects DDoS and port-scan attacks by measuring traffic distribution randomness.
- **Inline IPS performance constraint:** Must operate at line speed; throughput = $\dfrac{C}{L \times 8}$ pps.
- **Historical milestones for 1-mark questions:** Anderson (1980), Denning (1987 — IDES), Heberlein (1990 — NSM).
- **Real-world products:** Snort (open-source NIDS), Suricata, OSSEC/Wazuh (HIDS), Cisco Firepower (IPS).
- **Compliance drivers:** PCI-DSS 11.4, NIST SP 800-94, ISO 27001 A.12.4.
- **Always state $\log$ base** (base 2 → bits; base $e$ → nats) when computing entropy in derivation.
- **Always label the diagram axes/units** in any IDS/IPS architecture answer — KTU examiners reward precision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS): Definition and Purpose of IDS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS): Definition and Purpose of IDS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security technology—either hardware or software-based—that **monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts**, and produces reports to a management station or security administrator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> An IDS is a defensive mechanism that automates the **process of monitoring events** occurring in a computer system or network, **analyzes them for signs of possible incidents** (violations or imminent threats), and **issues warnings or alerts** when such incidents are detected. It does NOT prevent the attack directly—it only detects and reports it.

The entity or person that performs the intrusion analysis is called the **Intrusion Detection Entity (IDE)** or the IDS sensor/agent. The action of detecting an attack that compromises (or attempts to compromise) a system's **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA Triad)** is the central purpose of any IDS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The CCTV Burglar Alarm Model

Imagine your home is the **computer network** and your valuables are the **sensitive data and resources** inside it.

- **Theft / Break-in** → A cyber attack (e.g., port scan, malware injection, privilege escalation)
- **CCTV Camera / Motion Sensor** → The **IDS sensor** silently watching every corridor (network packet) and door (system log)
- **Burglar Alarm** → The **alert generation** mechanism of the IDS
- **Security Control Room** → The **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** console where the security analyst receives notifications
- **Police arriving after the alarm** → The **Incident Response team**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as a CCTV camera does NOT stop a thief, an IDS does NOT block an attack. It only **detects** it and **raises the alarm**. The prevention counterpart is called an **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)**, which acts like a security guard who can lock the door mid-burglary.

## 1.3 Primary Purpose of an IDS

The **purpose** of an IDS, as aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus outcomes, is summarized in the acronym **D-E-F-E-N-D**:

| Letter | Purpose | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| **D** | **Detect** | Identify known and unknown malicious activity in real-time or near real-time. |
| **E** | **Enforce** | Support enforcement of the organization's security policy. |
| **D** | **Document** | Provide forensic logs and audit trails for post-incident analysis. |
| **E** | **Educate** | Help identify weaknesses in existing security policies and inform administrators. |
| **F** | **Fulfill** | Meet regulatory compliance requirements (e.g., PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001). |
| **E** | **Enhance** | Improve the overall response time of the security operations center (SOC). |
| **N** | **Notify** | Alert administrators promptly when suspicious behavior is observed. |
| **D** | **Deter** | Act as a psychological deterrent (visible presence of monitoring discourages attackers). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome (CO1 Mapping):**
> After studying this topic, students must be able to **define an IDS, distinguish it from a firewall, and articulate its purpose in the layered defense-in-depth security model.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept — How an IDS Works (Step-by-Step)

The IDS operates through a structured pipeline. Each stage must succeed for the system to be useful:

- **Step 1 — Data Collection (Sensing Phase):** The IDS gathers raw data from its observation point.
  - *Network IDS (NIDS):* Captures packets via a **promiscuous mode** network interface.
  - *Host IDS (HIDS):* Reads operating system **audit logs, syscalls, file integrity hashes, and registry changes**.
  - *Wireless IDS (WIDS):* Sniffs radio frames (802.11 management/control frames).

- **Step 2 — Pre-processing:** Raw data is normalized, decoded (e.g., TCP/IP defragmentation), and converted into a standardized event format.

- **Step 3 — Detection Engine (Analysis Phase):** The core module applies detection algorithms.
  - **Signature-Based (Misuse Detection):** Compares events against a database of known attack patterns (similar to antivirus). Fast, but cannot detect **zero-day attacks**.
  - **Anomaly-Based Detection:** Establishes a baseline of "normal" behavior using statistical models, machine learning, or rule-based heuristics, and flags deviations. Can detect novel attacks but suffers from higher **false positive rates**.

- **Step 4 — Decision & Response:** When an event is classified as malicious (or suspicious), the IDS generates an **alert** (log entry, SNMP trap, email, SMS, or syslog message).

- **Step 5 — Storage & Reporting:** Alerts are forwarded to a centralized **SIEM** for correlation and long-term retention.

## 2.2 Placement of IDS in the Network — Strategic Considerations

| Placement Strategy | Location | Advantage | Limitation |
|--------------------|----------|-----------|------------|
| **In-line / Inline IDS (TAP)** | Directly on the traffic path (mirror port or network tap) | Sees all traffic including encrypted payloads if decryption is enabled | Performance bottleneck; single point of failure |
| **Passive IDS (SPAN Port)** | Receives a copy of traffic via Switched Port Analyzer | Non-disruptive to network flow | May miss packets under high load (oversubscription) |
| **Host-based IDS (HIDS)** | Installed on the endpoint itself | Sees encrypted payload after decryption at host; detects local rootkits | Consumes host resources; bypassed if host is compromised |
| **Hybrid IDS** | Combination of NIDS and HIDS with central correlation | Best coverage and context awareness | Higher complexity and cost |

## 2.3 Why an IDS is Needed (The "Why" — Defence in Depth)

A **firewall** alone is **not sufficient** for the following reasons, which justify the existence of an IDS:

- A firewall operates primarily at **Layer 3 / Layer 4 (Network/Transport)** and performs coarse-grained port/protocol filtering. It cannot deeply inspect **application-layer (Layer 7)** payloads.
- Firewalls cannot detect **insider threats**, **encrypted malware**, **zero-day exploits**, or **policy violations by legitimate users**.
- A firewall cannot correlate events across time to detect **slow, low-and-slow attacks** (e.g., Advanced Persistent Threats — APTs).
- The IDS provides the **second layer of the "defence-in-depth" model**, complementing firewalls, antivirus, and access control systems.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Performance Metrics of an IDS

These metrics are essential for evaluating the **effectiveness** of any IDS and frequently appear in KTU numerical questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table below, the vertical bar `|` is replaced with `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| Metric | Formula | Ideal Value | Engineering Meaning |
|--------|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| **True Positive Rate (TPR)** — also called *Detection Rate* or *Sensitivity* or *Recall* | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $\rightarrow 1$ (i.e., **100%**) | Probability that an actual attack is correctly flagged. |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — also called *False Alarm Rate* | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $\rightarrow 0$ | Probability that benign traffic is wrongly flagged as malicious. |
| **True Negative Rate (TNR)** — also called *Specificity* | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $\rightarrow 1$ | Probability that benign traffic is correctly ignored. |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** — also called *Miss Rate* | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | $\rightarrow 0$ | Probability that an actual attack slips through undetected. |
| **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $\rightarrow 1$ | Of all alerts raised, how many were real attacks. |
| **Accuracy** | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $\rightarrow 1$ | Overall correctness of the IDS. |
| **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)** | $F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | $\rightarrow 1$ | Balanced single-value metric for skewed datasets. |
| **Base Rate Fallacy** | $FPR \gg \text{Actual Attack Rate}$ | — | Even a 1% FPR causes alert fatigue when attack rate is 0.001%. |

Where:
- **TP** = True Positive (attack correctly detected)
- **TN** = True Negative (normal traffic correctly allowed)
- **FP** = False Positive (normal traffic wrongly flagged as attack — "false alarm")
- **FN** = False Negative (attack missed — "missed detection")

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

IDS is deployed in:
- **Enterprise SOCs** for continuous perimeter and internal monitoring.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA)** to detect protocol-level anomalies (Modbus, DNP3).
- **Cloud environments** (AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender) as cloud-native IDS.
- **Government and Defense** networks for compliance with **NIST SP 800-94** guidelines.
- **Banking & Financial** sectors for **PCI-DSS** mandated monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ROC Curve Relationship

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is a fundamental tool for evaluating IDS performance. The mathematical relationship between TPR and FPR forms the y-axis and x-axis of the ROC curve respectively.

### Step 1: Define the foundational quantities

By definition, for a given IDS and a labeled test dataset of $N$ total events:

$$TP + FN = P \quad \text{(Total actual positives / actual attacks)}$$

$$TN + FP = N \quad \text{(Total actual negatives / benign events)}$$

$$TP + FP + TN + FN = P + N = \text{Total Events}$$

### Step 2: Express the True Positive Rate (Recall)

The True Positive Rate is the fraction of all actual attacks that were successfully detected. Substituting the relationship from Step 1:

$$TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{TP}{P}$$

If $TP = P$, then $TPR = 1$, meaning the IDS detected **every single attack** — the **ideal detection scenario**.

### Step 3: Express the False Positive Rate

The False Positive Rate is the fraction of benign events that were wrongly classified as attacks. Substituting the relationship from Step 1:

$$FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{FP}{N}$$

If $FP = 0$, then $FPR = 0$, meaning the IDS raised **zero false alarms** — the **ideal specificity scenario**.

### Step 4: Derive the F1-Score from Precision and Recall

Starting from the harmonic mean definition, we substitute $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ and $Recall = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$:

$$F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}\right) + \left(\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}\right)}$$

### Step 5: Simplify the F1-Score numerator and denominator separately

**Numerator simplification:**

$$2 \cdot \dfrac{TP \cdot TP}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)} = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP^2}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}$$

**Denominator simplification:**

$$\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = TP \cdot \left[\dfrac{(TP + FN) + (TP + FP)}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}\right] = TP \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}$$

### Step 6: Final F1-Score formula

Dividing the simplified numerator by the simplified denominator, the $(TP + FP)(TP + FN)$ terms cancel:

$$F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot (2 \cdot TP + FP + FN)} = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

This is the **compact, exam-friendly form** of the F1-score.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):**
An IDS was tested on a dataset containing **10,000 events**, of which **100 are actual attacks** and **9,900 are benign**. The IDS raised **180 alerts**, of which **90 were true attacks** and **90 were false alarms**. Calculate: **(a)** TPR, **(b)** FPR, **(c)** Precision, **(d)** Accuracy, and **(e)** F1-Score.

### Step 1: Build the Confusion Matrix

Given: Total attacks $P = 100$, Total benign $N = 9900$, $TP = 90$, $FP = 90$.

Therefore:
- $FN = P - TP = 100 - 90 = 10$
- $TN = N - FP = 9900 - 90 = 9810$

The confusion matrix is:

| | **Predicted: Attack** | **Predicted: Benign** | **Total** |
|---|----------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Actual: Attack** | $TP = 90$ | $FN = 10$ | $100$ |
| **Actual: Benign** | $FP = 90$ | $TN = 9810$ | $9900$ |
| **Total** | $180$ | $9820$ | $10000$ |

### Step 2: Calculate True Positive Rate (Detection Rate)

$$TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{90}{90 + 10} = \dfrac{90}{100} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

### Step 3: Calculate False Positive Rate

$$FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{90}{90 + 9810} = \dfrac{90}{9900} \approx 0.00909 = 0.909\%$$

### Step 4: Calculate Precision

$$Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{90}{90 + 90} = \dfrac{90}{180} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

### Step 5: Calculate Accuracy

$$Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \dfrac{90 + 9810}{10000} = \dfrac{9900}{10000} = 0.99 = 99\%$$

### Step 6: Calculate F1-Score

$$F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 90}{2 \cdot 90 + 90 + 10} = \dfrac{180}{280} = 0.6429 = 64.29\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Interpretation for KTU:** Notice that **Accuracy is 99%** (looks excellent) but **F1-Score is only 64.29%** because the dataset is **highly imbalanced** (only 1% attacks). This demonstrates the **Accuracy Paradox** in IDS evaluation — always use F1-Score or ROC-AUC in KTU answers for imbalanced security datasets.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IDS vs. Firewall vs. IPS

| Property | Firewall | IDS | IPS |
|----------|----------|-----|-----|
| **Primary Function** | Filter traffic based on rules | Detect & alert on suspicious activity | Detect AND block suspicious activity |
| **OSI Layer Focus** | Layer 3 / 4 (some Layer 7) | Layer 2 – 7 (Deep Packet Inspection) | Layer 2 – 7 |
| **Inline / Passive** | Inline (must be) | Mostly passive (monitors) | Inline (must be to block) |
| **Action on Threat** | Allow / Deny | Log + Alert | Log + Alert + Drop/Reject/Reset |
| **Stateful Inspection** | Yes (modern) | Yes (stateful protocol analysis) | Yes |
| **Effect on Latency** | Minimal | Negligible (passive) | Adds latency (inline processing) |
| **Failure Mode** | Fail-open or fail-closed | Failure does not disrupt traffic | Must fail-open to avoid network outage |
| **Insider Threat Detection** | Poor | Good (especially HIDS) | Good |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an IDS

The following **Mermaid flowchart** illustrates the functional pipeline of a generic Intrusion Detection System, from raw data ingestion to alert generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Traffic / Host Logs] --> B[Data Source / Sensor]
    B --> C[Pre-processing Module: Packet Decoding and Normalization]
    C --> D{Detection Engine}
    D -- Known Pattern Match --> E[Signature Based Detection]
    D -- Behavioral Deviation --> F[Anomaly Based Detection]
    D -- Protocol State Violation --> G[Stateful Protocol Analysis]
    E --> H[Alert Generation Module]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Alert Storage and Correlation in SIEM]
    H --> J[Notification: Email, SMS, SNMP Trap, Syslog]
    I --> K[Security Analyst Console / SOC Dashboard]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Incident Response and Forensic Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Placement Diagram — IDS in a Typical Enterprise Network

This diagram shows the **strategic placement** of a Network IDS in a typical enterprise topology, illustrating how it monitors traffic between the internet, DMZ, and internal LAN.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Internet[Internet / External Network] --> FW[Perimeter Firewall]
    FW --> DMZ[DMZ Zone: Web, Mail, DNS Servers]
    FW --> TAP[Network TAP or SPAN Port]
    DMZ --> TAP
    TAP --> NIDS[Network Intrusion Detection System Sensor]
    NIDS --> SIEM[Centralized SIEM Server]
    InternalLAN[Internal LAN: User Workstations and Servers] --> HIDS[Host Based IDS Agents]
    HIDS --> SIEM
    SIEM --> SOC[Security Operations Center Analyst Console]
```

## 4.3 The Defence-in-Depth Layered Model

The following diagram shows **where the IDS sits** within the broader cybersecurity defence-in-depth framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Physical Security Locks, Biometrics, CCTV] --> L2[Layer 2: Perimeter Firewall and ACLs]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Intrusion Detection System IDS]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Host Based Controls Antivirus, HIDS, EDR]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Data Encryption at Rest and in Transit]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Application Layer WAF and API Gateways]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Identity and Access Management IAM, MFA]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8: Continuous Monitoring, SIEM, and SOC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Layer 3 (IDS) sits between the perimeter firewall (Layer 2) and the host-based controls (Layer 4). This is its **defence-in-depth position** — it catches what the firewall misses and provides contextual data to the host-based controls.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define an Intrusion Detection System (IDS). List any TWO limitations of signature-based IDS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Limit: 80–100 words):**

> An Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is a security mechanism that **monitors network traffic or host activities**, **analyzes events for signs of security incidents** such as unauthorized access or policy violations, and **issues alerts** to administrators. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Limitations of Signature-based IDS: (2 marks — 1 mark each)**
> 1. **Cannot detect zero-day attacks:** It relies on a database of known attack signatures; novel attacks with no existing signature slip through undetected.
> 2. **High resource consumption on high-speed networks:** Deep packet inspection against thousands of signatures is computationally expensive and may drop packets under load.
> 3. *(Any other valid point: evasion via encryption, fragmentation, polymorphism)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Firewall and an Intrusion Detection System.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular format preferred):**

| Parameter | Firewall | IDS |
|-----------|----------|-----|
| **Function** | Filters traffic based on predefined rules (allow/deny). | Monitors and analyzes traffic/activities for malicious patterns. |
| **Action on Threat** | Blocks traffic inline. | Generates alerts; does NOT block. |
| **Position** | Always inline. | Mostly passive (taps/SPAN). |
| **Detection Capability** | Limited to rule-based port/protocol filtering. | Deep Packet Inspection (DPI), behavioral analysis, signature matching. |
| **Insider Threat** | Poor detection. | Better detection (especially HIDS). |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** write "IDS prevents attacks" — it only **detects**. Prevention is the role of an **IPS**. Losing 1–2 marks for this single conceptual error is common.
> 2. **Always** mention the **two main detection approaches** (signature-based and anomaly-based) when asked about IDS types — examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **For numerical questions**, always **show the confusion matrix** derivation before plugging values into the formula. Jumping directly to the answer costs 2 marks.
> 4. When defining IDS, **do not omit** the phrase *"monitors and analyzes"*. It is the key verb examiners search for.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Define Intrusion Detection System. Explain in detail the **purpose and objectives** of an IDS in modern enterprise networks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)**. Compare NIDS with HIDS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Definition and Purpose of IDS (7 Marks):**

An IDS is a security tool that **monitors** network or host events, **analyzes** them against known patterns or behavioral baselines, and **issues alerts** when policy violations or attack indicators are detected. **(2 marks)**

**Objectives of IDS in Enterprise Networks (5 marks):**

1. **Early Threat Detection:** Identify attacks at the reconnaissance, scanning, or exploitation stage before they reach critical assets.
2. **Security Policy Enforcement:** Detect violations of the organization's acceptable-use policy (e.g., unauthorized P2P usage).
3. **Forensic Data Collection:** Maintain detailed logs of events for post-incident investigation and legal evidence.
4. **Compliance & Regulatory Requirements:** Meet audit mandates (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX, ISO 27001) which require continuous monitoring.
5. **Defence-in-Depth Layer:** Complement firewalls and antivirus by providing visibility into traffic that bypasses perimeter defenses.
6. **Incident Response Enablement:** Reduce Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) through real-time alerts.
7. **Vulnerability Identification:** Detect unpatched systems and misconfigurations (e.g., by spotting exploit attempts for known CVEs).

**(b) NIDS Architecture and NIDS vs HIDS Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Architecture of NIDS (5 marks):**

A NIDS consists of the following components:

1. **Network Tap / SPAN Port:** Receives a copy of the wire traffic.
2. **Packet Capturing Engine:** Uses libpcap / WinPcap to capture packets in promiscuous mode.
3. **Pre-processor:** Defragments packets, reassembles TCP streams, decodes protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMB).
4. **Detection Engine:** Applies signature matching (e.g., Snort rules) and anomaly detection algorithms.
5. **Alert/Logging Module:** Generates alerts in syslog, unified2, or JSON format.

**Working:** The packet capture module feeds packets to the pre-processor. The pre-processor normalizes and reassembles the data. The detection engine compares the data against a rule database. A match triggers an alert, which is sent to the SIEM or analyst console.

**NIDS vs HIDS (2 marks):**

| Feature | NIDS | HIDS |
|---------|------|------|
| Monitoring Scope | Entire network segment at wire-speed | Single host's logs, syscalls, file integrity |
| Visibility into Encrypted Traffic | Limited (only metadata) | Full (after decryption at host) |
| Resource Impact | Sensor machine only | Each protected host |
| Detection of Insider Threats | Limited (sees network only) | Excellent (sees user activity) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **two main approaches to intrusion detection**: **Signature-based (Misuse) detection** and **Anomaly-based detection**. Compare them on at least 5 parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An IDS was tested on **50,000 network events**. There were **500 actual attacks**, of which the IDS correctly detected **450**. The IDS raised a total of **600 alerts**, of which **150 were false alarms**. Calculate: **(i)** True Positive Rate, **(ii)** False Positive Rate, **(iii)** Precision, **(iv)** Accuracy, and **(v)** F1-Score. Comment on the suitability of this IDS for a production environment. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Two Approaches to Intrusion Detection (7 Marks):**

**1. Signature-Based (Misuse) Detection (3 marks):**
- Uses a **database of known attack patterns (signatures or rules)**.
- Each rule defines a pattern of bytes, protocol fields, or behavioral sequences that uniquely identify an attack.
- Example: Snort rule `alert tcp any any → any 80 (content: "/cgi-bin/phf"; msg:"PHF probe";)` detects the ancient PHF CGI exploit.
- **Advantages:** Very low false positive rate, fast matching, easy for analysts to understand.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot detect **zero-day attacks**, requires constant signature updates, easily evaded by polymorphism, encryption, and fragmentation.

**2. Anomaly-Based Detection (3 marks):**
- Builds a **statistical or ML-based model of "normal" behavior** during a training phase.
- Flags any event that **deviates significantly** from the learned baseline.
- Techniques include statistical profiling, clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN), neural networks, and Hidden Markov Models.
- **Advantages:** Can detect **previously unseen (zero-day) attacks**, adapts to network evolution.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher false positive rate (especially during initial training), requires clean training data, computationally heavier.

**Comparison (1 mark — concise table):**

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| Detects Zero-Day | No | Yes |
| False Positive Rate | Low | High |
| Speed | Fast | Slower (ML inference) |
| Maintenance | Frequent signature updates | Periodic retraining |
| Interpretability | High (rule readable) | Low (black box) |

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify Confusion Matrix values from the problem statement.**
- Total events = 50,000
- Actual attacks $P = 500$ → so Benign $N = 50{,}000 - 500 = 49{,}500$
- Detected attacks $TP = 450$
- Missed attacks $FN = P - TP = 500 - 450 = 50$
- Total alerts raised = 600 → so $FP = 600 - TP = 600 - 450 = 150$
- True Negatives $TN = N - FP = 49{,}500 - 150 = 49{,}350$

**Step 2: Compute True Positive Rate (1 mark):**

$$TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{450}{450 + 50} = \dfrac{450}{500} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

**Step 3: Compute False Positive Rate (1 mark):**

$$FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{150}{150 + 49{,}350} = \dfrac{150}{49{,}500} \approx 0.00303 = 0.303\%$$

**Step 4: Compute Precision (1 mark):**

$$Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{450}{450 + 150} = \dfrac{450}{600} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 5: Compute Accuracy (1 mark):**

$$Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{Total} = \dfrac{450 + 49{,}350}{50{,}000} = \dfrac{49{,}800}{50{,}000} = 0.996 = 99.6\%$$

**Step 6: Compute F1-Score (1 mark):**

$$F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 450}{2 \cdot 450 + 150 + 50} = \dfrac{900}{1100} = 0.8182 = 81.82\%$$

**Step 7: Comment on Production Suitability (1 mark):**

The IDS is **moderately suitable** for production. The TPR of 90% means 1 in 10 attacks is missed (50 attacks slip through — unacceptable for a high-security environment). The 75% Precision implies 1 in 4 alerts is a false alarm, which can cause **alert fatigue** in the SOC. For a production-grade deployment, **TPR should be >99%** and **Precision should be >90%**, achievable through **hybrid signature + anomaly detection** (e.g., Snort + ML module).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** IDS = Monitor + Analyze + Alert. It does **NOT block**. Blocking is the job of an **IPS**.
- **CIA Triad:** The IDS protects **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** of system resources.
- **Three Primary Types:** NIDS (network), HIDS (host), Hybrid IDS (both). Lesser-known: WIDS (wireless), NBA (network behavior analysis).
- **Two Detection Approaches:** **Signature-based** (low FP, no zero-day) vs **Anomaly-based** (zero-day capable, high FP). Modern systems use **hybrid / stateful protocol analysis**.
- **Key Components of IDS:** Sensor → Pre-processor → Detection Engine → Alert Generator → SIEM/SOC.
- **Placement:** Inline (TAP) or Passive (SPAN port) for NIDS; on-host for HIDS.
- **The Four Performance Metrics You MUST Memorize:** $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$, $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$.
- **Accuracy Paradox:** High accuracy can be misleading on imbalanced datasets (e.g., 99% accuracy with 1% attack rate). Always use **F1-Score** for security datasets.
- **Defence-in-Depth Position:** IDS is the **second layer** after the firewall and **before** host-based controls.
- **Real-World Examples to Quote in Answers:** Snort (open-source NIDS), Suricata (multi-threaded NIDS), OSSEC (open-source HIDS), Zeek (formerly Bro — network analysis framework), Cisco Firepower (commercial NIDS/IPS), AWS GuardDuty (cloud-native IDS).
- **Standards / Frameworks to Reference:** NIST SP 800-94 (Guide to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems), RFC 4769 (Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format — IDMEF), MITRE ATT&CK Framework.
- **Common Student Pitfall:** Confusing **False Positive (Type I error)** with **False Negative (Type II error)**. Remember: **Type I = False Alarm**, **Type II = Missed Detection**.
- **One-Line Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** *"An IDS is a burglar alarm for the network — it watches, listens, and shouts, but it does not lock the door."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of IDS - Network-based, Host-based, and Hybrid IDS Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of IDS - Network-based, Host-based, and Hybrid IDS Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism that monitors network traffic or system activities for malicious activities, policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts, and generates alerts upon detection. Based on the **source of data collection** and **deployment architecture**, IDSs are classified into three primary types:

1. **Network-based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)** — Monitors network traffic across an entire subnet by analyzing packets flowing through the network.
2. **Host-based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)** — Monitors activities on a single host/endpoint by analyzing system logs, file integrity, and process behavior.
3. **Hybrid Intrusion Detection System (Hybrid IDS)** — Integrates both NIDS and HIDS into a unified architecture to overcome the individual limitations of each.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification is based on **data source** (network packets vs. host logs) and **monitoring scope** (segment-level vs. endpoint-level), not on detection methodology (signature vs. anomaly).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of a **large residential apartment complex** as your computer network:

| Security Layer | Real-World Analogy | IDS Equivalent |
|----------------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Main gate CCTV** | Watches every car/visitor entering the building premises | **NIDS** |
| **Apartment door peephole** | Watches who enters YOUR specific apartment | **HIDS** |
| **Central control room with both cameras & door sensors** | Combined surveillance | **Hybrid IDS** |

- The **CCTV at the gate (NIDS)** sees *who comes and goes* but cannot tell what happens *inside* an apartment.
- The **door peephole (HIDS)** sees *who enters your room* but is *blind* to activity in other apartments or the corridor.
- The **Central control room (Hybrid IDS)** combines both views to detect coordinated or distributed threats that one system alone might miss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** No single IDS type is universally "best." A mature enterprise security posture deploys **defense-in-depth**, layering all three types — exactly why KTU emphasizes understanding *when to use what*.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Industry Standard)

The effectiveness of any IDS (NIDS, HIDS, or Hybrid) is quantified using these four canonical metrics:

- **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Detection Rate (DR)** — Fraction of actual attacks correctly identified.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — Fraction of normal events incorrectly flagged as attacks.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR)** — Fraction of actual attacks missed by the IDS.
- **Accuracy** — Overall proportion of correct classifications (both benign and malicious).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Principle:** Increasing detection sensitivity **lowers FNR** (catches more attacks) but **raises FPR** (more false alarms). KTU board questions frequently test this trade-off — examiners *love* this concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network-based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Operational Architecture
A NIDS operates in a **promiscuous mode**, capturing every packet traversing a network segment by placing its network interface card (NIC) into a state where it accepts *all* frames, not just those addressed to it.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. **Tap/Span Placement** — A network tap or a Switched Port Analyzer (SPAN) mirror port duplicates live traffic and feeds it to the NIDS sensor.
2. **Packet Capture** — A libpcap/WinPcap-based sniffer (e.g., Wireshark backend) captures raw packets.
3. **Protocol Decoding** — Packets are decoded up to Layer 7 (Application layer) using a protocol stack analyzer.
4. **Pattern Matching / Anomaly Scoring** — Signatures are matched (e.g., Snort rules) or statistical baselines compared.
5. **Alert Generation** — Matches above threshold produce logs and alerts sent to a SIEM console.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "promiscuous mode"?** A normal NIC discards frames not addressed to its MAC. Promiscuous mode disables this filter, allowing the sensor to "eavesdrop" on the *entire* collision domain or VLAN.

### 2.1.2 Strengths & Weaknesses of NIDS

**Strengths:**
- **Broad Coverage** — Monitors an entire subnet with a single deployment point.
- **Stealth** — Passive sensor; difficult for attackers to detect its presence.
- **Attack Pattern Visibility** — Sees *the full network conversation*, including multi-host attacks (e.g., port scans, DDoS).

**Weaknesses:**
- **Encrypted Traffic Blindness** — Cannot inspect payloads inside TLS/SSL tunnels.
- **Switched Network Limitations** — Modern switched networks require SPAN/tap configuration.
- **No Host Context** — Cannot tell *what process* on a host generated suspicious traffic.
- **High-Speed Network Overload** — On >1 Gbps links, packet drops become common.

## 2.2 Host-based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS) — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Operational Architecture
A HIDS is installed as **software on the individual host** being protected. It monitors internal host state through three primary data sources:

1. **Operating System Audit Logs** — e.g., Linux `auditd`, Windows Event Logs (Event ID 4624, 4625, 4688).
2. **File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)** — Hashes of critical files (e.g., Tripwire, OSSEC).
3. **System Call Sequences** — Traces of process syscalls (e.g., strace, ptrace-based monitors).

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. **Agent Installation** — Software agent deployed on each host (server, workstation, or critical endpoint).
2. **Baseline Establishment** — A "clean state" baseline is recorded (file hashes, system call patterns, log volumes).
3. **Continuous Monitoring** — The agent compares real-time activity against the baseline.
4. **Anomaly Detection** — Deviations trigger alerts (e.g., a system file's hash changed).
5. **Local Response / Reporting** — May block the process locally or forward alerts to a central server.

### 2.2.2 Strengths & Weaknesses of HIDS

**Strengths:**
- **Host-Context Awareness** — Can correlate an attack to a specific user, process, or file.
- **Encrypted Traffic Decryptable** — Operates *after* decryption at the application layer.
- **Detects Insider Threats** — Catches abuse by legitimate users with valid credentials.
- **Host-Specific Behavior** — Very low false positive rate for host-specific anomalies.

**Weaknesses:**
- **Limited Network Visibility** — Blind to attacks that don't touch the host (e.g., scans of other hosts).
- **Host Performance Overhead** — Consumes CPU/RAM/disk on the protected host.
- **Agent Management Complexity** — Every host must be patched, updated, and maintained.
- **Disablement Risk** — A successful rootkit can disable the HIDS agent.

## 2.3 Hybrid Intrusion Detection System (Hybrid IDS) — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Operational Architecture
A Hybrid IDS (sometimes called a **Distributed IDS** or **Integrated IDS**) combines the network-wide visibility of NIDS with the host-level detail of HIDS through a **centralized correlation engine**.

**Three-Tier Architecture:**
1. **Sensor Tier** — NIDS sensors on network segments + HIDS agents on critical hosts.
2. **Correlation Tier** — Central server (e.g., SIEM like Splunk, OSSIM, or Prelude) that aggregates events.
3. **Management/Console Tier** — Security analyst dashboard for triage and response.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. NIDS detects a suspicious port scan from IP $\rightarrow$ 192.168.1.50.
2. The central correlation engine **queries** the HIDS agent on 192.168.1.50.
3. HIDS reports an unusual process spawning (`cmd.exe` invoked by `winword.exe`).
4. **Correlation logic** confirms a multi-stage attack — NIDS saw the *reconnaissance*, HIDS saw the *compromise*.
5. Alert escalated with full context (who, what, when, where, how).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Pet Concept:** "Why is Hybrid IDS superior to standalone NIDS or HIDS?" The model answer: *Because neither NIDS nor HIDS can correlate cross-domain events. A NIDS sees the network, a HIDS sees the host — only a Hybrid IDS sees the **story**.*

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** Let $TP$ = True Positives, $TN$ = True Negatives, $FP$ = False Positives, $FN$ = False Negatives, $N$ = Total samples.

$$
\text{Detection Rate (DR / TPR / Recall)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
\text{False Negative Rate (FNR)} = \frac{FN}{TP + FN} = 1 - DR
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{F1-Score} = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
\text{Base Rate Fallacy Adjustment (Bayes)}: P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack})}{P(\text{Alert})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Base Rate Fallacy Trap:** Even with **99% DR** and **1% FPR**, if attacks are rare (e.g., 1 in 10,000 events), the probability that an alert is *actually* a real attack is **less than 1%**. KTU examiners test this with numerical questions.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility Summary

| Architecture | Best Deployed For | Real-World Examples |
|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **NIDS** | Perimeter defense, DDoS detection, scan identification | Snort, Suricata, Zeek |
| **HIDS** | Critical servers, insider threat detection, file integrity | OSSEC, Tripwire, Wazuh |
| **Hybrid IDS** | Enterprise SOC, multi-stage APT detection, compliance | Splunk + Snort, OSSIM, Microsoft Defender ATP |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Feature-by-Feature Comparison

| Feature | NIDS | HIDS | Hybrid IDS |
|---------|:----:|:----:|:----------:|
| **Data Source** | Network packets (PCAP) | OS logs, syscalls, file hashes | Both packet + host telemetry |
| **Deployment Location** | Network segment (tap/SPAN) | Individual host (agent) | Distributed + central server |
| **Encrypted Traffic Analysis** | Limited (payload blind) | Effective (post-decryption) | Partially effective |
| **Host Process Visibility** | None | Full (PID, UID, syscall) | Full via correlation |
| **Network Pattern Visibility** | Full | None (only local traffic) | Full |
| **False Positive Rate** | Higher (no host context) | Lower (host-specific baselines) | Lowest (correlated evidence) |
| **Implementation Cost** | Moderate (one sensor per segment) | High (agent per host) | Highest (full stack) |
| **Survives Host Compromise?** | Yes (external sensor) | No (agent can be killed) | Yes (NIDS still reports) |
| **Insider Threat Detection** | Weak | Strong | Strong |
| **Encrypted Channel Analysis** | Weak | Strong | Strong |
| **Scalability** | High (per segment) | Linear (per host) | High with good SIEM |

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Why Hybrid IDS Achieves Lower FPR

We will prove that the **correlated FPR** of a Hybrid IDS is mathematically *lower* than either component's FPR alone, **assuming statistical independence** of false alarms from NIDS and HIDS.

### Step 1: Define Component False Positive Rates

Let:
- $FPR_N$ = False Positive Rate of the NIDS component
- $FPR_H$ = False Positive Rate of the HIDS component

Both produce an alert when their respective thresholds are crossed.

### Step 2: Naive (Independent) Combination

If NIDS and HIDS both fire alerts **independently**, the probability that *at least one* fires falsely is:

$$
P(\text{False Alert}_{\text{union}}) = 1 - P(\text{Neither fires falsely})
$$

$$
P(\text{Neither fires falsely}) = (1 - FPR_N) \cdot (1 - FPR_H)
$$

$$
P(\text{False Alert}_{\text{union}}) = 1 - (1 - FPR_N)(1 - FPR_H)
$$

### Step 3: KTU Insight — Naive Combination *Worsens* FPR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Point:** If we alert on **either** NIDS OR HIDS triggering, the union's FPR is *higher*, not lower. This is the **OR-gate paradox** in IDS design.

**Worked Example (Numerical):**
Let $FPR_N = 0.10$ and $FPR_H = 0.05$.

$$
P(\text{False Alert}_{\text{OR}}) = 1 - (1 - 0.10)(1 - 0.05) = 1 - (0.90 \times 0.95) = 1 - 0.855 = 0.145
$$

So a naive OR-combination **increases** FPR from 10% to **14.5%**. The Hybrid IDS must therefore use a **correlation rule** to *avoid* this.

### Step 4: The Hybrid IDS Solution — AND-Gate with Correlation

A Hybrid IDS uses a **correlated AND** rule: alert *only* if the NIDS event and HIDS event **both** indicate the same attack within a time window $\Delta t$.

Under independence, the joint FPR is:

$$
FPR_{\text{Hybrid}} = FPR_N \cdot FPR_H
$$

**Worked Example (Numerical):**

$$
FPR_{\text{Hybrid}} = 0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.005 = 0.5\%
$$

This is **29 times lower** than the naive OR-combination (14.5% vs 0.5%).

### Step 5: Detection Rate Trade-off (Senior KTU Depth)

The Hybrid AND-rule's detection rate becomes:

$$
DR_{\text{Hybrid}} = DR_N \cdot DR_H
$$

If $DR_N = 0.90$ and $DR_H = 0.95$:

$$
DR_{\text{Hybrid}} = 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.855 = 85.5\%
$$

The Hybrid IDS trades **4.5% of detection rate** for a **29× reduction in false positives** — a favorable trade in operational security.

### Step 6: Final Comparison Table (Derived)

| Metric | NIDS | HIDS | Hybrid (AND) | Hybrid (OR) |
|--------|:----:|:----:|:-------------:|:-----------:|
| **FPR** | 0.100 | 0.050 | **0.005** | 0.145 |
| **DR** | 0.900 | 0.950 | 0.855 | 0.995 |
| **Accuracy** | 0.900 | 0.950 | 0.849 | 0.854 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Gold Insight:** A well-designed Hybrid IDS achieves the **best FPR** (0.5%) at the cost of slightly lower detection rate. The OR-gate is used only when missing attacks is more expensive than false alarms (e.g., nuclear/medical systems).

## 3.3 Application of Bayes' Theorem to IDS (KTU High-Yield Topic)

**Scenario:** A NIDS flags an alert. The IDS's detection rate is 95%, false positive rate is 2%. In the network, only 1 in 10,000 packets is actually malicious. What is the probability the alert indicates a *real* attack?

**Step 1: Define Events**
- $A$ = Packet is actually malicious
- $\bar{A}$ = Packet is benign
- $E$ = IDS raises an alert

**Step 2: Given Probabilities**
- $P(E \mid A) = 0.95$ (DR)
- $P(E \mid \bar{A}) = 0.02$ (FPR)
- $P(A) = 0.0001$ (Base rate)
- $P(\bar{A}) = 0.9999$

**Step 3: Total Probability of an Alert**

$$
P(E) = P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(E \mid \bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{A})
$$

$$
P(E) = (0.95 \times 0.0001) + (0.02 \times 0.9999)
$$

$$
P(E) = 0.000095 + 0.019998 = 0.020093
$$

**Step 4: Apply Bayes' Theorem**

$$
P(A \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}
$$

$$
P(A \mid E) = \frac{0.95 \times 0.0001}{0.020093} = \frac{0.000095}{0.020093} \approx 0.004728
$$

**Result:** $P(A \mid E) \approx 0.47\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **Striking Result:** Even with 95% accuracy, only **0.47%** of alerts are real attacks. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy**, and it is one of the most important KTU examiner traps.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NIDS Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    internet[Internet External Network] -->|Traffic Flows| fw[Enterprise Firewall]
    fw -->|Filtered Traffic| switch1[Core L3 Switch]
    switch1 -->|Span Port Mirror| sensor1[NIDS Sensor 1]
    switch1 -->|Normal Forwarding| lan1[Internal LAN Segment 1]
    switch1 -->|Normal Forwarding| lan2[Internal LAN Segment 2]
    sensor1 -->|Alerts and Logs| siem1[Central SIEM Console]
    analyst1[SOC Security Analyst] -->|Reviews Triage| siem1
    siem1 -->|Response Actions| fw
    siem1 -->|Block IP| isp[ISP Level Blocklist]

    style internet fill:#FFE0E0
    style fw fill:#FFD580
    style sensor1 fill:#80C0FF
    style siem1 fill:#80FF80
    style analyst1 fill:#C0C0C0
```

**Component Roles:**
- **Firewall** — Pre-filters obvious threats, reducing NIDS load.
- **SPAN Port** — Mirrors live traffic to the NIDS sensor.
- **NIDS Sensor** — Runs signature/anomaly engine on captured packets.
- **SIEM Console** — Aggregates and correlates alerts from multiple sensors.

## 4.2 HIDS Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    host[Protected Host or Server] -->|OS Audit Logs| agent1[HIDS Agent Software]
    host -->|System Call Traces| agent1
    host -->|File Hash Changes| agent1
    agent1 -->|Compare With Baseline| baseline[Local Baseline Database]
    baseline -->|Mismatch Detected| alert1[Local Alert Generator]
    alert1 -->|Alert Payload| log1[Central Log Server]
    log1 -->|Aggregated Events| siem2[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    analyst2[Security Analyst] -->|Investigates| siem2
    siem2 -->|Quarantine Host| edr[EDR Response Module]
    edr -->|Kill Process| host

    style host fill:#FFE0E0
    style agent1 fill:#80C0FF
    style baseline fill:#FFD580
    style log1 fill:#C0A0FF
    style siem2 fill:#80FF80
    style analyst2 fill:#C0C0C0
    style edr fill:#FF8080
```

**Component Roles:**
- **HIDS Agent** — OS-level sensor monitoring syscalls, logs, file changes.
- **Baseline DB** — Stores cryptographic hashes of known-good system files.
- **EDR Module** — Endpoint Detection & Response, the "active" counterpart that can isolate the host.

## 4.3 Hybrid IDS Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inet[External Network] -->|Traffic In| edgefw[Edge Firewall]
    edgefw -->|Pass Through| corsw[Core Switch With SPAN]
    corsw -->|Mirror Copy| nids1[NIDS Sensor Perimeter]
    corsw -->|Mirror Copy| nids2[NIDS Sensor DMZ]
    corsw -->|Normal Traffic| subnet1[Server Subnet]
    corsw -->|Normal Traffic| subnet2[Workstation Subnet]
    subnet1 -->|Host Events| hids1[HIDS Agent Server A]
    subnet1 -->|Host Events| hids2[HIDS Agent Server B]
    subnet2 -->|Host Events| hids3[HIDS Agent Endpoint X]
    nids1 -->|Network Alerts| corr[Central Correlation Engine]
    nids2 -->|Network Alerts| corr
    hids1 -->|Host Alerts| corr
    hids2 -->|Host Alerts| corr
    hids3 -->|Host Alerts| corr
    corr -->|Correlated Incidents| socdash[SOC Dashboard]
    socdash -->|Analyst Triage| socanalyst[SOC Analyst Team]
    socanalyst -->|Block Commands| edgefw
    socanalyst -->|Isolate Commands| subnet1
    socanalyst -->|Isolate Commands| subnet2

    style inet fill:#FFE0E0
    style edgefw fill:#FFD580
    style nids1 fill:#80C0FF
    style nids2 fill:#80C0FF
    style hids1 fill:#C0A0FF
    style hids2 fill:#C0A0FF
    style hids3 fill:#C0A0FF
    style corr fill:#80FF80
    style socdash fill:#FFA080
    style socanalyst fill:#C0C0C0
```

**Architectural Flow Explanation:**
1. NIDS sensors at **perimeter** and **DMZ** watch east-west and north-south traffic.
2. HIDS agents on every critical host watch local behavior.
3. Both feed the **central correlation engine** — this is the *brain* of the Hybrid IDS.
4. The SOC dashboard presents unified, contextualized alerts to human analysts.
5. Bidirectional response allows containment actions (block IP, isolate host).

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Pattern Recognition:** Notice how the Hybrid diagram **contains both NIDS and HIDS as sub-modules** feeding a higher correlation layer. This nesting visually represents the conceptual superiority of Hybrid IDS in KTU answers.

## 4.4 Detection Logic Flow — How a Hybrid IDS Decides

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Packet Arrives] --> p2{NIDS Check}
    p2 -->|No Match| p4[Allow and Log]
    p2 -->|Match Found| p3{Time Window Delta T}
    p3 -->|HIDS Co-Fires| p5[Hybrid Correlated Alert HIGH CONFIDENCE]
    p3 -->|No HIDS Co-Fire| p6[Standalone NIDS Alert MEDIUM CONFIDENCE]
    p1 --> p7[Host Syscall Event]
    p7 --> p8{HIDS Check}
    p8 -->|No Match| p4
    p8 -->|Match Found| p9{NIDS Co-Fires}
    p9 -->|Yes Within Delta T| p5
    p9 -->|No Co-Fire| p10[Standalone HIDS Alert MEDIUM CONFIDENCE]
    p5 --> p11[Escalate to SOC]
    p6 --> p12[Log and Auto-Investigate]
    p10 --> p12

    style p5 fill:#FF8080
    style p6 fill:#FFD580
    style p10 fill:#FFD580
    style p11 fill:#80FF80
    style p12 fill:#C0C0C0
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Differentiate between Network-based IDS (NIDS) and Host-based IDS (HIDS) with suitable examples.**
