**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | NIDS | HIDS |
|--------|------|------|
| **Data Source** | Network packets (PCAP) | OS logs, file integrity, syscalls |
| **Placement** | Network segment, tap/SPAN port | Software agent on individual host |
| **Visibility** | Entire network segment | Single host's internal activity |
| **Encrypted Traffic** | Cannot inspect inside TLS | Can analyze post-decryption |
| **Examples** | Snort, Suricata, Zeek | OSSEC, Tripwire, Wazuh |
| **Best For** | DDoS, port scans, reconnaissance | Insider threats, malware, file tampering |

*(1 mark for each clear difference across data source, placement, and visibility; 1 mark for examples.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q: What is a Hybrid IDS? Why is it considered superior to standalone NIDS or HIDS?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Hybrid IDS** is an integrated architecture that combines NIDS (network-wide packet monitoring) and HIDS (host-level log/file/process monitoring) under a **centralized correlation engine** (typically a SIEM).

It is considered superior because:
1. **Cross-Domain Correlation** — Detects multi-stage attacks (recon + exploitation) that neither NIDS nor HIDS can identify alone.
2. **Lower False Positive Rate** — Correlated AND-rule yields $FPR_{\text{Hybrid}} = FPR_N \cdot FPR_H$, mathematically lower than either component.
3. **Comprehensive Context** — Provides both *who* (host) and *what* (network) for every alert.
4. **Defense-in-Depth** — Survives single-point failures; if HIDS is disabled, NIDS still reports.

*(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for superiority justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[14 Marks — KTU University Exam Pattern]`

**Q (a)** Explain the architecture, working, strengths, and limitations of a **Network-based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)**. Illustrate with a suitable block diagram. **[7 Marks]**

**Q (b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Host-based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)** and explain how it complements NIDS in a hybrid deployment. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — NIDS Architecture [7 Marks]

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
A NIDS is a security mechanism that monitors and analyzes network traffic across an entire subnet by capturing packets in promiscuous mode and comparing them against known attack signatures or statistical baselines.

**2. Architecture Components (2 Marks):**
- **Network Tap / SPAN Port** — Mirrors live traffic to the sensor.
- **Packet Capture Engine** — Uses libpcap to capture raw frames.
- **Protocol Decoder** — Reassembles TCP/UDP streams and decodes Layer 7.
- **Detection Engine** — Signature-based (Snort rules) or anomaly-based (statistical/ML).
- **Alert/Logging Module** — Generates alerts and forwards to SIEM.

**3. Working Steps (2 Marks):**
1. Sensor is connected to a SPAN port of a switch.
2. NIC operates in promiscuous mode, capturing all frames.
3. Packets are decoded up to the application layer.
4. Detection engine matches against ruleset/baseline.
5. Matches trigger alerts sent to the central console.

**4. Strengths (1 Mark):**
- Single deployment monitors an entire subnet.
- Stealthy and difficult for attackers to detect.
- Detects network-wide patterns (scans, DDoS).

**5. Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Cannot inspect encrypted payloads (TLS blind spot).
- No host-level context (cannot identify the *process*).
- Drops packets on high-speed (>1 Gbps) links.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — HIDS & Hybrid Integration [7 Marks]

**1. HIDS Definition (1 Mark):**
A HIDS is a software agent installed on a single host that monitors local system activities — OS audit logs, file integrity hashes, and system call sequences — to detect malicious behavior.

**2. HIDS Architecture (2 Marks):**
- **Agent** — Runs as a background service (e.g., `ossec-agentd`).
- **Baseline Database** — Stores hashes of known-good files.
- **Log Collector** — Reads OS audit logs (e.g., `/var/log/audit/audit.log`).
- **System Call Monitor** — Traces process syscalls via ptrace.
- **Alert Forwarder** — Sends events to the central server.

**3. How HIDS Complements NIDS (2 Marks):**

| NIDS Sees | HIDS Sees | Combined Insight |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Port scan from external IP | Process spawn on scanned host | Reconnaissance phase of APT |
| Unusual outbound traffic volume | New service installed by `cmd.exe` | Data exfiltration by malware |
| SSH brute-force attempts | Successful login at 3 AM | Compromised credentials |

**4. Hybrid Deployment Architecture (2 Marks):**
A SIEM (Splunk, OSSIM) acts as the correlation engine. NIDS alerts trigger **look-up queries** to HIDS agents for host context. The combined view is presented to SOC analysts, who can then **isolate the host** (via EDR) or **block the IP** (via firewall) in real time.

---

### Question B `[14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice]`

**Q (a)** Discuss the **three-tier architecture of a Hybrid IDS** and explain how the correlation engine reduces false positives using a numerical example. **[7 Marks]**

**Q (b)** A NIDS has a detection rate of 92% and a false positive rate of 8%. In a network, only 1 in 5000 packets is actually malicious. Using **Bayes' theorem**, calculate the probability that a flagged alert represents a *true* attack. Comment on the result. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Hybrid IDS 3-Tier Architecture [7 Marks]

**1. Three-Tier Definition (1 Mark):**
A Hybrid IDS consists of (i) Sensor Tier, (ii) Correlation Tier, and (iii) Management Tier.

**2. Tier 1 — Sensor Layer (2 Marks):**
- **NIDS sensors** at network chokepoints (perimeter, DMZ, core switch SPAN).
- **HIDS agents** on critical servers, database hosts, and endpoints.
- Both generate raw events in standardized formats (CEF, LEEF, syslog).

**3. Tier 2 — Correlation Engine (2 Marks):**
The central server (e.g., OSSIM, ArcSight) applies correlation rules such as:
> *IF (NIDS detects port scan FROM IP X) AND (HIDS on any host in X's subnet reports unusual process) WITHIN 10 minutes → ESCALATE.*

**4. Numerical Example for FPR Reduction (2 Marks):**
Given $FPR_N = 0.10$ and $FPR_H = 0.05$, the AND-correlated FPR is:

$$
FPR_{\text{Hybrid}} = 0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.005 = 0.5\%
$$

This is a **20× reduction** compared to NIDS alone (10% → 0.5%).

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Bayes' Theorem Application [7 Marks]

**1. Define Events and Given Values (1 Mark):**
- $P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) = 0.92$ (Detection Rate)
- $P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Normal}) = 0.08$ (False Positive Rate)
- $P(\text{Attack}) = \frac{1}{5000} = 0.0002$ (Base Rate)
- $P(\text{Normal}) = 0.9998$

**2. Compute Total Alert Probability (2 Marks):**

$$
P(\text{Alert}) = P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack}) + P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Normal}) \cdot P(\text{Normal})
$$

$$
P(\text{Alert}) = (0.92 \times 0.0002) + (0.08 \times 0.9998)
$$

$$
P(\text{Alert}) = 0.000184 + 0.079984 = 0.080168
$$

**3. Apply Bayes' Theorem (2 Marks):**

$$
P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{0.92 \times 0.0002}{0.080168} = \frac{0.000184}{0.080168} \approx 0.002296
$$

$$
P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) \approx 0.23\%
$$

**4. Comment on the Result (2 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprising Insight:** Despite the NIDS having 92% detection accuracy, only **0.23% of alerts are genuine attacks**. The other **99.77% are false positives**. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy** — when malicious events are rare, even highly accurate detectors produce mostly false alarms. This justifies the need for **Hybrid IDS with correlation logic** to filter noise, and emphasizes why SOC analysts must use multi-source evidence before acting on an alert.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating "signature-based" with "NIDS"** — Signature vs. anomaly is the *detection method*; NIDS vs. HIDS is the *data source*. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the AND vs. OR correlation distinction** — Students often state "Hybrid IDS reduces FPR" without explaining *how*. Always mention the AND-gate with joint probability $FPR_N \times FPR_H$.
> 3. **Missing Bayes' theorem in numerical questions** — Many KTU questions are designed to test the Base Rate Fallacy. Skipping the prior probability $P(A)$ loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing the diagram** — In a 14-mark question, the block diagram is worth **2–3 marks**. Omitting it is a guaranteed deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PECPT753 Module 1**

- ✅ **IDS Classification** is based on **data source**, not detection method. Three types: NIDS, HIDS, Hybrid IDS.
- ✅ **NIDS** = Network-wide packet monitoring via SPAN/tap. Best for DDoS, port scans, reconnaissance. **Cannot** inspect encrypted payloads.
- ✅ **HIDS** = Per-host agent monitoring OS logs, file integrity, syscalls. Best for insider threats, malware, file tampering. **Can** analyze post-decryption traffic.
- ✅ **Hybrid IDS** = NIDS + HIDS + Central Correlation Engine (SIEM). Achieves the **lowest FPR** via AND-rule correlation.
- ✅ **Promiscuous Mode** is the operational prerequisite for any NIDS sensor.
- ✅ **SPAN Port** (Switched Port Analyzer) is the modern mechanism to feed NIDS sensors in switched networks.
- ✅ **False Positive Rate (FPR)** and **Detection Rate (DR)** are the two cardinal IDS performance metrics. There is an inherent **trade-off** between them.
- ✅ **AND-Rule Hybrid Correlation Formula:** $FPR_{\text{Hybrid}} = FPR_N \times FPR_H$ (assuming independence).
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is essential: $P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack})}{P(\text{Alert})}$.
- ✅ **Base Rate Fallacy** explains why high-accuracy IDSs still produce mostly false alarms when attacks are rare — the mathematical foundation of *why Hybrid IDS exists*.
- ✅ **NIDS limitations**: encrypted traffic, no host context, packet drops at high speeds.
- ✅ **HIDS limitations**: host performance overhead, agent management complexity, disablement risk on host compromise.
- ✅ **Hybrid IDS limitations**: highest implementation cost, complexity in correlation rule design, single point of failure in the correlation engine.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** principle: deploy **all three** (NIDS + HIDS + Hybrid correlation) in any mature enterprise SOC.
- ✅ **Real-world examples** to remember: NIDS → **Snort/Suricata/Zeek**; HIDS → **OSSEC/Tripwire/Wazuh**; Hybrid → **Splunk + Snort, OSSIM, Microsoft Defender ATP**.
- ✅ **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Compare NIDS and HIDS" (3 marks) OR "Explain Hybrid IDS with correlation mathematics" (14 marks) — practice both.
- ✅ **Always draw a block diagram** in 7+ mark answers — it is worth 2–3 marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDS Components and Functionality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IDS Components and Functionality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security technology — either hardware or software-based — that monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts, and produces reports to a centralized management station.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology (aligned with NIST SP 800-94 and RFC 4949), an IDS is defined as:

> A software or hardware system that **automatically identifies and responds to** suspicious or malicious activity on a host or network by analyzing audit data, comparing it against known patterns (signatures), or detecting deviations from established behavioral baselines (anomalies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Definition Expected Answer):**
> An Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is a security mechanism that collects and analyzes information from various points within a computer system or network to identify potential security breaches, including intrusions (both external and internal), misuse, and policy violations. It does not block traffic by itself — it detects and alerts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an IDS as a **CCTV surveillance system with a smart guard** installed in a building:

- **CCTV Cameras** → IDS **Sensors/Agents** that capture raw events (network packets, log entries, system calls).
- **Recording Room (DVR)** → IDS **Detection Engine / Analyzer** where footage is scrutinized frame by frame.
- **Rule Book / Watchlist of known criminals** → **Signature Database / Knowledge Base** containing patterns of known attacks.
- **Guard watching screens in real time** → **Anomaly Detection Engine** identifying unusual behavior (a person walking at 3 AM when the office is closed).
- **Siren + Walkie-Talkie to Police** → **Alert/Response Module** that notifies the security operations center (SOC).
- **Security Control Room Display** → **Management Console** (web dashboard) where the chief security officer views all alerts.

The crucial difference from a burglar alarm: an IDS **watches and reports**, while an **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** not only watches but also **physically blocks** the intruder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **IDS = Detect + Alert** (passive, out-of-band)
> - **IPS = Detect + Alert + Block/Prevent** (active, inline)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in IDS

| Metric | Description | Typical Value |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **True Positive (TP)** | Actual attack correctly flagged | Goal: Maximize |
| **True Negative (TN)** | Legitimate traffic correctly passed | Goal: Maximize |
| **False Positive (FP)** | Legitimate activity wrongly flagged as attack | Goal: Minimize |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Real attack missed by IDS | Goal: Minimize |
| **Detection Rate (DR)** | TP / (TP + FN) | **> 95%** desired |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | FP / (FP + TN) | **< 1%** desired |
| **Base Rate Fallacy** | Imbalance in rare-event detection | Critical in IDS evaluation |

## 1.4 IDS Placement and Fundamental Goal

The fundamental goal of any IDS is to achieve **high detection accuracy with minimal false positives**, thereby preserving **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** of the protected assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Placement Modes (must know):**
> - **NIDS (Network-based IDS):** Placed at choke points (e.g., behind firewall, DMZ).
> - **HIDS (Host-based IDS):** Installed on individual endpoints/servers.
> - **Hybrid IDS:** Combines both NIDS and HIDS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed IDS Architecture and Functional Layers

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify and explain **each component** of an IDS. A modern IDS is a modular pipeline of six (6) functional components:

### Component 1: **Data Source / Sensor (Event Generator)**
- The "eyes and ears" of the IDS.
- Captures raw input data from one of three sources:
  - **Network-based sensor:** Libpcap, raw socket packet capture (Layer 2 to Layer 7).
  - **Host-based sensor:** OS audit logs, syslog, application logs, file integrity checkers.
  - **Application-based sensor:** Web server logs, database logs, API logs.
- **Output:** Normalized events passed to the preprocessor.

### Component 2: **Preprocessor / Normalizer**
- Decodes protocols (Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP).
- Performs packet defragmentation and TCP stream reassembly.
- Eliminates evasion techniques (e.g., IP fragmentation overlap attacks).
- Standardizes data format for the detection engine.

### Component 3: **Detection Engine (Analyzer) — The Brain**
The most critical component. It applies analytical methods:

**A. Signature-based Detection (Misuse Detection)**
- Pattern matching against a database of known attack signatures.
- Example rule (Snort syntax):
  `alert tcp any any -> 192.168.1.0/24 80 (content:"/etc/passwd"; msg:"Password file access attempt";)`
- Uses algorithms: Boyer-Moore, Aho-Corasick, Regular Expression engines.

**B. Anomaly-based Detection**
- Establishes a baseline of "normal" behavior using statistical profiling or machine learning.
- Flags deviations beyond a defined threshold.
- Techniques: Statistical methods, clustering (k-means), neural networks, Hidden Markov Models.

**C. Stateful Protocol Analysis**
- Understands protocol state machines (e.g., RFC-compliant TCP handshake).
- Detects deviations from expected protocol state transitions.

**D. Hybrid Detection**
- Modern systems combine A + B + C for higher accuracy.

### Component 4: **Knowledge Base / Signature Database**
- Stores attack signatures, behavioral profiles, rule sets.
- Updated regularly (daily/weekly) — e.g., **Snort** has a community rule feed (VRT — Vulnerability Research Team).
- Also contains whitelist/blacklist, vulnerability database (CVE mapping).

### Component 5: **Alert/Response Module**
- Generates alerts on detection of suspicious activity.
- Alert formats: **IDMEF (Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format)** — XML-based standard from IETF (RFC 4765).
- Response types:
  - **Passive:** Log, e-mail, SMS, SNMP trap, syslog.
  - **Active (in IPS):** Drop packet, reset TCP connection, block source IP via firewall rule push.

### Component 6: **Management Console / User Interface**
- Web-based or GUI dashboard for security analysts.
- Shows real-time alerts, dashboards, reports, correlation views.
- Tools: **Snorby, BASE (Basic Analysis and Security Engine), Kibana, Splunk, AlienVault OSSIM**.

## 2.2 Functional Workflow (Step-by-Step)

The operational sequence of an IDS is:

1. **Capture** — Sensors collect raw data from network/host sources.
2. **Normalize** — Preprocessor decodes and standardizes data.
3. **Analyze** — Detection engine applies rules, statistical models, or ML classifiers.
4. **Decide** — Compares results against threshold/knowledge base.
5. **Classify** — Labels event as *Benign*, *Suspicious*, or *Malicious*.
6. **Alert** — Triggers notification via chosen channels.
7. **Log** — Records event in database for forensic analysis and reporting.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Use in IDS Context |
|------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Detection Rate (DR) | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Measures sensitivity of IDS |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | Measures false alarm rate |
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of alerts |
| Recall (Sensitivity) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of true attacks |
| F1-Score (harmonic mean) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Balanced metric for IDS evaluation |
| Accuracy | $A = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| ROC AUC | Area under TP vs FP curve | Model comparison metric |
| Bayesian Detection | $P(I \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(E)}$ | Likelihood of intrusion given evidence |
| Base Rate Fallacy | $\text{High FP due to low } P(I)$ | Real-world IDS evaluation pitfall |
| Threshold $\theta$ | Alarm if $S(x) > \theta$ | Anomaly score cutoff |
| Mahalanobis Distance | $D = \sqrt{(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)}$ | Multivariate anomaly detection |
| Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Network traffic entropy for anomaly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bayesian Interpretation (favourite KTU 14-mark topic):**
> - $P(I)$ = Prior probability of intrusion
> - $P(E \mid I)$ = Likelihood of observing evidence E given an intrusion
> - $P(E)$ = Marginal probability of evidence
> - $P(I \mid E)$ = Posterior — what we want to compute.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC (Security Operations Center):** IDS is the cornerstone of 24/7 threat monitoring.
- **Compliance:** Required by **PCI-DSS 11.4, HIPAA, ISO 27001 A.12.4.1, NIST 800-53 SI-4**.
- **Forensic Investigation:** IDS logs are primary evidence source.
- **Threat Intelligence:** Feeds data into SIEM/SOAR platforms.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS):** Specialized IDS for SCADA networks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Detection Engine Decision Function

The detection engine of an IDS can be mathematically modeled as a **binary classifier** that maps an input feature vector to a decision.

### Step 1: Define Input Feature Vector

Let the input event be represented as a feature vector extracted by the preprocessor:

$$
\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n)
$$

where each $x_i$ is a feature such as packet length, flag bits, port number, protocol type, inter-arrival time, byte distribution, etc.

### Step 2: Define Anomaly Score Function

The detection engine computes a score $S(\mathbf{x})$ which measures the degree of deviation from "normal":

$$
S(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ represents the model parameters (mean, variance, weights, etc.).

### Step 3: Apply Decision Rule

The IDS triggers an alert if and only if:

$$
\text{Decision} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Intrusion (Alert)} & \text{if } S(\mathbf{x}) > \theta \\
\text{Normal} & \text{if } S(\mathbf{x}) \leq \theta
\end{cases}
$$

where $\theta$ is the operating threshold chosen to balance detection rate and false positive rate.

### Step 4: Statistical Anomaly Detection — Gaussian Model

Assuming normal behavior follows a multivariate Gaussian distribution:

$$
p(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{normal}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \right)
$$

Anomaly score is then defined as the **negative log-likelihood**:

$$
S(\mathbf{x}) = -\log p(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{normal}) = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) + \frac{1}{2}\log \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert + \frac{n}{2}\log 2\pi
$$

Since constant terms do not affect ranking, the effective score reduces to the **squared Mahalanobis distance**:

$$
S(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})
$$

### Step 5: Confusion Matrix Construction

After running the IDS over a labelled dataset, the confusion matrix is constructed:

|  | Predicted Attack | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Normal** | FP | TN |

### Step 6: Compute Performance Metrics

$$
\text{Detection Rate} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}
$$

$$
\text{False Positive Rate} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

### Step 7: Bayesian Likelihood Ratio Test (Advanced)

For two competing hypotheses — Normal ($H_0$) and Intrusion ($H_1$) — the optimal classifier uses the **likelihood ratio test**:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)}
$$

Decision rule:

$$
\text{Decide } H_1 \text{ if } \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > \eta
$$

where $\eta$ is the threshold chosen to meet a target false positive rate (Neyman-Pearson criterion).

### Step 3.2 Numerical Example (Worked Out)

**Problem:** An IDS evaluates 10,000 events. Of these, 100 are actual attacks. The IDS flags 120 as attacks, of which 90 are real attacks.

**Given:** Actual attacks = 100, Total flagged = 120, Correctly flagged attacks = 90.

**Step 1: Compute TP, FP, FN, TN**

$$
TP = 90, \quad FP = 120 - 90 = 30
$$

$$
FN = 100 - 90 = 10, \quad TN = 10000 - 100 - 30 = 9870
$$

**Step 2: Compute Detection Rate**

$$
DR = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute False Positive Rate**

$$
FPR = \frac{30}{30 + 9870} = \frac{30}{9900} \approx 0.00303 = 0.303\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute Precision**

$$
P = \frac{90}{90 + 30} = \frac{90}{120} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute F1-Score**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.90 \times 0.75}{0.90 + 0.75} = \frac{1.35}{1.65} \approx 0.818
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This IDS detects 90% of attacks but generates ~0.3% false positives — acceptable in most enterprise environments where $FPR < 1\%$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IDS Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NETWORK TRAFFIC] -->|raw packets| B[SENSOR]
    C[HOST LOGS] -->|syslog events| B
    D[APP LOGS] -->|application events| B
    B --> E[PREPROCESSOR]
    E --> F[DETECTION ENGINE]
    F --> G[KNOWLEDGE BASE]
    F --> H[ALERT RESPONSE MODULE]
    H --> I[MANAGEMENT CONSOLE]
    H --> J[SIEM INTEGRATION]
    I --> K[SECURITY ANALYST]
    K -->|feedback| F
```

## 4.2 Detailed IDS Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_Layer [INPUT LAYER]
        s1[Network Tap]
        s2[Host Agent]
        s3[Application Log]
    end
    
    subgraph Processing_Layer [PROCESSING LAYER]
        p1[Packet Decoder]
        p2[Protocol Normalizer]
        p3[Feature Extractor]
    end
    
    subgraph Analysis_Layer [ANALYSIS LAYER]
        d1[Signature Matcher]
        d2[Anomaly Detector]
        d3[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]
    end
    
    subgraph Decision_Layer [DECISION LAYER]
        dec1{Threat Score > Threshold}
        dec2[Classify Event]
    end
    
    subgraph Output_Layer [OUTPUT LAYER]
        o1[Generate Alert IDMEF]
        o2[Log to Database]
        o3[Send to SIEM]
        o4[Display on Dashboard]
    end
    
    s1 --> p1
    s2 --> p1
    s3 --> p1
    p1 --> p2 --> p3
    p3 --> d1
    p3 --> d2
    p3 --> d3
    d1 --> dec1
    d2 --> dec1
    d3 --> dec1
    dec1 -->|YES| dec2
    dec1 -->|NO| o2
    dec2 --> o1
    o1 --> o3
    o1 --> o4
```

## 4.3 IDS Placement in Enterprise Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INET[INTERNET] --> FW[PERIMETER FIREWALL]
    FW --> DMZ[DMZ ZONE]
    DMZ -->|mirror port| NIDS1[NIDS OUTSIDE]
    FW --> LAN[INTERNAL LAN]
    LAN -->|tap| NIDS2[NIDS INSIDE]
    LAN --> SRV[SERVERS]
    SRV -->|host agent| HIDS[HIDS]
    LAN -->|tap| NIDS3[NIDS CORE]
    NIDS1 --> SOC[SOC CONSOLE]
    NIDS2 --> SOC
    NIDS3 --> SOC
    HIDS --> SOC
    SOC --> ANALYST[SECURITY ANALYST]
```

## 4.4 Signature-Based vs Anomaly-Based Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IDS[TYPES OF IDS DETECTION]
    IDS --> S[SIGNATURE BASED]
    IDS --> A[ANOMALY BASED]
    IDS --> ST[STATEFUL PROTOCOL]
    IDS --> H[HYBRID]
    
    S --> S1[Known pattern matching]
    S --> S2[Low FP, Zero-day blind]
    
    A --> A1[Behavioral baseline]
    A --> A2[Detects zero-day, High FP]
    
    ST --> ST1[Protocol state validation]
    ST --> ST2[Detects protocol misuse]
    
    H --> H1[Combines all methods]
    H --> H2[Modern enterprise standard]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Intrusion Detection System. Differentiate between IDS and IPS.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is a security tool that monitors network or host activities for malicious behaviour, policy violations, or unauthorized access, and generates alerts without actively blocking the traffic.

**Difference (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | IDS | IPS |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Operation Mode | Passive (monitors copy of traffic) | Active (inline) |
| Response | Detects and alerts | Detects, alerts, and blocks |
| Placement | Out-of-band (via port mirror) | Inline in traffic path |
| Latency impact | None | Possible (inline processing) |
| Example Tools | Snort (IDS mode), Suricata | Suricata (IPS mode), Cisco Firepower |

---

### Question 2: List the main components of an IDS with a brief note on the Detection Engine.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The main components of an IDS are:
1. **Sensor / Data Source** — captures raw events.
2. **Preprocessor** — decodes and normalizes data.
3. **Detection Engine** — applies rules or models to identify attacks.
4. **Knowledge Base** — stores signatures and behavioural profiles.
5. **Alert/Response Module** — generates notifications.
6. **Management Console** — provides analyst interface.

**Detection Engine (1.5 marks):** It is the analytical brain of the IDS that uses techniques such as signature matching, statistical anomaly detection, and stateful protocol analysis to classify events as normal or malicious.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the various components of an Intrusion Detection System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Detection Engine in modern IDS. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

An IDS is a modular system consisting of the following components:

**(1) Sensors / Data Collectors (1 mark):** These are software agents or hardware probes that monitor network traffic (Network IDS) or system events (Host IDS). Network sensors use promiscuous mode NICs and tools like libpcap to capture packets, while host sensors read OS audit trails, system logs, and file integrity hashes.

**(2) Preprocessor (1 mark):** The captured raw data is decoded through protocol layers, defragmented, and reassembled. The preprocessor also normalizes data formats and strips protocol-level evasion tricks such as overlapping fragments or unicode encoding.

**(3) Detection Engine (2 marks):** The core analytical module. It applies:
- **Signature-based detection:** Pattern matching using algorithms like Aho-Corasick against known attack signatures stored as rules.
- **Anomaly-based detection:** Builds a profile of normal behaviour and flags statistical deviations using Mahalanobis distance, entropy, or ML classifiers.
- **Stateful protocol analysis:** Validates that protocol exchanges follow their defined state machines (e.g., RFC 793 for TCP).

**(4) Knowledge Base (1 mark):** A continuously updated database of attack signatures (e.g., Snort VRT rules), vulnerability mappings (CVE), and behavioural profiles.

**(5) Alert/Response Module (1 mark):** Generates structured alerts in IDMEF (RFC 4765) format, sends notifications via syslog, SNMP, e-mail, and in IPS mode performs active blocking.

**(6) Management Console (1 mark):** A web-based or GUI dashboard that allows security analysts to monitor alerts in real time, generate reports, and tune detection rules.

**Role of Detection Engine in Modern IDS (Bonus content for full marks):** Modern detection engines integrate **machine learning** (Random Forests, SVMs, Deep Learning) and **threat intelligence feeds** to detect advanced persistent threats (APTs) and zero-day attacks, going beyond traditional static signatures.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 diagram of this note — students may draw simplified version)*

---

**(b)** Compare signature-based and anomaly-based intrusion detection. Discuss the Base Rate Fallacy and its impact on IDS performance. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Signature-based | Anomaly-based |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Principle | Pattern matching | Behavioural deviation |
| Detection of known attacks | Excellent | Good |
| Detection of zero-day | Poor | Excellent |
| False Positive Rate | Low | High |
| False Negative Rate | Higher (for novel attacks) | Lower |
| Resource consumption | Low | High (training required) |
| Update frequency | Frequent (signature DB) | Periodic (model retraining) |
| Examples | Snort signatures, YARA rules | Statistical profiling, ML models |

**Base Rate Fallacy (3 marks):**

The **Base Rate Fallacy** is a critical statistical issue in IDS evaluation. Since the prior probability of an attack in typical network traffic is extremely low (often $< 0.01\%$), even an IDS with seemingly good accuracy (e.g., 99%) can produce an unacceptably high number of false positives.

**Mathematical Demonstration (3 marks):**
Let $P(I) = 0.0001$ (1 attack in 10,000 events) and assume IDS sensitivity and specificity are both 99%.

$$
P(I \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(\text{Alert})}
$$

$$
P(\text{Alert}) = (0.99)(0.0001) + (0.01)(0.9999) = 0.000099 + 0.009999 = 0.010098
$$

$$
P(I \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{0.99 \times 0.0001}{0.010098} \approx 0.0098 \approx 1\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with 99% accuracy, when the IDS raises an alarm, there is only a 1% chance that the alert represents a real attack. This makes analyst alert fatigue severe and underscores why low FPR is more critical than high raw accuracy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Describe the working of an IDS with the help of a functional flow diagram. Explain each stage of the detection pipeline. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The working of an IDS is best described as a sequential pipeline of six stages:

**Stage 1 — Data Collection (1 mark):** Sensors placed on the network or hosts capture raw events. Network sensors (e.g., NetFlow collectors, packet sniffers) collect Layer 2–7 data. Host sensors (e.g., OSSEC, Tripwire) read system logs, registry changes, and file integrity hashes.

**Stage 2 — Preprocessing (1 mark):** Raw packets are decoded, defragmented, reassembled, and normalized. TCP streams are reconstructed. Protocol-specific anomalies (e.g., malformed HTTP headers) are flagged early.

**Stage 3 — Feature Extraction (1 mark):** Relevant features are extracted for the detection engine — packet size, inter-arrival time, payload bytes, protocol flags, source/destination ports.

**Stage 4 — Detection / Analysis (2 marks):** The detection engine compares features against the knowledge base:
- Signature matching for known patterns.
- Statistical/ML models for anomaly detection.
- Protocol state machines for stateful analysis.

**Stage 5 — Decision (1 mark):** A confidence score or alarm flag is generated. Threshold $\theta$ determines whether an alert is raised.

**Stage 6 — Response & Reporting (1 mark):** Alerts are logged, displayed on console, sent to SIEM, or (in IPS mode) trigger active blocking actions like TCP RST or ACL updates.

**Functional Flow Diagram (bonus):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 in these notes.)*

---

**(b)** What are the different types of IDS based on data source? Explain the strengths and limitations of Network-based IDS (NIDS) and Host-based IDS (HIDS). **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of IDS by Data Source (2 marks):**
1. **NIDS (Network-based IDS):** Monitors network traffic.
2. **HIDS (Host-based IDS):** Monitors single host activities.
3. **Hybrid IDS:** Combines NIDS and HIDS.
4. **Application-based IDS:** Monitors specific applications (e.g., web app firewall).

**Strengths and Limitations of NIDS (2.5 marks):**

| Strengths | Limitations |
|-----------|-------------|
| Covers entire subnet with one sensor | Cannot analyse encrypted payload |
| Non-intrusive (passive monitoring) | Struggles under high-speed networks (10 Gbps+) |
| Detects scans, DoS, worms effectively | Limited host context |
| Cost-effective for large networks | Vulnerable to evasion (fragmentation, encoding) |
| Real-time detection | Cannot stop attacks (passive) |

**Strengths and Limitations of HIDS (2.5 marks):**

| Strengths | Limitations |
|-----------|-------------|
| Verifies success/failure of attack | Host-specific deployment is costly |
| Detects insider threats and host-level changes | Consumes host resources (CPU, disk) |
| Can analyse encrypted traffic (after decryption) | Limited network-wide visibility |
| Provides detailed forensic context | Disabled if host is compromised |
| Operates even on switched networks | Difficult to manage at scale |

**Conclusion:** Modern enterprises deploy a **defence-in-depth** strategy combining NIDS at perimeter and HIDS on critical servers, integrated through a SIEM platform for correlation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse IDS with IPS** — Many students incorrectly state that IDS blocks traffic. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **In Base Rate Fallacy questions** — Always show the numerical calculation with $P(I)$ value; do not just state the concept.
> 3. **Confusion matrix labels** — Students often swap FP and FN. Remember: **False Positive = Type I error (false alarm)** and **False Negative = Type II error (missed attack)**.
> 4. **Block diagram is mandatory** in 7-mark questions on IDS components — without it, expect 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Use correct terminology** — say "Detection Engine" not "Detection System" inside IDS.
> 6. **Mention placement strategies** when asked about NIDS/HIDS — examiners value network topology awareness.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick Revision Checklist

- **IDS = Detect + Alert (Passive)**, **IPS = Detect + Alert + Block (Active/In-line)**.
- **6 Core Components of IDS:** Sensor, Preprocessor, Detection Engine, Knowledge Base, Alert/Response Module, Management Console.
- **3 Main Detection Methods:** Signature-based (low FP, no zero-day), Anomaly-based (zero-day detection, high FP), Stateful Protocol Analysis.
- **Types by Source:** NIDS, HIDS, Hybrid IDS, Application-based IDS.
- **Key Metrics:** DR, FPR, Precision, Recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC.
- **Base Rate Fallacy:** When $P(I)$ is very small, even high-accuracy IDS produce overwhelming false positives — must be addressed via threshold tuning.
- **Bayesian Approach:** $P(I \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid I) P(I)}{P(E)}$ — fundamental probabilistic foundation of IDS.
- **IDMEF (RFC 4765):** Standard XML format for IDS alert exchange.
- **Placement:** NIDS at choke points (perimeter, DMZ, core); HIDS on critical servers/hosts.
- **Compliance Relevance:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, NIST 800-53 SI-4 mandate IDS deployment.
- **Threshold Rule:** $\text{Alert if } S(\mathbf{x}) > \theta$ — controls the DR vs FPR trade-off.
- **Open Source Tools:** Snort (signature), Suricata (multi-threaded), OSSEC (HIDS), Zeek (formerly Bro).
- **Signature Update:** Critical operational task; without updates, IDS becomes blind to new threats.
- **Defense-in-Depth:** IDS is one layer; combine with firewall, antivirus, SIEM, and IPS for holistic security.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Definition (for 2-mark questions):**
> "An IDS is a security mechanism that monitors and analyses system or network events to detect unauthorized activity and policy violations, producing alerts for further investigation."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signature-based vs. Anomaly-based Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Signature-based vs. Anomaly-based Detection # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** fundamentally rely on two primary detection paradigms mandated by the KTU 2024 PECPT753 syllabus: **Signature-based Detection** (also termed *Misuse-based* or *Knowledge-based Detection*) and **Anomaly-based Detection** (also termed *Behavior-based Detection*). **Signature-based Detection** is a detection methodology that identifies malicious activity by comparing observed network traffic, system calls, or log entries against a predefined database of known attack patterns, called *signatures* or *rules*. Each signature is a unique fingerprint (byte sequence, hash, regex pattern, or protocol field value) that characterises a specific, previously catalogued threat. **Anomaly-based Detection** is a detection methodology that constructs a statistical or machine-learning model representing *normal* system/network behaviour, and subsequently flags any significant statistical deviation from this baseline profile as a potential intrusion. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Security Guard at a Building > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy** Imagine a security guard posted at the entrance of a high-security building. There are two ways the guard can identify intruders: - **Signature-based guard**: Carries a **mugshot book** containing photos (signatures) of all known criminals. He scrutinises every entrant and arrests anyone whose face matches a photo. He is excellent at catching **known criminals** but completely blind to a new criminal whose photo is not in the book. - **Anomaly-based guard**: Has no mugshot book. Instead, he has carefully studied the **routine** of the building for 6 months — he knows that employees enter between 8 AM and 6 PM, walk calmly, don't loiter near the server room, and don't carry large packages at midnight. The moment someone behaves *abnormally* (e.g., enters at 2 AM with a toolbox), he raises an alarm — even if the person is a brand-new, never-before-seen intruder. | Guard Type | Detects Known Threats | Detects Zero-Day Threats | False Alarm Risk | |------------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------| | Signature-based (Mugshot) | ✅ Excellent | ❌ None | **Low** | | Anomaly-based (Routine) | ⚠️ Only if deviates from normal | ✅ Excellent | **High** | ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Both Paradigms (Highlighted) The following **standardised performance metrics** are universally used to evaluate any IDS, regardless of detection paradigm: - **True Positive (TP)**: Malicious activity correctly identified as malicious. - **False Positive (FP)**: Legitimate activity incorrectly flagged as malicious (False Alarm). - **True Negative (TN)**: Legitimate activity correctly allowed. - **False Negative (FN)**: Malicious activity incorrectly allowed (Missed Detection). - **Detection Rate (DR) = TP / (TP + FN)** - **False Alarm Rate (FAR) = FP / (FP + TN)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Signature-based Detection — Operational Logic Steps 1. **Signature Authoring**: Security analysts (or automated vulnerability feeds such as **CVE/NIST NVD**, **Snort VRT rules**, or **Emerging Threats**) author signatures that describe known attacks. A signature could be a string match (`"GET /cgi-bin/phf"`), a hash (`MD5: a1b2c3d4...`), or a complex regular expression across packet headers and payload. 2. **Pattern Compilation**: Signatures are compiled into efficient data structures — typically **Aho-Corasick automata** for multi-pattern string matching, or **Decision Trees / Bloom Filters** for fast pre-screening. 3. **Traffic Capture**: The IDS sensor taps network traffic (NIDS) or hooks system calls (HIDS) and feeds packets/logs into the matching engine. 4. **Pattern Matching**: The matching engine compares each observed event against the signature database in **O(n)** or **O(n + m)** time, where *n* is the event length and *m* is the signature length. 5. **Alert Generation**: Upon a successful match, an alert is raised with the signature ID, severity, and contextual metadata. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Limitation**: Signature-based systems are *reactive* — they can only detect attacks that have already been discovered, catalogued, and for which a signature has been distributed. They are completely ineffective against **Zero-Day exploits** and **polymorphic malware** that mutates its byte pattern. ## 2.2 Anomaly-based Detection — Operational Logic Steps 1. **Training Phase (Baseline Construction)**: During a *training window* (typically 2–8 weeks of clean traffic), the system constructs a profile of *normal* behaviour. The profile can be statistical (mean, variance, entropy), rule-based, or learned via **Machine Learning (ML)** algorithms (e.g., **One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, LSTM networks**). 2. **Feature Extraction**: For each event, quantitative features are extracted: packet rate, connection duration, bytes transferred, CPU utilisation, system call frequency, entropy of payload, etc. 3. **Deviation Scoring**: During the *detection phase*, the same features are extracted from live traffic and compared against the baseline. A **deviation score** (or *anomaly score*) is computed. 4. **Thresholding**: If the score exceeds a configurable threshold (often governed by the **Mahalanobis distance**, **cosine similarity**, or a learned ML decision boundary), an alert is raised. 5. **Feedback / Model Update**: Optionally, the model is retrained periodically to adapt to evolving legitimate behaviour (concept drift). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Application Context | |---|-------------------|--------|---------------------| | 1 | Detection Rate | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Effectiveness of detection | | 2 | False Alarm Rate | $FAR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Operational cost of false alerts | | 3 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Trustworthiness of an alert | | 4 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced metric under class imbalance | | 5 | Z-score (Statistical Anomaly) | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Deviation of feature *x* from baseline mean $\mu$ | | 6 | Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^T \, \Sigma^{-1} \, (x - \mu)}$ | Multivariate anomaly detection using covariance $\Sigma$ | | 7 | Shannon Entropy (Payload) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Detects encrypted / obfuscated payloads in anomaly engines | | 8 | Aho-Corasick Complexity | $O(n + m + z)$ | Multi-pattern matching in signature engines (n=text, m=pattern, z=matches) | | 9 | Bayesian Posterior | $P(I \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(E)}$ | Likelihood of Intrusion *I* given Evidence *E* | | 10 | ROC Curve | Plot of $TPR$ vs $FPR$ | Threshold-tuning visual for anomaly detectors | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility**: Signature-based engines dominate in **enterprise perimeter defence** (e.g., Snort, Suricata, Zeek with signatures) where known threats constitute >95% of malicious traffic. Anomaly-based engines are indispensable in **Insider Threat Detection, SCADA/ICS networks, and Zero-Day Hunting platforms** (e.g., Darktrace, Vectra AI). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — Computing a Z-Score Anomaly Score The simplest form of anomaly detection is univariate statistical detection. We derive the score formally. > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context**: A baseline profile for HTTP requests/minute on a web server has been built over 30 days. The mean request rate is $\mu = 120$ req/min and the standard deviation is $\sigma = 15$ req/min. Determine whether an observed request rate of $x = 210$ req/min constitutes an anomaly. **Step 1 — State the definition of the Z-score.** $$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the observed value $x = 210$.** $$Z = \frac{210 - 120}{15}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the numerator.** $$210 - 120 = 90$$ **Step 4 — Perform the division.** $$Z = \frac{90}{15} = 6.0$$ **Step 5 — Interpret using the empirical 68-95-99.7 rule.** For a Gaussian baseline, $Z = 3$ corresponds to the 99.7th percentile (≈0.3% probability of natural occurrence). Our computed $Z = 6.0$ lies far beyond this — it is a **6-σ event**, with a natural probability of occurrence of approximately: $$P(Z \geq 6) \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-10}$$ **Step 6 — Conclusion.** Since the observed rate lies **6 standard deviations** from the baseline mean, the IDS raises a **HIGH-SEVERITY ANOMALY ALERT**, likely indicative of a **Denial-of-Service (DoS) flood** or **HTTP scraping attack**. ## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough — Multivariate Anomaly via Mahalanobis Distance When multiple correlated features must be analysed jointly (e.g., packet size, inter-arrival time, and port number), a single Z-score is insufficient. The **Mahalanobis distance** accounts for feature covariance. > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context**: A Network Behaviour Anomaly Detection (NBAD) system tracks two features: $x_1$ (packet size in bytes) and $x_2$ (inter-arrival time in ms). The training baseline yields: $$\mu = \begin{bmatrix} 500 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 4000 & 50 \\ 50 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$ **An observed event has the feature vector:** $$x = \begin{bmatrix} 800 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Compute the Mahalanobis distance and determine anomaly status using threshold $D_M > 3$.** **Step 1 — Write the Mahalanobis formula.** $$D_M = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^T \, \Sigma^{-1} \, (x - \mu)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the deviation vector $d = x - \mu$.** $$d = \begin{bmatrix} 800 - 500 \\ 5 - 20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 300 \\ -15 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Invert the covariance matrix $\Sigma$.** The determinant of $\Sigma$ is: $$\det(\Sigma) = (4000)(25) - (50)(50) = 100000 - 2500 = 97500$$ The inverse is: $$\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{97500} \begin{bmatrix} 25 & -50 \\ -50 & 4000 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.000256 & -0.000513 \\ -0.000513 & 0.041026 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $\Sigma^{-1} \cdot d$.** $$\Sigma^{-1} \cdot d = \begin{bmatrix} (0.000256)(300) + (-0.000513)(-15) \\ (-0.000513)(300) + (0.041026)(-15) \end{bmatrix}$$ $$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.0768 + 0.0077 \\ -0.1539 - 0.6154 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0845 \\ -0.7693 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $d^T \cdot (\Sigma^{-1} \cdot d)$.** $$d^T (\Sigma^{-1} d) = (300)(0.0845) + (-15)(-0.7693)$$ $$= 25.36 + 11.54 = 36.90$$ **Step 6 — Take the square root to obtain the Mahalanobis distance.** $$D_M = \sqrt{36.90} \approx 6.07$$ **Step 7 — Threshold comparison.** Since $D_M = 6.07 > 3$, the event is flagged as a **statistical anomaly**. The combination of an unusually large packet size and a very short inter-arrival time suggests potential **port-scanning** or **data-exfiltration** activity. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Signature vs Anomaly | Dimension | Signature-based Detection | Anomaly-based Detection | |-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------| | **Knowledge Required** | Database of known attack signatures | Profile of normal behaviour | | **Zero-Day Detection** | ❌ Ineffective | ✅ Highly effective | | **False Positive Rate** | **Low** (0.1% – 1%) | **High** (5% – 30%) | | **False Negative Rate** | High for novel attacks | Low for novel attacks | | **Computational Cost** | Low (string matching) | High (ML inference) | | **Setup Complexity** | Easy (drop in rules) | Difficult (training, tuning) | | **Adaptability to New Threats** | Requires manual signature update | Automatic via retraining | | **Polymorphic Malware** | Bypassed easily | Detected via behavioural drift | | **Examples** | Snort, Suricata, ClamAV | Darktrace, Vectra AI, OSSEC anomaly mode | | **KTU-Mandated Synonyms** | Misuse-based / Knowledge-based | Behaviour-based / Statistical-based | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Signature-based Detection Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Network Traffic Capture] --> B[Packet Decoder] B --> C[Pre-processor: Defragment, Reassemble, Decode HTTP] C --> D[Pattern Matching Engine] D --> E{Signature Database
Snort Rules, CVE Patterns} E -- Match Found --> F[Generate Alert with
Signature ID and Severity] E -- No Match --> G[Forward as Benign] F --> H[Log and Forward to SIEM] G --> H ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Anomaly-based Detection Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Network or Host Event Stream] --> B[Feature Extractor
Packet Rate, Entropy, CPU] B --> C[Trained ML or Statistical Model
One-Class SVM, Autoencoder, Z-score] C --> D{Anomaly Score} D -- Score less than Threshold --> E[Classify as Normal] D -- Score greater than Threshold --> F[Raise Anomaly Alert] F --> G[Alert Verification by Analyst] E --> H[Update Rolling Statistics] G --> I[Feedback to Model Retraining] H --> C I --> C ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hybrid Detection Architecture (Best Practice) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Events] --> B[Anomaly Engine] A --> C[Signature Engine] B --> D{Suspicious?} C --> E{Known Match?} D -- Yes --> F[High-Severity Alert] D -- No --> G[Benign] E -- Yes --> F E -- No --> G F --> H[Correlation Engine] H --> I[Unified Alert to SOC] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Choosing a Paradigm ```mermaid flowchart TD A[New IDS Deployment] --> B{Primary Threat Type?} B -- Known Malware and Web Attacks --> C[Use Signature-based
e.g., Snort] B -- Insider Threats and Zero-Days --> D[Use Anomaly-based
e.g., ML Engine] B -- Mixed Enterprise Environment --> E[Deploy Hybrid Architecture] C --> F[Update signature feeds weekly] D --> G[Retrain model monthly] E --> H[Tune both engines and correlate alerts] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *Define signature-based intrusion detection. List two of its primary advantages and two inherent limitations.* **Model Answer:** Signature-based detection identifies intrusions by matching observed events against a database of pre-defined attack patterns (signatures), where each signature uniquely characterises a known threat. **Advantages (any two):** 1. Very **low false-positive rate** because signatures are precise fingerprints of known attacks. 2. **Fast and computationally cheap** — relies on efficient string-matching algorithms (Aho-Corasick). 3. Easy to deploy and update; the methodology is well understood by analysts. **Limitations (any two):** 1. **Cannot detect zero-day attacks** for which no signature yet exists. 2. **Easily evaded by polymorphism**, encryption, or simple obfuscation that alters the byte pattern. 3. Requires constant manual signature updates to remain effective. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *What is anomaly-based detection? Why does it typically produce a higher false-positive rate than signature-based detection?* **Model Answer:** Anomaly-based detection constructs a statistical or machine-learning model of *normal* system or network behaviour during a training phase, and subsequently flags any significant deviation from this baseline as a potential intrusion. **Reason for Higher False-Positive Rate:** Legitimate user behaviour is inherently **non-stationary and diverse** — new applications, seasonal traffic patterns, software updates, or novel user workflows can all deviate from the trained baseline. The detector, lacking semantic context, raises an alert for *any* deviation, even when the cause is benign. Signature-based systems, by contrast, only alert on a precise known pattern, eliminating most legitimate variability. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *(a)* Explain in detail the architecture and operational workflow of a **Signature-based Intrusion Detection System**. Discuss the role of the pattern-matching engine and the typical data structures used. *(7 marks)* *(b)* With a suitable diagram, describe the **Anomaly-based detection methodology**. Compare its strengths and weaknesses against signature-based detection in a tabular form. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Architecture of a Signature-based IDS:** 1. **Packet Capture Subsystem**: Uses *libpcap* (Linux) or *WinPcap/Npcap* (Windows) to place the NIC in promiscuous mode and capture raw packets off the wire. 2. **Decoder / Pre-processor**: Reassembles fragmented IP packets, re-orders TCP segments, decodes application-layer protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMTP) into normalised representations. 3. **Detection Engine**: The core of the IDS. It applies the signature rules to every decoded event. Rules typically have two parts: - **Rule Header**: Defines the *action* (alert, log, drop), *protocol* (TCP/UDP/ICMP), *source/destination IP* and *ports*. - **Rule Options**: Defines the *payload match* (content, pcre, byte_test). 4. **Signature Database**: A large, regularly updated collection of rules. Example: Snort VRT releases rules daily. Each rule carries a unique **Signature ID (SID)**. 5. **Alert/Logging Subsystem**: Alerts are written to a unified log file, forwarded to a SIEM (Splunk, ELK), or sent via SYSLOG/SNMP. **Pattern-Matching Data Structures Used:** | Data Structure | Used For | Complexity | |----------------|----------|------------| | **Aho-Corasick Automaton** | Multi-pattern string matching in linear time | $O(n + m + z)$ | | **Bloom Filter** | Fast probabilistic pre-screening of packet hashes | $O(1)$ lookup, $FPR = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ | | **Tries / Suffix Trees** | Hierarchical pattern matching | $O(L)$ per character | | **Decision Trees** | Conditional rule evaluation | $O(\log N)$ | The Aho-Corasick automaton is the de-facto industry standard because it can match thousands of signatures against a payload in a *single* linear pass, making it ideal for high-speed networks (10 Gbps+). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Anomaly-based Detection Methodology — Diagram:** *(Refer to Mermaid diagram in SECTION_4.2.)* **Process Steps:** 1. **Training Phase**: Collect clean baseline data (typically 2–8 weeks). Extract features such as packet rate, byte distribution, protocol mix, entropy, connection duration. 2. **Model Construction**: Train a model on the baseline. Common algorithms include: - Statistical: Gaussian Mixture Models, Z-score, Mahalanobis distance. - ML: One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, LSTMs. 3. **Detection Phase**: Compute the same features for live events. Pass them through the model to obtain an **anomaly score**. 4. **Thresholding & Alerting**: Compare the score to a threshold (tuned via ROC analysis). Exceed the threshold → raise an alert. 5. **Periodic Retraining**: Update the model to handle concept drift (e.g., new legitimate applications). **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Signature-based | Anomaly-based | |--------|-----------------|---------------| | Zero-day detection | Poor | Excellent | | False positives | Low | High | | Computational cost | Low | High | | Setup effort | Easy | Complex | | Adaptability | Manual update | Automatic retraining | | Best for | Known threats | Unknown threats | | Example tools | Snort, Suricata | Darktrace, OSSEC | --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the **Hybrid Intrusion Detection System** that combines both signature and anomaly engines. Justify why modern enterprises prefer this architecture. *(7 marks)* *(b)* A baseline traffic profile on a server shows that the average number of failed SSH login attempts per hour follows a Gaussian distribution with $\mu = 4$ attempts/hour and $\sigma = 1.5$ attempts/hour. Using the Z-score method, determine whether an observed count of $x = 11$ failed attempts in a single hour should be classified as an anomaly at a threshold of $Z > 3$. Justify your answer with calculations. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Hybrid IDS Architecture — Diagram:** *(Refer to Mermaid diagram in SECTION_4.3.)* **Explanation:** In a Hybrid IDS, the same event stream is processed in **parallel** by two engines: 1. **Signature Engine** scans for known attack patterns. A match is high-confidence because the pattern is known. 2. **Anomaly Engine** computes a deviation score from the learned normal baseline. A high score is suspicious but requires analyst verification. The **Correlation Engine** then merges alerts from both engines using rules such as: - An alert raised by *both* engines → **Critical Severity** (highly likely attack). - An alert raised *only* by the anomaly engine → **Medium Severity** (requires investigation). - An alert raised *only* by the signature engine → **High Severity** (known attack in progress). **Why Modern Enterprises Prefer Hybrid IDS:** - It mitigates the **high false-positive rate** of pure anomaly systems (analyst verifies the alert using the signature engine). - It mitigates the **zero-day blindness** of pure signature systems (anomaly engine catches what signatures miss). - It provides **defence in depth**, satisfying regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001) which demands layered security. - Examples: **Cisco Firepower**, **Palo Alto Networks Cortex XDR**, **Suricata with ML plugins**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given Information:** - Baseline mean of failed SSH login attempts: $\mu = 4$ attempts/hour. - Baseline standard deviation: $\sigma = 1.5$ attempts/hour. - Observed count in one hour: $x = 11$. - Anomaly threshold: $Z > 3$. **Step 1 — State the Z-score formula.** $$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$Z = \frac{11 - 4}{1.5}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the numerator.** $$11 - 4 = 7$$ **Step 4 — Perform the division.** $$Z = \frac{7}{1.5} = 4.667$$ **Step 5 — Compare with the threshold.** $$Z = 4.667 > 3 \implies \text{Anomaly Threshold is Exceeded}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** The observed count is **4.67 standard deviations** above the mean. Under the Gaussian assumption, such an event occurs with a natural probability of approximately: $$P(Z \geq 4.67) \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$$ This is an extraordinarily rare event under normal conditions. The anomaly engine will **raise a HIGH-SEVERITY ALERT**, which is most likely indicative of a **brute-force SSH password-guessing attack** (a textbook signature for tools like *Hydra* or *Medusa*). The signature engine, working in parallel, will also match the attacker's tool-specific payload pattern (e.g., rapid `SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_xxx` connection strings from a single IP), escalating the alert to **CRITICAL** in the correlation engine. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning** > - In Part A, students often forget to mention the **operational tradeoff** between false positives and zero-day coverage. Examiners specifically award a mark for this insight — do not omit it. > - In Part B, the most common error is **unit confusion** between *attempts/minute* and *attempts/hour*. Always re-state units explicitly in the final answer. > - When drawing the Hybrid IDS diagram, students frequently **forget the Correlation Engine**. Without it, the two engines produce duplicate alerts, and the examiner will deduct **2 marks**. > - For the Mahalanobis question, marks are lost when students **fail to compute the matrix inverse** correctly. Re-verify the determinant calculation; a single sign error invalidates the entire distance. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Signature-based detection** = *mugshot book* approach. It is **fast**, has **low false positives**, but **cannot detect zero-days**. - **Anomaly-based detection** = *routine-watching* approach. It **detects zero-days** but suffers from **high false positives** due to legitimate behavioural drift. - The **Aho-Corasick automaton** is the workhorse of signature engines — multi-pattern matching in $O(n + m + z)$ time. - The **Z-score** is the simplest anomaly score: $Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$. Threshold of $Z > 3$ is the standard. - The **Mahalanobis distance** is used for **multivariate** anomaly detection and accounts for feature covariance. - The **Shannon Entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is a powerful anomaly feature for detecting **encrypted** or **packed** payloads. - The **Hybrid IDS** combines both engines under a **Correlation Engine** and is the de-facto industry standard (Cisco, Palo Alto, Suricata). - **ROC curves** (TPR vs FPR) are the standard tool for threshold tuning in anomaly engines. - **Concept drift** requires periodic model retraining; this is a major engineering challenge in production anomaly systems. - For KTU 2024 examinations: focus your answer on the **operational tradeoff** — examiners reward conceptual clarity over rote definitions.

topics.title: Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS): Definition and Purpose of IPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS): Definition and Purpose of IPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS):** A *network security appliance* or *software-based control system* that monitors network and/or system activities for malicious traffic, policy violations, and anomalous behaviour, and **proactively blocks, drops, resets, or quarantines** detected threats in **real-time** at line speed, without requiring human intervention.

An IPS is essentially an *in-line* security control — meaning all traffic **must** traverse it for it to inspect and act upon packets. It is the evolution of the Intrusion Detection System (IDS), which was purely *passive* (only alerted). The IPS transforms detection into **automated prevention**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, an IPS is formally classified under the broader category of **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** ecosystem controls, sitting between the firewall (Layer 3/4 filtering) and the endpoint (host-based controls).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction (Mandatory for KTU 2024):**
> - **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)** → *Passive* → Detects & *alerts* only.
> - **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** → *Active* → Detects & *blocks/prevents* in real time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bouncer at a Club Door"**
>
> Imagine a high-end nightclub. A **CCTV camera** in the lobby is like an **IDS** — it *watches* suspicious people, records them, and sends an alert to the security manager. But it does **not** stop the troublemaker.
>
> An **IPS** is the **bouncer standing at the door**. Every guest must pass through him. The moment he identifies a known troublemaker (signature match) or observes weird behaviour (anomaly), he **immediately denies entry, removes them, and locks the door**. The bouncer does not call the manager first — he acts *in-line* and *immediately*.
>
> - **Signature** → Troublemaker on the blacklist.
> - **Anomaly** → Someone acting drunk/odd (deviation from normal).
> - **Inline placement** → Standing at the door (every packet must pass through).

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics of an IPS

The following are the **operational performance benchmarks** evaluated by KTU examiners and industry:

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)** for modern IPS.
- **Mean Time to Prevent (MTTP):** Action latency between detection and block — must be **< 1 ms** for inline deployment.
- **Throughput:** Measured in **Gigabits per second (Gbps)** — modern enterprise IPS handles **10 Gbps to 100 Gbps**.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Should be **< 0.1%** in production.
- **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Detection Rate:** Should be **> 99%** for known signatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The defining metric of an IPS vs IDS is **in-line latency**. An IPS that cannot block at line speed is a *failed design* — it becomes a network bottleneck.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of an IPS — Step-by-Step Breakdown

An IPS does not just "block things." It executes a deterministic pipeline. Every KTU 2024 examiner expects you to know these 6 stages:

1. **Packet Capture & Normalization**
   * *Why:* Raw packets must be decoded, defragmented, and reassembled before deep inspection. The IPS rebuilds the protocol stack (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application).
   * *How:* Uses a packet capture engine (often `libpcap`-based or ASIC-based in hardware appliances).

2. **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**
   * *Why:* Layer 3/4 headers are insufficient. Modern attacks (SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflows) live in **Layer 7 payload**.
   * *How:* The IPS reassembles the full TCP stream and inspects the application payload against **signatures**, **protocol decoders**, and **heuristics**.

3. **Detection Engine Evaluation**
   * *Why:* The IPS must classify the packet as *malicious* or *benign* with high confidence.
   * *How:* Three primary techniques are applied:
     - **Signature-based (Misuse Detection):** Pattern matching against a known-attack database (e.g., Snort rules).
     - **Anomaly-based:** Statistical deviation from a learned baseline model.
     - **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Validates protocol behaviour against RFC specifications.

4. **Decision/Action Engine**
   * *Why:* Detection alone is not prevention — an action must follow.
   * *How:* The IPS triggers one of the following responses:
     - **Deny/Drop:** Silently discards the malicious packet.
     - **Reject (TCP Reset / ICMP Unreachable):** Actively notifies the sender.
     - **Block Session:** Tears down the entire TCP flow.
     - **Quarantine:** Redirects the source to a captive portal/VLAN.
     - **Rate-Limit / Throttle:** Reduces bandwidth for the offender.

5. **Logging & Alert Generation**
   * *Why:* Forensic evidence and SIEM correlation.
   * *How:* Logs are sent via **syslog, CEF, LEEF, or REST API** to a SIEM/SOAR platform.

6. **Policy Update & Signature Refresh**
   * *Why:* New threats emerge daily. An outdated IPS is functionally dead.
   * *How:* Vendor feeds (e.g., Cisco Talos, Emerging Threats) push signature updates — typically every **15 to 30 minutes** in enterprise deployments.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Concept Matrix

Since IPS is conceptual, the "formulas" here are the **defining relationships** between IPS performance parameters:

| **Parameter** | **Relationship / Definition** | **Standard Unit** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| True Positive Rate (TPR) | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) | Measures detection effectiveness |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) | Measures false alarm burden |
| Detection Accuracy (ACC) | $\text{ACC} = \dfrac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) | Overall classifier correctness |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Packets Inspected}}{\text{Time}}$ | $\text{Mpps}$ or $\text{Gbps}$ | Capacity planning for inline deployment |
| Block Latency | $\text{Latency} = T_{\text{block}} - T_{\text{arrive}}$ | $\text{ms}$ or $\mu\text{s}$ | Determines inline viability |
| Availability Impact | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$ | Percentage (%) | Failure of an IPS drops the network (single point of failure) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where TP, FP, TN, FN stand:**
> - **TP** = True Positive (correctly blocked a real attack)
> - **FP** = False Positive (incorrectly blocked legitimate traffic)
> - **TN** = True Negative (correctly allowed legitimate traffic)
> - **FN** = False Negative (failed to block a real attack)

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Telecom backbones:** Inline IPS at 100 Gbps line rate (e.g., Cisco Firepower 9300).
- **Data Centers:** East-West traffic IPS to prevent lateral movement of malware.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA):** Protocol-aware IPS for Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104.
- **Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP):** Host-based IPS via agents or VPC traffic mirroring.
- **5G Core Networks:** IPS at the N6 interface (between data network and 5G core) to filter UE-to-Internet traffic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of IPS Operational Framework

While IPS is not a "derivation-heavy" topic, the KTU 2024 examiner expects students to demonstrate the **mathematical reasoning** behind detection performance. The most commonly asked analytical content is the **ROC curve** derivation from TPR and FPR.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: From Confusion Matrix to Detection Performance

The IPS detection engine can be modelled as a **binary classifier**. Let us derive the relationships between the four fundamental outcomes.

### Step 1 — Define the Universe of Packets

Consider a finite inspection window of $N$ packets entering the IPS pipeline. Each packet is independently classified.

$$N = \text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{TN} + \text{FN}$$

*Explanation:* The total inspected packets $N$ is partitioned into exactly four disjoint subsets based on the classifier's decision and the ground-truth label.

### Step 2 — Define Class-Conditional Counts

Let $P$ = number of *actual* malicious packets in the window, and $Q$ = number of *actual* benign packets.

$$P = \text{TP} + \text{FN}$$

$$Q = \text{FP} + \text{TN}$$

*Explanation:* Among truly malicious packets, the IPS either catches them (TP) or misses them (FN). Among truly benign packets, the IPS either wrongly flags them (FP) or correctly passes them (TN).

### Step 3 — Express Detection Rate (TPR) and False Alarm Rate (FPR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TPR} &= \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{\text{TP}}{P} \\
\text{FPR} &= \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \frac{\text{FP}}{Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* TPR is the fraction of all malicious packets that the IPS successfully blocks. FPR is the fraction of all legitimate packets that are incorrectly blocked. These are the two axes of the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve**.

### Step 4 — Derive the Overall Detection Accuracy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACC} &= \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{N} \\
&= \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{TN} + \text{FN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Accuracy is the proportion of *all* correct decisions (correct blocks + correct passes) out of the total inspected volume.

### Step 5 — Express the F1-Score (Balanced Performance Metric)

For imbalanced datasets (where legitimate traffic vastly outnumbers attacks), accuracy alone is misleading. The F1-score is the harmonic mean of **precision** and **recall**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision} &= \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} \\
\text{Recall} &= \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \text{TPR} \\
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* $F_1$ penalises a classifier that is heavily skewed — e.g., an IPS with 100% recall (catches everything) but 90% false positives is unusable. KTU 2024 examiners frequently test this metric.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Question:** An IPS inspects **10,000 packets** in one minute. Of these, **200** are actual attacks. The IPS correctly blocks **180** attacks, **misses 20**, incorrectly flags **15** legitimate packets, and correctly passes the rest. Compute TPR, FPR, and Accuracy.

**Given:**
- $\text{TP} = 180$
- $\text{FN} = 20$
- $\text{FP} = 15$
- $\text{TN} = 10{,}000 - 180 - 20 - 15 = 9{,}785$

**Step 1 — Compute TPR:**

$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FPR:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{15}{15 + 9{,}785} = \frac{15}{9{,}800} \approx 0.00153 = 0.153\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Accuracy:**

$$
\text{ACC} = \frac{180 + 9{,}785}{10{,}000} = \frac{9{,}965}{10{,}000} = 0.9965 = 99.65\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The IPS catches **90% of attacks** (acceptable) and incorrectly blocks only **0.15%** of legitimate traffic (excellent). The accuracy is high, but as KTU examiners will note — accuracy on imbalanced data is *not* the right metric; F1-score would be more honest.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IPS Inline Placement & Decision Flow

The following diagram illustrates the **in-line operational flow** of an IPS, showing how every packet must traverse the device and the decision/action engine that follows detection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Network Traffic] --> B[Packet Capture Engine]
    B --> C[Protocol Normalization and Defragmentation]
    C --> D[Deep Packet Inspection DPI Layer]
    D --> E{Signature and Anomaly Detection Engine}
    E -->|Match Found| F[Decision and Action Engine]
    E -->|No Match| G[Forward Packet to Destination]
    F --> H{Action Type Selector}
    H -->|Block Critical| I[Drop Packet and Reset Session]
    H -->|Suspicious| J[Quarantine Source IP in VLAN]
    H -->|High Volume| K[Rate Limit the Source]
    I --> L[Log to SIEM via Syslog]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    G --> M[Log Clean Pass to SIEM]
    L --> N[Continue Inspection Loop]
    M --> N
```

*Explanation:* Notice how every packet — even clean ones — must traverse the IPS. This is the **in-line property** that distinguishes an IPS from an IDS. The decision engine is the heart of prevention.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — High-Level IPS Deployment Architecture

This diagram shows the **physical placement** of an IPS within a typical enterprise perimeter architecture (a question frequently asked in KTU exams).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Internet[Internet and External Network] --> FW[Perimeter Firewall Layer 3 and 4]
    FW --> IPS[IPS Appliance Inline in DMZ]
    IPS --> SW[Internal Core Switch]
    SW --> SRV1[Web Server Farm]
    SW --> SRV2[Database Server Cluster]
    SW --> SRV3[Application Servers]
    SRV1 --> HIPS1[Host IPS on Web Servers]
    SRV2 --> HIPS2[Host IPS on Database Servers]
    SRV3 --> HIPS3[Host IPS on Application Servers]
    SIEM[Central SIEM and SOC Console] -.Logs and Alerts.-> IPS
    SIEM -.Logs and Alerts.-> HIPS1
    SIEM -.Logs and Alerts.-> HIPS2
    SIEM -.Logs and Alerts.-> HIPS3
```

*Explanation:* This is a **defence-in-depth** model. The network IPS sits in the DMZ filtering north-south traffic. Host-based IPS (HIPS) on each server protects against east-west lateral movement. All logs converge into a central SIEM for the Security Operations Center (SOC).

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — NIPS vs HIPS Comparison Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Class[Intrusion Prevention System IPS] --> NIPS[Network Based IPS NIPS]
    Class --> HIPS[Host Based IPS HIPS]
    Class --> WIPS[Wireless IPS WIPS]
    NIPS --> N1[Deployment: Inline at Network Chokepoints]
    NIPS --> N2[Scope: Entire Subnet Traffic]
    NIPS --> N3[Examples: Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto, Snort]
    HIPS --> H1[Deployment: Installed on Endpoint]
    HIPS --> H2[Scope: Local System Calls and Logs]
    HIPS --> H3[Examples: OSSEC, Wazuh, CrowdStrike]
    WIPS --> W1[Deployment: Airspace Monitor]
    WIPS --> W2[Scope: 802.11 Frames and Rogue APs]
    WIPS --> W3[Examples: Aruba WIPS, Cisco Adaptive WIPS]
```

*Explanation:* The KTU 2024 syllabus categorises IPS by deployment scope. Students must know all three classifications and be able to draw a comparison table in the exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Define Intrusion Prevention System (IPS). How is it different from an Intrusion Detection System (IDS)?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
