An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is a network security device or software that monitors network traffic for malicious activities and *proactively blocks* detected threats in real time. It is deployed **in-line**, meaning all traffic must pass through it.

**Differences:**

| **Aspect** | **IDS** | **IPS** |
|---|---|---|
| Operation Mode | Passive (monitors copy of traffic) | Active (in-line) |
| Response | Alerts only | Blocks, drops, resets, quarantines |
| Failure Impact | No traffic disruption | Can disrupt network if it fails |
| Latency Tolerance | Non-critical | Must be < 1 ms |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "IPS is a *type* of IDS." This is **wrong**. The correct relationship is: *IPS evolved from IDS* — it is a *separate class* of control with active prevention capability. Writing "IPS = IDS + blocking" is acceptable, but "IPS is a subtype of IDS" will lose a mark.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]
**List and briefly explain the three main detection techniques used by an IPS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Signature-based Detection (Misuse Detection):** Matches packet contents against a database of known attack patterns (signatures/rules). High accuracy for known threats, zero-day blind.
2. **Anomaly-based Detection:** Builds a statistical baseline of normal behaviour; flags deviations. Effective for zero-days, but higher false-positive rate.
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Verifies that protocol exchanges (e.g., HTTP, DNS, SMB) conform to RFC specifications. Detects protocol abuse and malformed packets.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often miss the **third technique** (stateful protocol analysis) and only list two. Listing all three correctly earns full marks.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture and operational pipeline** of a Network-based Intrusion Prevention System (NIPS) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) in this pipeline.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three deployment modes of an IPS** (inline, passive, and tap-mode). Compare their advantages and limitations in a production enterprise network.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Architecture & DPI

**Architecture of a NIPS (7 Marks):**

A Network-based IPS consists of the following components arranged in a strict pipeline:

1. **Packet Capture Engine:** Uses raw socket access or hardware ASIC to grab every packet traversing the network segment.
2. **Protocol Decoder / Normalizer:** Reassembles fragmented packets, terminates TCP flows in memory, and reconstructs Layer 7 payloads.
3. **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) Engine:** Inspects the *content* of the packet payload, not just headers. DPI is the heart of the IPS — it looks for known attack strings (e.g., `union select`, `../../etc/passwd`, shellcode patterns) and protocol anomalies.
4. **Detection Engine:** Runs three parallel classifiers (signature, anomaly, stateful protocol analysis) and aggregates their verdicts.
5. **Action Engine:** On a "malicious" verdict, it executes a pre-configured response (drop, reset, quarantine, rate-limit).
6. **Logging & Reporting Module:** Emits structured logs (CEF/LEEF) to a SIEM.

**Role of DPI (Key Points):**
- Operates at **OSI Layer 7 (Application Layer)**.
- Reassembles full TCP streams to prevent evasion via fragmentation.
- Detects application-layer attacks (SQLi, XSS, RCE, malware C2 traffic).
- Resource-intensive — requires hardware acceleration (FPGA/ASIC) at high throughput.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse DPI with simple header inspection. DPI specifically means inspecting **payload bytes**, not just IP/TCP headers. Losing the distinction costs 1–2 marks.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Deployment Modes

| **Mode** | **Operation** | **Advantages** | **Limitations** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inline Mode** | IPS sits physically in the data path; all traffic *must* pass through it. | Real-time blocking; full prevention capability. | Single point of failure; introduces latency; can become bottleneck. |
| **Passive Mode (TAP)** | IPS receives a *copy* of traffic via a network TAP or SPAN port. | No impact on network availability; ideal for monitoring. | **Cannot block** — equivalent to IDS, not true prevention. |
| **Hybrid / Tap-to-Inline** | IPS initially in TAP mode, switches to inline after tuning. | Safe tuning; reduces false-positive risk during deployment. | Complex configuration; brief detection gap during switchover. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often claim "passive mode" can block traffic. **It cannot.** Passive mode is observation-only — to block, the IPS *must* be inline. This is a 2-mark deduction if confused.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **difference between Network-based IPS (NIPS) and Host-based IPS (HIPS)**. List any four commercial examples for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A corporate IPS processes **50,000 packets per second**. In a one-minute window, it blocks **300 malicious packets**, misses **25 attacks**, and falsely flags **40 legitimate packets** as malicious, while correctly passing the rest. Calculate the **TPR, FPR, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score** of the IPS.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — NIPS vs HIPS

| **Feature** | **NIPS** | **HIPS** |
|---|---|---|
| Deployment Location | Network perimeter, DMZ, data center core. | Individual endpoint/server. |
| Traffic Visibility | All north-south and east-west traffic on the segment. | Only traffic to/from that host. |
| Inspection Scope | Full packet payload + headers. | System calls, log files, registry, file integrity. |
| Resource Consumption | Centralized hardware (ASIC/FPGA). | Consumes host CPU/RAM. |
| Coverage of Encrypted Traffic | Limited (needs SSL/TLS inspection). | Can inspect post-decryption at the endpoint. |
| Examples | Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto Networks, Snort, Suricata. | OSSEC, Wazuh, CrowdStrike Falcon, Symantec Endpoint Protection. |

**Diagram (NIPS):** Already provided in Section 4.2 (HIPS can be shown as agents on each server).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical Computation

**Given Data (one-minute window):**
- Total packets = $50{,}000 \times 60 = 3{,}000{,}000$
- $\text{TP} = 300$ (correctly blocked attacks)
- $\text{FN} = 25$ (missed attacks)
- $\text{FP} = 40$ (false alarms)
- Total actual attacks = $\text{TP} + \text{FN} = 325$
- Total legitimate = $\text{Total} - 325 = 2{,}999{,}675$
- $\text{TN} = 2{,}999{,}675 - 40 = 2{,}999{,}635$

**Step 1 — True Positive Rate (Recall):**

$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{300}{300 + 25} = \frac{300}{325} \approx 0.9231 = 92.31\%
$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{40}{40 + 2{,}999{,}635} = \frac{40}{2{,}999{,}675} \approx 1.334 \times 10^{-5} = 0.00133\%
$$

**Step 3 — Accuracy:**

$$
\text{ACC} = \frac{300 + 2{,}999{,}635}{3{,}000{,}000} = \frac{2{,}999{,}935}{3{,}000{,}000} \approx 0.99998 = 99.998\%
$$

**Step 4 — Precision:**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{300}{300 + 40} = \frac{300}{340} \approx 0.8824 = 88.24\%
$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8824 \times 0.9231}{0.8824 + 0.9231} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8146}{1.8055} \approx 0.9024 = 90.24\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **total packet count** — forgetting to multiply $50{,}000 \text{ pps} \times 60 \text{ s} = 3{,}000{,}000$ packets. Also, the **TN value must be derived** (not given directly) — a common deduction point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — IPS: Definition & Purpose**

- ✅ An **IPS is an active, in-line** security control — it is **NOT** a passive monitor.
- ✅ Key difference from IDS: **IPS prevents; IDS only detects.**
- ✅ The **three detection techniques** are: **Signature-based, Anomaly-based, and Stateful Protocol Analysis** — *all three must be listed*.
- ✅ The **three deployment modes** are: **Inline, Passive (TAP), and Hybrid** — passive mode cannot block.
- ✅ The **three IPS classifications** are: **NIPS, HIPS, and WIPS** — know at least two examples each.
- ✅ **DPI = Layer 7 payload inspection**, not just header inspection. This is the engine of modern IPS.
- ✅ The **action engine** can execute: **Drop, Reject (TCP Reset), Block Session, Quarantine, Rate-Limit.**
- ✅ A key performance metric is the **ROC curve** built from **TPR (Y-axis) vs FPR (X-axis)**.
- ✅ F1-score is **better than accuracy** on imbalanced datasets (legitimate traffic >> attack traffic).
- ✅ The **single point of failure** risk is the biggest architectural drawback of inline IPS — hence **bypass switches** and **HA (high-availability) pairs** are mandatory in production.
- ✅ Signature updates must be **frequent (15–30 min cycles)** — an outdated IPS is operationally dead.
- ✅ Logs are sent to a **SIEM/SOAR** in **CEF, LEEF, or syslog** formats.
- ✅ KTU 2024 examiners explicitly test the **inline latency requirement (< 1 ms)** and the **defence-in-depth** model combining NIPS + HIPS.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) — Definition and Purpose**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differences between IDS and IPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differences between IDS and IPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Detection System (IDS):** A **passive** security control (hardware or software) that monitors network or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access. The primary function of an IDS is to **detect** suspicious activities and generate **alerts/logs** for the security administrator. It does **not** actively block traffic on its own.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS):** An **active/active-inline** security control that sits directly in the path of network traffic flow and **detects as well as prevents/blockstops** identified malicious activities in real-time. It is essentially an IDS combined with an automated response mechanism (often a firewall-like capability) that can drop packets, reset connections, or quarantine hosts.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** parlance, both fall under the umbrella of **Network Security Monitoring (NSM)** devices, but their *operational philosophy* and *placement in the network* differ fundamentally.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

To make this distinction crystal clear, let us use a real-world analogy:

**IDS = A CCTV Camera with a Burglar Alarm (but no lock on the door)**
- Imagine your house has a security camera that records every movement and an alarm that rings loudly when it sees a thief.
- The alarm **notifies you** (the security admin), but the thief can still walk in unless *you* physically intervene.
- The IDS **watches**, **records**, and **alerts** — it does not physically stop the intruder.

**IPS = A Bouncer Standing at the Door (active blocking)**
- Now imagine a muscular bouncer standing right at the entrance. When a suspicious person approaches, the bouncer **physically stops** them, refuses entry, and may even call the police.
- The IPS **watches**, **decides**, and **acts in real-time** — dropping malicious packets before they enter the network.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Inline Mode** | Device sits directly in the traffic path (mandatory for IPS) |
| **Promiscuous / Tap Mode** | Device passively copies traffic for analysis (typical for IDS) |
| **False Positive** | Benign traffic wrongly flagged as malicious |
| **False Negative** | Malicious traffic wrongly allowed through |
| **Signature-based** | Detection via known attack patterns (Snort rules) |
| **Anomaly-based** | Detection via deviation from baseline behavior |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the syllabus, this topic is foundational for Module 2 because it sets the stage for understanding **Snort (IDS)**, **Suricata (IDS/IPS)**, and **Cisco IPS appliances**, which are covered in subsequent modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The Core Divide

The fundamental difference between IDS and IPS lies in **three pillars**: **Placement**, **Action**, and **Response Latency**.

#### Pillar 1: Network Placement
- **IDS (Passive / Out-of-Band):** Connected via a **network tap** or **SPAN/mirror port** on a switch. Traffic is *copied* to the IDS for inspection. The original traffic flow is **unaffected**.
- **IPS (Inline / In-Line):** Placed **directly in series** with the network cable (between the router/firewall and the internal LAN). All traffic **must pass through** the IPS.

#### Pillar 2: Action Capability
- **IDS:** Action = **Alert** (send email, log to SIEM, trigger SNMP trap, dashboard notification).
- **IPS:** Action = **Alert + Block** (drop packet, reset TCP connection, block source IP via ACL, quarantine host).

#### Pillar 3: Response Latency
- **IDS:** Latency-tolerant (asynchronous). Detection is enough.
- **IPS:** Latency-critical (must operate at wire speed, typically **sub-millisecond** to avoid network slowdown).

### 2.2 Detection Methodologies (Common to Both)

| Method | How It Works | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Signature-based (Misuse)** | Pattern matching against known attack signatures (e.g., `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (msg:"SQL Injection"; content:"' OR 1=1--";)`) | High accuracy for known attacks | **Zero-day blind** |
| **Anomaly-based** | Statistical deviation from baseline (e.g., sudden spike in port 445 traffic) | Detects novel/zero-day attacks | High false positives |
| **Stateful Protocol Analysis** | Tracks protocol state (TCP handshake, DNS query-response) | Detects protocol misuse | Resource intensive |
| **Hybrid** | Combines signatures + anomaly + heuristics | Balanced | Complex to tune |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important study artifact** for this topic. Memorize it thoroughly — it appears almost verbatim in KTU University Exams.

| Parameter | **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)** | **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Operational Mode** | **Passive** (Promiscuous / Tap) | **Active / Inline** |
| **Position in Network** | Off the main traffic path (SPAN port) | **Directly in-line** with traffic flow |
| **Primary Function** | **Detect** and **alert** | **Detect, alert, and block/prevent** |
| **Response Mechanism** | Notification only (email, log, SIEM) | Automated blocking (drop, reset, quarantine) |
| **Latency Introduced** | Negligible (passive monitoring) | Some latency (must process at line rate) |
| **Failure Impact** | Graceful — traffic continues | **Single point of failure** (if it crashes, network dies) |
| **Bypass Mode** | Not required (already out-of-band) | Often has **fail-open / fail-closed** mode |
| **Detection Engine** | Signature + Anomaly | Signature + Anomaly (same engines) |
| **Examples** | **Snort** (in IDS mode), OSSEC, Zeek | **Snort** (with `-A` and `reject` rules), Suricata, Cisco Firepower |
| **Resource Cost** | Lower (no inline processing) | Higher (deep packet inspection at wire speed) |
| **Use Case** | Monitoring, forensics, compliance audit | Active defense of critical segments |
| **False Positive Cost** | Annoyance (alert fatigue) | **Service disruption** (legitimate traffic dropped) |
| **Placement Example** | Behind firewall on SPAN port | Between firewall and internal LAN (DMZ edge) |
| **OSI Operation** | L2–L7 (monitoring) | L2–L7 (inline filtering) |
| **Data Path** | Reads copy of traffic | **Modifies** the actual traffic flow |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In enterprise networks, you will typically see a **layered deployment**:
1. **Perimeter Firewall** — first line of defense (stateful packet filtering).
2. **Inline IPS** — second line, deep packet inspection and blocking.
3. **Passive IDS** — third line, tap/mirror monitoring for forensics, threat hunting, and compliance.
4. **SIEM** — central log aggregation from both IDS and IPS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A common exam trap is the statement *"IDS can also block traffic."* This is **FALSE** in its pure form. However, modern tools like **Snort** can be configured in *inline mode* to act as an IPS. So the technology is the same — the **deployment mode** differs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which One Do I Need?"

For a systematic, exam-friendly analytical approach, let us derive a **decision tree** to determine whether to deploy an IDS or IPS in a given scenario.

#### Step 1: Identify the Security Objective

$$
\text{Objective} = \begin{cases} \text{Monitor \& Log} \rightarrow \text{IDS} \\ \text{Block in Real-Time} \rightarrow \text{IPS} \end{cases}
$$

#### Step 2: Assess Network Criticality

We define a **Criticality Index (CI)** based on three sub-factors:

$$
CI = w_1 \cdot B + w_2 \cdot I + w_3 \cdot C
$$

Where:
- $B$ = **Business Impact** score (1–5) of the segment being protected
- $I$ = **Data Sensitivity** score (1–5)
- $C$ = **Compliance Requirement** score (1–5)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = weighted coefficients (typically $w_1 = 0.4$, $w_2 = 0.4$, $w_3 = 0.2$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } CI &\geq 4.0 \rightarrow \text{Mandate IPS (inline blocking required)} \\
\text{If } 2.5 \leq CI &< 4.0 \rightarrow \text{Hybrid: IPS at edge + IDS for monitoring} \\
\text{If } CI &< 2.5 \rightarrow \text{IDS only (cost-effective monitoring)} 
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Evaluate Tolerance for False Positives

$$
\text{False Positive Tolerance (FPT)} = \frac{\text{Legitimate Traffic Blocked}}{\text{Total Legitimate Traffic}} \times 100\%
$$

- **IDS scenario:** $FPT$ can be high (e.g., 5%) — annoying but harmless.
- **IPS scenario:** $FPT$ must be near zero (e.g., $\leq 0.01\%$) — otherwise business is disrupted.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision Rule:} \quad 
&\text{If } FPT_{acceptable} > 1\% \rightarrow \text{IDS acceptable} \\
&\text{If } FPT_{acceptable} \leq 0.1\% \rightarrow \text{IPS with tuning required}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Performance Trade-off Derivation

Let us derive the **throughput impact** of placing an IPS inline.

**Given:**
- Raw network throughput: $T_{raw}$ (in Gbps)
- IPS processing time per packet: $t_{proc}$ (in microseconds)
- Average packet size: $S_{avg}$ (in bytes)

**Step 1:** Compute the maximum packets per second the IPS can handle:

$$
PPS_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{proc}} \quad \text{(packets per second)}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert this to a maximum data throughput:

$$
T_{max} = PPS_{max} \times S_{avg} \times 8 \quad \text{(bits per second)}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the throughput efficiency:

$$
\eta_{IPS} = \frac{T_{max}}{T_{raw}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4:** The **bottleneck condition** arises when:

$$
\eta_{IPS} < 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Network is bottlenecked by IPS}
$$

**Example Calculation (KTU-style):**
- $t_{proc} = 5 \,\mu s$ → $PPS_{max} = 200{,}000$ packets/sec
- $S_{avg} = 750$ bytes
- $T_{max} = 200{,}000 \times 750 \times 8 = 1.2 \times 10^9$ bits/sec = **1.2 Gbps**
- If $T_{raw} = 10$ Gbps, then $\eta_{IPS} = 12\%$ → **IPS becomes a severe bottleneck**.

This is why high-performance IPS appliances use **hardware ASICs** and **FPGA acceleration** to push $t_{proc}$ down to sub-microsecond levels.

### 3.3 Failure Mode Analysis (Step-by-Step)

A critical difference is how IDS and IPS behave **under failure**.

**IDS failure:**
- IDS crashes → traffic continues normally → you lose visibility but not connectivity.
- Failure mode = **Fail-Open** (naturally).

**IPS failure:**
- IPS crashes → traffic **stops** (because the cable literally goes through it) → network outage.
- Mitigation = **Fail-Open Bypass** (electrical relay bypasses the IPS on power loss).
- OR **Fail-Closed** (strict mode, maximum security but maximum downtime risk).

$$
\text{Failure Behavior} = \begin{cases} 
\text{IDS:} & \text{Fail-Open (graceful degradation)} \\ 
\text{IPS (default):} & \text{Fail-Closed (DoS risk)} \\ 
\text{IPS (with bypass):} & \text{Fail-Open (configurable)}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often ask: *"Why is an IPS considered a single point of failure?"* The answer lies exactly in this inline architecture — physically breaking the cable breaks the network.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IDS Network Placement (Passive / Out-of-Band)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet[Internet] --> router[Perimeter Router]
    router --> firewall[Firewall]
    firewall --> switchCore[Core Switch]
    switchCore --> server1[Internal Server 1]
    switchCore --> server2[Internal Server 2]
    switchCore --> spanPort{SPAN Mirror Port}
    spanPort -.copy.-> ids[IDS Sensor]
    ids --> siem[SIEM Dashboard]
    siem --> admin[Security Admin Alert]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = actual traffic flow.
- **Dotted arrow** = mirrored copy of traffic (the IDS only *sees*, never *touches*).
- The IDS is **never in the path**, so its failure cannot disrupt traffic.

### 4.2 IPS Network Placement (Active / Inline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet[Internet] --> router[Perimeter Router]
    router --> firewall[Firewall]
    firewall --> ips{IPS Inline Appliance}
    ips -->|clean traffic| switchCore[Core Switch]
    ips -->|malicious dropped| drop[Packet Dropped / Reset]
    switchCore --> server1[Internal Server 1]
    switchCore --> server2[Internal Server 2]
    ips --> siem[SIEM Dashboard]
    siem --> admin[Security Admin Alert]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The IPS is **physically inline** between firewall and switch.
- All traffic **must traverse** the IPS.
- The IPS has two output channels: forward (clean) or terminate (malicious).
- Failure of the IPS box = network outage (unless bypass relay exists).

### 4.3 Hybrid Deployment Architecture (Industry Best Practice)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Perimeter["Perimeter Zone"]
        inet[Internet] --> fw1[Edge Firewall]
    end
    subgraph DMZInline["DMZ Inline Layer"]
        fw1 --> ips1[IPS Sensor 1 Inline]
        ips1 --> dmz[DMZ Web Servers]
    end
    subgraph InternalMonitor["Internal Monitoring Layer"]
        core[Core Switch] --> span2{SPAN Port}
        span2 -.copy.-> ids1[IDS Sensor Passive]
        ids1 --> siem[Central SIEM]
    end
    dmz --> core
    core --> servers[Internal Servers]
    ips1 --> siem
    siem --> soc[SOC Analyst Console]
```

**Key Insight:** The perimeter uses an **IPS** (active blocking), while the internal core uses a **passive IDS** (deep visibility, no disruption). This is the architecture seen in banks, ISPs, and large enterprises.

### 4.4 IDS vs IPS Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Security Requirement] --> q1{Need to block traffic in real time?}
    q1 -->|YES| q2{Can tolerate any downtime risk?}
    q1 -->|NO| ids[Deploy Passive IDS]
    q2 -->|NO| hybrid[Deploy IPS with Fail-Open Bypass]
    q2 -->|YES| ips[Deploy IPS Inline]
    ids --> monitor[Monitor and Log]
    ips --> block[Detect and Block]
    hybrid --> block
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between an IDS and an IPS in terms of operational mode and network placement.

**Model Answer:**
- An **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)** operates in **passive mode**. It monitors a copy of network traffic (typically received via a SPAN port or network tap) without being in the direct path of data flow. Its primary function is to **detect and alert** the administrator about suspicious activity; it does not block traffic.
- An **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** operates in **active/inline mode**. It is placed **directly in the path** of network traffic, sitting between two network segments (e.g., firewall and core switch). It not only detects malicious traffic but also **prevents/blocks** it in real-time by dropping packets, resetting connections, or blocking source IPs.

#### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Why is an IPS considered a single point of failure, while an IDS is not?

**Model Answer:**
An IPS is placed **inline** in the network, meaning all traffic must physically pass through it. If the IPS device fails (crashes, power loss, hardware fault), the network path is broken, resulting in a complete denial of service for that segment.
An IDS, in contrast, operates **out-of-band** via a SPAN/tap port. It only receives a *copy* of the traffic for analysis. If the IDS fails, the original traffic flow continues unaffected — the network suffers only a loss of *visibility*, not connectivity. Hence, IDS failure is non-disruptive, while IPS failure is critical.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the architectural differences between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS). Include a comparison of their network placement, detection methods, and response mechanisms.
**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the various detection methodologies used in IDS/IPS (signature-based, anomaly-based, stateful protocol analysis) with suitable examples. Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of each.

**Model Answer for Part (a):**
1. **Network Placement:** IDS is connected via a network tap or SPAN port (passive, out-of-band). IPS is placed inline between two network segments (e.g., firewall and switch).
2. **Operational Mode:** IDS operates in promiscuous/promiscuous listen mode. IPS operates in inline filtering mode.
3. **Response Mechanism:** IDS sends alerts (email, log, SIEM) but takes no blocking action. IPS can drop packets, send TCP RST, block IPs, or quarantine hosts.
4. **Detection Methods:** Both can use signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis. The difference is not in *how* they detect, but in *what they do after* detection.
5. **Failure Behavior:** IDS fails open (no impact on traffic). IPS fails closed (network down) unless equipped with bypass hardware.
6. **Performance Impact:** IDS adds negligible latency. IPS adds processing latency and may become a bottleneck if not hardware-accelerated.
7. **Use Cases:** IDS is preferred for monitoring, forensics, and compliance. IPS is preferred for active defense of high-value assets (DMZ, payment systems).

**Model Answer for Part (b):**
1. **Signature-based Detection:** Uses predefined patterns of known attacks (e.g., Snort rules matching the string `"%27 OR 1=1--"` for SQL injection). *Strengths:* High accuracy, low false positives for known attacks. *Weaknesses:* Cannot detect zero-day attacks; signature database must be continuously updated.
2. **Anomaly-based Detection:** Establishes a baseline of normal behavior (e.g., average packets/sec on port 80) and flags deviations. *Strengths:* Can detect novel/zero-day attacks. *Weaknesses:* High false positive rate; requires extensive training period.
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Tracks the state of network connections (e.g., TCP three-way handshake, DNS query-response correlation). *Strengths:* Detects protocol misuse and evasion techniques. *Weaknesses:* Resource-intensive, complex to implement.
4. **Hybrid Approach:** Modern systems (e.g., Suricata) combine all three for balanced detection coverage.
5. **Example with Snort Rule (signature):** `alert tcp any any -> 192.168.1.0/24 80 (msg:"Possible SQLi"; content:"UNION SELECT"; sid:1000001; rev:1;)` — this rule generates an alert when HTTP traffic contains the string "UNION SELECT", indicative of SQL injection.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**
**(a)** [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the deployment architectures of IDS and IPS. Justify why many enterprises deploy both in a hybrid model.
**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the challenges and limitations of deploying an IPS in high-throughput networks. Suggest engineering solutions to mitigate these challenges.

**Model Answer for Part (a):**
*(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4.1, 4.2, and 4.3 of these notes.)*
1. **IDS Deployment:** Connected via SPAN port to a switch; receives mirrored traffic. Diagram shows the IDS as a side branch, not in the main path.
2. **IPS Deployment:** Placed inline between firewall and internal network. Diagram shows the IPS as a gateway that all traffic must pass through.
3. **Justification for Hybrid Model:** Enterprises need both *active blocking* (for known threats at the perimeter) and *deep visibility* (for threat hunting, forensic analysis, and compliance). The hybrid model uses an IPS at the perimeter (inline) and an IDS internally (passive monitoring of east-west traffic). This provides defense-in-depth without the risk of a single IPS failure taking down the entire network.

**Model Answer for Part (b):**
1. **Challenge 1: Throughput Bottleneck:** Inline processing at wire speed is hard. *Solution:* Use **hardware-accelerated IPS** (ASICs, FPGAs, DPUs from Intel/Mellanox).
2. **Challenge 2: Latency:** Deep packet inspection adds delay, unacceptable for real-time applications (VoIP, gaming). *Solution:* Use **bypass switches** that route traffic directly during high load, and apply DPI only to suspicious flows.
3. **Challenge 3: False Positives:** Legitimate traffic may be dropped. *Solution:* Use **tuning, whitelisting, and a staged rollout** (start in IDS mode, then switch to IPS once tuned).
4. **Challenge 4: Encrypted Traffic (TLS 1.3):** Cannot inspect encrypted payloads. *Solution:* Deploy **SSL/TLS inspection** (man-in-the-middle proxy) — though this raises privacy concerns.
5. **Challenge 5: Encrypted Traffic (TLS 1.3) and Encrypted Threats:** *Solution:* Use **JA3/JA3S fingerprinting** to detect malicious TLS clients without decryption.
6. **Challenge 6: Single Point of Failure:** *Solution:* Deploy **redundant IPS in HA (High Availability) active-passive** mode, with automatic failover.
7. **Challenge 7: Encrypted Traffic (TLS 1.3) and Performance:** *Solution:* Offload TLS termination to a dedicated load balancer and feed decrypted traffic to the IPS.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Conflating "detection engine" with "deployment mode"** — Many students wrongly state *"IDS and IPS use different detection algorithms."* Wrong. They use the **same engines** (signatures, anomalies). The difference is **placement and action**. Expect to lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the "single point of failure" argument** — KTU examiners specifically look for this phrase in IPS answers. Missing it costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning SPAN/Tap port for IDS** — A vague answer like *"IDS monitors traffic"* without specifying *how* it gets the traffic is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Confusing HIDS vs NIDS with IDS vs IPS** — These are orthogonal classifications. HIDS/NIDS = *what* is monitored (host vs network). IDS/IPS = *how* it responds (passive vs active). Mixing them up is a classic KTU trap.
> 5. **Omitting the "fail-open" concept for IPS** — Always discuss failure mode in IPS answers. Use the phrase *"bypass relay"* or *"fail-open mode"*.
> 6. **Not drawing diagrams** — Part B questions carrying 7+ marks almost always expect a diagram. Skipping it can cost 2–3 marks even if the text is perfect.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Must-Know" Bullets for the Exam:**
> - **IDS = Passive, Out-of-Band, Detect + Alert** (does NOT block).
> - **IPS = Active, Inline, Detect + Block** (can drop packets, reset TCP).
> - IDS gets traffic via **SPAN port** or **network tap** (mirrored copy).
> - IPS sits **directly in the traffic path** (between firewall and switch).
> - IPS = **Single Point of Failure** → mitigated by **fail-open bypass** or **HA clustering**.
> - Detection methods are **common** to both: Signature, Anomaly, Stateful Protocol Analysis, Hybrid.
> - **Examples:** Snort (IDS mode vs IPS mode), Suricata, Cisco Firepower, OSSEC.
> - **HIDS/NIDS** (host vs network) is a *different* axis from **IDS/IPS** (passive vs active).
> - **False positives** in IDS = alert fatigue; **false positives** in IPS = service disruption.
> - **Throughput bottleneck** is calculated as: $T_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{proc}} \times S_{avg} \times 8$.
> - **Hybrid deployment** (IPS at perimeter + IDS internally) is the industry best practice.
> - **TLS 1.3 encryption** is a modern challenge → mitigated by **JA3 fingerprinting** or **TLS inspection proxies**.
> - **Real-world analogy:** IDS = CCTV with alarm (watches and rings); IPS = Bouncer at the door (watches and physically stops).
> - **Key phrase for exams:** *"The technology is identical — the deployment mode and response action differ."*
> - **Snort rule example (signature):** `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (msg:"Test"; content:"hack"; sid:1; rev:1;)`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IPS Techniques and Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IPS Techniques and Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) Techniques and Mechanisms** refer to the **active**, **inline** set of methodologies, algorithms, and architectural components used to **autonomously detect**, **classify**, **decide upon**, and **actively block or mitigate** malicious network traffic or host-level activity *before* it reaches the protected target. Unlike a passive IDS, an IPS sits directly in the traffic flow (typically in *promiscuous inline mode*) and employs a combination of **signature-based detection**, **anomaly-based detection**, **stateful protocol analysis**, and **behavioral heuristics** to enforce a security policy in real time.

The **mechanisms** within an IPS are generally classified into four functional pillars:
1. **Detection Mechanisms** – How the IPS recognizes an attack.
2. **Prevention / Response Mechanisms** – How it stops the attack.
3. **Deployment Mechanisms** – Where and how it is placed in the network.
4. **Correlation & Management Mechanisms** – How it integrates with broader SIEM/SOAR ecosystems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Airport Security Checkpoint**
> 
> Imagine an international airport terminal. A *CCTV camera* mounted on the ceiling is an **IDS** — it *watches* passengers and raises an alarm if someone behaves suspiciously, but the passenger has already boarded the plane.
> 
> An **IPS**, however, is the *security officer standing at the boarding gate* with a metal detector and X-ray scanner. The officer doesn't just observe; he **stops** the passenger, **inspects** the bag (**signature check**), **measures** unusual weight or behavior (**anomaly check**), and either **allows**, **denies**, or **redirects** the passenger (**prevention/response**) *in real time, before harm occurs*.
> 
> The **Techniques** are the scanning technologies (X-ray, metal detector, sniffer dog). The **Mechanisms** are the rules of engagement — when to stop, when to flag, when to quarantine.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in IPS

The following metrics are universally used to evaluate IPS performance and are **high-yield for KTU exams**:

- **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Detection Rate (DR):** $\text{DR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **Precision:** $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Throughput / Latency:** Measured in **Gbps** and **microseconds ($\mu s$)** respectively.
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** — measured in **seconds**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of IPS Techniques

An IPS employs **three primary detection techniques** that operate either independently or in a hybrid (composite) fashion:

#### 2.1.1 Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)
- Operates by matching observed traffic/payload against a **database of known attack patterns** (signatures).
- Each signature is essentially a rule such as: *"If packet payload contains the byte sequence `\x90\x90\x90` (NOP sled) followed by shellcode → ALERT/BLOCK."*
- **Analogy:** A bouncer at a club with a *photograph book of known troublemakers*. If your face matches → entry denied.
- **Pros:** Low FPR, fast for known attacks. **Cons:** Zero-day blind, signature-update lag.

#### 2.1.2 Anomaly-Based Detection
- Builds a **statistical or machine-learning model** of "normal" behavior (baseline) and flags deviations.
- Uses **probability distributions**, **clustering (k-means)**, **neural networks**, or **one-class SVMs**.
- **Analogy:** A bank that flags your card if you suddenly withdraw ₹50,000 from a casino in Macau at 3 AM — statistically anomalous, even if not *known* fraud.
- **Pros:** Can detect zero-day attacks. **Cons:** Higher FPR, training overhead.

#### 2.1.3 Stateful Protocol Analysis (Deep Packet Inspection - DPI)
- Reconstructs the **state machine** of a protocol (TCP, HTTP, DNS, FTP) and validates each packet against the expected transition.
- Detects attacks that violate protocol semantics (e.g., a *FIN* packet with no prior *SYN*).
- **Analogy:** A grammar teacher who knows that a sentence ending with a preposition is *technically wrong* even if every word is in the dictionary.

#### 2.1.4 Hybrid Detection
- Combines two or more of the above to balance **detection rate (DR)** and **false positive rate (FPR)**.
- Modern enterprise IPS (e.g., Cisco Firepower, Snort with pre-processors) are predominantly **hybrid**.

### 2.2 IPS Response / Prevention Mechanisms

Once an attack is *classified*, the IPS executes one or more of the following **prevention mechanisms**:

| # | Mechanism | Action Description | OSI Layer |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Packet Drop** | Silently discards the malicious packet. | L2–L7 |
| 2 | **TCP Reset (RST)** | Sends a spoofed TCP RST to both ends of the session. | L4 |
| 3 | **Session Termination** | Flushes the entire flow entry from the connection table. | L4 |
| 4 | **Shun / Block** | Adds the source IP to a temporary block list (ACL). | L3–L4 |
| 5 | **Rate Limiting / Throttling** | Reduces bandwidth available to the offending flow. | L3–L4 |
| 6 | **Quarantine (Host IPS)** | Isolates the infected endpoint via NAC/802.1X. | L2–L7 |
| 7 | **Packet Modification** | Sanitizes payload (e.g., strips shellcode) and forwards. | L7 |
| 8 | **Logging + Alerting** | Forwards event to SIEM. | Management |

### 2.3 IPS Deployment Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Inline vs. Passive Modes**
> - **Inline Mode (IPS mode):** Traffic *physically* passes through the IPS. Failure mode = fail-open or fail-closed (Bypass).
> - **Tap / SPAN (IDS mode):** IPS receives a *copy* via port mirroring; cannot block — degraded to IDS functionality.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes |
|------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Detection Rate (DR) | $\text{DR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Range $\vert 0, 1 \vert$ |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Lower is better |
| Accuracy (ACC) | $\text{ACC} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{DR}}{\text{Precision} + \text{DR}}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R |
| Throughput (T) | $T = \frac{N_{\text{packets}}}{t_{\text{seconds}}}$ | **Mbps** or **Gbps** |
| Latency (L) | $L = t_{\text{out}} - t_{\text{in}}$ | **$\mu s$** (microseconds) |
| Snort Rule Action | `alert, log, pass, drop, reject, sdrop` | Snort-specific syntax |
| Bayes' Theorem (Anomaly) | $P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Posterior probability |
| Shannon Entropy (Anomaly) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bytes/entropy of payload |
| Z-Score (Anomaly) | $Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std dev |

> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used by vendors (Palo Alto, Cisco, Fortinet) in their product datasheets and are the foundation of *any* KTU numerical question on IDS/IPS performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Computing Detection Rate and FPR from a Confusion Matrix

> **KTU Numerical Pattern (frequently asked in ESE, 3–7 marks):**
> *"An IPS evaluated on 10,000 sessions produced the following confusion matrix values. Compute the Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Accuracy, and F1-Score."*

**Given Data:**
- True Positives (TP) = 950
- False Negatives (FN) = 50
- False Positives (FP) = 80
- True Negatives (TN) = 8,920
- Total = $TP + TN + FP + FN = 950 + 8920 + 80 + 50 = 10{,}000$ ✓

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (Recall, Sensitivity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DR} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\[4pt]
\text{DR} &= \frac{950}{950 + 50} \\[4pt]
\text{DR} &= \frac{950}{1000} \\[4pt]
\text{DR} &= 0.95 = 95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate (FPR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FPR} &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\[4pt]
\text{FPR} &= \frac{80}{80 + 8920} \\[4pt]
\text{FPR} &= \frac{80}{9000} \\[4pt]
\text{FPR} &= 0.00888 \approx 0.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Precision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\[4pt]
\text{Precision} &= \frac{950}{950 + 80} \\[4pt]
\text{Precision} &= \frac{950}{1030} \\[4pt]
\text{Precision} &= 0.9223 \approx 92.23\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy (ACC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACC} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\[4pt]
\text{ACC} &= \frac{950 + 8920}{10{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{ACC} &= \frac{9870}{10{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{ACC} &= 0.987 = 98.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of Precision and Recall):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{DR}}{\text{Precision} + \text{DR}} \\[4pt]
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.9223 \cdot 0.95}{0.9223 + 0.95} \\[4pt]
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.8762}{1.8723} \\[4pt]
F_1 &= 2 \cdot 0.4680 \\[4pt]
F_1 &= 0.9360 \approx 93.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation:**
A high DR (95%) and low FPR (0.89%) indicate the IPS is *both sensitive* (catches most attacks) and *specific* (rarely raises false alarms). Such a system is operationally deployable.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Z-Score Anomaly Detection

> **KTU Pattern:** *"A network sensor records the average packet size of legitimate flows as 500 bytes with $\sigma = 40$ bytes. A flow with packet size 620 bytes is observed. Is it an anomaly?"*

**Step 1 — State the Hypothesis:**
- $H_0$ (Null): The observed packet is *normal* (part of baseline distribution).
- $H_1$ (Alt): The observed packet is an *anomaly*.

**Step 2 — Compute the Z-Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \\[4pt]
Z &= \frac{620 - 500}{40} \\[4pt]
Z &= \frac{120}{40} \\[4pt]
Z &= 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Threshold Decision (Standard 3-sigma rule):**
- $\vert Z \vert > 3 \Rightarrow$ **Anomaly** (99.7% confidence interval breached).

**Step 4 — Decision:** Since $Z = 3.0$ lies *exactly* on the boundary, the IPS will flag this as a **high-confidence anomaly** and trigger a prevention action (e.g., rate limit or drop).

---

### 3.3 IPS Technique vs. Attack-Type Decision Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Attack Type | Best IPS Technique | Reason | Typical Signature/Heuristic |
|-------------|---------------------|--------|-----------------------------|
| **SQL Injection** | Signature + DPI | Known string patterns (`' OR 1=1 --`) | Regex on URL/payload |
| **DDoS (SYN Flood)** | Anomaly + Stateful | Volume-based, not signature-based | Z-score on SYN rate |
| **Buffer Overflow** | Signature (NOP sled) | Known byte patterns (`\x90\x90\x90`) | Pattern matching |
| **Port Scan (Nmap)** | Anomaly + Stateful | Sequential port access pattern | Connection rate anomaly |
| **Zero-Day Malware** | Anomaly / Behavioral | No signature exists | ML model on syscall trace |
| **Protocol Violation (HTTP smuggling)** | Stateful Protocol Analysis | HTTP request structure malformed | RFC compliance check |
| **DNS Tunneling** | Anomaly (Entropy) | High entropy in DNS TXT records | Shannon entropy > threshold |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an IPS (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Incoming Packet Stream] --> A1[Preprocessor and Decoder]
    A1 --> A2[Packet Reassembler and TCP Stream Reconstructor]
    A2 --> A3[Detection Engine Core]
    A3 --> B1[Signature Matcher Aho Corasick Engine]
    A3 --> B2[Anomaly Detector Statistical and ML Model]
    A3 --> B3[Protocol State Machine Validator]
    A3 --> B4[Heuristic Engine Rule Scoring Module]
    B1 --> C1[Event Aggregator and Correlation Unit]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Policy Decision Point PDP]
    D1 --> E1[Response Action Module]
    E1 --> F1[Action 1 Drop Packet]
    E1 --> F2[Action 2 TCP Reset]
    E1 --> F3[Action 3 Shun Source IP]
    E1 --> F4[Action 4 Rate Limit Flow]
    E1 --> F5[Action 5 Log to SIEM]
    E1 --> G1[Forward Clean Traffic to Destination]
    C1 --> H1[Admin Console Dashboard and Alerting]
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- **Top-to-Bottom Flow:** The packet enters, gets decoded, reassembled, and then runs *in parallel* through all four detection engines. This is the hallmark of a **hybrid IPS**.
- **C1 (Correlation Unit):** Combines weak signals from multiple engines to produce a *high-confidence* event (reduces FPR).
- **D1 (Policy Decision Point):** Maps the classified attack to a *pre-configured action* (e.g., DROP, RESET, SHUN).
- **G1 (Forwarding):** Only clean packets are forwarded — this is the **inline** nature of an IPS.

---

### 4.2 IPS Deployment Placement Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INET[Internet Edge Router] --> FW1[Perimeter Firewall]
    FW1 --> IPS1[IPS Inline Placement 1 DMZ Boundary]
    IPS1 --> DMZ[DMZ Web Mail DNS Servers]
    IPS1 --> FW2[Internal Core Firewall]
    FW2 --> IPS2[IPS Inline Placement 2 Core Switch Uplink]
    IPS2 --> LAN[Internal LAN Workstations and Servers]
    IPS3[Host IPS on Server] -.-> LAN
    SIEM[(SIEM Central Log Server)] --- IPS1
    SIEM --- IPS2
    SIEM --- IPS3
```

**Interpretation:**
- **IPS1** protects the **DMZ** (internet-facing servers).
- **IPS2** protects the **internal LAN** (lateral movement defense).
- **IPS3** is a **Host-based IPS (HIPS)** installed on critical servers.
- All three feed logs to a **central SIEM** for correlation — this is the modern **defense-in-depth** posture.

---

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Packet Handling Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Packet Arrives at IPS] --> P1{Is it malformed or fails protocol decode?}
    P1 -- YES --> P2[ACTION DROP and Log Protocol Anomaly]
    P1 -- NO --> P3{Does it match any signature in the rule database?}
    P3 -- YES --> P4[ACTION DROP or REJECT per rule action]
    P3 -- NO --> P5{Does it deviate from statistical baseline by more than 3 sigma?}
    P5 -- YES --> P6[ACTION Throttle or Quarantine and Alert]
    P5 -- NO --> P7{Does it violate the protocol state machine?}
    P7 -- YES --> P8[ACTION TCP RESET and Drop]
    P7 -- NO --> P9[ACTION FORWARD packet to destination]
    P9 --> P10[Update session state and forward]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) with respect to deployment mode, latency, and failure semantics.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IDS | IPS |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Deployment Mode** | Passive (tap/SPAN) — receives a *copy* of traffic. | Inline — traffic *physically* passes through it. |
| **Action on Attack** | Alerts only; cannot block. | Detects **and** actively blocks/prevents. |
| **Latency Tolerance** | High (not in data path). | Very low (microseconds) — sits in the data path. |
| **Failure Mode** | Failure = no alerts, no traffic impact. | Failure = traffic disruption; hence *fail-open* (bypass) or *fail-closed* is required. |
| **Use Case** | Post-event forensics, monitoring. | Real-time enforcement at network choke points. |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any **four** response/prevention mechanisms used by an IPS and state one limitation of signature-based detection.*

**Model Answer:**

*Four IPS response mechanisms:*
1. **Packet Drop** — discards malicious packet silently.
2. **TCP Reset (RST)** — injects a forged RST to tear down the session.
3. **Shun / Block** — blacklists the source IP for a defined period.
4. **Quarantine** (Host IPS) — isolates the compromised endpoint from the network.

*Limitation of signature-based detection:*
It is **ineffective against zero-day attacks** because no pre-existing signature exists. It also requires *constant signature updates*; otherwise, the system quickly becomes outdated as new attack variants emerge.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)** — *IPS Detection Techniques*

### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified Past Pattern]**
*Explain the **three primary detection techniques** used in an Intrusion Prevention System: signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis. For each, state one strength and one weakness.*

**Model Answer:**

#### 1. Signature-Based (Misuse) Detection
- **How it works:** Compares observed traffic against a database of known attack patterns (signatures). Each signature is a rule with a pattern (string, regex, byte sequence) and an action.
- **Example:** Snort rule — `alert tcp any any → any 80 (msg:"SQL Injection Attempt"; content:"' OR 1=1 --"; sid:1000001;)`.
- **Strength:** Very **low false positive rate**; excellent for known attacks.
- **Weakness:** **Zero-day blind** — cannot detect attacks not in the signature database; requires regular updates.

#### 2. Anomaly-Based Detection
- **How it works:** Builds a baseline model of "normal" behavior (statistical, ML-based) and flags deviations beyond a threshold (e.g., Z-score > 3).
- **Example:** A sudden spike in outbound DNS traffic (DNS tunneling), unusual login time, or abnormal payload entropy.
- **Strength:** Can detect **previously unseen (zero-day)** attacks and insider threats.
- **Weakness:** **Higher false positive rate** because legitimate-but-unusual behavior (e.g., a backup job running at 2 AM) is also flagged.

#### 3. Stateful Protocol Analysis (Deep Packet Inspection)
- **How it works:** Reconstructs the **state machine** of network protocols (TCP, HTTP, DNS, FTP) and validates every packet against the expected transition. Detects violations like *TCP FIN with no prior SYN* or *HTTP request smuggling*.
- **Strength:** Detects attacks that **abuse protocol semantics** and would evade simple string matching.
- **Weakness:** **Computationally expensive**; needs up-to-date protocol decoders; can be evaded by encryption.

> **Conclusion:** Modern IPS engines use a **hybrid** of all three to balance detection rate and false positives.

---

### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified Past Pattern]**
*An IPS in inline mode recorded the following performance during a 24-hour test window against 50,000 sessions: TP = 4,750, FN = 250, FP = 300, TN = 44,700. Compute the **Detection Rate**, **False Positive Rate**, **Accuracy**, and **F1-Score**. Comment on the operational suitability of the IPS.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Verify the total:**
$$
TP + FN + FP + TN = 4750 + 250 + 300 + 44700 = 50{,}000 \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Detection Rate (DR / Recall):**

$$
\text{DR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{4750}{4750 + 250} = \frac{4750}{5000} = 0.95 = 95\%
$$

**Step 3 — False Positive Rate (FPR):**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{300}{300 + 44700} = \frac{300}{45000} = 0.00667 = 0.67\%
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy (ACC):**

$$
\text{ACC} = \frac{TP + TN}{50000} = \frac{4750 + 44700}{50000} = \frac{49450}{50000} = 0.989 = 98.9\%
$$

**Step 5 — Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{4750}{4750 + 300} = \frac{4750}{5050} = 0.9406 = 94.06\%
$$

**Step 6 — F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot \text{DR}}{P + \text{DR}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9406 \cdot 0.95}{0.9406 + 0.95} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8936}{1.8906} = 0.9453 \approx 94.53\%
$$

**Step 7 — Operational Commentary:**
- DR = 95% → catches most attacks.
- FPR = 0.67% → very low false alarm rate.
- F1 = 94.53% → excellent balance.
- **Verdict:** This IPS is **operationally suitable** for production deployment in a high-throughput enterprise environment.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** — *IPS Mechanisms and Deployment*

### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Past Pattern]**
*Explain the **inline deployment architecture** of an IPS. With a neat block diagram, describe the **fail-open vs fail-closed** design choices and the role of **Bypass Switches** in high-availability deployments.*

**Model Answer:**

#### Inline Deployment
In **inline mode**, the IPS is placed *physically* in the network data path — typically between the perimeter firewall and the core switch. All traffic to/from the protected segment *must* traverse the IPS. This is the only mode in which the IPS can **actively prevent** intrusions (drop, reset, shun).

#### Fail-Open vs Fail-Closed
- **Fail-Open:** If the IPS crashes/power-fails, traffic is **automatically bypassed** through a hardware relay so that the network remains available. *Risk:* the protected segment is exposed until the IPS recovers.
- **Fail-Closed:** If the IPS fails, traffic is **completely blocked**. *Risk:* network outage, but **zero exposure**.

#### Role of Bypass Switch (TAP/Bypass)
A **Bypass Switch** is a hardware device placed in series with the IPS. It continuously monitors IPS "heartbeat" signals. On loss of heartbeat:
- It **reroutes** traffic around the failed IPS (fail-open mode).
- It may also signal the IPS to power up or trigger a standby IPS (active-passive HA pair).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NET[Network Ingress] --> BS[Bypass Switch]
    BS -- Normal --> IPS[IPS Inline Appliance]
    IPS -- Clean --> BS
    BS -- Forward --> OUT[Egress to LAN]
    BS -. Heartbeat Loss .-> BS
```

**Conclusion:** High-availability enterprise deployments use a **bypass switch in fail-open mode + active-passive IPS pair** to balance security and availability.

---

### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Past Pattern]**
*Describe any **four IPS response/prevention mechanisms** in detail. For each, mention the **OSI layer** it operates at and a **real-world attack scenario** where it is the most effective response.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Mechanism | OSI Layer | Description | Real-World Scenario |
|---|-----------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Packet Drop** | L2–L7 | The IPS silently discards the offending packet; sender times out. | Blocking a single malicious HTTP request containing a SQL injection payload. |
| 2 | **TCP Reset (RST)** | L4 (Transport) | The IPS spoofs and sends a TCP RST to both client and server, abruptly closing the connection. | Terminating an active C2 (Command-and-Control) beacon session. |
| 3 | **Shun / Block** | L3 (Network) | The source IP is added to a temporary Access Control List (ACL) for N seconds. | Stopping a brute-force SSH attack from a single IP. |
| 4 | **Quarantine (HIPS)** | L2 (Data Link) | The compromised host is moved to a quarantine VLAN via 802.1X / NAC. | Isolating a laptop infected with ransomware before it spreads laterally. |

> Each mechanism represents a different *intensity* of response — from surgical (drop) to catastrophic (quarantine). IPS policies are tuned to choose the *least disruptive* effective response.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS:** Writing *"IPS sends an alert"* — **wrong**. An IPS *blocks*. This single error can cost **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write **$\mu s$ (microseconds)** for IPS latency, **Gbps** for throughput, and **% (percent)** for DR/FPR. Examiners deduct marks for missing units.
> 3. **Signature-based limitations:** A common trap is saying "signature-based is slow." It is *not* slow — it is **fast but blind to zero-days**. Use the correct phrasing.
> 4. **Numerical slips:** When computing F1-Score, students often forget the **harmonic mean** and use arithmetic mean. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Diagram omission:** KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** specifically for diagrams. Always include a *neatly labeled* block diagram of the IPS architecture or deployment.
> 6. **Inline vs Passive confusion:** Always clarify that **only inline IPS can prevent**; passive deployment reduces the system to IDS functionality.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **IPS = Active + Inline** (key differentiator from IDS).
- Three primary **detection techniques**: **Signature**, **Anomaly**, **Stateful Protocol Analysis**; modern systems are **hybrid**.
- Four core **prevention mechanisms**: **Drop**, **TCP Reset**, **Shun**, **Quarantine**.
- **Inline deployment** is mandatory for active prevention; **Bypass Switches** provide HA.
- **DR** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, **FPR** = $\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$, **F1** = harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **Z-score > 3σ** is the standard anomaly threshold.
- **Snort rule actions:** `alert, log, pass, drop, reject, sdrop` (memorize for direct questions).
- **Fail-Open** = availability-first; **Fail-Closed** = security-first.
- **Defense-in-Depth** architecture: Perimeter IPS + Core IPS + Host IPS + SIEM.
- **Snort**, **Suricata**, **Cisco Firepower**, **Palo Alto Networks** are industry-standard IPS examples.
- Always write **units** in numerical answers; always include a **diagram** in 7/14-mark answers.
- **Zero-day attacks** are best handled by **anomaly / behavioral** detection, not signatures.
- **Shannon entropy** is a high-yield concept for detecting **encrypted C2 channels** and **DNS tunneling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deployment Strategies for IPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deployment Strategies for IPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is an active network security control that sits **in-line** with traffic flow, performing real-time packet inspection, attack signature matching, protocol anomaly detection, and policy enforcement, while simultaneously **dropping**, **resetting**, **blocking**, or **re-routing** malicious traffic before it reaches the protected asset.

**Deployment Strategy** refers to the architectural placement, operational mode (Inline vs Passive), sensor type (Network-based NIPS, Host-based HIPS, Wireless WIPS, Network Behavior Analysis NBA), and fail-open/fail-closed behavior chosen when positioning an IPS within the network topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *An IPS is differentiated from an IDS by its in-band operational position and active response capability. The deployment strategy determines visibility, performance impact, single-point-of-failure risk, and detection efficacy.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Security Checkpoint

Imagine a high-security airport terminal. There are **two ways** to install a security scanner:

| Analogy Element | Real-World Mapping |
|---|---|
| 👮 **Security Gate (Inline)** | IPS — every passenger MUST pass through; scanner can stop & reject threats |
| 📷 **CCTV Camera (Passive)** | IDS — camera only watches; threat must be intercepted elsewhere |
| 🚪 **Door that stays open if power fails (Fail-Open)** | Network traffic continues even if IPS dies |
| 🚪 **Door that stays locked if power fails (Fail-Closed)** | All traffic blocked if IPS dies (high security, high downtime) |
| 🏢 **Guard at the building entrance (Host-based)** | HIPS — protects one specific host |
| 🛣️ **Highway patrol camera (Network-based)** | NIPS — protects the whole network segment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Unlike an IDS (which is a *silent observer* like a CCTV), an IPS is an *active bouncer* — traffic physically cannot bypass it.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Expected operational hours between IPS hardware/software failures. Industry standard: **50,000–100,000 hours**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average recovery time after a failure. Typical: **2–24 hours**.
- **Inline Latency:** Added packet delay, typically **< 1 ms** for enterprise NIPS.
- **Throughput:** Maximum inspected bandwidth — ranging from **100 Mbps to 100 Gbps** in modern systems.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Legitimate traffic incorrectly blocked. Target: **< 0.1%**.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR):** Malicious traffic incorrectly passed. Target: **< 0.01%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of IPS Deployment Strategies

### 2.1.1 By Sensor Location (KTU High-Priority)

1. **Network-Based IPS (NIPS):** Appliance placed at network choke points (perimeter, data center core, DMZ).
2. **Host-Based IPS (HIPS):** Software agent installed on the protected endpoint (server, workstation).
3. **Wireless IPS (WIPS):** Sensors monitoring 802.11 (Wi-Fi) RF spectrum for rogue APs, evil twin attacks.
4. **Network Behavior Analysis (NBA) / Anomaly-Based IPS:** Detects traffic-flow deviations (DDoS, worm propagation) using statistical baselines.

### 2.1.2 By Operational Mode

| Mode | Position | Active Response | Failure Behavior | Visibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inline (In-Band)** | Traffic MUST traverse IPS | Yes — drop/reset/block | Fail-Open or Fail-Closed | Layer 2–7 |
| **Tap (Passive Copy)** | SPAN port / TAP mirror | No — alert only (IDS-like) | N/A (passive) | Layer 2–7 |
| **Inline-Tap (Hybrid)** | Traffic traverses IPS, copy mirrored | Yes + off-box analysis | Configurable | Layer 2–7 |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Inline Deployment

**Why inline?** Because prevention requires the ability to stop packets *before* they reach the victim. An IPS can only drop a packet if it physically lies on the data path.

**How it works (operational sequence):**

1. **Packet enters IPS ingress port** (Layer 2 switch port).
2. **Layer 2–7 deep packet inspection (DPI)** is performed.
3. **Signature matching** compares payload against attack rule database (e.g., Snort, Suricata).
4. **Anomaly detection** checks deviations from protocol RFC and traffic baseline.
5. **Decision engine** triggers one of:
   - `Permit` → forward packet
   - `Drop` → silently discard
   - `Reject` → send TCP RST / ICMP unreachable
   - `Block` → add IP to blacklist (timer-based)
6. **Logging & alert generation** forwarded to SIEM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inline sensors are Layer-2 bridge devices (Bump-in-the-Wire).** They have **no IP address on the inline interface** — this is critical to avoid becoming an attack target.

## 2.3 Fail-Open vs Fail-Closed

| Behavior | Mechanism | Use Case | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fail-Open** | Bypass relay (optical/mechanical) closes internal bypass when power fails → traffic flows unsensed | Latency-critical links (VoIP, trading) | IPS offline = exposed network |
| **Fail-Closed** | Relay opens → traffic stops | High-security enclaves (military, SCADA) | Single IPS failure = network outage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Concept:** Most enterprise NIPS support **dual-bypass NICs** — pairs of NICs that physically short together on power loss, providing hardware-level fail-open.

## 2.4 Deployment Placement Topologies

### 2.4.1 Perimeter / Edge Placement
- Positioned between firewall and Internet edge router.
- Inspects inbound attacks (SQLi, RCE, DoS) and outbound C2 traffic.

### 2.4.2 Internal / Core Placement
- Behind internal firewall, before critical server farm.
- Catches **lateral movement** post-initial breach.

### 2.4.3 DMZ Placement
- In front of public-facing servers (Web, Mail, DNS).
- Protects high-value services with custom signatures.

### 2.4.4 Hybrid Distributed
- Multiple sensors coordinated by central management (e.g., Cisco Firepower Management Center, TippingPoint SMS).
- Combines NIPS + HIPS + WIPS visibility.

## 2.5 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Network Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless ratio (e.g., 0.99999 = "five nines") |
| **Annual Downtime** | $D_{annual} = (1 - A) \times 8760$ | Hours/year |
| **Inline Latency Budget** | $L_{total} = L_{wire} + L_{IPS} + L_{queue}$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| **Wire Delay** | $L_{wire} = \dfrac{Distance}{2 \times 10^8 \, m/s}$ | seconds |
| **Maximum Concurrent Sessions** | $S_{max} = \dfrac{Throughput \times Avg\_Packet\_Size}{8 \times Avg\_Session\_Duration}$ | Sessions |
| **Drop Probability (Queuing)** | $P_{drop} = \left( \dfrac{\rho^C}{1 - \rho} \right) \cdot C$ where $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ | Probability |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Ratio |
| **Detection Rate (Recall)** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Ratio |
| **Precision** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Ratio |
| **Bypass Switching Time** | $T_{bypass}$ (hardware relay) | 5–50 ms |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing these formulas in your KTU exam, never use `|` (vertical bar) inside the table. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Finance / Banking:** Fail-open IPS with sub-millisecond latency to prevent trading-floor disruption.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Inline NIPS at EMR database ingress to prevent ransomware encryption.
- **Industrial Control (IEC 62443):** Fail-closed IPS between corporate IT and OT/SCADA network.
- **Cloud (AWS, Azure):** Host-based IPS (e.g., Trend Micro, CrowdStrike) inside VM instances; inline NIPS via cloud-native NGFW (AWS Network Firewall, Azure Firewall Premium).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Network Availability (A) from MTBF / MTTR

**Goal:** Prove that availability of an inline IPS affects network uptime.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair (hours)

**Step 2 — Total operational cycle.**
The system alternates between **uptime** and **downtime**.

$$
T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

**Step 3 — Fraction of time the system is operational.**

$$
A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage ("nines").**

$$
A_\% = A \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Annual downtime calculation.**

$$
D_{annual} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

### Worked Example
An enterprise NIPS has $MTBF = 100{,}000$ hours and $MTTR = 4$ hours.

$$
A = \frac{100{,}000}{100{,}000 + 4} = \frac{100{,}000}{100{,}004} \approx 0.99996
$$

$$
D_{annual} = (1 - 0.99996) \times 8760 = 0.00004 \times 8760 \approx 0.35 \text{ hours} \approx 21 \text{ minutes}
$$

This is a **"four-and-a-half nines"** availability — acceptable for a fail-open perimeter IPS.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Sessions per IPS Throughput

**Step 1 — Define packet and session parameters.**
- $B$ = IPS throughput (bits per second)
- $S$ = average packet size (bits)
- $T_s$ = average session duration (seconds)

**Step 2 — Packets processed per second.**

$$
P = \frac{B}{S}
$$

**Step 3 — Sessions per packet ratio.**
Assume an average session = $N$ packets.

$$
S_{max} = \frac{P}{N} = \frac{B}{S \cdot N}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of session duration.**
For an average flow rate $R$ bits/s per session:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{B}{R \cdot T_s}
$$

### Worked Example
IPS throughput $B = 10$ Gbps $= 10^{10}$ bps, avg flow rate $R = 100$ kbps $= 10^5$ bps, avg session $T_s = 60$ s.

$$
S_{max} = \frac{10^{10}}{10^5 \times 60} = \frac{10^{10}}{6 \times 10^6} = 1666 \text{ concurrent sessions}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Deployment Mode

| Decision Criterion | Inline (In-Band) | Tap (Passive) | Hybrid (Inline-Tap) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requires active blocking? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (alert only) | ✅ Yes |
| Risk of network outage on IPS failure? | ⚠️ High (mitigated by fail-open) | ✅ None | ⚠️ High |
| Can detect zero-day anomalies? | ✅ Yes (with anomaly engine) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Off-box forensics capture? | ❌ No (inline forwards/drops) | ✅ Yes (full copy) | ✅ Yes |
| Throughput bottleneck risk? | ⚠️ Yes (must match link speed) | ✅ No (passive) | ⚠️ Yes |
| Cost (CAPEX) | 💰💰💰 High | 💰 Low | 💰💰 Medium |
| Typical placement | Perimeter, DMZ, Core | SPAN aggregation switches | Critical segments |
| Recommended for | Latency-tolerant, security-critical | Forensic / compliance | SOC + NOC dual purpose |

## 3.4 Decision Algorithm: Where to Deploy NIPS

```
IF (traffic_volume < IPS_throughput × 0.7)
   IF (mission_critical == TRUE)
        → INLINE + FAIL-CLOSED
   ELSE
        → INLINE + FAIL-OPEN
   END
ELSE
   → UPGRADE IPS THROUGHPUT or use TAP
END

IF (requires_lateral_movement_detection == TRUE)
   → INTERNAL CORE PLACEMENT
ELSE
   → PERIMETER PLACEMENT
END

IF (wireless_environment == TRUE)
   → ADD WIPS sensor overlay
END
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IPS Deployment Topology — Enterprise Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet[Internet / External Network]
    edgeRouter[Edge Router]
    fw[Perimeter Firewall]
    ips1[NIPS - Perimeter Inline]
    core[Core Layer-3 Switch]
    ips2[NIPS - Internal Core]
    dmzfw[DMZ Firewall]
    webSrv[Web Server Farm]
    mailSrv[Mail Server]
    userSwitch[User Access Switch]
    userPC[User Workstations]
    wipsCtrl[WIPS Controller]
    ap1[Corporate AP]
    mgmt[IPS Management / SIEM]
    hipSrv[HIPS Agent on Server]
    hidsPC[HIPS Agent on Laptop]

    internet --> edgeRouter --> fw --> ips1 --> core
    core --> dmzfw --> webSrv
    core --> dmzfw --> mailSrv
    core --> ips2 --> userSwitch --> userPC
    ap1 -.wireless.-> wipsCtrl
    ips1 -.telemetry.-> mgmt
    ips2 -.telemetry.-> mgmt
    wipsCtrl -.alerts.-> mgmt
    webSrv -.host agent.-> hipSrv
    userPC -.host agent.-> hidsPC
```

## 4.2 Inline vs Tap Mode Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INLINE[Inline In-Band Mode]
        i1[Source Host] -->|traffic MUST pass| i2[NIPS Engine]
        i2 -->|permit/drop| i3[Destination Host]
        i2 -.bypass relay.-> i2b[Fail-Open Bridge]
    end

    subgraph TAPMODE[Passive Tap Mode]
        t1[Source Host] -->|original flow| t2[Destination Host]
        t1 -.mirror copy.-> t3[Network TAP / SPAN]
        t3 --> t4[IPS - Monitor Only]
    end
```

## 4.3 IPS Placement Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Protected Asset] --> B{Need Active Blocking?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Criticality Level}
    B -- No --> D[Deploy IDS / Tap Mode]
    C -- High --> E[Inline + Fail-Closed]
    C -- Medium --> F[Inline + Fail-Open + Bypass]
    C -- Low --> G[Inline + Fail-Open]
    E --> H{Internal Lateral Movement Risk?}
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Add Internal Core NIPS]
    H -- No --> J[Single Perimeter NIPS]
    I --> K{Wireless Network Present?}
    J --> K
    K -- Yes --> L[Deploy WIPS Overlay]
    K -- No --> M[Done]
    L --> M
    D --> M
```

## 4.4 Hybrid Distributed IPS Architecture (SOC-Centric)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    soc[SOC / SIEM Platform]
    mgr[Central IPS Manager]

    nipsP[NIPS - Perimeter]
    nipsC[NIPS - Data Center Core]
    wips[WIPS Sensors]
    hipSrv[HIPS - Servers]
    hipPC[HIPS - Endpoints]

    soc <-->|syslog/NetFlow| mgr
    mgr --- nipsP
    mgr --- nipsC
    mgr --- wips
    mgr --- hipSrv
    mgr --- hipPC

    nipsP -.inline.-> fwA[Perimeter FW]
    nipsC -.inline.-> coreA[Core Switch]
    wips -.air monitor.-> air[RF Spectrum]
    hipSrv -.syscall hook.-> srvA[App Server]
    hipPC -.file/syscall.-> epA[Endpoint]
```

## 4.5 Inline Inspection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Packet In] --> p2[L2 Decapsulation]
    p2 --> p3[L3 IP Validation]
    p3 --> p4[L4 TCP State Tracking]
    p4 --> p5[L7 Deep Packet Inspection]
    p5 --> p6[Signature Match]
    p5 --> p7[Anomaly Score]
    p6 --> p8{Decision Engine}
    p7 --> p8
    p8 -->|Permit| p9[Forward]
    p8 -->|Drop| p10[Silently Discard]
    p8 -->|Reject| p11[Send TCP RST]
    p8 -->|Block| p12[Blacklist IP]
    p8 -.log.-> p13[SIEM Alert]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Inline IPS deployment and Passive Tap deployment. When would you choose each?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inline IPS | Passive Tap (IDS mode) |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic Path | Must traverse IPS | Mirror copy only |
| Active Response | Can drop, reset, block | Alert only — cannot block |
| Failure Impact | Single point of failure (mitigated by bypass) | None (passive observation) |
| Latency Added | Yes (sub-ms typical) | No |

**Choice Criterion (1 mark):** Use **inline** when active prevention of attacks is required (perimeter, DMZ). Use **tap/IDS** when only monitoring, forensics, or compliance visibility is needed without risking network availability.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the difference between Fail-Open and Fail-Closed modes in an inline IPS. Give one example scenario for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Fail-Open:** On IPS failure, the internal bypass relay closes, and traffic continues flowing **unsensed**. *(1 mark)*
- **Fail-Closed:** On IPS failure, traffic is **blocked**; network segment becomes unavailable. *(1 mark)*
- **Fail-Open Example:** Trading-floor network where continuity > inspection. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Fail-Closed Example:** SCADA/ICS network where compromise > downtime. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the different **deployment strategies for a Network-based IPS (NIPS)** in an enterprise network. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each placement location with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** Compare and contrast **Host-based IPS (HIPS)** with **Network-based IPS (NIPS)**. Under what circumstances would an organization deploy both together in a hybrid model? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a):**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
A NIPS deployment strategy is determined by organizational security policy, network architecture, performance budget, and the threat model. The three primary placement locations are: *Perimeter, Internal Core, and DMZ.*

**2. Perimeter Deployment (2 marks):**
- **Position:** Between firewall and Internet edge router.
- **Function:** Inspects inbound (SQLi, buffer overflow, DoS) and outbound (C2 beaconing, data exfiltration) traffic.
- **Advantages:** First line of defense; protects against external attackers; centralized visibility.
- **Limitations:** Cannot detect internal lateral movement; encrypted traffic blind-spot.

**3. Internal / Core Deployment (2 marks):**
- **Position:** Behind internal firewall, before critical server segments.
- **Function:** Detects insider threats, lateral movement (Pass-the-Hash, ransomware spread), East-West traffic.
- **Advantages:** Catches post-breach activity invisible at perimeter.
- **Limitations:** Higher cost (multiple sensors); performance impact on high-speed core links.

**4. DMZ Deployment (1 mark):**
- **Position:** In front of public-facing servers (Web, Mail, DNS).
- **Function:** Custom signatures for application-layer attacks (XSS, CSRF, SMTP relay abuse).
- **Advantages:** Tailored detection rules; protects customer-facing assets.
- **Limitations:** Does not protect internal assets.

**5. Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the layered perimeter–DMZ–core–user topology with NIPS placed at each segment.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b):**

| Parameter | HIPS | NIPS |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Installed on host (OS-level) | Inline appliance on network segment |
| Visibility | Host syscalls, file system, registry, processes | Network packets, flows, protocols |
| Encrypted Traffic | Can inspect after TLS termination at host | Blind (cannot decrypt) |
| Resource Usage | Consumes host CPU/RAM | None on host |
| Coverage | Per-host | Per-segment / per-flow |
| Failure Impact | Only that host at risk | Whole segment affected (fail-open needed) |

**Hybrid Deployment Scenario (2 marks):**
An organization should deploy **both** when:
- The network has **encrypted traffic (TLS 1.3)** — HIPS handles post-decryption inspection at endpoint.
- **BYOD** endpoints are present — NIPS covers the network; HIPS protects sensitive corporate data on devices.
- **Insider threat** is a concern — HIPS detects host-level behavior (USB insertion, process injection); NIPS correlates network anomalies.
- A **defense-in-depth** posture is mandated by compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q.B (a)** Describe the **Inline In-Band deployment mode** of an IPS. Explain the role of **bypass relays**, **fail-open vs fail-closed behavior**, and how the IPS performs Layer 2–7 deep packet inspection. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** An enterprise NIPS has **MTBF = 80,000 hours** and **MTTR = 6 hours**. Calculate: **(i)** System availability ratio and percentage; **(ii)** Expected annual downtime in hours and minutes. Comment on the suitability of this IPS for a **fail-open** perimeter deployment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a):**

**1. Inline In-Band Mode Defined (1 mark):**
