$$\text{Odds} = \frac{P(\text{Attack} \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(\text{Normal} \mid \mathbf{x})}$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE — it is a high-yield KTU Board Examination reference.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Symbol Notes | Used For |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | Sample Mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | First moment | Baseline profile |
| 2 | Sample Variance | $s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Second central moment | Dispersion baseline |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ | Same units as $x$ | Threshold tuning |
| 4 | Univariate Gaussian | $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2 / 2\sigma^2}$ | $\mu, \sigma^2$ | 1-D anomaly scoring |
| 5 | Multivariate Gaussian | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ PD | Multi-feature IDS |
| 6 | Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | Reduced to $\|\mathbf{x}\|^2$ if $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{I}$ | Anomaly scoring |
| 7 | Chi-square Threshold | $\tau^2 = \chi^2_{d, 1-\alpha}$ | $d$ = dimensions | Setting detection cut-off |
| 8 | KDE Estimator | $\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n h^d}\sum K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$ | $h$ = bandwidth | Non-parametric density |
| 9 | Markov Joint Probability | $P(S) = P(s_1)\prod_{t} P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t)$ | $S$ = sequence | Sequence anomaly |
| 10 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(A \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x}\mid A)P(A)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | $P(A)$ = prior | Bayesian IDS |
| 11 | Information Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits | Entropy-based IDS |
| 12 | KL Divergence | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum p_i \log(p_i / q_i)$ | Non-negative | Distribution shift |
| 13 | Grubbs' Statistic | $G = \frac{\max \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{s}$ | Outlier test | Univariate outlier |
| 14 | Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) | $S_t = \max(0,\, S_{t-1} + x_t - \mu_0 - k)$ | $k$ = slack | Change detection |
| 15 | Z-Score | $z_i = (x_i - \bar{x}) / s$ | Standard units | Outlier flagging |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Statistical anomaly detection is deployed across diverse engineering fields:

- **Network IDS** — Snort and early Bro/Zeek systems used statistical thresholds for traffic spikes.
- **Smart Grid Security** — Detecting abnormal power-consumption patterns that may indicate SCADA attacks.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)** — CUSUM charts flag gradual drift in sensor readings indicating stealthy attacks.
- **Cybersecurity Operations Centers (SOCs)** — Bayesian spam filters (the classic example) are deployed at scale.
- **Aerospace Telemetry** — Mahalanobis distance monitors rocket-engine sensor health in real time.
- **Medical IoT** — Statistical baselines detect pacemaker anomalies.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places statistical methods *before* machine learning methods (Module 3+) because they offer **interpretability, mathematical rigor, and low computational cost** — properties still highly valued in defense and critical-infrastructure deployments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE) of Gaussian Parameters

The derivation below is **mandatory reading** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE — numericals of this form appear regularly.

**Problem Statement:** Given $n$ i.i.d. samples $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ drawn from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, derive the MLE estimates $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}$.

**Step 1 — Write the likelihood function.**  
The likelihood is the joint probability of observing all $n$ samples, assuming independence:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid X) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Gaussian PDF.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid X) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constant term.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid X) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \prod_{i=1}^{n} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert the product of exponentials into a single exponential.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid X) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2 \right)$$

**Step 5 — Take the log-likelihood** (monotonic transformation; maximizer is preserved).

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = \log \mathcal{L} = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\log(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \cdot 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)(-1) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}$.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \bar{x}$$

**Step 8 — Differentiate the log-likelihood w.r.t. $\sigma^2$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}$.**

$$\frac{n}{2\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}} = \frac{1}{2(\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}})^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

$$\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** The MLE estimate of variance uses $n$ in the denominator, but the **unbiased** estimate uses $n-1$. The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests this distinction — **always specify which is being asked**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Mahalanobis Anomaly Detection

**Problem (KTU-Style Numerical):**  
A network IDS monitors **two features**: $x_1$ = packet size (bytes) and $x_2$ = inter-arrival time (ms). From 1000 normal packets, the following statistics are computed:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 500 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 100^2 & 50 \\ 50 & 5^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10000 & 50 \\ 50 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$

A new observation arrives: $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} = (800,\, 35)^\top$. Use $\alpha = 0.01$ to decide whether the observation is anomalous.

**Step 1 — Compute the deviation vector.**

$$\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 800 - 500 \\ 35 - 20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 300 \\ 15 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverse of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.**

The determinant is:

$$\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = (10000)(25) - (50)(50) = 250000 - 2500 = 247500$$

The cofactor matrix is:

$$\text{adj}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = \begin{bmatrix} 25 & -50 \\ -50 & 10000 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \frac{1}{247500}\begin{bmatrix} 25 & -50 \\ -50 & 10000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Mahalanobis distance squared.**

$$D_M^2 = (\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

First, compute $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$:

$$\frac{1}{247500}\begin{bmatrix} 25 & -50 \\ -50 & 10000 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 300 \\ 15 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{247500}\begin{bmatrix} 7500 - 750 \\ -15000 + 150000 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{247500}\begin{bmatrix} 6750 \\ 135000 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now dot with $(300,\, 15)$:

$$D_M^2 = \frac{1}{247500}\left( 300 \times 6750 + 15 \times 135000 \right) = \frac{1}{247500}\left( 2{,}025{,}000 + 2{,}025{,}000 \right) = \frac{4{,}050{,}000}{247500}$$

$$D_M^2 = 16.36$$

**Step 4 — Look up the chi-square critical value.**

For $d = 2$ dimensions and significance $\alpha = 0.01$:

$$\chi^2_{2,\, 0.99} = 9.21$$

**Step 5 — Apply the decision rule.**

Since $D_M^2 = 16.36 > 9.21 = \tau^2$, we **reject the null hypothesis** and flag the observation as **anomalous**.

> [!NOTE]
> The observation is roughly **1.33 standard deviations** beyond the 99% confidence contour of the training distribution — strong evidence of an attack.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Markov Chain-Based Sequence Anomaly

**Problem:**  
Consider a Markov chain over the alphabet of system calls $\{ \text{open}, \text{read}, \text{write}, \text{close} \}$. From training data, the transition probability matrix $P$ is:

| From \ To | open | read | write | close |
|-----------|------|------|-------|-------|
| open | 0.0 | 0.6 | 0.3 | 0.1 |
| read | 0.0 | 0.4 | 0.5 | 0.1 |
| write | 0.0 | 0.3 | 0.5 | 0.2 |
| close | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.2 | 0.0 |

A new observed sequence: $S = (\text{open}, \text{write}, \text{write}, \text{close})$. Initial state probability: $P(\text{open}) = 1.0$.

**Step 1 — Compute the joint probability.**

$$P(S) = P(\text{open}) \cdot P(\text{write} \mid \text{open}) \cdot P(\text{write} \mid \text{write}) \cdot P(\text{close} \mid \text{write})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute from the matrix.**

$$P(S) = (1.0)(0.3)(0.5)(0.2) = 0.03$$

**Step 3 — Compare with threshold.**  
If $\tau = 0.01$ is the anomaly threshold, then $P(S) = 0.03 > 0.01$, so the sequence is **NOT flagged**. If a different threshold $\tau = 0.05$ is used, the sequence **is** flagged.

## 3.4 Derivation: Why Chi-Square Threshold Works for Mahalanobis

If $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$, then the random variable:

$$Y = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

follows a **chi-square distribution** with $d$ degrees of freedom, denoted $Y \sim \chi^2_d$.

**Why?** Perform an eigendecomposition $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{U}^\top$, where $\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_d)$. Define the standardized vector $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{\Lambda}^{-1/2}\mathbf{U}^\top(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$. Then $Y = \|\mathbf{z}\|^2 = \sum_{i=1}^d z_i^2$, where each $z_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$. By definition, the sum of $d$ independent squared standard normals is $\chi^2_d$.

Therefore, the threshold $\tau^2 = \chi^2_{d, 1-\alpha}$ guarantees that exactly $1-\alpha$ of normal observations will be correctly accepted (i.e., the false-positive rate is bounded by $\alpha$).

## 3.5 Summary: Analytical Matrix for Quick Recall

| Step # | What To Do | Why It Matters |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Collect training data $X$ of normal traffic | Foundation of any statistical model |
| 2 | Choose feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ | Determines what anomalies are detectable |
| 3 | Compute $\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}$ via MLE | Unbiased estimates of distribution |
| 4 | Validate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}$ is PD (eigenvalues $> 0$) | Required for Mahalanobis math |
| 5 | Set threshold $\tau^2 = \chi^2_{d, 1-\alpha}$ | Controls the false-positive rate |
| 6 | For each new $\mathbf{x}$, compute $D_M^2(\mathbf{x})$ | Anomaly score |
| 7 | Compare $D_M^2$ to $\tau^2$; flag if exceeds | Final decision |
| 8 | Periodically retrain $\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ | Adapts to concept drift |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Functional Architecture of a Statistical IDS

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **complete pipeline** of statistical anomaly detection — from raw data ingestion to alert generation. This is the architecture you are expected to draw (or describe) in a KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Ingestion<br>Network Packets / System Calls] --> B[Feature Extraction<br>Packet Size, IAT, Protocol]
    B --> C[Feature Vector x<br>d-dimensional]
    C --> D{Phase?}

    D -- Training --> E[Statistical Model Learning<br>Compute mu and Sigma via MLE]
    E --> F[Model Storage<br>Baseline Profile DB]
    F --> G[Threshold Selection<br>tau squared = chi-square d, 1 minus alpha]
    G --> H[Model Ready]

    D -- Detection --> I[Acquire New Observation x_new]
    I --> J[Compute Mahalanobis Distance<br>Dm squared = x_new minus mu transpose Sigma inverse x_new minus mu]
    J --> K{Compare Dm squared with tau squared}
    K -- Dm squared less than equal to tau squared --> L[Normal Event<br>Log to Audit Trail]
    K -- Dm squared greater than tau squared --> M[Anomaly Detected<br>Trigger Alert]
    M --> N[Incident Response<br>Block, Quarantine, Notify SOC]
    L --> O[Continuous Monitoring Loop]
    N --> O
    O --> I

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style E fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style J fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style M fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style H fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.2 Statistical Methods Taxonomy (Classification Map)

The diagram below classifies the major statistical techniques covered in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[Statistical Anomaly<br>Detection Methods]

    Root --> P[Parametric]
    Root --> NP[Non-Parametric]
    Root --> SP[Stochastic Process]

    P --> P1[Univariate Gaussian<br>Z-Score Test]
    P --> P2[Multivariate Gaussian<br>Mahalanobis Distance]
    P --> P3[Regression-based<br>Residual Analysis]

    NP --> NP1[Histogram-based<br>Density Estimation]
    NP --> NP2[Kernel Density<br>Estimation KDE]
    NP --> NP3[Grubbs Test<br>Dixon Q Test]

    SP --> SP1[Markov Chains<br>Sequence Modeling]
    SP --> SP2[Hidden Markov Models<br>HMM with States]
    SP --> SP3[Time Series<br>ARIMA and CUSUM]
    SP --> SP4[Bayesian Networks<br>Posterior Inference]

    style Root fill:#003366,stroke:#000033,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style NP fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style SP fill:#e5ccff,stroke:#660099
```

## 4.3 Decision Boundary Visualization (Conceptual)

The schematic below illustrates how a 2-D Gaussian decision boundary is shaped by $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. **Use this in your KTU answer scripts to earn full visualization marks.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DS["Decision Space: 2-D Feature Plane"]
        direction TB
        C[Center = Mean Vector mu]
        E1[Inner Ellipse<br>chi-square 2, 0.50 = 1.39<br>50 percent confidence]
        E2[Middle Ellipse<br>chi-square 2, 0.90 = 4.61<br>90 percent confidence]
        E3[Outer Ellipse<br>chi-square 2, 0.99 = 9.21<br>99 percent confidence]
        E4[Outside E3 = ANOMALY ZONE]
    end

    C --- E1
    E1 --- E2
    E2 --- E3
    E3 --- E4

    style C fill:#00cc00,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900
    style E2 fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#996600
    style E3 fill:#ff9966,stroke:#993300
    style E4 fill:#ff3333,stroke:#660000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Real-Time Detection Flowchart (Time-Series)

For time-series / streaming data, statistical IDS follows a **sliding window** protocol:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Streaming Input<br>x1, x2, x3, ... xt] --> B[Sliding Window<br>of size W]
    B --> C[Compute Window Statistics<br>mu_W and sigma_W squared]
    C --> D[Compute Test Statistic<br>Z-Score or CUSUM]
    D --> E{Statistic greater than threshold?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Generate Anomaly Alert]
    E -- No --> G[Update Baseline<br>Exponentially Weighted MA]
    F --> H[Slide Window by 1]
    G --> H
    H --> A

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style F fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** Always label your diagrams with **explicit mathematical symbols** (e.g., $\mu$, $\Sigma$, $\chi^2_{d,1-\alpha}$). Examiners reward diagrams that bridge the visual and the symbolic — pure boxes-and-arrows without math lose 2–3 marks on a 14-mark question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Define statistical anomaly detection. List any two statistical measures commonly used as anomaly indicators.**

**Model Answer:**

> Statistical anomaly detection is a quantitative technique in intrusion detection that builds a probabilistic model of normal system behavior and flags observations whose probability of occurrence under that model falls below a predefined threshold.

Two commonly used statistical measures are:
1. **Z-Score** $z_i = (x_i - \bar{x})/s$ — measures deviation in standard-deviation units.
2. **Mahalanobis Distance** $D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ — multivariate anomaly score.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** Definition = 1 mark, two measures with formulas = 1 mark each. Always include the formula to be safe.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Explain the role of the chi-square distribution in threshold selection for Mahalanobis-distance-based IDS.**

**Model Answer:**

> When features follow a multivariate Gaussian, the squared Mahalanobis distance $D_M^2(\mathbf{x})$ follows a $\chi^2_d$ distribution with $d$ degrees of freedom. The threshold $\tau^2 = \chi^2_{d, 1-\alpha}$ at significance $\alpha$ (commonly $\alpha = 0.01$) provides a mathematically rigorous false-positive rate guarantee. Observations with $D_M^2 > \tau^2$ are flagged as anomalies.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question has an internal choice. Both alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are given below with full sub-parts and complete model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE) of the parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ of a univariate Gaussian distribution from $n$ i.i.d. samples. (7 Marks)**

**(b) For a 2-D feature space with $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (100, 50)^\top$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 25 & 5 \\ 5 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the Mahalanobis distance of the observation $\mathbf{x} = (110, 60)^\top$. Using $\alpha = 0.05$, state whether the observation is anomalous. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** *(7 marks: Likelihood 1, Log-likelihood 1, ∂/∂μ 2, ∂/∂σ² 2, Final answer 1)*

The likelihood of $n$ i.i.d. samples from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

The log-likelihood is:

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\log(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

Setting $\partial \ell / \partial \mu = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

Setting $\partial \ell / \partial \sigma^2 = 0$:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

**(b) Numerical Computation** *(7 marks: deviation 1, inverse 2, distance 2, decision 2)*

Deviation vector:

$$\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 110 - 100 \\ 60 - 50 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determinant: $\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = 25 \times 16 - 5 \times 5 = 400 - 25 = 375$

Inverse:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \frac{1}{375}\begin{bmatrix} 16 & -5 \\ -5 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$

Mahalanobis distance squared:

$$D_M^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{375}\begin{bmatrix} 16 & -5 \\ -5 & 25 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{375}\left( 10 \times 110 + 10 \times 200 \right) = \frac{3100}{375} = 8.27$$

Critical value: $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.99$.

Since $D_M^2 = 8.27 > 5.99$, the observation is **ANOMALOUS**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Markov-chain-based approach for sequence anomaly detection. How is the joint probability of an observed sequence computed, and how is the anomaly decision made? (7 Marks)**

**(b) The training transition matrix of a Markov chain over system calls $\{A, B, C\}$ is given below. A new sequence $S = (A, C, B, A)$ is observed. Initial state $P(A) = 1$. Test whether $S$ is anomalous using a threshold $\tau = 0.02$. (7 Marks)**

| From \ To | A | B | C |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| A | 0.1 | 0.2 | 0.7 |
| B | 0.3 | 0.3 | 0.4 |
| C | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.2 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory** *(7 marks: chain definition 2, joint prob 2, anomaly rule 2, example 1)*

A Markov chain models a stochastic process where the next state depends only on the current state (Markov property). In IDS, system calls, network states, or command sequences are modeled as Markov chains. The joint probability of a sequence $S = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n)$ is:

$$P(S) = P(s_1) \prod_{t=1}^{n-1} P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t)$$

**Anomaly decision:** If $P(S) < \tau$, the sequence is flagged as anomalous. Low joint probability means the sequence is statistically improbable under the trained model, which often indicates malicious activity (e.g., a buffer overflow exploiting unusual call sequences).

**(b) Numerical** *(7 marks: substitution 3, multiplication 2, comparison 2)*

$$P(S) = P(A) \cdot P(C \mid A) \cdot P(B \mid C) \cdot P(A \mid B)$$

$$= (1.0)(0.7)(0.3)(0.3) = 0.063$$

Since $P(S) = 0.063 > 0.02 = \tau$, the sequence is **NOT anomalous** under the given threshold.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **MLE vs unbiased variance confusion (–2 marks):** Students often write $n$ instead of $n-1$ when asked for the *unbiased* estimator. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **Skipping log-likelihood step in MLE derivation (–2 marks):** Examiners expect the full log-likelihood equation before differentiation. Skipping the log step is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Mahalanobis with Euclidean (–3 marks):** The Mahalanobis formula **must** include $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$. A bare $\|\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}\|$ loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Chi-square degrees of freedom forgotten (–1 mark):** Always write $\chi^2_d$ explicitly where $d$ = number of features.
> 5. **Markov chain order assumption (–1 mark):** State the **first-order Markov assumption** $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, s_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t)$ explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Statistical Methods for Anomaly Detection**

- ✅ Statistical anomaly detection has **two phases**: training (model building) and detection (scoring).
- ✅ The **univariate Gaussian** PDF: $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.
- ✅ The **multivariate Gaussian** PDF uses mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ — must be **positive definite**.
- ✅ **MLE estimate of $\mu$** is the sample mean $\bar{x}$.
- ✅ **MLE estimate of $\sigma^2$** uses denominator $n$; **unbiased** uses $n-1$.
- ✅ **Mahalanobis distance** $D_M^2$ follows a **chi-square distribution** with $d$ degrees of freedom under the null hypothesis.
- ✅ Threshold: $\tau^2 = \chi^2_{d, 1-\alpha}$, where $\alpha$ is the chosen significance (e.g., 0.01 or 0.05).
- ✅ **Z-score** $z = (x - \bar{x})/s$ flags observations beyond $|z| > 2$ or $3$ (the 95% / 99.7% rule).
- ✅ **Markov chain** assumes the first-order property: $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, s_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t)$.
- ✅ **Joint sequence probability** $P(S) = P(s_1) \prod_t P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t)$.
- ✅ **Bayes' theorem** for IDS: $P(\text{Attack} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{Attack}) P(\text{Attack})}{P(\mathbf{x})}$.
- ✅ **KDE** is the go-to non-parametric alternative when Gaussian assumption fails.
- ✅ **Information entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ — high entropy in normal traffic, low entropy in attacks (or vice versa, depending on context).
- ✅ **CUSUM** detects gradual shifts; useful for slow-burn attacks.
- ✅ **Grubbs' test** identifies a single outlier in univariate data.
- ✅ Statistical IDS suffers from **concept drift** — periodic retraining is essential.
- ✅ Always draw the **ROC curve** in 14-mark questions to access full marks.
- ✅ Common KTU pitfalls: MLE vs unbiased variance, missing $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$ in Mahalanobis, forgetting chi-square degrees of freedom, not stating Markov assumption.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark statistical questions, structure your answer as **(i) Theory 4 marks → (ii) Derivation/Algorithm 5 marks → (iii) Numerical/Example 4 marks → (iv) Diagram 1 mark**. This 4-5-4-1 split consistently scores above 90% in KTU valuation reports.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Information and Event Management (SIEM): Introduction to SIEM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Information and Event Management (SIEM): Introduction to SIEM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** is an integrated security management approach that combines **Security Information Management (SIM)** — the long-term collection, storage, analysis, and reporting of log data — with **Security Event Management (SEM)** — the real-time monitoring, correlation, and alerting of events across an organization's information systems.

A SIEM platform is a **centralized software solution** that ingests log and event data from heterogeneous sources (firewalls, IDS/IPS, servers, endpoints, applications, cloud services), normalizes the data into a uniform schema, applies **correlation rules and analytics** to detect suspicious activity, and presents actionable intelligence via **dashboards, alerts, and reports**.

> [!NOTE]
> **SANS Institute Definition (paraphrased):** SIEM is the centralized aggregation of logs and events from network devices, servers, and applications, correlated in real time to detect deviations from policy and to support incident response and compliance reporting.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Hospital ICU Control Room"**
> 
> Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. Each ward (cardiology, neurology, emergency) continuously generates thousands of patient vitals — heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen levels. The hospital's **ICU Control Room** is a single room where:
> 1. All ward vitals are **collected** into one place.
> 2. The data is **standardized** (so a BP reading from a wrist monitor looks the same as one from a bedside monitor).
> 3. A senior doctor **correlates** them — "If heart rate spikes AND oxygen drops AND patient had a prior cardiac history → alert!"
> 4. **Alerts** are raised only when meaningful patterns appear, not on every single beep.
> 5. **Dashboards** show the entire hospital's health at a glance.
> 6. **Reports** are generated daily for administration and regulators.
> 
> In this analogy, the **wards are your network devices, servers, and applications**. The **ICU Control Room is your SIEM**. The **senior doctor is the correlation engine**.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Terminology (KTU High-Yield Terms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following terms appear in **every** KTU question on SIEM. Memorize these verbatim.

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **EPS (Events Per Second)** | The rate at which a SIEM ingests event records; the primary **performance benchmark** for SIEM sizing. |
| **FIM (File Integrity Monitoring)** | A SIEM function that detects unauthorized changes to critical files. |
| **NDR (Network Detection and Response)** | A complementary technology to SIEM, focused on network telemetry. |
| **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation and Response)** | The next evolution after SIEM — automates response actions triggered by SIEM alerts. |
| **UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics)** | A SIEM module that uses ML to baseline "normal" user/host behavior and flag deviations. |
| **MTTD / MTTR** | Mean Time To Detect / Mean Time To Respond — the two KPIs SIEM is judged on. |
| **Log Retention** | Duration logs are stored, often mandated by regulation (e.g., PCI-DSS = **1 year**, HIPAA = **6 years**). |
| **Normalized Event** | A raw log converted to a standardized schema (e.g., CEF, LEEF). |
| **Correlation Rule** | A logical condition (AND/OR/SEQUENCE) that triggers an alert when met. |
| **False Positive** | A benign event incorrectly flagged as malicious — the **#1 SIEM tuning challenge**. |

### 1.4 SIEM vs. Traditional Log Management

> [!NOTE]
> Many students confuse **SIEM** with a plain **Log Server**. They are not the same. The table below is a **favourite KTU question**.

| Feature | Log Management (e.g., syslog server) | SIEM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary purpose | **Storage and retrieval** of logs | **Detection and response** via correlation |
| Real-time correlation | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Alerting | ❌ No (or rudimentary) | ✅ Yes (rule + anomaly based) |
| Dashboards | Basic | Advanced, role-based |
| Compliance reporting | Manual | Automated templates |
| Threat intelligence feeds | ❌ No | ✅ Native integration |
| Incident timeline reconstruction | Limited | Full forensic timeline |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Problems That Forced SIEM into Existence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners often ask:** "Why was SIEM developed when firewalls and IDS already exist?"

In the pre-SIEM era (pre-2005), enterprises faced three critical problems:

1. **Log Sprawl** — A medium enterprise generated **millions of log lines per day** from firewalls, IDS, servers, applications, antivirus, databases, and Active Directory. No human could read them.
2. **Isolated Visibility** — A firewall alert said "blocked port scan"; an IDS alert said "signature match"; a server log said "failed login". **Individually, they looked benign.** The **attack became visible only when correlated** across all three.
3. **Regulatory Pressure** — Regulations like **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX, GDPR** mandated centralized log retention, integrity, and auditable reporting.

SIEM was engineered to solve all three simultaneously.

## 2.2 Core Operational Components (The SIEM Pipeline)

A SIEM operates as a **five-stage pipeline**. Understand each stage — KTU questions frequently test the pipeline.

### Stage 1: Data Collection (Ingestion)
- **Sources:** Firewalls, IDS/IPS, routers/switches, Windows Event Logs, Linux syslog, EDR agents, databases, web servers, cloud services (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log), applications.
- **Methods:** Agent-based (e.g., Splunk Forwarder, WMI), Agentless (syslog forwarding, API polling), NetFlow/IPFIX taps.
- **Volume metric:** Measured in **Events Per Second (EPS)**.

### Stage 2: Normalization (Parsing)
- **Why:** A Windows login failure looks like `4625`; a Linux failure looks like `auth failure`. The SIEM must **convert all events into a common schema** (e.g., CEF — Common Event Format, or LEEF — Log Event Extended Format).
- **Output:** A normalized event has fields like `timestamp`, `source_ip`, `destination_ip`, `user`, `action`, `outcome`, `severity`.

### Stage 3: Aggregation & Enrichment
- **Aggregation:** Combining repeated identical events (e.g., 10,000 failed SSH attempts → one aggregated event of count 10,000).
- **Enrichment:** Adding context — GeoIP lookup, Asset Criticality, User Role, Threat Intelligence feed (is the source IP on a known botnet list?).

### Stage 4: Correlation (The Brain)
- **Rule-based correlation:** `IF (5 failed logins from same IP within 60s) AND (target user is admin) THEN alert "Brute Force Attempt"`.
- **Statistical correlation:** Baseline normal activity, alert on statistical deviations.
- **Cross-device correlation:** Link events from firewall, IDS, and server to reconstruct an attack chain.

### Stage 5: Presentation (Dashboards, Alerts, Reports)
- **Real-time dashboards** for SOC analysts.
- **Alerts** routed to ticketing systems (ServiceNow, Jira) or SOAR platforms.
- **Compliance reports** (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001) generated on demand.
- **Forensic timelines** for incident reconstruction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is your **exam-day reference**. SIEM sizing and performance use these formulas.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| EPS (Events Per Second) | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{Total Events}}{\text{Time Window in Seconds}}$ | $\text{events/s}$; used for **licensing and sizing** |
| Daily Event Volume | $\text{Events/day} = \text{EPS} \times 86400$ | $86400 = 24 \times 60 \times 60$ |
| Storage Required | $\text{Storage} = \text{EPS} \times \text{AvgSize} \times \text{Retention} \times 86400$ | $\text{AvgSize}$ in bytes; **Retention** in days |
| Compression Ratio | Typical SIEM compression = $\mathbf{10:1}$ | Applied before storage |
| MTTD | $\text{MTTD} = \text{Detection Time} - \text{Attack Start Time}$ | Lower is better; **SIEM reduces this**. |
| MTTR | $\text{MTTR} = \text{Resolution Time} - \text{Detection Time}$ | SIEM + SOAR reduces this. |
| Detection Coverage | $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Log Sources Integrated}}{\text{Total Critical Assets}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as **percentage** |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{False Positives}}{\text{Total Alerts}} \times 100\%$ | **Target: < 5%** in mature SIEMs. |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{\text{True Positives}}{\text{False Positives}}$ | Higher is better. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world SOC sizing, the Storage formula above determines whether an organization needs a 10 TB, 100 TB, or petabyte-class SIEM cluster. Mid-sized enterprises typically see **5,000 – 25,000 EPS**; large enterprises exceed **100,000 EPS**.

## 2.4 The "How" — Why Correlation is the Differentiator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A simple log server tells you WHAT happened. A SIEM tells you WHY it matters.**

Consider this real-world attack chain — **a KTU favourite**:

| Step | Device | Event | Severity Alone |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Firewall | Port scan from $203.0.113.45$ | Low |
| 2 | Firewall | One connection opened to port 445 | Low |
| 3 | Windows DC | Failed logon (Event 4625) | Medium |
| 4 | Windows DC | Successful logon (Event 4624) — unusual user "admin_temp" | Medium |
| 5 | EDR | `powershell.exe` spawned by `winword.exe` | High |
| 6 | EDR | Encoded command line (Base64) | Critical |

> **Individually**, steps 1–4 are common and would be ignored. **Correlated together**, the SIEM raises **one high-confidence alert**: "Possible Phishing → Lateral Movement → C2 Establishment" — within seconds.

This is the **fundamental value proposition of SIEM** — the answer to nearly every "Explain the need for SIEM" question in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> SIEM is conceptual, not algebraic. Therefore, this section replaces mathematical derivation with an **analytical matrix** — the *exact* workflow a SIEM follows, with worked numeric examples for the storage and EPS formulas.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The SIEM Operational Workflow

The following matrix is the **canonical answer** to "Describe the working of a SIEM". Memorize the flow direction.

| Pipeline Stage | Input | Process Performed | Output | Real-World Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Collection** | Raw logs from N devices | Forwarding via agent/syslog/API | Raw log stream at $E$ EPS | Vitals arriving at ICU monitors |
| **2. Parsing** | Raw text log | Regex / Grok parser extracts fields | Field-value pairs (CEF/LEEF) | Nurse reading BP and writing it in a standard chart |
| **3. Normalization** | Heterogeneous field names | Mapping to common schema | Standardized event object | All vitals on a single screen |
| **4. Enrichment** | Standardized event | Add GeoIP, threat intel, asset context | Enriched event | Doctor noting "patient travelled abroad" |
| **5. Correlation** | Stream of enriched events | Apply rules / ML models | Correlated incident / alert | Senior doctor recognizing a syndrome |
| **6. Alerting** | Alert object | Severity scoring, deduplication | Ticket in SIEM console | Paging the on-call specialist |
| **7. Reporting** | Indexed events | Aggregation, charting, compliance templates | PDF/HTML report | Daily hospital report to the Ministry |
| **8. Retention** | Indexed events | Compression + archival | Long-term searchable store | Patient history records |

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — SIEM Sizing (EPS & Storage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a classic 7-mark KTU numerical.** Practice this twice.

**Problem:** An organization has **2,000 endpoints**, each generating an average of **3 log events per second** during peak hours. Each event averages **250 bytes**. The SIEM retention policy is **90 days**. Assume a **10:1 compression ratio**. Calculate:
1. Peak EPS
2. Daily event volume
3. Raw uncompressed storage required
4. Actual storage needed after compression

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate Peak EPS**

$$
\text{EPS} = \text{Endpoints} \times \text{EPS per endpoint}
$$

$$
\text{EPS} = 2000 \times 3 = 6000 \; \text{events/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Daily Event Volume**

$$
\text{Events/day} = \text{EPS} \times 86400
$$

$$
\text{Events/day} = 6000 \times 86400 = 518{,}400{,}000 \; \text{events/day}
$$

(That is **518.4 million events per day** — a typical mid-sized enterprise load.)

**Step 3 — Calculate Raw Uncompressed Storage**

$$
\text{Raw Storage (bytes/day)} = \text{Events/day} \times \text{AvgSize}
$$

$$
\text{Raw Storage} = 518{,}400{,}000 \times 250 = 129{,}600{,}000{,}000 \; \text{bytes/day}
$$

Converting to GB (divide by $1024^3$):

$$
\text{Raw Storage} = \frac{129{,}600{,}000{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 120.7 \; \text{GB/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 90-Day Retention (Uncompressed)**

$$
\text{Total Raw Storage} = 120.7 \times 90 = 10{,}863 \; \text{GB} \approx 10.6 \; \text{TB}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 10:1 Compression**

$$
\text{Compressed Storage} = \frac{10.6 \; \text{TB}}{10} = 1.06 \; \text{TB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The SIEM cluster must handle **6,000 EPS peak** and allocate **~1.1 TB** of compressed storage for 90 days. This justifies a 2-node SIEM deployment with hot-warm storage tiers.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Correlation Rule Construction

**Problem:** Design a SIEM correlation rule to detect **SSH brute force** with **subsequent successful login**.

### Solution — Rule Logic (Pseudocode, NOT Python)

The rule has two stages:

**Stage 1 — Detect the brute force pattern:**

> **IF** count of `Event = "SSH Auth Failure"`, `Source IP = X`, within a **5-minute sliding window** $\geq 10$ events, **THEN** raise intermediate event `Possible_BruteForce` tagged to IP X.

**Stage 2 — Detect the successful compromise:**

> **IF** `Possible_BruteForce` exists for IP X **AND** within **10 minutes after** it, an `Event = "SSH Auth Success"` occurs for the same IP X, **THEN** raise **HIGH severity** alert `Confirmed_BruteForce_Compromise` with full context enrichment.

**Why two stages?** A single threshold on failed logins produces many false positives (users forget passwords). A two-stage rule — *failures followed by a success* — drastically reduces false positives and represents a **kill-chain aware** correlation. This is the **core difference** between a naïve log filter and a true SIEM correlation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SIEM vs. Adjacent Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks comparison questions.** This matrix is exam-ready.

| Dimension | SIEM | EDR | NDR | SOAR | Log Management |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Data Source** | Logs from all sources | Endpoint telemetry | Network traffic | Alerts from SIEM/EDR/NDR | Logs |
| **Detection Method** | Rule + correlation | Behavior + signature | Behavior + signature | N/A (executes playbooks) | None |
| **Response Action** | Alerting only | Contain endpoint | Block at network | Automated multi-system response | None |
| **Storage Focus** | Long-term indexed | Short-term on endpoint | Short-term on sensor | N/A | Long-term archive |
| **Typical Vendor** | Splunk, QRadar, ArcSight | CrowdStrike, SentinelOne | Darktrace, Vectra | Palo Alto XSOAR, Splunk SOAR | syslog-ng, Graylog |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern SOC architecture:** SIEM is the **central nervous system**; EDR/NDR are the **senses**; SOAR is the **motor system** that takes action.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level SIEM Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[FW1 Firewall] --> collectorA[SIEM Collector]
    srcB[IDS IPS Sensor] --> collectorA
    srcC[Windows AD Server] --> collectorA
    srcD[Linux Web Server] --> collectorA
    srcE[Cloud AWS CloudTrail] --> collectorA
    srcF[EDR Endpoint Agent] --> collectorA
    collectorA --> parser[Parser and Normalizer]
    parser --> enricher[Enrichment GeoIP ThreatIntel AssetDB]
    enricher --> indexer[Indexed Event Store]
    indexer --> corrEngine[Correlation Engine Rules and ML]
    corrEngine --> alertMgr[Alert Manager and Ticketing]
    corrEngine --> dash[Analyst Dashboard]
    corrEngine --> report[Compliance and Audit Reports]
    indexer --> archive[Long Term Archive 90 days plus]
    alertMgr --> soar[SOAR Orchestrator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows **left → right** from heterogeneous sources into the SIEM. The Collector is the entry point. The Parser/Normalizer converts everything to a common format. The Enricher adds context. The Indexer stores events for fast search. The Correlation Engine is the "brain" that raises alerts. Alerts flow to the Alert Manager and SOAR for response.

## 4.2 SIEM Correlation Workflow (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    e1[Raw Event Arrives] --> e2{Parse Successful}
    e2 -- No --> e3[Quarantine Bad Event]
    e2 -- Yes --> e4[Normalize to CEF Schema]
    e4 --> e5[Enrich with Threat Intel]
    e5 --> e6{Threshold Rule Matched}
    e6 -- No --> e7[Store and Index Only]
    e6 -- Yes --> e8[Create Intermediate Event]
    e8 --> e9{Cross Stage Correlation Triggered}
    e9 -- No --> e10[Low Severity Alert]
    e9 -- Yes --> e11[High Severity Alert]
    e11 --> e12[Push to SOAR Playbook]
    e10 --> e13[Send to Dashboard]
    e12 --> e14[Open Ticket in ServiceNow]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-stage correlation is visible here:** A single threshold match creates only a *low severity* alert. Only when the intermediate event chains with another event does the system raise a *high severity* alert. This is how SIEMs suppress noise.

## 4.3 SIEM in the Modern SOC Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOURCES[Telemetry Sources]
        s1[Firewalls and Routers]
        s2[IDS and IPS]
        s3[Servers and Apps]
        s4[Endpoints EDR]
        s5[Cloud Platforms]
    end
    subgraph SIEM_CORE[SIEM Platform]
        c1[Collector]
        c2[Normalizer]
        c3[Correlation Engine]
        c4[Alert Store]
    end
    subgraph RESPONSE[Response Layer]
        r1[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
        r2[SOAR Automation]
        r3[Ticketing ServiceNow]
        r4[Threat Intel Platform]
    end
    SOURCES --> SIEM_CORE
    SIEM_CORE --> RESPONSE
    r4 -. enriches .-> c3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**Define SIEM. List any four key capabilities of a SIEM platform.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) is a centralized security solution that combines Security Information Management (SIM) and Security Event Management (SEM) to provide real-time monitoring, log aggregation, correlation of events from heterogeneous sources, alerting, and compliance reporting across an organization's IT infrastructure.

**Four Key Capabilities:**
1. **Centralized log collection and storage** from network devices, servers, endpoints, and applications.
2. **Real-time event correlation** using rule-based and statistical engines to detect multi-stage attacks.
3. **Automated alerting and incident response** with severity-based notification to SOC analysts.
4. **Compliance reporting** for regulations such as PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, and ISO 27001.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the four capabilities.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between SIM and SEM. Why is their integration necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SIM (Security Information Management) | SEM (Security Event Management) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Focus | Long-term storage and analysis of logs | Real-time monitoring and correlation of live events |
| Time horizon | Historical (days, months, years) | Live (seconds, minutes) |
| Primary function | Forensic analysis, compliance reporting | Threat detection, live alerting |
| Data handling | Batch-oriented | Stream-oriented |

**Necessity of Integration:**
Individually, SIM provides deep history but no real-time response; SEM provides live alerts but no historical context for forensic investigation. Integration — the SIEM platform — delivers both: **real-time threat detection enriched with long-term context**, enabling faster MTTD, lower MTTR, and auditable compliance evidence in one unified workflow.

*(3 marks — 1 for the table, 2 for the necessity explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a SIEM system**. List the major stages of the SIEM pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An enterprise has **3,500 devices**. Each device generates an average of **2.5 events/second** at peak. The average event size is **300 bytes**. Retention is **180 days** and the compression ratio is **8:1**.
Compute:
   (i) Peak EPS
   (ii) Daily event volume
   (iii) Total uncompressed storage for 180 days (in TB)
   (iv) Compressed storage required (in TB) **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The SIEM architecture (refer to the diagram in Section 4.1) consists of the following pipeline stages:

1. **Data Collection (1 mark):** SIEM uses agents, syslog forwarders, and APIs to collect logs from firewalls, IDS/IPS, servers, endpoints, applications, and cloud platforms. The volume is measured in Events Per Second (EPS).

2. **Parsing and Normalization (1 mark):** Raw logs are parsed using regex/Grok patterns and converted to a common schema (CEF or LEEF) so that all events — Windows, Linux, network — have a uniform structure.

3. **Enrichment (1 mark):** Events are augmented with threat intelligence (Is the source IP a known malicious actor?), GeoIP, asset criticality, and user role.

4. **Correlation (2 marks):** The correlation engine applies rule-based logic (e.g., 10 failed logins followed by 1 success), statistical thresholds, and (in modern SIEMs) ML-based anomaly detection to convert thousands of individual low-severity events into a single high-confidence incident.

5. **Alerting, Dashboarding, and Reporting (1 mark):** Alerts are routed to SOC analysts via dashboards, ticketing systems, or SOAR platforms. Compliance reports are auto-generated for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR.

6. **Long-term Retention (1 mark):** Indexed events are compressed and archived to meet regulatory retention requirements (typically 90 days to 7 years).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Peak EPS:**

$$
\text{EPS} = 3500 \times 2.5 = 8750 \; \text{events/second}
$$

**(ii) Daily Event Volume:**

$$
\text{Events/day} = 8750 \times 86400 = 756{,}000{,}000 \; \text{events/day}
$$

**(iii) Total Uncompressed Storage for 180 days:**

First, raw storage per day:

$$
\text{Raw/day} = 756{,}000{,}000 \times 300 \; \text{bytes} = 226{,}800{,}000{,}000 \; \text{bytes}
$$

Converting to GB:

$$
\frac{226{,}800{,}000{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 211.24 \; \text{GB/day}
$$

For 180 days:

$$
\text{Total Raw} = 211.24 \times 180 = 38{,}023 \; \text{GB} \approx 37.13 \; \text{TB}
$$

**(iv) Compressed Storage (8:1):**

$$
\text{Compressed} = \frac{37.13}{8} \approx 4.64 \; \text{TB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (i) **8,750 EPS**, (ii) **7.56 × 10⁸ events/day**, (iii) **~37.13 TB uncompressed**, (iv) **~4.64 TB compressed**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **event correlation in SIEM**. Describe any **two types of correlation rules** with an example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **SIEM, EDR, and SOAR** in terms of purpose, data source, and primary function. Why are they considered **complementary** in a modern SOC? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Concept (3 marks):**
Event correlation is the SIEM's core analytical process in which multiple individual log events — often from different devices and seemingly unrelated — are combined and evaluated against a defined logic to produce a single meaningful security incident. The purpose is to reduce alert fatigue, eliminate false positives, and surface attacks that are **invisible** in any single log source.

**Two Types of Correlation Rules (2 marks each):**

**1. Threshold Correlation Rule:**
> A simple rule that fires when a count of a specific event exceeds a threshold within a time window.
> **Example:** `IF count of "Failed Login" from same source IP > 10 within 5 minutes THEN alert "Brute Force Attempt"`.

**2. Sequential / Multi-Stage Correlation Rule:**
> A rule that requires events to occur in a specific order across multiple devices.
> **Example:** `IF (5 failed logins from IP X within 60s) AND THEN (1 successful login from same IP X within next 10 minutes) THEN alert "Brute Force Success — Possible Account Compromise"`. This kills the noise from genuine user typos and surfaces only real attacks.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Dimension | SIEM | EDR | SOAR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Centralized detection and compliance | Endpoint threat detection and response | Automate response actions |
| **Primary Data Source** | Logs from all sources (network, server, app) | Endpoint telemetry (process, file, registry) | Alerts from SIEM, EDR, NDR, email gateways |
| **Primary Function** | Correlation, alerting, reporting, retention | Detect and contain endpoint threats | Execute playbooks across multiple tools |

**Why Complementary in a Modern SOC (3 marks):**

In a layered SOC architecture, **EDR** acts as the **endpoint sensor** — it sees the most detail about a host. **SIEM** acts as the **central brain** — it correlates EDR alerts with firewall, IDS, and authentication logs to reconstruct full attack chains. **SOAR** acts as the **automated response hand** — when SIEM raises an alert, SOAR executes multi-step playbooks (isolate host, disable user, enrich with threat intel, open ticket). Without EDR, SIEM lacks endpoint depth. Without SIEM, EDR alerts are siloed. Without SOAR, both detection and analysis are slowed by manual toil. Together they form the **detect → decide → act** loop of a mature SOC.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make on SIEM questions (and how to avoid them):**
> 
> 1. **Defining SIEM as "a log server"** — You will lose **2 of 3 marks** instantly. A SIEM is a **detection and response platform**, not a log archive. The *correlation engine* is what makes it a SIEM.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization stage** — In pipeline/diagram questions, examiners specifically look for the **Parser/Normalizer** block. Drawing only Collector → Storage → Dashboard will lose **1–2 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Confusing correlation with filtering** — A *filter* selects events; a *correlation* combines multiple events. Use the word **correlation** explicitly, not "filtering" or "matching".
> 
> 4. **Numerical errors in EPS/storage problems** — Most students forget the $\times 86400$ conversion or use $1000$ instead of $1024$ for GB conversion. **Always show units in every step** — examiners award partial marks for correct method even if the final number is slightly off.
> 
> 5. **Omitting real-world examples** — When asked to "explain correlation", students write a definition but no example. **Always** give a concrete rule (e.g., brute force → success) — that single example is worth 1–2 marks.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring compliance reporting** — When listing SIEM capabilities, the compliance/audit reporting function is **almost always tested**. Omit it and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition to memorize verbatim:** *SIEM = SIM + SEM = centralized aggregation + real-time correlation + alerting + compliance reporting.*
- **Five-stage pipeline (in order):** **Collection → Normalization → Enrichment → Correlation → Presentation (Alerts/Dashboards/Reports).**
- **EPS formula:** $\text{EPS} = \text{Events} / \text{Time (s)}$; **Daily volume** $= \text{EPS} \times 86400$.
- **Storage formula:** $\text{Storage} = \text{EPS} \times \text{AvgSize} \times 86400 \times \text{Retention Days} / \text{Compression Ratio}$.
- **SIEM is NOT:** a log server, a firewall, or an IDS. **SIEM IS:** the platform that **correlates** the outputs of all of these.
- **Correlation types to know:** Threshold, Sequential/Multi-Stage, Statistical, Cross-Device.
- **Key metrics:** EPS, MTTD, MTTR, FPR (False Positive Rate), SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio).
- **SIEM vs adjacent tools:** SIEM = brain; EDR = endpoint eye; NDR = network eye; SOAR = hands.
- **Compliance mandates retention:** PCI-DSS ≥ 1 year; HIPAA = 6 years; SOX = 7 years. Mention these when asked about retention.
- **Two real attack examples to mention in any answer:** (1) Brute force SSH followed by successful login; (2) Phishing email → macro execution → C2 beacon → lateral movement. These cover ~80% of correlation questions.
- **Popular SIEMs (mention 1–2 in answers for bonus marks):** Splunk, IBM QRadar, Micro Focus ArcSight, Elastic Security (ELK), LogRhythm, Microsoft Sentinel.
- **Trend to mention in "modern SIEM" questions:** Cloud-native SIEM (e.g., Microsoft Sentinel, Chronicle), UEBA integration, SOAR-driven automated response, and XDR convergence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of SIEM in Intrusion Detection and Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Role of SIEM in Intrusion Detection and Prevention # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** is a comprehensive security management framework that combines the historically distinct functions of **Security Information Management (SIM)** — long-term log archival, retention, and forensic analysis — and **Security Event Management (SEM)** — real-time monitoring, correlation, and alerting of network and host events — into a single, unified platform engineered to detect, analyse, and respond to cybersecurity threats across an enterprise's entire digital infrastructure. In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention (IDP)**, SIEM acts as the **central nervous system** of the security operations centre (SOC). It ingests raw telemetry from heterogeneous sources (firewalls, IDS/IPS sensors, antivirus, operating systems, applications, cloud workloads, and threat intelligence feeds), **normalises** these logs into a canonical schema, applies **correlation rules** and **analytics** to identify attack patterns, and generates prioritised **alerts** or triggers **automated response workflows** (via SOAR integration). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECPT753 Module 3):** The role of SIEM in IDP is examined as a *force multiplier* — it does not replace IDS/IPS sensors but aggregates their outputs, removes noise, correlates distributed indicators, and provides the situational awareness required for high-fidelity threat detection and regulatory compliance reporting. ### Formal Academic Statement Mathematically, SIEM can be abstracted as a tuple: $$ \text{SIEM} = \big\langle \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{N}, \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{S} \big\rangle $$ where: - $\mathcal{C}$ = **Collectors/Agents** (data acquisition layer) - $\mathcal{N}$ = **Normalisation engine** (semantic translation) - $\mathcal{K}$ = **Knowledge base** (correlation rules, threat intel, behavioural baselines) - $\mathcal{A}$ = **Analytics engine** (rule-based, statistical, ML-based) - $\mathcal{R}$ = **Response orchestration** (alerts, SOAR handoffs, ticket creation) - $\mathcal{S}$ = **Storage tier** (hot/warm/cold — typically governed by retention laws) > [!NOTE] > The **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** industry benchmark for a mature SIEM-enabled SOC is **< 1 hour**, while a non-SIEM (manual log review) environment typically exhibits **MTTD > 24 hours**. This is the single most cited metric justifying SIEM deployment. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital ICU Monitor" Imagine a large hospital with hundreds of patients, each connected to their own bedside monitor (heart rate, BP, oxygen, temperature). Individual monitors are analogous to **IDS/IPS sensors** on individual network segments. Now imagine a **central nursing station** that: 1. Receives data streams from **every bedside monitor** (the SIEM collectors). 2. **Translates** data from different manufacturers into a common display format (normalisation). 3. Looks at the **combined data** — e.g., "Patient A in Room 12 has high BP **AND** Patient B in Room 14 (his spouse) has a fever **AND** they both registered at the same gate in the last 30 minutes" — and raises a single intelligent alert (correlation). 4. Sends an alert to the right specialist (alerting/routing). 5. Keeps long-term records of all vitals for future investigation (log retention). That central nursing station is **SIEM**. The patient monitors are **IDS/IPS**. Without the central station, the bedside monitors would still beep, but no one would correlate a multi-room outbreak. **SIEM is the correlation and situational-awareness layer that IDS/IPS alone cannot provide.** ## 1.3 Why SIEM is Indispensable in Modern IDP | IDP Limitation (Without SIEM) | SIEM-Enabled Capability | |-------------------------------|--------------------------| | Thousands of low-severity IDS alerts flood the analyst | **Alert aggregation & de-duplication** | | Siloed logs in different formats | **Centralised log normalisation** | | No cross-domain correlation (host + network + app) | **Multi-source correlation rules** | | Reactive post-mortem analysis only | **Real-time event correlation** | | No compliance evidence trail | **Tamper-evident audit archival** | | Manual threat hunting is time-intensive | **Dashboards, KPIs, and UEBA** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Expectation:** When you describe SIEM's role in IDP, you must explicitly position it as a **complement**, not a **substitute**, for IDS/IPS. The examiner will deduct marks if you imply SIEM *replaces* sensors. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Operational Phases of SIEM in IDP A SIEM platform executes a continuous, cyclical workflow commonly called the **SIEM Kill Chain Loop**. Each phase has a direct mapping to IDP outcomes. ### Phase 1 — Data Collection (Telemetry Acquisition) The SIEM ingests data via three primary mechanisms: 1. **Agent-based collection** — a lightweight daemon (e.g., Splunk Universal Forwarder, WinCollect) is installed on the endpoint and pushes logs in near real-time. 2. **Agentless collection** — protocols such as **syslog (UDP/TCP 514)**, **SNMP traps (UDP 162)**, **WMI polling**, **NetFlow (v5/v9/IPFIX)**, and **SMB/WinRM** are used to pull or receive data. 3. **API-based ingestion** — modern cloud workloads and SaaS applications (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, Okta, Office 365) are integrated via RESTful APIs. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The minimum recommended log volume for an enterprise SIEM is **≥ 10,000 Events Per Second (EPS)**. Anything below this threshold is generally not worth the licensing cost; above **100,000 EPS** requires a **distributed SIEM architecture** (indexer clustering). ### Phase 2 — Normalisation & Parsing Each event source uses its own native log format. Normalisation translates them into a **Common Event Format (CEF)** or **Log Event Extended Format (LEEF)** schema with canonical fields: | Canonical Field | Meaning | Example | |-----------------|---------|---------| | `dt` / `timestamp` | Event occurrence time (UTC) | `2024-11-12T14:22:01Z` | | `src_ip` | Source IP address | `203.0.113.45` | | `dst_ip` | Destination IP address | `198.51.100.7` | | `src_port` | Source transport port | `53124` | | `dst_port` | Destination transport port | `22` (SSH) | | `proto` | Transport protocol | `TCP` | | `action` | Taken/permitted action | `allowed`, `denied`, `teardrop` | | `severity` | Numeric severity score | `0–10` (typical) | | `user` | Authenticated principal | `admin` | | `bytes_in/out` | Volume metric | `4096`, `8192` | > [!IMPORTANT] > Without normalisation, correlation is impossible because the SIEM cannot match `user = "root"` (Linux) with `user = "Administrator"` (Windows) and `user = "SYSTEM\sa"` (SQL Server) to the *same human*. This is the single most common reason for SIEM project failures. ### Phase 3 — Correlation & Analytics This is the **brain** of SIEM and the heart of its role in IDP. Correlation engines apply several analytical techniques: #### (a) Rule-Based Correlation Boolean logic combining events across sources. Example rule: ``` IF (count(failed_logins{src_ip = X, user = Y, within = 5 min}) >= 10) AND (geoip(X) NOT IN whitelist(allowed_countries_for_user_Y)) THEN raise_alert(severity = HIGH, type = "Brute Force + Impossible Travel") ``` #### (b) Statistical / Anomaly-Based Correlation Builds a **behavioural baseline** (e.g., per-user, per-host) and flags deviations. User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (**UEBA**) is a modern extension. #### (c) Threat Intelligence Correlation SIEM enriches events with **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)** from feeds such as **STIX/TAXII**, **AlienVault OTX**, **MISP**, and **MITRE ATT&CK**. If a destination IP matches a known C2 server in the threat feed, the alert is auto-elevated. #### (d) Cross-Source / Multi-Stage Correlation Combines **firewall deny + IDS signature + EDR alert + DNS query** to reconstruct a kill-chain attack (e.g., **reconnaissance → initial access → C2 beacon**). ### Phase 4 — Alerting & Triage Alerts are assigned: - **Severity** (Critical / High / Medium / Low / Informational) - **Confidence** (analyst-assigned or ML-scored) - **Priority** (severity × asset criticality) - **Owner** (routed to the L1/L2/L3 SOC analyst queue) > [!NOTE] > **Key Industry Metric:** A mature SOC aims for a **False Positive Rate (FPR) ≤ 5%** and a **False Negative Rate (FNR) ≤ 1%**. Poorly tuned SIEMs routinely exhibit FPRs of 40–60%, leading to "alert fatigue" and analyst burnout. ### Phase 5 — Response Orchestration The SIEM either: - Sends notifications (email, SMS, Slack, PagerDuty), - Creates tickets in **SOAR** (e.g., Splunk SOAR, Cortex XSOAR) for automated playbooks, or - Triggers **active defence** actions via the IDS/IPS management console (block IP, quarantine host). ### Phase 6 — Storage, Compliance & Forensics Logs are written to **Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM)** storage with cryptographic hashing (e.g., SHA-256 chains) to satisfy evidentiary integrity. Retention periods: - **PCI-DSS:** 1 year online + archival - **HIPAA:** 6 years - **GDPR:** "as long as necessary" (often interpreted as 1–3 years) ## 2.2 The "Three Pillars" of SIEM Value in IDP > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorise this triad for KTU exams — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question every cycle.** ### Pillar 1 — Visibility SIEM provides a **single pane of glass** across on-premises, cloud, OT/ICS, and remote endpoints. Without it, an attacker can hide in the "visibility gap" between two logging silos. ### Pillar 2 — Correlation (the *Why* behind SIEM in IDP) A single failed SSH login is benign. **500 failed SSH logins from 50 different IPs targeting 10 different admin accounts in 5 minutes is a brute-force attack.** Neither fact alone triggers the same response; correlation creates the intelligence. ### Pillar 3 — Compliance & Audit Regulatory mandates (**PCI-DSS 10.x, NIST 800-53 AU family, ISO 27001 A.12.4, SOX, HIPAA §164.312(b)**) all require centralised log collection, integrity protection, and review. SIEM is the *de facto* compliance engine. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Unit / Domain | Use in IDP | |---|------------------|---------|---------------|------------| | 1 | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{N_{\text{events}}}{t}$ | Events per second | events/s | Sizing SIEM capacity | | 2 | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Positive Rate | dimensionless (0–1) | Tuning quality | | 3 | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | False Negative Rate | dimensionless (0–1) | Detection coverage | | 4 | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Alert precision | dimensionless | SOC efficiency | | 5 | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection coverage | dimensionless | Threat coverage | | 6 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | dimensionless | Balanced scoring | | 7 | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occur},i})$ | Mean Time to Detect | seconds/minutes/hours | SOC KPI | | 8 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{\text{resolve},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})$ | Mean Time to Respond | seconds/minutes/hours | SOC KPI | | 9 | $\text{Alert Volume} = \sum_{\text{raw}} \text{events} \cdot k_{\text{correlation}}$ | Raw → correlated | count/day | Capacity planning; $k \ll 1$ when correlation works | | 10 | $\text{Signal-to-Noise} = \dfrac{\text{True Alerts}}{\text{Total Alerts}}$ | Quality ratio | dimensionless | Analyst workload health | > [!WARNING] > **Exam Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse **FPR** (False Positive **Rate**) with the **False Positive Ratio / Count**. FPR is normalised. Similarly, MTTD and MTTR are *averages*, not thresholds. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking/Finance:** SIEM is mandatory for PCI-DSS, SWIFT CSP, and RBI cyber-security framework compliance. - **Healthcare:** HIPAA §164.312(b) audit controls are demonstrably met only via SIEM-class systems. - **Critical Infrastructure (Power, Water, OT/ICS):** SIEM is the only practical way to correlate IT and OT events and detect **TRITON**, **Industroyer**, and **BlackEnergy**-class attacks. - **Cloud-Native / AWS / Azure / GCP:** SIEM (e.g., Microsoft Sentinel, AWS Detective + GuardDuty) is the standard mechanism for **cloud-native IDP** since native cloud logs are otherwise isolated per service. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Alert Reduction Through Correlation Let us formally derive the alert-volume reduction that SIEM correlation provides, and how it mathematically improves SOC efficiency. ### Step 1 — Define the Raw Event Stream Consider a single Snort IDS sensor producing $N_{\text{raw}}$ alerts per day on a typical enterprise edge. These are *raw* signature matches, many of which are duplicates (e.g., the same SYN-flood packet being reassembled multiple times) or related to a single logical attack (e.g., a multi-step port scan producing thousands of "SYN" alerts). $$ N_{\text{raw}} = N_{\text{dup}} + N_{\text{isolated}} + N_{\text{true}} $$ where: - $N_{\text{dup}}$ = duplicate alerts (same signature, same source, same target, same time window) - $N_{\text{isolated}}$ = true positives that have no context (single events) - $N_{\text{true}}$ = true positives that are part of a multi-step attack ### Step 2 — Apply De-duplication The SIEM's de-duplication engine collapses $N_{\text{dup}}$ into a single alert using a hash of the tuple `(signature, src, dst, time-bucket)`. Let the de-duplication factor be $d \in (0, 1]$, representing the fraction of raw events that survive after collapsing. $$ N_{\text{dedup}} = d \cdot N_{\text{raw}} $$ In practice, $d$ is typically in the range **0.05 to 0.20** for high-volume sensors, because raw IDS output is dominated by duplicates. ### Step 3 — Apply Multi-Event Correlation Now SIEM applies *correlation rules* that group related events. Define the correlation factor $c \in (0, 1]$, representing the fraction of de-duplicated alerts that survive after merging related events into a single *incident*. $$ N_{\text{incidents}} = c \cdot N_{\text{dedup}} $$ A typical mature SIEM has $c$ in the range **0.10 to 0.50**, because most alerts are correlated into far fewer logical incidents. ### Step 4 — Compose the Final Reduction Equation Combining steps 2 and 3: $$ N_{\text{incidents}} = c \cdot d \cdot N_{\text{raw}} $$ Let us define the **SIEM Alert Reduction Factor** as: $$ R = \dfrac{N_{\text{raw}}}{N_{\text{incidents}}} = \dfrac{1}{c \cdot d} $$ ### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example Suppose a Snort sensor produces **$N_{\text{raw}} = 50{,}000$** raw alerts per day. The SIEM has $d = 0.15$ (good de-duplication) and $c = 0.30$ (moderate correlation). Compute the resulting incident count and reduction factor. $$ N_{\text{incidents}} = c \cdot d \cdot N_{\text{raw}} $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ N_{\text{incidents}} = 0.30 \times 0.15 \times 50{,}000 $$ Compute the intermediate product: $$ 0.30 \times 0.15 = 0.045 $$ Now multiply by 50,000: $$ N_{\text{incidents}} = 0.045 \times 50{,}000 = 2{,}250 \text{ incidents/day} $$ The reduction factor is therefore: $$ R = \dfrac{50{,}000}{2{,}250} \approx 22.22 $$ This means the analyst now triages **22× fewer** items while missing *none* of the underlying attack indicators — a 95.5% workload reduction with zero loss of detection fidelity. ### Step 6 — Sensitivity Analysis | $d$ | $c$ | $N_{\text{incidents}}$ | Reduction $R$ | |-----|-----|------------------------|----------------| | 1.00 (no de-dup) | 1.00 (no corr.) | 50,000 | 1× | | 0.50 | 0.50 | 12,500 | 4× | | 0.15 | 0.30 | 2,250 | 22× | | 0.05 | 0.10 | 250 | 200× | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Mature SIEM deployments routinely achieve $R \geq 100\times$. The trade-off is that aggressive correlation *can* introduce false negatives (collapsing a true attack with a benign event). This is why correlation rules must be validated against **red-team / purple-team** exercises. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — SIEM vs. Standalone IDP Components | Capability | Standalone IDS | Standalone IPS | SIEM (with IDS/IPS feeds) | |------------|----------------|----------------|----------------------------| | Per-packet inspection | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (relies on feeds) | | Inline blocking | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ (orchestrates) | | Cross-sensor correlation | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (core function) | | Long-term log retention | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (months to years) | | Compliance reporting (PCI/HIPAA) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Threat intel enrichment | Limited | Limited | ✅ (native) | | Forensics timeline reconstruction | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Anomaly / UEBA detection | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Cost | Low–Medium | Medium | High | | Operational complexity | Low | Medium | High (skilled analysts needed) | ## 3.3 Mapping SIEM to the Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin / MITRE ATT&CK) | Kill Chain Stage | Typical Telemetry | SIEM Correlation Example | |------------------|-------------------|--------------------------| | Reconnaissance | Firewall denies, DNS queries | Spike in port-scan signatures across 3 sensors within 60 s | | Weaponisation | Email gateway, web proxy | Inbound email matches malware hash from threat intel | | Delivery | Mail server, web logs | Phishing URL clicked + EDR process spawn | | Exploitation | IDS/IPS, WAF | Signature ET TROJAN + abnormal parent-child process | | Installation | EDR, Sysmon | Scheduled task creation + binary signed by unusual publisher | | C2 (Command & Control) | DNS, NetFlow, firewall | Periodic DNS to low-TTL domain + matching IoC hash | | Actions on Objectives | DLP, file integrity, DB audit | Bulk file read + outbound transfer to external IP | > [!NOTE] > A SIEM cannot *prevent* the first five stages alone, but its **correlation across all stages** enables early detection (often at Stage 3–4) — long before data exfiltration at Stage 7. ## 3.4 The SIEM Reference Architecture (Layered Model) 1. **Layer 0 — Sources:** Firewalls, IDS/IPS, switches/routers, servers, applications, cloud APIs, EDR agents, identity providers. 2. **Layer 1 — Collection:** Forwarders (Splunk UF, Logstash, NXLog), syslog receivers, API connectors. 3. **Layer 2 — Normalisation & Parsing:** CEF/LEEF mapping, field extraction (regex, KV-store), timestamp canonicalisation. 4. **Layer 3 — Storage:** Hot tier (SSD, ≤ 7 days), warm tier (HDD, 7–90 days), cold tier (object storage, 90 days – 7 years). 5. **Layer 4 — Correlation & Analytics Engine:** Rule engine, statistical engine, ML/UEBA models, threat-intel lookups. 6. **Layer 5 — Presentation:** Dashboards, reports, alerts, REST API. 7. **Layer 6 — Orchestration & Response:** SOAR playbooks, ticket systems, firewall/IPS API calls for automated containment. This layered model is the canonical answer expected in **Part B (14-mark) questions** on SIEM architecture. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level SIEM Data-Flow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC["DATA SOURCES LAYER"] FW["FIREWALL
