Partial diagrams that omit storage tiers or threat-intel feeds are marked down by examiners. ## 4.4 Use-Case State Machine: From Event to Closed Incident ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> RawEvent: Telemetry arrives at collector RawEvent --> Normalised: Parser maps fields to CEF Normalised --> Indexed: Stored in hot tier Indexed --> UnderAnalysis: Correlation engine evaluates rules UnderAnalysis --> Correlated: Multi-event match found UnderAnalysis --> Dropped: Below confidence threshold Correlated --> Alerted: Priority score assigned Alerted --> Triaged: L1 analyst review Triaged --> Escalated: L1 cannot close Triaged --> Closed: True positive handled Escalated --> Containment: L2 triggers SOAR playbook Containment --> Closed: Action confirmed and logged Closed --> [*] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define SIEM. List any four core capabilities of a SIEM in the context of Intrusion Detection and Prevention. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** is an integrated security management framework that combines **Security Information Management (SIM)** and **Security Event Management (SEM)** to provide centralised collection, normalisation, correlation, analysis, and retention of security-relevant log data generated across an enterprise IT environment. **Four core capabilities in IDP context:** 1. **Centralised Log Aggregation** — Ingests events from heterogeneous sources (firewalls, IDS/IPS, EDR, servers, cloud) into a single repository. 2. **Event Correlation** — Applies rule-based, statistical, and threat-intelligence-driven logic to identify multi-stage attacks that single sensors cannot see. 3. **Real-Time Alerting and Triage** — Generates prioritised, context-enriched alerts routed to appropriate SOC analyst queues. 4. **Compliance Reporting and Forensic Retention** — Produces tamper-evident, long-term audit trails for regulatory mandates (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001) and post-incident forensics. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for the four capabilities — adjust phrasing but not the substance.)* --- ### Q2. Differentiate between SIM and SEM. Why must they be combined in modern SIEM? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | SIM (Security Information Management) | SEM (Security Event Management) | |--------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------| | Primary focus | Long-term storage, archival, and **forensic analysis** of logs | **Real-time** monitoring, correlation, and alerting | | Time horizon | Weeks to years (historical) | Seconds to minutes (live) | | Use case | Compliance audits, post-incident investigation | Active threat detection, SOC dashboarding | | Data volume | Very high (cold storage) | Filtered / hot data only | **Why combined:** Modern enterprises require both — SEM detects the *attack in progress* while SIM preserves the *evidence for later*. A detection without retention fails compliance; retention without detection is a forensic archive of defeats. Modern SIEM unifies both into a single platform with **tiered storage** to economically satisfy both needs. *(1 mark for the table entries, 1 mark for the contrast, 1 mark for the rationale.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **Instructions (per KTU 2024):** *Answer any one full question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.* --- ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the layered architecture of a SIEM with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of each layer and how it supports intrusion detection and prevention. **Model Answer:** A SIEM is implemented as a **six-layer architecture**, each layer performing a discrete function in the detection pipeline. **Layer 1 — Data Sources (Sensors and Applications):** Sources include firewalls, IDS/IPS, routers/switches, servers, databases, EDR agents, identity providers, cloud workload APIs, and application logs. This layer produces the raw telemetry required for detection. **Layer 2 — Collection Layer:** Forwarders (e.g., Splunk Universal Forwarder, Logstash Beats) and syslog receivers push data to the SIEM in near real-time. Cloud sources are polled via REST APIs. This layer ensures **no data is lost** during transit and handles buffering during network outages. **Layer 3 — Normalisation and Parsing Layer:** Raw logs in vendor-specific formats are parsed using regex, KV-store extraction, and CEF/LEEF mappings. Timestamps are synchronised to UTC, IP addresses and user identifiers are canonicalised. Without normalisation, correlation across heterogeneous sources is impossible. **Layer 4 — Storage Layer:** A **tiered storage** model is used: **Hot tier (SSD, 0–7 days)** for fast queries, **Warm tier (HDD, 7–90 days)** for active investigations, and **Cold tier (object storage, 90 days to 7 years)** for compliance and forensic archival. Data is cryptographically hashed (SHA-256) and stored in **WORM** format for tamper evidence. **Layer 5 — Analytics and Correlation Layer:** This is the **intelligence layer**. It runs: - *Rule-based correlation* (boolean logic across event types), - *Statistical / anomaly detection* (deviation from behavioural baselines — UEBA), - *Threat intelligence correlation* (matching against STIX/TAXII IoCs), - *Kill-chain correlation* (multi-stage attack reconstruction). **Layer 6 — Presentation and Response Layer:** Analysts interact via dashboards, run ad-hoc queries, and receive prioritised alerts. Automated playbooks in **SOAR** platforms are triggered to push containment actions back to firewalls, IPS, and EDR APIs. **Role in IDP:** The layered architecture enables SIEM to **detect** attacks (Layers 3–5), **prevent** them (Layer 6 orchestration), **document** them (Layer 4 retention), and **comply** with regulations (Layer 4 evidence). A single IDS cannot achieve any of these end-to-end. *(Block diagram: see Section 4.1 — full marks awarded only with a labelled diagram showing all six layers.)* #### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of correlation in SIEM. With a suitable example, show how multi-event correlation helps reduce false positives and detect sophisticated attacks. **Model Answer:** **Definition of correlation:** Correlation is the analytical process of **combining multiple individual events from diverse sources** to infer a higher-order security event that none of the individual events would indicate alone. **Types of correlation in SIEM:** 1. **Rule-based correlation** — Predefined boolean rules combining event conditions. 2. **Statistical correlation** — Deviation from established baselines. 3. **Temporal correlation** — Events occurring within a defined time window. 4. **Cross-source correlation** — Events from different log sources combined (e.g., firewall + IDS + authentication). 5. **Threat-intel correlation** — Matching against known-bad indicators (IPs, hashes, domains). 6. **Behavioural correlation (UEBA)** — Long-term user/entity behaviour baselining. **Worked example — Brute force + lateral movement:** Consider three independent events on a typical Tuesday afternoon: - **Event 1 (Firewall):** 50 blocked SSH connections from IP `203.0.113.45` to the jump host within 60 seconds. - **Event 2 (IDS):** Snort alerts `ET SCAN SSH Brute Force Attempt` from the same source IP. - **Event 3 (Domain Controller):** 12 `Event 4625` (failed logon) entries targeting the `admin` account. Individually, each event has low-to-medium severity. **Event 1 alone** is just a noisy attacker. **Event 2 alone** could be a legitimate pen-test. **Event 3 alone** is a user typo. **Correlation rule:** ``` IF (count(firewall_denies{src=203.0.113.45, dst=22} >= 50, window=60s) AND count(ids_alert{sig=brute_force, src=203.0.113.45} >= 1, window=60s) AND count(failed_logins{user=admin, src=203.0.113.45} >= 10, window=5m)) THEN raise_incident(severity=CRITICAL, type="Coordinated SSH Brute Force") ``` **Outcome:** The SIEM produces **a single, high-confidence, high-priority incident** instead of three low-priority alerts. The false-positive rate is reduced because the **joint probability** of all three benign explanations occurring simultaneously is vanishingly small. **Reduction metric:** $$ \text{Alert Reduction Factor} = R = \frac{1}{c \cdot d} $$ For $c = 0.3$ and $d = 0.15$, $R \approx 22\times$ — the analyst triages 22× fewer items without losing detection. **Detection of sophisticated attacks:** Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are by design **low-and-slow**, evading individual IDS signatures. SIEM correlation defeats this by **stitching together thousands of low-severity events** spread over weeks — something no human and no single IDS can do. *(Award 7 marks: 2 for correlation types, 3 for the worked example, 1 for the formula, 1 for the APT insight.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the SIEM-enabled Intrusion Detection and Prevention framework. How does SIEM complement IDS and IPS rather than replace them? **Model Answer:** **Framework (Block Diagram):** ``` +-----------------------------------+ | PREVENTION LAYER (Inline) | | Firewall, IPS, WAF, NAC | +-----------------+-----------------+ | logs v +-----------------------------------+ | DETECTION LAYER (Passive) | | NIDS, HIDS, EDR, Honeypots | +-----------------+-----------------+ | alerts + logs v +-----------------------------------+ | SIEM CORRELATION HUB | | - Aggregation & Normalisation | | - Rule / ML / UEBA Analytics | | - Threat-Intel Enrichment | +-----------------+-----------------+ | prioritised incidents v +-----------------------------------+ | RESPONSE ORCHESTRATION | | SOAR, EDR Quarantine, Tickets | +-----------------------------------+ ``` **Complementary — not replacement — argument:** | Function | IDS/IPS | SIEM | Combined | |----------|---------|------|----------| | Per-packet deep inspection | ✅ | ❌ (cannot inspect at line rate) | ✅ via IDS feeds | | Real-time inline blocking | ✅ (IPS only) | ❌ | ✅ via orchestration | | Cross-sensor correlation | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Long-term retention | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Compliance reporting | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | **Why SIEM complements rather than replaces:** 1. **Line-rate inspection:** SIEM cannot examine packets at gigabit speeds; IDS/IPS sensor is the only practical way. 2. **Inline blocking:** SIEM is an out-of-band analyser; only IPS can drop packets in real time. 3. **Context and intelligence:** SIEM correlates the *outputs* of multiple IDS/IPS sensors, providing organisational-scale situational awareness. 4. **Bidirectional feedback:** SIEM sends **threat-intel** updates *back* to IDS/IPS sensors (e.g., push new block-list), improving their detection. 5. **Forensic closure:** SIEM retains the *evidence* of the attack, while IDS/IPS only generate the alert. In short: **IDS/IPS are the eyes and hands; SIEM is the brain.** *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for complementary table, 2 for closing argument.)* #### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the key performance metrics used to evaluate a SIEM in a SOC. Derive the formula for Alert Reduction Factor and discuss its operational significance. **Model Answer:** **Key Performance Metrics:** 1. **EPS (Events Per Second)** — Throughput; sizing and capacity metric. 2. **FPR / FNR** — Detection quality; tuning effectiveness. 3. **Precision, Recall, F1-Score** — Information-retrieval metrics adapted to security. 4. **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)** — Latency from attack occurrence to detection. 5. **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond)** — Latency from detection to containment. 6. **Alert Reduction Factor (R)** — Workload optimisation metric. 7. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** — Quality of alert stream. **Derivation of Alert Reduction Factor:** Starting from the raw event stream: $$ N_{\text{raw}} = N_{\text{dup}} + N_{\text{isolated}} + N_{\text{true}} $$ After de-duplication: $$ N_{\text{dedup}} = d \cdot N_{\text{raw}} $$ After multi-event correlation: $$ N_{\text{incidents}} = c \cdot d \cdot N_{\text{raw}} $$ The reduction factor is therefore: $$ R = \frac{N_{\text{raw}}}{N_{\text{incidents}}} = \frac{1}{c \cdot d} $$ **Worked numerical example:** For $N_{\text{raw}} = 50{,}000$, $d = 0.15$, $c = 0.30$: $$ N_{\text{incidents}} = 0.30 \times 0.15 \times 50{,}000 = 2{,}250 $$ $$ R = \frac{50{,}000}{2{,}250} \approx 22.22 $$ **Operational significance:** - **Analyst workload:** Each alert takes ~15 minutes to triage. Reducing from 50,000 to 2,250 saves **~12,000 analyst-hours/year** — approximately **6 FTE-years**. - **Detection fidelity preserved:** Reduction comes from *collapsing duplicates* and *correlating related events*, not from suppressing real attacks. - **Cost justification:** SIEM licensing is often priced per EPS ingested. By reducing 50,000 raw events to 2,250 incidents, the SOC can *prioritise* high-fidelity incidents and tune cheaper retention policies. - **Burnout prevention:** Alert fatigue (a documented cause of analyst turnover) is mitigated when $R$ is high. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not** write "$R = c \cdot d$" — that is the *fraction* of remaining incidents, not the *reduction factor*. The reduction factor is the **inverse**. > 2. **Do not** confuse MTTD (detect) with MTTR (respond). Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 3. **Do not** claim SIEM *replaces* IDS/IPS — this is a factual error and the most heavily penalised misconception. > 4. **Do not** forget units on EPS (events/s) and MTTD/MTTR (hours/minutes). Unitless answers lose 0.5 mark each. > 5. **Do not** omit the diagram in layered-architecture questions. A textual description without a block diagram is capped at 60% marks for that sub-part. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **SIEM = SIM + SEM.** Memorise this equation; it is the most-tested one-liner. - [x] **SIEM does not replace IDS/IPS** — it correlates their outputs. - [x] **Six-layer architecture:** Sources → Collection → Normalisation → Storage → Analytics → Response. - [x] **Tiered storage:** Hot (SSD, ≤ 7d) / Warm (HDD, 7–90d) / Cold (Object, 90d–7y). - [x] **Normalisation** uses CEF / LEEF formats and is the *prerequisite* for correlation. - [x] **Correlation types:** Rule-based, statistical, temporal, cross-source, threat-intel, UEBA. - [x] **Alert Reduction Factor:** $R = \dfrac{1}{c \cdot d}$ — not $c \cdot d$. - [x] **Key metrics:** EPS, FPR, FNR, Precision, Recall, F1, MTTD, MTTR, $R$, Signal-to-Noise. - [x] **MTTD benchmark (mature SIEM-SOC):** < 1 hour. **FPR target:** ≤ 5%. - [x] **Compliance frameworks requiring SIEM:** PCI-DSS 10.x, HIPAA §164.312(b), ISO 27001 A.12.4, NIST 800-53 AU, SOX, GDPR. - [x] **Threat-intel standards:** STIX (data format) + TAXII (transport protocol). - [x] **SIEM ≠ SOAR:** SIEM detects and alerts; SOAR orchestrates the response. They integrate but are distinct. - [x] **Industry-leading SIEMs:** Splunk Enterprise Security, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic Security, Exabeam, Securonix, LogRhythm. - [x] **SIEM value triad in IDP:** Visibility + Correlation + Compliance. - [x] **Always include a diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions — partial marks if missing. - [x] **Avoid the traps:** wrong direction of $R$, MTTD/MTTR swap, SIEM-replaces-IDS claim, missing units.

topics.title: Log Management and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Log Management and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log Management and Analysis** is the systematic process of generating, transmitting, storing, analyzing, and disposing of log data generated by computing systems, network devices, security appliances, and applications. It is a foundational pillar of any modern Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) architecture and is critical for incident detection, forensic investigation, regulatory compliance, and operational intelligence.

In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention**, log management transforms raw, voluminous, and often noisy event records into **structured, normalized, and correlated security intelligence** that supports both real-time alerting (prevention) and post-incident reconstruction (detection).

The two halves of the discipline are formally defined as follows:

- **Log Management**: The infrastructure-level discipline dealing with the **collection, normalization, storage, retention, rotation, and protection** of log records. It addresses the *what, where, and how long* questions.
- **Log Analysis**: The intelligence-level discipline dealing with **parsing, correlation, anomaly detection, pattern recognition, and reporting** on log data. It addresses the *so what* question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic is examined under **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Analyze log sources and apply log management techniques for security monitoring and incident response."* Expect direct questions on log normalization, correlation rules, and SIEM architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Patient Monitoring" Model

Imagine a large hospital with hundreds of patients, each connected to multiple monitoring devices (heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen). Each device continuously emits a stream of readings.

| Hospital Element | Log Management Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Each patient's monitor | A log source (firewall, server, application) |
| The continuous beep/printout | A log event/record |
| The central nurses' station | The log collector / SIEM |
| The triage nurse normalizing formats (paper charts vs. digital) | Log parsing and normalization |
| The senior doctor looking at all charts together | Log correlation engine |
| The hospital's medical record archive | Log storage / data warehouse |
| The 7-year retention law for medical records | Regulatory log retention policy |

Just as a hospital cannot operate without a central monitoring system that consolidates diverse data streams into a single patient record, an enterprise Security Operations Center (SOC) cannot function without a centralized log management pipeline that consolidates device-specific formats into a unified, queryable security narrative.

### 1.3 The Two Pillars of Log Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Fundamental Log Categories:**
> 1. **Security Logs** — Authentication events, authorization decisions, policy violations, malware alerts, firewall denials. *Used directly for threat detection.*
> 2. **Operational Logs** — System startup/shutdown, service health, performance metrics, application errors. *Used for forensics, availability, and contextual enrichment.*

A mature security program treats both as **equally essential** because operational anomalies (e.g., a service crashing repeatedly) often precede or accompany security incidents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Stages of the Log Lifecycle

The log management lifecycle is universally modeled as a five-stage pipeline. Each stage has distinct technical objectives, tools, and failure modes.

#### Stage 1 — Log Generation (Source)
Logs are emitted by heterogeneous sources, each with its own native format.

- **Operating Systems**: Windows Event Logs (EVT/EVTX), Linux Syslog, macOS Unified Logging System (ULS).
- **Network Devices**: Routers, switches, firewalls (Cisco ASA, Palo Alto, FortiGate) — typically Syslog over UDP/TCP (port **514**) or secure Syslog over TLS (port **6514**).
- **Security Appliances**: IDS/IPS (Snort, Suricata), antivirus, web proxies, DLP systems.
- **Applications**: Web servers (Apache, Nginx access/error logs), databases (audit logs), custom enterprise apps.
- **Cloud Platforms**: AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, GCP Cloud Audit Logs, Kubernetes audit logs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Port Reference (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Syslog (UDP):** Port **514** — connectionless, no guaranteed delivery.
> - **Syslog (TCP):** Port **601** — reliable delivery, still plaintext.
> - **Syslog over TLS (RFC 5425):** Port **6514** — encrypted, mutually authenticated.

#### Stage 2 — Log Collection & Transport
A **log collector** (or **syslog server / forwarder**) aggregates logs from distributed sources.

- **Agent-based collection**: An agent (e.g., Wazuh, Beats, Splunk Universal Forwarder, OSSEC) is installed on the host. The agent reads local log files and forwards them. *Pro: reliable, supports filtering. Con: requires installation.*
- **Agentless collection**: The source pushes logs directly via Syslog, SNMP traps, or API calls. *Pro: no footprint. Con: limited filtering, network-bound.*
- **Pull-based collection**: The collector periodically polls the source via SSH, WinRM, or REST API. *Pro: works across NAT. Con: latency.*

#### Stage 3 — Log Normalization & Parsing
The single most important stage for downstream analysis. Different sources produce different formats:

```
Cisco ASA:  %ASA-6-106100: access-list outside denied tcp outside_x/x(x.x.x.x) -> inside_y/y(y.y.y.y) (port)
Linux Auth:  Mar 15 14:22:01 server sshd[12345]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.50
Windows:     <Event xmlns='http://schemas.microsoft.com/win/2004/08/events/event'><System><EventID>4625</EventID>...
```

Normalization converts these into a **Common Event Format (CEF)** or **Log Event Extended Format (LEEF)** with a unified schema. A typical normalized event contains:

- **Timestamp** (ISO 8601 format, UTC)
- **Event ID / Category** (mapped to a standard taxonomy)
- **Source IP, Destination IP**
- **Source Port, Destination Port**
- **Protocol**
- **User identity**
- **Action** (allow, deny, authenticate, execute)
- **Outcome** (success, failure)
- **Raw message** (preserved for forensic completeness)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Schema Reference (CEF Field Order):**
> `CEF:Version|Device Vendor|Device Product|Device Version|Signature ID|Name|Severity|Extension`

#### Stage 4 — Log Storage & Retention
Logs must be stored with appropriate **integrity**, **confidentiality**, and **availability** guarantees.

- **Hot Storage**: Fast SSD-backed, queried in real-time. Retained for **30–90 days** typically. High cost per GB.
- **Warm Storage**: Slower disk, queried on demand. Retained for **6–12 months**.
- **Cold Storage**: Tape or object storage (S3 Glacier), queried rarely. Retained for **1–7 years** per compliance.
- **Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM)**: Immutable storage to prevent tampering. Required by SEC, FINRA, HIPAA in many contexts.

#### Stage 5 — Log Analysis & Disposal
The intelligence layer. Analysis techniques (detailed in §2.3) operate on the normalized, stored data. Disposal follows the retention policy with cryptographic erasure or physical destruction of media.

### 2.2 Log Correlation — The Heart of Detection

Log correlation is the process of **linking multiple individual log events that are causally or temporally related** into a single, higher-confidence incident narrative.

#### Correlation Types

- **Rule-Based Correlation**: Predefined if-then logic. *Example: "5 failed logins within 60 seconds from the same source IP, followed by 1 successful login" → trigger brute-force alert.*
- **Statistical Correlation**: Establishes baselines and flags deviations. *Example: 400% spike in outbound DNS traffic at 03:00.*
- **Temporal Correlation**: Events occurring within a defined time window are linked.
- **Cross-Reference Correlation**: Looks up IP addresses, file hashes, or user IDs against threat intelligence feeds.
- **Behavioral Correlation**: User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) — learns normal behavior per entity and flags deviations.
- **Causal / Kill-Chain Correlation**: Maps events to stages of an attack framework (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK).

#### Correlation Confidence Scoring

Many SIEMs use a weighted scoring system. A conceptual formula:

$$ \text{Incident Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i $$

where $w_i$ is the weight of indicator $i$ and $s_i$ is the severity (0–10). An alert fires when:

$$ \text{Incident Score} \geq T_{\text{threshold}} $$

### 2.3 Log Analysis Techniques

| Technique | Description | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Pattern Matching (Regex)** | Grep-like search for known signatures | Detecting known CVEs in web logs |
| **Threshold Alerting** | Alert when a count exceeds a limit in a window | Brute-force login detection |
| **Baseline / Anomaly Detection** | Statistical deviation from historical norm | Insider exfiltration, beaconing |
| **Frequency Analysis** | Tracks event rates over time | DDoS detection |
| **Sequence Analysis** | Order-sensitive event matching | Multi-stage attack reconstruction |
| **Cross-Event Correlation** | Joins events from multiple sources | Lateral movement detection |
| **Threat Intelligence Enrichment** | Augments logs with IOC feeds | Known-bad IP / hash detection |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the high-yield equations, port numbers, and standards you must memorize for the KTU ESE.**

| Concept | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Syslog UDP port | **514** | Connectionless, plaintext |
| Syslog TCP port | **601** | Reliable, plaintext |
| Syslog over TLS port | **6514** | Encrypted, RFC 5425 |
| Windows Event Log port (WinRM) | **5985 (HTTP) / 5986 (HTTPS)** | Used by WEC collectors |
| SNMP Trap default port | **162 (UDP)** | For network device traps |
| Log volume (approximate enterprise) | **\vert$ 1-10 $\vert$ GB/day per 1000 users** | Highly variable |
| Event severity (CEF) | **0–10** (0=lowest, 10=highest) | Standardized scale |
| Time format (normalization) | **ISO 8601 / RFC 3339** | `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ` |
| Incident score | $I = \sum w_i s_i \geq T$ | Weighted sum, threshold-based |
| True Positive Rate (TPR) | $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection effectiveness |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Alert fatigue metric |
| Log retention (PCI-DSS) | **\vert$ \geq 1 $\vert$ year online, 3 months immediately available** | Audit trail requirement |
| Log retention (HIPAA) | **\vert$ \geq 6 $\vert$ years** | Healthcare compliance |
| Log retention (GDPR) | **As long as necessary, minimized** | Privacy-driven |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Log management and analysis is not academic — it is the **operational backbone** of every SOC. It directly enables:

1. **Incident Detection** (real-time correlation) — the core function of any IDS/IPS.
2. **Incident Response** (forensic timeline reconstruction) — chain of custody for logs.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** (audit trails for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX, GDPR).
4. **Threat Hunting** (proactive query-driven investigation).
5. **Operational Reliability** (root-cause analysis of outages).
6. **Legal Evidence** (court-admissible, tamper-evident logs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Incident Score from Multi-Event Correlation

Let us derive the formal correlation score used by a SIEM engine to decide whether a set of correlated events constitutes an alert.

**Step 1 — Define individual event severity.**

Each normalized event $E_i$ carries an intrinsic severity $s_i \in [0, 10]$ assigned by the source device vendor or by the normalization mapping.

$$ E_i = \{ s_i, t_i, a_i, u_i, src_i, dst_i \} $$

where $t_i$ is the timestamp, $a_i$ is the action, $u_i$ is the user, $src_i$ is source, and $dst_i$ is destination.

**Step 2 — Apply per-indicator weights.**

Different indicators contribute differently to the overall confidence of an attack. For example, a "successful login after 5 failures" has a higher weight than a "single failure."

$$ w_i \in [0, 1] $$

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted sum.**

$$ I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i $$

**Step 4 — Normalize to a 0–100 scale.**

The raw score is normalized so that operators can reason about it consistently:

$$ I_{\text{norm}} = \frac{I}{10 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i} \times 100 $$

**Step 5 — Apply the alerting threshold.**

The SIEM fires an alert only if:

$$ I_{\text{norm}} \geq T_{\text{threshold}} $$

Common threshold values:
- $T_{\text{threshold}} = 30$ → Low-severity informational alert.
- $T_{\text{threshold}} = 60$ → Medium-severity, requires analyst review.
- $T_{\text{threshold}} = 85$ → High-severity, immediate escalation.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Consider a brute-force attack scenario: 5 failed SSH logins followed by 1 successful login from IP `203.0.113.45` against user `root`.

| Event | $s_i$ | $w_i$ | $w_i \cdot s_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Failed login 1 | 4 | 0.6 | 2.4 |
| Failed login 2 | 4 | 0.6 | 2.4 |
| Failed login 3 | 4 | 0.6 | 2.4 |
| Failed login 4 | 4 | 0.6 | 2.4 |
| Failed login 5 | 4 | 0.6 | 2.4 |
| Successful login (root) | 8 | 0.9 | 7.2 |
| **Total** | | $\sum w_i = 3.9$ | $\sum w_i s_i = 19.2$ |

Applying the normalization:

$$ I_{\text{norm}} = \frac{19.2}{10 \times 3.9} \times 100 = \frac{19.2}{39.0} \times 100 \approx 49.23 $$

Since $I_{\text{norm}} = 49.23 < 60$, this would NOT trigger a medium-severity alert but WOULD trigger a low-severity one. The SOC analyst can then promote it manually.

**Step 7 — Refine with temporal decay (advanced).**

Real correlation engines apply temporal decay so that older events contribute less:

$$ I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i \cdot e^{-\lambda (t_{\text{now}} - t_i)} $$

where $\lambda$ is the decay constant (e.g., $\lambda = 0.01$ per second).

### 3.2 Derivation: Detection Performance Metrics

The effectiveness of a log-based detection system is measured by the confusion-matrix-derived metrics. Let us derive the formal definitions.

**Step 1 — Define the four outcomes of a detection decision.**

- **True Positive (TP):** A real attack occurred AND the system raised an alert. ✓
- **False Positive (FP):** No real attack occurred BUT the system raised an alert. ✗ (alert fatigue)
- **True Negative (TN):** No real attack occurred AND no alert was raised. ✓
- **False Negative (FN):** A real attack occurred BUT no alert was raised. ✗ (missed detection)

**Step 2 — Compute the True Positive Rate (Sensitivity / Recall).**

$$ \text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$

This is the fraction of actual attacks that the system catches. A value of 1.0 means every attack was detected.

**Step 3 — Compute the False Positive Rate.**

$$ \text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} $$

This is the fraction of benign activity incorrectly flagged. Lower is better.

**Step 4 — Compute Precision.**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$

Of all alerts raised, what fraction are true attacks?

**Step 5 — Compute the F1-Score (harmonic mean of precision and recall).**

$$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} $$

**Step 6 — Worked Example.**

Suppose over a 30-day window, an IDS processed 1,000,000 events. Of these, 200 were true attacks. The system raised 250 alerts, of which 180 were true attacks.

| | Attack (actual) | Benign (actual) |
|---|---|---|
| **Alert raised** | TP = 180 | FP = 70 |
| **No alert** | FN = 20 | TN = 999,730 |

Computing:

$$ \text{TPR} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\% $$

$$ \text{FPR} = \frac{70}{70 + 999{,}730} = \frac{70}{999{,}800} \approx 0.00007 = 0.007\% $$

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{180}{180 + 70} = \frac{180}{250} = 0.72 = 72\% $$

$$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.72 \times 0.90}{0.72 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.648}{1.62} \approx 0.80 $$

Interpretation: The IDS catches 90% of attacks (good sensitivity) with very few false alarms (excellent specificity), but 28% of its alerts are false positives — a typical real-world balance.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Tool & Standard Comparison

| Tool / Standard | Type | Transport | Encryption | Format | KTU-Relevant Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Syslog (RFC 5424)** | Standard | UDP/TCP | Optional (TLS variant) | Plaintext key-value | Universal log forwarding |
| **CEF (ArcSight)** | Vendor format | Any | N/A (transport-agnostic) | Pipe-delimited | SIEM normalization |
| **LEEF (IBM QRadar)** | Vendor format | Any | N/A | Tab-delimited | SIEM normalization |
| **JSON (ECS — Elastic Common Schema)** | Modern standard | HTTPS | TLS | JSON | Cloud-native, Elasticsearch |
| **Windows Event Forwarding (WEF)** | Microsoft | WinRM | Kerberos/HTTPS | EVTX | Windows environments |
| **OSSEC / Wazuh** | Open-source HIDS | Agent | AES | Plaintext + rules | Open-source SOCs |
| **Splunk** | Commercial SIEM | Various | TLS | Indexes raw + parsed | Enterprise SOC |
| **Elastic Stack (ELK)** | Open-source SIEM | Beats/Logstash | TLS | JSON | Cost-effective SOC |
| **Snort / Suricata** | NIDS | N/A (generates) | N/A | Unified2 / EVE JSON | Network intrusion logs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Log Management & Analysis Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete log pipeline from heterogeneous source generation to analyst-driven response, with all five lifecycle stages, normalization, correlation, and feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["STAGE 1: LOG SOURCES"]
        fw1[Firewall]
        sv1[Linux Server]
        sv2[Windows Server]
        nw1[Router Switch]
        ap1[Web Application]
        cl1[Cloud Platform]
    end

    subgraph COL["STAGE 2: COLLECTION AND TRANSPORT"]
        ag1[Agent Based Collector]
        ag2[Syslog Receiver]
        ag3[API Puller]
    end

    subgraph PAR["STAGE 3: PARSING AND NORMALIZATION"]
        p1[Regex Parser]
        p2[Schema Mapper]
        p3[CEF LEEF Normalizer]
        p4[Enrichment Threat Intel]
    end

    subgraph STO["STAGE 4: STORAGE LAYER"]
        st1[Hot Storage SSD 0 to 90 days]
        st2[Warm Storage Disk 6 to 12 months]
        st3[Cold Storage Object 1 to 7 years]
    end

    subgraph ANA["STAGE 5: ANALYSIS ENGINE"]
        an1[Correlation Rules Engine]
        an2[Statistical Anomaly Detection]
        an3[UEBA Behavioral Baseline]
        an4[MITRE ATT and CK Mapper]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        op1[Real Time SOC Dashboard]
        op2[Alert Ticketing System]
        op3[Forensic Timeline Viewer]
        op4[Compliance Audit Report]
    end

    fw1 --> ag2
    sv1 --> ag1
    sv2 --> ag1
    nw1 --> ag2
    ap1 --> ag3
    cl1 --> ag3

    ag1 --> p1
    ag2 --> p1
    ag3 --> p1

    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4

    p4 --> st1
    st1 --> st2
    st2 --> st3

    st1 --> an1
    st1 --> an2
    st1 --> an3
    an1 --> an4

    an1 --> op1
    an2 --> op1
    an3 --> op2
    an4 --> op2
    st1 --> op3
    st2 --> op4
    st3 --> op4
```

### 4.2 Correlation Decision Flow

The following diagram models the decision logic inside a SIEM correlation engine — from event ingestion through scoring, threshold comparison, and alert dispatch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Normalized Event Arrives] --> check1{Is Event in Active Window}
    check1 -->|No| bufferA[Discard or Archive]
    check1 -->|Yes| lookupA{Look Up Existing Correlation Case}
    lookupA -->|Found caseID| addEv[Append to Existing Case]
    lookupA -->|No match| newCase[Create New Correlation Case]
    addEv --> scoreA[Recompute Incident Score]
    newCase --> scoreA
    scoreA --> threshA{Score Above Threshold}
    threshA -->|Below| watchA[Continue Monitoring]
    threshA -->|Above| sevA{Severity Classification}
    sevA -->|Low| logA[Log to Dashboard]
    sevA -->|Medium| ticketA[Open Analyst Ticket]
    sevA -->|High| pageA[Page On Call Analyst]
    sevA -->|Critical| blockA[Trigger Automated Response Block IP Kill Session]
    watchA --> startA
```

### 4.3 Log Format Normalization Process

This diagram shows how a heterogeneous raw log (Cisco ASA format) is parsed, field-extracted, and rewritten into the Common Event Format (CEF).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA[Raw Cisco ASA Log] --> parseA[Regex Field Extractor]
    parseA --> field1[Timestamp]
    parseA --> field2[Action]
    parseA --> field3[Source IP]
    parseA --> field4[Destination IP]
    parseA --> field5[Port]
    parseA --> field6[Protocol]
    field1 --> mapA[Field Name Mapper]
    field2 --> mapA
    field3 --> mapA
    field4 --> mapA
    field5 --> mapA
    field6 --> mapA
    mapA --> cefA[CEF Formatted Event]
    cefA --> indexA[Indexed in SIEM]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define log normalization. Why is it considered the most critical stage in the log management pipeline?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Log normalization is the process of converting heterogeneous log records from diverse sources (firewalls, operating systems, applications) into a unified, standardized schema — typically **Common Event Format (CEF)** or **Log Event Extended Format (LEEF)** — where fields such as timestamp, source IP, destination IP, action, and outcome have consistent names and data types.

It is the most critical stage because **(1 mark)** all downstream correlation, searching, and analytics depend on a common schema — without it, a SIEM cannot join events from different sources, **(1 mark)** it enables consistent alerting, threat-hunting queries, and reporting across vendors, and **(1 mark)** it preserves semantic meaning (e.g., "denied" from Cisco maps to "block" in Palo Alto) so that correlation rules do not have to be rewritten per device.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*List and briefly explain any three types of log correlation techniques used in SIEM systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Rule-Based Correlation (1 mark):** Predefined if-then logic that links events matching specific patterns within a time window. *Example:* 5 failed logins followed by 1 success → brute-force alert.
2. **Statistical Correlation (1 mark):** Establishes a baseline of normal activity and alerts on statistical deviations (e.g., z-score > 3 on outbound traffic volume).
3. **Behavioral / UEBA Correlation (1 mark):** Builds per-user, per-entity behavioral profiles over time and flags deviations. *Example:* a user suddenly logging in at 03:00 from a new geography.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the five stages of the log management lifecycle. Discuss the security considerations at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of log correlation in SIEM. Derive the incident-scoring formula and apply it to a brute-force SSH attack scenario with five failed logins followed by one successful login as root, given the per-event weights and severities listed. State whether the alert fires at a threshold of 60. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Five Stages of the Log Management Lifecycle (7 marks):**

1. **Log Generation (1 mark):** Logs are generated by operating systems (Windows Event Logs, Linux Syslog), network devices (routers, firewalls via Syslog on port 514), security appliances (IDS/IPS, antivirus), and applications. *Security consideration:* ensure source clocks are synchronized via **NTP** to guarantee timestamp accuracy across distributed systems — without this, correlation across time zones becomes impossible.

2. **Log Collection and Transport (1 mark):** Logs are aggregated via agent-based collectors (Splunk Forwarder, Wazuh, Beats), agentless syslog reception (UDP 514, TCP 601, TLS 6514), or pull-based APIs. *Security consideration:* **Syslog over TLS (port 6514)** must be used to prevent eavesdropping and log injection attacks; mutual certificate authentication prevents rogue log sources.

3. **Parsing and Normalization (1 mark):** Raw logs are parsed using regex or vendor-specific connectors and rewritten into a common schema (CEF, LEEF, ECS). *Security consideration:* the normalization engine itself must be hardened, as a compromised parser can silently drop or alter security events.

4. **Storage and Retention (1.5 marks):** Logs are stored in tiered storage (hot SSD, warm disk, cold object) with WORM (Write-Once-Read-Many) immutability to prevent tampering. *Security consideration:* **cryptographic hashing (SHA-256)** of log records, signed by a hardware security module, provides chain-of-custody for legal admissibility; access control via RBAC ensures analysts see only what they need.

5. **Analysis and Disposal (1.5 marks):** Logs are correlated, anomaly-detected, and reported. Disposal follows retention policy with cryptographic erasure. *Security consideration:* alert pipelines must be **tamper-evident** (any modification to a stored log is detectable), and disposal must be **complete** (no residual data on decommissioned media).

A labeled block diagram (the one in §4.1) is expected for full marks.

---

**(b) Log Correlation and Incident Scoring (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** Log correlation is the process of linking multiple log events that are causally or temporally related into a single higher-confidence incident. It reduces alert noise and surfaces multi-stage attacks that no individual log reveals. Common techniques include rule-based, statistical, temporal, and behavioral correlation.

**Derivation (2 marks):** The incident score is computed as:

$$ I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i $$

Normalized to a 0–100 scale:

$$ I_{\text{norm}} = \frac{I}{10 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i} \times 100 $$

Alert fires when $I_{\text{norm}} \geq T_{\text{threshold}}$.

**Worked Application (3 marks):** Given the brute-force scenario in §3.1, the table yields $\sum w_i s_i = 19.2$ and $\sum w_i = 3.9$, so:

$$ I_{\text{norm}} = \frac{19.2}{10 \times 3.9} \times 100 = \frac{19.2}{39.0} \times 100 \approx 49.23 $$

Since $49.23 < 60$, the alert does **NOT** fire at the medium-severity threshold. It would, however, fire as a low-severity alert (threshold 30), prompting an analyst to investigate and potentially promote it.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the various log sources in a typical enterprise network. Compare agent-based and agentless log collection with their respective advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define True Positive Rate and False Positive Rate. A SIEM processed 1,000,000 events over 30 days. There were 200 real attacks. The system raised 250 alerts, of which 180 were true attacks. Compute TPR, FPR, Precision, and F1-score, and comment on the detection quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Log Sources and Collection Methods (7 marks):**

**Log Sources (3.5 marks):** A typical enterprise has five categories of log sources:
1. **Network Devices:** Firewalls (Cisco ASA, Palo Alto, FortiGate), routers, switches — emit Syslog, typically on UDP 514 or TLS 6514.
2. **Servers:** Linux (Syslog, auth.log, secure), Windows (Event Logs — Security, System, Application) — typically collected via WEF or agents.
3. **Security Appliances:** IDS/IPS (Snort EVE JSON, Suricata), antivirus, web proxies, DLP, VPN concentrators.
4. **Applications:** Web servers (Apache/Nginx access.log), databases (MySQL, MSSQL audit), ERP/CRM systems.
5. **Cloud and Identity:** AWS CloudTrail, Azure AD Sign-in logs, Okta, Kubernetes audit logs, SaaS application logs (Office 365, Salesforce).

**Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Agent-Based | Agentless |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Software installed on source host reads local files | Source pushes logs via Syslog/API; collector pulls via SSH/WinRM |
| **Reliability** | High — agent can buffer when network is down | Lower — UDP syslog can drop packets; network-bound |
| **Filtering** | Rich — agent can preprocess, filter, mask PII before sending | Limited — only what source can natively filter |
| **Footprint** | Requires installation, consumes host resources | No host footprint |
| **Scalability** | Scales well for thousands of endpoints | Better for low-volume network devices |
| **Security** | Agent can be a tampering target if host is compromised | Reduced attack surface, no host software |
| **Typical Tools** | Splunk UF, Wazuh agent, Beats, OSSEC | rsyslog receiver, SNMP collector, API scrapers |

---

**(b) Detection Performance Metrics (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **TPR (Sensitivity / Recall):** $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — fraction of real attacks that were detected.
- **FPR (Fall-out):** $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ — fraction of benign events incorrectly flagged.

**Confusion Matrix Construction (2 marks):** From the data: TP = 180, FP = 70, FN = 20, TN = 999,730.

**Computation (2 marks):**

$$ \text{TPR} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\% $$

$$ \text{FPR} = \frac{70}{70 + 999{,}730} = \frac{70}{999{,}800} \approx 7 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.007\% $$

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{180}{180 + 70} = \frac{180}{250} = 0.72 = 72\% $$

$$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.72 \times 0.90}{0.72 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.648}{1.62} \approx 0.80 $$

**Commentary (1 mark):** The system has **excellent sensitivity (90% TPR)** and **extremely low false alarm rate (0.007% FPR)**, indicating a well-tuned detection rule set. However, **precision of 72%** means roughly 1 in 4 alerts is a false positive — still a manageable alert volume, but any further improvement in precision (via better correlation or whitelisting) would reduce analyst fatigue.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Log Management Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ports:** Students often write "Syslog uses port 514" without specifying UDP vs TCP (601) vs TLS (6514). Examiners expect the three-way distinction. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Skipping the normalization step in diagrams:** A lifecycle diagram without the **parsing/normalization** block in the middle is incomplete. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Writing correlation "joins logs":** Correlation is NOT just JOINing. You must explicitly name the technique (rule-based, statistical, temporal, behavioral) and ideally a formula. **Lose 1 mark for vagueness.**
> 4. **Forgetting units in metric questions:** TPR, FPR, Precision, F1 are dimensionless ratios — write them as percentages or decimals consistently. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Omitting compliance context:** When asked about retention, students often give a single number. Examiners expect **multiple compliance regimes** (PCI-DSS 1 year, HIPAA 6 years, GDPR as-needed) to show breadth. **Lose 1 mark.**

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Log Management and Analysis:**

- ☐ **Lifecycle:** Generation → Collection → Normalization → Storage → Analysis/Disposal. Memorize the five-stage pipeline.
- ☐ **Syslog Ports:** **514 (UDP)**, **601 (TCP)**, **6514 (TLS)** — encrypted transport is the modern default.
- ☐ **Normalization Schemas:** **CEF** (ArcSight, pipe-delimited), **LEEF** (QRadar, tab-delimited), **ECS** (Elastic, JSON-based).
- ☐ **Correlation Formula:** $I = \sum w_i s_i$, normalized to 0–100, threshold-based alerting.
- ☐ **Detection Metrics:** TPR = TP/(TP+FN), FPR = FP/(FP+TN), Precision = TP/(TP+FP), F1 = harmonic mean.
- ☐ **Retention Requirements:** PCI-DSS (1 yr online + 3 mo immediate), HIPAA (6 yr), SOX (7 yr), GDPR (minimization).
- ☐ **Collection Methods:** Agent-based (reliable, filterable) vs Agentless (no footprint, network-bound).
- ☐ **Storage Tiers:** Hot (SSD, 30–90 d), Warm (disk, 6–12 mo), Cold (object/tape, 1–7 yr), WORM for immutability.
- ☐ **Security Controls:** TLS encryption in transit, SHA-256 hashing at rest, RBAC for access, NTP for clock sync.
- ☐ **SIEM Tools to Know:** Splunk (commercial), Elastic/ELK (open-source), Wazuh (open-source HIDS), QRadar (IBM), ArcSight (Micro Focus).
- ☐ **NIDS Log Formats:** Snort (Unified2), Suricata (EVE JSON) — both feed into SIEM pipelines.
- ☐ **Compliance is the Business Driver:** Every retention policy, every audit, every forensic investigation is grounded in a regulatory or contractual obligation — frame your answers accordingly.
- ☐ **Correlation ≠ JOINing:** Always articulate the **technique** (rule, statistical, temporal, behavioral) and ideally a **quantitative measure** (score, threshold, confidence).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlation of Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Correlation of Events

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Event Correlation (in IDPS Context)**
> Event Correlation is the **systematic process of aggregating, normalizing, and causally linking multiple discrete security events** collected from heterogeneous sources (firewalls, IDS/IPS, host logs, application logs, SIEM feeds) to identify, reconstruct, and report a coherent multi-step attack scenario or security incident. The process transforms a *voluminous stream of low-level alerts* into a *small number of high-confidence, actionable security incidents*.

The formal definition adopted by the **NIST SP 800-94 framework** treats correlation as the analytical phase that consumes normalized event tuples and produces a higher-order **incident object** characterized by an attack hypothesis, confidence score, attack graph, and severity rating.

### 1.1.1 Mathematical Form of a Correlated Event

A single raw event is modeled as a tuple:

$$E_i = (t_i, s_i, a_i, o_i, c_i)$$

Where:
- $t_i$ = timestamp of occurrence
- $s_i$ = source identifier (IP, host, user)
- $a_i$ = action or event class
- $o_i$ = target / object
- $c_i$ = context attributes (protocol, payload, etc.)

A **correlated incident** is a set:

$$I = \{E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n\} \quad \text{such that} \quad \phi(E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n) \geq \tau$$

where $\phi$ is a correlation function (rule, statistical, or causal) and **τ** is the **confidence threshold** (typically $0.7 \leq \tau \leq 0.95$).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think like a hospital Emergency Room (ER) triage system.**
> Imagine a city has 50,000 scattered small "patient visits" per day — a child coughing here, a man clutching his chest there, a fever there. Alone, each visit is a low-signal data point. Now introduce a **centralized ER board**: nurses **aggregate** (group fever cases), **normalize** (convert all data to a single chart), and **correlate** (notice that 15 chest-pain patients, all from the same neighborhood, all arrived within 90 minutes). The system immediately raises a single high-level alert: *Suspected environmental contamination event in Sector 7*.
>
> The 50,000 low-signal events collapse into **1 actionable incident**.

In IDPS, the **Correlation Engine** plays the role of that ER board. The 500,000 raw syslog/SNMP/IDS alerts are aggregated, normalized, and correlated into a handful of **true incident tickets** that a SOC analyst can actually act upon.

---

## 1.3 Why Correlation is a Pillar of Modern IDPS

| Problem Without Correlation | Solution Provided by Correlation |
|---|---|
| **Alert Fatigue** — Analysts face millions of alerts daily | **Alert Fusion** — Reduces thousands of alerts into a few incidents |
| **High False Positive Rate (FPR)** of single sensors | **Multi-source Verification** — Confidence boosted via corroboration |
| **No attack context** — Alerts are isolated packets | **Attack Reconstruction** — Full multi-step kill chain visibility |
| **Missed slow/low-and-slow attacks** | **Temporal Correlation** — Links events across time windows |
| **Disjointed tools** — SIEM, IDS, FW speak different languages | **Normalization Layer** — Common event schema (CEF/IDMEF) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Performance Metric (KTU-favoured):**
> **True Positive Rate (TPR)** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ is a core exam term. After correlation, the **effective TPR** of the SOC improves because multiple weak signals fuse into one strong signal, even though the underlying sensors remain unchanged.

---

## 1.4 Scope Within KTU Module 3

The KTU 2024 scheme places Event Correlation as a **transition topic** between low-level *Detection Engines* (Modules 1 & 2) and high-level *Response Architecture* (Modules 4 & 5). You are expected to master:

1. Types of correlation
2. The correlation pipeline
3. Correlation rules and pattern languages
4. Causal vs. statistical correlation
5. Tools and standards (IDMEF, CEF, STIX/TAXII)
6. Challenges: clock skew, event storms, ambiguity

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Correlation Pipeline (NIST-Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> Every production correlation engine (ArcSight, QRadar, Splunk ES, Prelude) implements these five logical stages. Examiners frequently diagram this pipeline.

### Stage 1 — **Event Collection (Ingestion)**
- Pulls events from sensors via **syslog, SNMP traps, NetFlow, JDBC, API polling, or agents**.
- Sources: NIDS (Snort, Suricata), HIDS (OSSEC), Firewalls, WAF, EDR, Auth servers.
- Performance metric: **EPS (Events Per Second)**. Enterprise SIEMs are rated at **≥ 10,000 EPS** sustained.

### Stage 2 — **Normalization**
- Converts vendor-specific formats into a **Common Event Format (CEF)** or **Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format (IDMEF)**.
- Example: Snort alert "1:2012:5" + raw payload → IDMEF XML with fields `classificaton.text`, `source.ip`, `target.ip`, `severity`.

### Stage 3 — **Aggregation (Deduplication / Merging)**
- Combines identical or near-identical events from the same source targeting the same destination within a **time window** $W_a$.
- Aggregation rule:
$$\text{Aggregate if } \text{src}(E_1) = \text{src}(E_2) \;\wedge\; \text{dst}(E_1) = \text{dst}(E_2) \;\wedge\; |\text{class}(E_1) - \text{class}(E_2)| \leq \delta \;\wedge\; \Delta t \leq W_a$$

### Stage 4 — **Correlation (Pattern Matching / Causal Inference)**
- The intelligence layer. Applies rules, statistical tests, or causal models to detect **multi-event patterns** that individually would be low-significance.
- Output: **Correlated Incident Object** with confidence score, kill-chain phase, MITRE ATT&CK technique ID.

### Stage 5 — **Escalation & Reporting**
- Tier-1 SOC triage → Tier-2 investigation → Tier-3 incident response → CISO escalation.
- Notifications via email, SMS, SOAR webhook, or ticket creation in Jira/ServiceNow.

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Correlation Techniques

| # | Technique | Core Idea | Mathematical Basis | KTU-Favourite? |
|---|-----------|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Rule-Based Correlation** | IF–THEN rules on event predicates | First-order logic | ✅ Most asked |
| 2 | **State-Based / Finite-State Correlation** | System moves through states; events drive transitions | Finite Automata, DFA | ✅ |
| 3 | **Temporal Correlation** | Events correlated if they occur within a time window | Time-series windowing | ✅ |
| 4 | **Statistical Correlation** | Co-occurrence probability exceeds baseline | Bayesian, Markov, χ² test | ✅ |
| 5 | **Causal Correlation** | Event B could not occur without Event A | Attack graphs, Bayesian networks | ✅ |
| 6 | **Fuzzy Logic Correlation** | Handles imprecise/uncertain events | Fuzzy membership functions | ⚠️ |
| 7 | **Neural / ML Correlation** | Learns patterns from labelled corpora | RNN, LSTM, GNN | ⚠️ Modern |
| 8 | **Asset- / Vulnerability-Aware Correlation** | Weight alert by target's CVSS score | Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact | ✅ |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — at least **3 of these formulas appear annually** in either Part A derivations or Part B numerical sub-questions.

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True Positive Rate (Sensitivity / Recall) | $0.85$ – $0.99$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Positive Rate | $0.01$ – $0.10$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{PPV (Precision)} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | $0.90$ – $0.99$ | dimensionless |
| $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{PPV} \cdot \text{TPR}}{\text{PPV} + \text{TPR}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall | maximize | dimensionless |
| $\text{Risk} = P(\text{Threat}) \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Risk-prioritized alert scoring | $0$ – $10$ | CVSS-like |
| $\text{Confidence} = w_1 C_{\text{src}} + w_2 C_{\text{temp}} + w_3 C_{\text{caus}}$ | Composite correlation confidence | $0$ – $1$ | weighted sum |
| $\text{Alert Volume Reduction} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{incidents}}}{N_{\text{alerts}}}$ | Effectiveness of correlation | $0.95$ typical | ratio |
| $\text{Window}(W)$ | Time window for temporal correlation | $60$ – $3600$ s | seconds |
| $\tau$ | Correlation confidence threshold | $0.70$ – $0.95$ | dimensionless |

> **Critical Notation Rule:** In all KTU answers, the symbol $\tau$ refers to the **correlation threshold** — never confuse with $\tau$ as time delay. Always define it in the first line of your answer.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event Correlation is the **analytical brain of the SOC**. It directly enables:

1. **MITRE ATT&CK Kill-Chain Reconstruction** — Linking Initial Access → Execution → Persistence → Lateral Movement → Exfiltration.
2. **Insider Threat Detection** — Correlating badge-in, login, file access, and email events.
3. **Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) Detection** — Single-event detection is hopeless for APTs; only multi-stage correlation succeeds.
4. **Compliance Reporting** — PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, and RBI mandates require *correlated* incident evidence, not raw logs.
5. **SOAR Triggering** — Correlation incidents are the only alerts that should auto-trigger playbooks in a Security Orchestration, Automation & Response system.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **examination-grade**. KTU evaluators award full marks only when **every algebraic transition is shown** with a one-line textual justification. Skipping the intermediate step is the most common cause of partial marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Confidence-Weighted Correlation Score

A correlation engine evaluates three independent evidence streams and fuses them into a single confidence score $C$:

**Stream 1** — Source reliability $C_{\text{src}}$ (from sensor's historic accuracy).
**Stream 2** — Temporal co-occurrence $C_{\text{temp}}$ (based on a sliding window).
**Stream 3** — Causal feasibility $C_{\text{caus}}$ (based on attack graph reachability).

The composite confidence is defined as a **weighted linear fusion**:

$$C = w_1 C_{\text{src}} + w_2 C_{\text{temp}} + w_3 C_{\text{caus}}$$

subject to the constraint:

$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1, \quad w_i \geq 0$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the evidence streams as conditional probabilities:**

- $C_{\text{src}} = P(\text{alert} \mid \text{sensor reliable})$
- $C_{\text{temp}} = P(\text{co-occurrence} \mid \text{within } W)$
- $C_{\text{caus}} = P(\text{effect} \mid \text{cause occurred})$

These are *a priori* values configured by the SOC admin or learned from labelled historical incidents.

**Step 2 — Apply the linear opinion pool (LOF) assumption of independence:**

Under the assumption of conditionally independent evidence streams, the joint confidence is approximated by a weighted sum. This is the simplest, most defensible, and most KTU-friendly fusion rule.

$$C = w_1 C_{\text{src}} + w_2 C_{\text{temp}} + w_3 C_{\text{caus}}$$

**Step 3 — Enforce normalization:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i = 1$$

ensures $C \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 4 — Decision rule:**

$$\text{Incident} = \begin{cases} \text{Escalate to Tier-2 SOC} & \text{if } C \geq \tau \\ \text{Log for forensics} & \text{if } C < \tau \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (typical KTU sub-question):**

Given $C_{\text{src}} = 0.9$, $C_{\text{temp}} = 0.7$, $C_{\text{caus}} = 0.8$ and weights $w_1 = 0.4$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.3$ and threshold $\tau = 0.75$:

$$C = (0.4)(0.9) + (0.3)(0.7) + (0.3)(0.8)$$

$$C = 0.36 + 0.21 + 0.24 = 0.81$$

Since $C = 0.81 \geq \tau = 0.75$, the event is **escalated as a true incident**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Temporal Window Aggregation Function

Define $N(t)$ = number of raw alerts received in time window $[t, t + W]$. The **baseline rate** is the long-term average:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{T} N(t_i)$$

A **temporal burst** is detected when:

$$N(t) > \lambda + k\sigma$$

where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of $N$ and $k$ is the sensitivity multiplier (typically $k = 2$ or $3$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the empirical mean:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} N(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} (N(t) - \lambda)^2 \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Establish the burst threshold:**

$$N_{\text{threshold}} = \lambda + k\sigma$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule:**

$$\text{Burst detected} = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } N(t) \geq N_{\text{threshold}} \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Worked example:**

Over the last hour, $T = 60$ minutes, mean $\lambda = 50$ alerts/min, $\sigma = 12$ alerts/min, $k = 3$.

$$N_{\text{threshold}} = 50 + (3)(12) = 50 + 36 = 86 \text{ alerts/min}$$

A measured $N(t) = 110$ alerts/min is a **burst**, and is correlated as a possible **DDoS reconnaissance** pattern.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Risk-Weighted Alert Scoring (Vulnerability-Aware Correlation)

The classic **NIST 800-30 risk formula**:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

is applied per correlated incident. An alert on a non-vulnerable system is suppressed; an alert on a critical system is escalated.

**Step 1 — Threat Probability $T$** is derived from the alert's confidence score $C$:

$$T = C$$

**Step 2 — Vulnerability $V$** is the CVSS base score normalized to $[0, 1]$:

$$V = \frac{\text{CVSS}_{\text{base}}}{10}$$

**Step 3 — Impact $I$** is the business criticality weight of the asset:

$$I \in \{0.1, 0.5, 1.0\}$$

**Step 4 — Compute risk $R$:**

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:**

A correlated incident with $T = 0.81$, target CVSS = $8.5$, asset criticality $I = 1.0$:

$$R = 0.81 \times \frac{8.5}{10} \times 1.0 = 0.81 \times 0.85 = 0.6885$$

Since $R \geq 0.6$, the incident is **High Risk** and requires immediate escalation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Strengths & Weaknesses of Correlation Techniques

| Technique | Strength | Weakness | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rule-Based | Simple, explainable, fast | Brittle; rule explosion | Known signature attacks |
| State-Based | Models multi-step attacks | Requires state maintenance | Kill-chain reconstruction |
| Temporal | Catches slow-burn attacks | Sensitive to clock skew | DDoS, brute-force |
| Statistical | Detects novel attacks | False positives on outliers | Anomaly correlation |
| Causal | Highly accurate | Computationally expensive | APT detection |
| ML-Based | Scales to big data | "Black box" — non-explainable | UEBA, SOC analytics |
| Fuzzy | Handles uncertainty | Membership tuning hard | Mixed-quality feeds |
| Asset-Aware | Prioritizes real risk | Requires CMDB maintenance | Business-critical infra |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
