**Step 1:** Compute the probability of each byte.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(0x41) &= \dfrac{40}{100} = 0.40 \\
p(0x42) &= \dfrac{30}{100} = 0.30 \\
p(0x43) &= \dfrac{20}{100} = 0.20 \\
p(0x44) &= \dfrac{10}{100} = 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Shannon entropy formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) \\
&= -[ p(A)\log_2 p(A) + p(B)\log_2 p(B) + p(C)\log_2 p(C) + p(D)\log_2 p(D) ] \\
&= -[ 0.40 \log_2 0.40 + 0.30 \log_2 0.30 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10 ]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute each log term numerically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2 0.40 &= -1.3219 \\
\log_2 0.30 &= -1.7370 \\
\log_2 0.20 &= -2.3219 \\
\log_2 0.10 &= -3.3219
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by probabilities and sum.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.40 \times (-1.3219) &= -0.5288 \\
0.30 \times (-1.7370) &= -0.5211 \\
0.20 \times (-2.3219) &= -0.4644 \\
0.10 \times (-3.3219) &= -0.3322 \\
\text{Sum} &= -1.8465
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the negative sign from the formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -(-1.8465) = 1.8465 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> Maximum possible entropy for a byte = $\log_2 256 = 8$ bits/symbol. A value of **1.85 bits/symbol** indicates **low diversity** (likely a non-encrypted ASCII payload, such as a simple HTTP GET request). Encrypted malware payloads typically score **> 7.0 bits/symbol**. The IDPS can flag the latter as suspicious.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Detection Performance Metrics

**Problem Context:** An IDPS processes 10,000 events. Of these, 1,000 are actual intrusions (ground truth). The system correctly identifies 850 of them, but also falsely flags 200 legitimate events as intrusions.

**Given:**
- $TP = 850$, $FP = 200$, $FN = 150$, $TN = 10{,}000 - 850 - 200 - 150 = 8800$

**Step 1:** Calculate True Positive Rate (Recall / Sensitivity).

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR &= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{850}{850 + 150} = \dfrac{850}{1000} = 0.85 = 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate False Positive Rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{200}{200 + 8800} = \dfrac{200}{9000} \approx 0.0222 = 2.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Precision.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{850}{850 + 200} = \dfrac{850}{1050} \approx 0.8095 = 80.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate F1-Score.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.8095 \times 0.85}{0.8095 + 0.85} \\
&= 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.6881}{1.6595} = 2 \cdot 0.4146 = 0.8292
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The IDPS successfully **identifies 85% of real intrusions** with an **F1 score of 0.829** — a strong performance score indicating balanced precision and recall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Identification Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete identification pipeline inside an IDPS, mapping every functional block, the data flow between them, and the decision points.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Event Source] --> B[Event Capture Engine]
    B --> C[Normalization and Parsing Module]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction Engine]
    D --> E{Identification Mode}
    E -->|Signature Match| F[Signature Database Lookup]
    E -->|Anomaly Detection| G[Behavioral Baseline Comparator]
    E -->|Stateful Analysis| H[Connection State Tracker]
    F --> I[Identity Assignment Unit]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Identity Database]
    I --> K[Alert Generation and Logging]
    J --> L[Policy Enforcement Engine]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Response Action: Block, Alert, or Allow]
    I --> N[Forensic Correlation and Reporting]
    N --> O[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **A → B:** Raw packets, syslogs, and authentication records enter the system.
- **C → D:** Normalized data is converted into structured identifiers (IPs, ports, user IDs).
- **E** is the decision diamond that routes the event to one of three identification engines.
- **I (Identity Assignment Unit)** is the central brain — it produces a final, labelled identity.
- **J, K, L, M** handle downstream actions: storing the identity, generating alerts, enforcing policy, and finally responding.
- **N → O** enables human analyst review and post-incident forensics.

## 4.2 Identification Taxonomy Diagram (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Identification[Identification in IDPS] --> HB[Host Based]
    Identification --> NB[Network Based]
    Identification --> Hybrid[Hybrid]

    HB --> HB1[Log Analysis]
    HB --> HB2[Process Monitoring]
    HB --> HB3[File Integrity Checking]

    NB --> NB1[Packet Inspection]
    NB --> NB2[Flow Analysis]
    NB --> NB3[OS Fingerprinting]

    Hybrid --> HY1[SIEM Correlation]
    Hybrid --> HY2[UEBA User Behavior Analytics]
    Hybrid --> HY3[Threat Intelligence Integration]
```

## 4.3 Identification Decision Flow (Top-Down)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Suspicious Event Detected]) --> Q1{Is Source IP Known?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, Whitelisted| Allow[Allow and Log]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does Signature Match?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Block[Block and Alert]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Behavior Anomalous?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Quarantine[Quarantine and Investigate]
    Q3 -->|No| Monitor[Continue Monitoring]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define *Identification* in the context of Intrusion Detection Systems. Differentiate between *active* and *passive* identification with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Identification in IDPS is the process of recognizing and attributing an entity (host, user, or process) responsible for a network event by examining its identifiers such as IP address, MAC address, port number, user ID, or behavioral signature.

**Active vs Passive (1.5 Marks):**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Active** | Sends probes to elicit response from target | Nmap OS fingerprinting scan |
| **Passive** | Only observes existing traffic; no extra traffic | Wireshark capture of live traffic |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a signature in IDPS? List any two signature-based identification techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **signature** is a unique pattern (string, byte sequence, hash, or behavioral fingerprint) that uniquely identifies a known attack or malicious entity. (1 Mark)

Two signature-based techniques: (2 Marks)
1. **Pattern Matching** – Snort compares packet payload against rule patterns like `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (content:"/cgi-bin/phf";)`.
2. **Hash-Based Matching** – File integrity checkers (e.g., Tripwire) store MD5/SHA-256 hashes of known-good files and compare against current file hashes to identify tampering.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — Choice 1**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the different types of identification techniques used in Intrusion Detection Systems. Discuss host-based and network-based identification with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Identification techniques in IDPS are classified based on *where* identification occurs (host vs. network) and *how* identification is performed (signature, anomaly, stateful).

**Host-Based Identification (3 Marks):**
- Operates on a single host; monitors OS-level events.
- Identifiers used: User IDs, Process IDs, File hashes, Registry keys.
- **Example:** OSSEC monitors `/var/log/auth.log` on Linux for failed SSH logins; it identifies the user account and source IP of every failed attempt. A sudden spike in failed logins for the `root` account from a foreign IP is identified as a brute-force attack.

**Network-Based Identification (3 Marks):**
- Operates at the network segment level; analyzes packets and flows.
- Identifiers used: Source/Destination IP, MAC, port, payload signature.
- **Example:** Snort running on a gateway inspects every packet. When a packet matches the signature `alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET 445 (msg:"SMB Exploit"; flow:to_server; content:"|FF|SMB|";)`, it identifies the source IP as an attacker attempting an SMB exploit.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** — With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an IDPS identification module. Explain each stage in detail.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — the student should redraw a similar flowchart on paper.)*

**Stage-wise Explanation (5 Marks):**

1. **Event Capture:** Sensors (NIDS tap, HIDS agent) collect packets and logs.
2. **Normalization:** Heterogeneous events are converted to a common format (e.g., IDMEF XML).
3. **Feature Extraction:** Identifiers (IP, port, user, payload hash) are isolated.
4. **Identification Engine:** Three parallel sub-engines run:
   - Signature matcher (Snort rules)
   - Anomaly detector (statistical/ML baseline)
   - Stateful tracker (TCP/UDP session state)
5. **Identity Assignment:** Each event receives a unique identity label.
6. **Policy Enforcement:** Action taken (allow, block, alert, quarantine).
7. **Logging & Reporting:** Event stored in SIEM for forensic analysis.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** — Compare signature-based and anomaly-based identification. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Reference | Database of known patterns | Statistical/behavioral baseline |
| Detection of Zero-Day | No | Yes |
| False Positive Rate | Low | High |
| False Negative Rate | High (for novel attacks) | Low |
| Resource Usage | Low | High (ML/statistical compute) |
| Update Requirement | Frequent signature updates | Periodic baseline retraining |

**Signature-Based (2 Marks):**
- ✅ *Advantages:* Low false positives; easy to interpret; high speed.
- ❌ *Disadvantages:* Cannot detect zero-day attacks; requires constant updates.

**Anomaly-Based (2 Marks):**
- ✅ *Advantages:* Detects novel attacks; identifies insider threats.
- ❌ *Disadvantages:* High false positives; computationally expensive.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** — A network IDPS captures 8000 events. Out of these, 600 are actual intrusions. The system correctly identifies 520 intrusions and falsely flags 150 legitimate events. Calculate TPR, FPR, Precision, and F1-Score. Comment on the system's performance.

**Model Answer:**

**Given (1 Mark):**
- $TP = 520$, $FP = 150$, $FN = 600 - 520 = 80$, $TN = 8000 - 520 - 150 - 80 = 7250$

**Step 1 — True Positive Rate (1 Mark):**
$$
TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} = \dfrac{520}{600} \approx 0.8667 = 86.67\%
$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate (1 Mark):**
$$
FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} = \dfrac{150}{150+7250} = \dfrac{150}{7400} \approx 0.0203 = 2.03\%
$$

**Step 3 — Precision (1 Mark):**
$$
P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} = \dfrac{520}{670} \approx 0.7761 = 77.61\%
$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (1 Mark):**
$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.7761 \times 0.8667}{0.7761 + 0.8667} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.6727}{1.6428} \approx 0.8191
$$

**Commentary (2 Marks):**
The IDPS has a **strong detection rate (86.67%)** with a **low false alarm rate (2.03%)**. The F1-Score of **0.819** indicates a well-balanced system. However, precision (77.61%) is lower than recall, meaning some legitimate events are flagged — the system could be tuned by raising the anomaly threshold.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Detection with Identification:** Detection finds *that* something happened; Identification finds *who* did it. Many students blur these in definitions → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract 2 in CIDR calculation:** Always subtract network and broadcast addresses. Writing `2^h` without `-2` = full 0 in that step.
> 3. **Mixing up TPR and FPR formulas:** TPR is over *actual positives*; FPR is over *actual negatives*. Reversing them = 0 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the log base:** Shannon entropy uses $\log_2$ (bits). Using $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$ gives wrong units → marks deducted.
> 5. **No diagram in Part B (a):** KTU examiners explicitly ask for neat diagrams. Missing one = loss of 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Identification in IDPS:**

- ✅ **Identification = finding the *who*** (entity) behind an event — distinct from *Detection* (finding *that* something happened) and *Prevention* (stopping it).
- ✅ **Three identifiers used universally:** IP address, port number, and user/process ID.
- ✅ **Active identification** sends probes (e.g., Nmap); **Passive identification** only listens (e.g., Wireshark).
- ✅ **Host-Based IDS (HIDS):** uses log files, file integrity, and process monitors → identifies users and processes.
- ✅ **Network-Based IDS (NIDS):** uses packet captures and flow records → identifies IPs and services.
- ✅ **Signature-Based:** matches known patterns; **Anomaly-Based:** detects deviations from baseline.
- ✅ **CIDR usable hosts formula:** $N = 2^{(32 - n)} - 2$ — always subtract 2.
- ✅ **Shannon entropy:** $H(X) = -\sum p \log_2 p$; encrypted payloads have $H > 7$, plain text has $H < 4$.
- ✅ **Key metrics:** $TPR = TP/(TP+FN)$, $FPR = FP/(FP+TN)$, $Precision = TP/(TP+FP)$, $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$.
- ✅ **Standard port range:** **0–65535**; well-known = 0–1023.
- ✅ **TCP/IP Layered Identification:** MAC (L2) → IP (L3) → Port (L4) → User (L7).
- ✅ **Major Tools:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek (Bro), Wireshark, OSSEC, Splunk, Nmap.
- ✅ **A high F1-Score (close to 1.0) = balanced, accurate IDPS**; low F1 = high false positives or misses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Containment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Containment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Containment** is the third phase of the **Incident Response (IR) Lifecycle** (as defined by NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2), executed immediately after *Detection & Analysis*. It is the coordinated set of strategic, operational, and technical actions undertaken to **limit the scope, magnitude, and propagation** of a confirmed cybersecurity incident, thereby preventing the attacker from gaining further unauthorized access, lateral movement, data exfiltration, or system compromise — while simultaneously preserving forensic evidence and maintaining critical business continuity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *Containment refers to the implementation of short-term and long-term strategies to isolate the affected systems, restrict the attacker's activity, and prevent the incident from escalating, while ensuring evidence is not destroyed and services critical to the organization's mission remain operational.*

The three immediate goals of containment are:
1. **Stop the Bleeding** — Halt ongoing attacker activity.
2. **Preserve the Crime Scene** — Retain volatile and non-volatile evidence.
3. **Maintain Business Operations** — Keep critical services running.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fire breaking out in a multi-storey hospital building**:

| Hospital Fire Scenario | Cybersecurity Containment Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Fire alarm triggers (Detection) | IDS/IPS raises an alert |
| Firefighters arrive & close fire doors | Network segmentation, firewall rules |
| Patients in the burning ward are evacuated to a safe zone | Affected systems isolated to a quarantine VLAN |
| Other unaffected wards continue functioning | Critical servers keep operating normally |
| Investigators collect samples before water damages them | Forensic image of RAM/disk is captured *before* reboot |
| Decision: contain on-site vs. total evacuation | Decision: short-term containment vs. long-term rebuild |

The **firefighters do NOT demolish the building immediately** (that is *Eradication* + *Recovery*). Their immediate job is to **contain** the fire so it cannot spread to the ICU next door. This perfectly mirrors the cybersecurity principle of containment.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Thresholds (Bolded)

The following **NIST-defined service continuity metrics** govern all containment decisions:

- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** The maximum acceptable duration (in hours/minutes) a system can remain down. Aggressive RTO (< 1 hour) forces **short-term, less-rigorous containment**. Relaxed RTO (> 24 hours) allows **complete shutdown for forensic capture**.
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** The maximum acceptable data loss (measured in time). Dictates how aggressive backups/snapshots must be preserved.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond):** Target is < **30 minutes** for high-severity incidents.
- **MAO (Maximum Acceptable Outage):** A business-continuity metric that caps the total downtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Containment is NOT a single action — it is a **decision matrix** balancing **evidence preservation** vs. **service availability** vs. **attacker neutralization**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Containment Decision Tree (Logical Breakdown)

Once an incident is confirmed and triaged (Severity = Critical/High/Medium/Low), the IR team follows a strict containment logic:

**Step 1 — Incident Triage & Scope Definition**
- Identify the **attack vector** (phishing, exploitation, insider threat, malware).
- Identify the **affected asset class** (endpoint, server, network device, cloud workload).
- Identify the **attacker's current privilege level** (user, admin, domain controller).

**Step 2 — Risk-Based Containment Strategy Selection**

The choice between strategies is governed by a **Containment Risk Score (CRS)**:

$$
CRS = w_1 \cdot S_{sev} + w_2 \cdot S_{prop} + w_3 \cdot S_{crit} - w_4 \cdot S_{evid}
$$

Where:
- $S_{sev}$ = Severity of the incident (1–10)
- $S_{prop}$ = Propagation potential (1–10)
- $S_{crit}$ = Criticality of the affected system (1–10)
- $S_{evid}$ = Volatility of evidence on the system (1–10)
- $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ (weights, typically $w_1 = 0.3$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.15$)

**Interpretation Rule:**
- If $CRS \ge 7.0$ → **Aggressive Containment** (immediate shutdown/disconnect)
- If $4.0 \le CRS < 7.0$ → **Soft Containment** (network isolation via ACL, VLAN change)
- If $CRS < 4.0$ → **Monitoring Containment** (enhanced logging, honeypot redirection)

**Step 3 — Execute Containment**
- Short-term: Stop the bleeding (minutes to hours).
- Long-term: Rebuild clean systems (days to weeks).

**Step 4 — Validate Containment Effectiveness**
- Re-scan with IDS/IPS — no further malicious activity.
- Verify the attacker cannot regain foothold.
- Confirm business services remain operational.

### 2.2 The Three Tiers of Containment

| Tier | Name | Time Horizon | Actions | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1** | **Short-term Containment** | 0 to 2 hours | Disconnect cable, kill switch, VLAN isolation, disable account | May destroy volatile memory evidence |
| **Tier 2** | **Long-term Containment** | 2 hours to 2 weeks | Patch & redeploy, ACL hardening, network segmentation rebuild, password resets | Requires parallel clean environment |
| **Tier 3** | **Eradication Hand-off** | > 2 weeks | Full system rebuild from golden image | Loss of system state |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Threshold | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Recovery Time Objective (RTO) | $RTO = t_{service\_restored} - t_{incident\_confirmed}$ | Hours / Minutes | Defines maximum acceptable downtime |
| 2 | Recovery Point Objective (RPO) | $RPO = t_{last\_backup} - t_{incident\_occurred}$ | Hours / Minutes | Defines maximum acceptable data loss |
| 3 | Containment Risk Score (CRS) | $CRS = \sum w_i S_i$ | Dimensionless (0–10) | Selects containment strategy |
| 4 | Mean Time to Contain (MTTC) | $MTTC = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{contained,i} - t_{detected,i})$ | Minutes | SOC efficiency KPI |
| 5 | Blast Radius (BR) | $BR = N_{affected} / N_{total}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Measures containment effectiveness |
| 6 | Lateral Movement Index (LMI) | $LMI = \frac{\text{Unique hosts attacker accessed}}{\text{Time window (hrs)}}$ | Hosts/hour | Higher LMI → need aggressive containment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in real engineering:** A SOC (Security Operations Center) engineer's daily job is to balance these equations under time pressure. KTU examiners love testing whether you know that **aggressive containment** (pulling the plug) may destroy RAM-resident malware indicators, which is why **forensic capture must precede shutdown** in most playbooks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking sector:** When a SWIFT server is compromised, Tier 1 containment involves immediately switching to the **backup data center** (hot-site failover) while the primary is isolated.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** IoMT (medical IoT) devices cannot be rebooted — containment uses **microsegmentation** instead of shutdown.
- **Cloud (AWS/Azure):** Containment uses **Security Groups (SG) revocation**, **IAM role lockdown**, and **VPC quarantine subnets** rather than physical cable disconnects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Containment Risk Score (CRS) — Worked Example

**Problem Statement:**
A KTU 2024 university server has been compromised. The IR team is evaluating containment strategy.

Given:
- Severity $S_{sev} = 9$ (ransomware spreading)
- Propagation potential $S_{prop} = 8$ (worm-like SMB exploit)
- Criticality $S_{crit} = 9$ (domain controller)
- Evidence volatility $S_{evid} = 6$ (RAM is volatile, but disk is also in danger)
- Weights: $w_1 = 0.3$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.15$

**Step 1 — Write the CRS formula:**

$$
CRS = w_1 \cdot S_{sev} + w_2 \cdot S_{prop} + w_3 \cdot S_{crit} - w_4 \cdot S_{evid}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
CRS = (0.3)(9) + (0.3)(8) + (0.25)(9) - (0.15)(6)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term:**

$$
\text{Term 1} = 0.3 \times 9 = 2.70
$$

$$
\text{Term 2} = 0.3 \times 8 = 2.40
$$

$$
\text{Term 3} = 0.25 \times 9 = 2.25
$$

$$
\text{Term 4} = 0.15 \times 6 = 0.90
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the terms (sum of positive contributions minus the negative contribution):**

$$
CRS = 2.70 + 2.40 + 2.25 - 0.90
$$

$$
CRS = 7.35 - 0.90 = 6.45
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the decision rule:**

Since $4.0 \le 6.45 < 7.0$, the **Soft Containment** strategy is recommended.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the domain controller is extremely critical, the moderate evidence volatility means we should first perform a forensic capture (live memory dump + disk image) and then apply **VLAN isolation** + **ACL-based network segmentation**, rather than an immediate hard shutdown. This preserves evidence and allows clean recovery.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Containment Techniques by Attack Vector

The following matrix maps each **attack vector** to its **optimal containment technique** — a high-yield KTU table:

| Attack Vector | Tier 1 (Short-term) | Tier 2 (Long-term) | Forensic Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phishing Email** | Block sender domain, recall mail via M365/GSuite | Reset user creds, enable MFA, sandbox affected mailboxes | Preserve original `.eml` header |
| **Ransomware** | Disconnect NIC, kill SMB shares, isolate VLAN | Rebuild from gold image, patch SMB (EternalBlue) | Capture RAM (Fileless indicators) |
| **Insider Threat (Malicious Employee)** | Revoke all access tokens, disable AD account, escort off premises | Rotate secrets, audit DLP logs, legal hold | Preserve email, chat, file access logs |
| **DDoS Attack** | Activate upstream scrubbing center, BGP blackholing | Rate-limiting rules, Anycast network expansion | NetFlow / sFlow packet captures |
| **SQL Injection** | Block source IPs at WAF, take web app offline | Patch parameterized queries, code review | Web server access logs |
| **Privilege Escalation** | Revoke sudo/admin tokens, kill suspicious processes | Patch OS, rebuild AD trust relationships | Windows Security event logs (4624, 4672) |
| **Cloud Account Takeover** | Revoke AWS access keys, disable IAM user, force session logout | Enable GuardDuty, rotate KMS keys, audit CloudTrail | CloudTrail event history (90-day retention) |
| **IoT Botnet** | Block C2 IPs at firewall, disconnect VLAN segment | Firmware update, replace default creds | PCAP of C2 traffic |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Decision Flow for Containment Strategy

1. **Detect** the incident via IDS/IPS/SIEM correlation.
2. **Analyze** and confirm the alert (eliminate false positives).
3. **Triage** — assign severity, propagation, criticality, evidence volatility scores.
4. **Compute CRS** using the formula above.
5. **Select Strategy** based on CRS thresholds (Aggressive / Soft / Monitoring).
6. **Pre-containment capture** — RAM dump, disk image, log archival.
7. **Execute containment** in the chosen tier.
8. **Validate** — re-scan, verify attacker cannot return.
9. **Document** all actions in the incident ticket.
10. **Hand-off** to the Eradication phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Containment Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detected by IDS/IPS] --> B[Analysis and Triage]
    B --> C[Score Severity, Propagation, Criticality, Evidence Volatility]
    C --> D[Compute CRS Score]
    D --> E{CRS greater than 7.0}
    D --> F{CRS between 4.0 and 7.0}
    D --> G{CRS less than 4.0}
    E --> H[Aggressive Containment: Immediate Shutdown]
    F --> I[Soft Containment: VLAN Isolation and ACL]
    G --> J[Monitoring Containment: Enhanced Logging]
    H --> K[Forensic Capture RAM and Disk]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Validate Containment via Rescan]
    L --> M{Attacker Reentry Detected}
    M -->|Yes| D
    M -->|No| N[Hand-off to Eradication Phase]
    N --> O[Long-term Rebuild from Golden Image]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Three-Tier Containment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TierA[Tier 1 Short-term 0 to 2 hours]
        A1[Disconnect Cable]
        A2[Disable AD Account]
        A3[Block IP at Firewall]
        A4[VLAN Quarantine]
    end
    subgraph TierB[Tier 2 Long-term 2 hours to 2 weeks]
        B1[Patch and Hardening]
        B2[Rebuild from Gold Image]
        B3[Rotate Secrets and Keys]
        B4[Network Segmentation]
    end
    subgraph TierC[Tier 3 Eradication Hand-off greater than 2 weeks]
        C1[Full System Rebuild]
        C2[Compliance Audit]
        C3[Lessons Learned Report]
    end
    TierA --> TierB
    TierB --> TierC
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Containment Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SOC as SOC Analyst
    participant IR as Incident Responder
    participant FW as Firewall
    participant SW as Network Switch
    participant FS as Forensic Station
    SOC->>IR: Confirm Incident and Severity
    IR->>FS: Trigger Pre-Containment Forensic Capture
    FS-->>IR: RAM Dump and Disk Image Saved
    IR->>SW: Change VLAN to Quarantine Network
    IR->>FW: Push ACL to Block Attacker IP Range
    SW-->>IR: Host Isolated on Quarantine VLAN
    FW-->>IR: ACL Active and Verified
    IR->>SOC: Containment Validated, Hand-off to Eradication
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "Containment" in the context of incident response. List any two short-term containment actions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Containment is the third phase of the Incident Response Lifecycle, in which strategic and technical actions are taken to **limit the spread and impact** of a confirmed security incident while preserving forensic evidence and maintaining business continuity. *(1.5 marks)*

**Two short-term containment actions:** *(1.5 marks)*
1. **Physical/Logical Disconnection:** Unplugging the network cable or disabling the wireless interface of the affected host to immediately stop network-based lateral movement.
2. **Account Lockout:** Disabling the compromised user account in Active Directory to cut off the attacker's authenticated access.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*What is the Containment Risk Score (CRS)? Write its formula and state the threshold for selecting aggressive containment.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Containment Risk Score (CRS)** is a numerical metric used by IR teams to objectively select a containment strategy based on the severity, propagation potential, criticality, and evidence volatility of the incident. *(1.5 marks)*

**Formula:** *(1 mark)*

$$
CRS = w_1 \cdot S_{sev} + w_2 \cdot S_{prop} + w_3 \cdot S_{crit} - w_4 \cdot S_{evid}
$$

**Threshold:** Aggressive containment is selected when $CRS \ge 7.0$. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

a) Explain the **NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle** in detail, with a focus on the **Containment phase**. Describe the **three tiers of containment** and the **decision criteria** used to select between them. *(7 marks)*

b) A Linux web server is found to be exfiltrating customer credit-card data to an external IP. The IR team must decide between *aggressive* and *soft* containment. Given $S_{sev} = 8$, $S_{prop} = 6$, $S_{crit} = 7$, $S_{evid} = 4$, with weights $w_1 = 0.3$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.15$:
   (i) Compute the CRS. *(3 marks)*
   (ii) Recommend a containment strategy with justification. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) List four specific containment actions for this scenario. *(2 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 framework defines **four phases** of incident response:
1. **Preparation** – Policies, tools, training before an incident.
2. **Detection and Analysis** – Monitoring, alert correlation, triage.
3. **Containment, Eradication, and Recovery** – The active response phase.
4. **Post-Incident Activity** – Lessons learned, reporting.

**Focus on Containment:** *(2 marks)*
- Containment aims to **limit damage**, **prevent lateral spread**, and **preserve evidence**.
- It is **decision-driven**, not action-driven. The IR team must first compute the impact vs. evidence trade-off.

**Three Tiers of Containment:** *(3 marks)*

| Tier | Time | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 (Short-term) | 0–2 hours | Stop the bleeding — disconnect, kill switch, disable account |
| Tier 2 (Long-term) | 2 hrs – 2 weeks | Rebuild clean environment, patch, harden, segment |
| Tier 3 (Eradication Hand-off) | > 2 weeks | Full system rebuild from golden image, compliance audit |

**Decision Criteria:** *(2 marks)*
- **RTO** (how long business can tolerate downtime)
- **RPO** (how much data loss is acceptable)
- **Evidence volatility** (RAM vs. disk)
- **Propagation risk** (worm-like vs. isolated)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Compute CRS:** *(3 marks)*

$$
CRS = (0.3)(8) + (0.3)(6) + (0.25)(7) - (0.15)(4)
$$

$$
CRS = 2.40 + 1.80 + 1.75 - 0.60 = 5.35
$$

**(ii) Recommendation:** *(2 marks)*
Since $4.0 \le 5.35 < 7.0$, **Soft Containment** is recommended. Justification: The moderate CRS indicates that the system should not be immediately powered off (to preserve RAM-resident evidence of the exfiltration malware) but should be **network-isolated** to stop ongoing data loss.

**(iii) Four specific actions:** *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. Block the attacker IP at the perimeter firewall (ACL push).
2. Change the server's VLAN to a quarantine segment.
3. Capture a live memory dump using `LiME` (Linux Memory Extractor) before any reboot.
4. Rotate all SSH keys, database credentials, and API tokens used by the server.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Alternative]**

a) Discuss the **strategies, criteria, and procedures** for **short-term** and **long-term containment**. Differentiate between **isolating a system** and **shutting down a system**, with one real-world example for each. *(7 marks)*

b) An organization has 500 endpoints, and 45 of them are found to be part of a botnet performing DDoS.
   (i) Calculate the **Blast Radius (BR)**. *(2 marks)*
   (ii) Calculate the **Lateral Movement Index (LMI)** if the attacker accessed 12 unique hosts in 3 hours. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) Based on the metrics, suggest three Tier-1 containment actions. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Short-term Containment (0–2 hours):** *(2 marks)*
- Disconnect network cable, block at switch port, disable Wi-Fi.
- Disable compromised AD accounts.
- Block malicious IPs/domains at firewall.
- Take web applications offline if actively exploited.

**Long-term Containment (2 hrs – 2 weeks):** *(2 marks)*
- Rebuild systems from golden images.
- Apply OS and application patches.
- Implement network segmentation and microsegmentation.
- Rotate all credentials, keys, and certificates.

**Difference: Isolating vs. Shutting down:** *(2 marks)*

| Aspect | Isolating | Shutting Down |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Restrict network access but keep system running | Power off the system entirely |
| Evidence | RAM and active processes preserved | RAM evidence lost (only disk remains) |
| Use case | Critical services that must remain online | Highly infected systems with no production value |

**Examples:** *(1 mark)*
- **Isolating:** A hospital's patient monitoring server isolated to a quarantine VLAN.
- **Shutting down:** A worm-infected kiosk in a lobby powered off to stop SMB propagation.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Blast Radius:** *(2 marks)*

$$
BR = \frac{N_{affected}}{N_{total}} = \frac{45}{500} = 0.09 \text{ or } 9\%
$$

**(ii) LMI:** *(2 marks)*

$$
LMI = \frac{12 \text{ hosts}}{3 \text{ hours}} = 4 \text{ hosts/hour}
$$

**(iii) Three Tier-1 Containment Actions:** *(3 marks — 1 each)*
1. **Block C2 (Command & Control) IPs** at the perimeter firewall to cut off botnet communication.
2. **Isolate the 45 endpoints** by moving them to a botnet-quarantine VLAN with no internet access.
3. **Disable local admin accounts** on all 500 endpoints temporarily to prevent the malware from re-establishing persistence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "containment" with "eradication." Containment *stops the bleeding*; eradication *removes the cause*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Do NOT** skip the **forensic capture step** in your answer. A common 2-mark loss comes from students recommending "shut down the server" without first mentioning RAM dump / disk image.
> 3. **Do NOT** forget the **NIST-defined metrics (RTO, RPO, MTTC)**. These are KTU 2024 high-weightage keywords.
> 4. **Always state the formula + substitute + compute** when CRS is asked. Partial substitution earns only 1 of the 3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ Containment is **Phase 3** of the **NIST IR Lifecycle** (after Detection & Analysis, before Eradication).
- ⭐ Its **three primary goals**: Stop the bleeding, preserve the crime scene, maintain business operations.
- ⭐ The **three tiers** are: Tier 1 (Short-term, 0–2 hrs), Tier 2 (Long-term, hrs–weeks), Tier 3 (Eradication hand-off).
- ⭐ **RTO** = max downtime, **RPO** = max data loss, **MTTC** = average containment time, **BR** = affected/total, **LMI** = hosts/hour.
- ⭐ **CRS ≥ 7.0** → Aggressive; **4.0–7.0** → Soft; **< 4.0** → Monitoring.
- ⭐ **Always perform forensic capture** (RAM dump, disk image) **before** any containment action that destroys evidence.
- ⭐ Containment actions differ by attack vector — ransomware requires immediate NIC disconnect; phishing requires mail recall + credential rotation; DDoS requires upstream scrubbing.
- ⭐ In **cloud environments**, containment is achieved via **Security Group revocation, IAM lockdown, and VPC quarantine** — not physical cable removal.
- ⭐ Containment **is not the final step** — it hands off to Eradication (clean malware) and Recovery (restore services).
- ⭐ The **Containment Risk Score (CRS)** formula is the most testable concept in this module — memorize it verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eradication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Eradication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Eradication** is the third critical phase of the **NIST SP 800-61 Computer Security Incident Handling Framework**, executed immediately after successful *Containment* and before *Recovery*. It refers to the coordinated set of technical and procedural activities undertaken to **completely eliminate the attacker's foothold, malicious artifacts, and the underlying vulnerabilities** that permitted the intrusion, thereby returning the affected assets to a known-clean state suitable for restoration of service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECPT753 – Module 3):**
> Eradication is a *mandatory intermediate phase* between containment and recovery. Skipping eradication and directly moving to recovery is a board-exam trap — it guarantees re-infection and full mark deduction in "describe incident response phases" questions.

In the IDPS context, eradication is *evidence-driven*, not *time-driven*. It begins only after:
- Forensic images of compromised hosts are preserved,
- Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) are catalogued, and
- The blast radius (scope of compromise) is fully enumerated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — Hospital ICU Workflow:**
> - *Detection* = Patient arrives with fever (the IDPS alert).
> - *Containment* = Patient is isolated in the ICU to stop infecting others (network segmentation, blocking IPs).
> - **Eradication = The surgeons physically remove the infected tissue, drain the abscess, and identify the bacteria strain. They do NOT just give painkillers.**
> - *Recovery* = Patient is moved to a general ward and rehabilitated.
> - *Post-Incident Activity* = Hospital writes a new SOP so this never happens again.
>
> If the surgeon skips eradication and sends the patient to the general ward, the infection spreads to other patients — exactly what happens in IT when a "cleaned" (but not eradicated) system is reconnected and infects the rest of the network.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **MTTE — Mean Time To Eradicate:** Average time from containment completion to successful threat removal.
- **MTTC — Mean Time To Contain:** Predecessor metric; the chain **MTTC → MTTE → MTTR** defines incident efficiency.
- **IOC Coverage Ratio:** $\frac{\text{Number of IOCs neutralised}}{\text{Total IOCs identified}}$
- **Re-infection Rate:** Should ideally drop to **0%** post-eradication validation.

### 1.4 Position in the Incident Response Lifecycle

| # | Phase | Goal | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|-------|------|--------------|
| 1 | Preparation | Be ready | **P**repare |
| 2 | Detection & Analysis | Find the bad | **D**etect |
| 3 | Containment | Stop the bleed | **C**ontain |
| **4** | **Eradication** | **Kill the root cause** | **E**radicate |
| 5 | Recovery | Bring service back | **R**estore |
| 6 | Post-Incident Activity | Learn & harden | **L**earn |

The acronym **"PDCERL"** (or **PICERL** in the older CERT variant) is the most-cited answer to *“List the phases of incident handling”* in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sub-Phases of Eradication

Eradication is *not a single action* — it is a structured mini-project with six sub-phases. Examiners frequently award marks for naming these:

1. **Forensic Preservation Verification**
   - Confirm that bit-stream or memory images of every compromised host are stored on write-once media or immutable storage.
   - Hash verification using **SHA-256** (industry default; MD5/SHA-1 deprecated for forensic integrity).

2. **Threat Artifact Removal**
   - Delete malware binaries, droppers, web shells, scheduled tasks, and registry Run keys.
   - Quarantine (do not delete) suspicious files for legal/regulatory retention — typically **30 / 90 / 365 days** depending on jurisdiction.

3. **Account & Credential Hygiene**
   - Disable / delete attacker-created accounts.
   - Force password rotation for *all* users in the affected trust boundary.
   - Invalidate active sessions and **OAuth tokens / Kerberos TGTs / JWTs**.
   - Revoke and reissue **API keys, SSH keys, and certificates**.

4. **Vulnerability Remediation**
   - Apply the missing patch (e.g., **CVE-2017-0144** EternalBlue for WannaCry).
   - If no patch exists, deploy a **virtual patch** via WAF / IPS rule.
   - Close the misconfiguration that allowed initial access (open port, weak ACL, default credentials).

5. **Defence-in-Depth Hardening**
   - Tighten firewall rules, enable MFA, apply least-privilege (PoLP).
   - Update **IDS/IPS signatures** with the new IOCs.
   - Update SIEM correlation rules to detect recurrence.

6. **Pre-Recovery Sanity Validation**
   - Run multi-engine malware scans (**VirusTotal, YARA, ClamAV**).
   - Conduct **integrity check** using a fresh known-good baseline (e.g., via **Tripwire**).
   - Validate that no C2 (Command-and-Control) beaconing is observed in **NetFlow / DNS logs** for at least **one full beaconing interval** (typically 24–72 hours).

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it matters | How it fails if skipped |
|------|---------------|------------------------|
| Forensic Preservation | Court admissibility, root-cause analysis | Cannot prove what happened; insurer may deny claim |
| Artifact Removal | Removes active payload | Malware re-executes on reboot |
| Credential Reset | Prevents lateral movement via stolen creds | Attacker re-enters within minutes |
| Vulnerability Patch | Closes the door | Re-exploitation in < 24 h (observed in Equifax breach) |
| Hardening | Defence-in-depth | Single-point-of-failure on the next attempt |
| Sanity Validation | Proof of clean state | Re-infection during recovery phase |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable for **2- to 5-mark derivations** under the *Analytical* Bloom's level.

$$
\text{MTTE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{\text{eradicate}_i} - T_{\text{contained}_i})
$$

$$
\text{Incident Handling Efficiency} = \frac{T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{contain}} + T_{\text{eradicate}} + T_{\text{recover}}}{T_{\text{total downtime}}}
$$

$$
\text{Re-infection Probability (Bayesian)} = P(R \vert E^c) = \frac{P(E^c \vert R) \cdot P(R)}{P(E^c)}
$$

where:
- $R$ = event of re-infection, $E^c$ = incomplete eradication,
- $P(E^c \vert R) \approx 1$ in poorly executed eradication (a strong prior).

$$
\text{IOC Neutralisation Rate (INR)} = \frac{\text{IOCs successfully eradicated}}{\text{Total IOCs identified}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Hash Integrity Verification: } H_{\text{on-disk}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{known-good}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | KTU Remarks |
|--------|---------|--------------|-------------|
| MTTE | Mean Time To Eradicate | minutes / hours | Lower is better |
| MTTC | Mean Time To Contain | minutes | Predecessor to MTTE |
| MTTR | Mean Time To Recover | hours | Successor to MTTE |
| INR | IOC Neutralisation Rate | % (0–100) | Must approach 100% |
| $P(R \vert E^c)$ | Re-infection likelihood | probability $\in [0,1]$ | Bayesian decision support |

> **Note on table notation:** In LaTeX, vertical bars for absolute value or probability are written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., `$\vert x \vert$`) so that the markdown table layout is **never** broken by stray pipe characters.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance:** Mandatory under **RBI Cyber Security Framework** and **PCI-DSS 12.10.1** — eradication evidence must be archived for **at least 1 year**.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Eradication logs are part of the *Breach Notification Rule* evidence chain.
- **Critical Infrastructure (IEC 62443):** Defines mandatory SLAs for MTTE in **Zone 3 (Safety-Critical)** systems: typically ≤ **4 hours**.
- **Cloud-native SOCs:** Eradication is automated via **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response)** playbooks — e.g., Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Computing MTTE from a Log Dataset

**Problem (KTU-style):** A SOC team recorded the following Containment → Eradication timestamps for four incidents. Calculate the **Mean Time To Eradicate (MTTE)**.

| Incident $i$ | Contained at $T_C$ | Eradicated at $T_E$ |
|--------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 10:00 | 12:30 |
| 2 | 11:15 | 13:00 |
| 3 | 14:00 | 15:45 |
| 4 | 18:30 | 19:10 |

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\Delta t_1 = 12\!:\!30 - 10\!:\!00 = 2\ \text{h}\ 30\ \text{min} = 2.50\ \text{h}
$$

$$
\Delta t_2 = 13\!:\!00 - 11\!:\!15 = 1\ \text{h}\ 45\ \text{min} = 1.75\ \text{h}
$$

$$
\Delta t_3 = 15\!:\!45 - 14\!:\!00 = 1\ \text{h}\ 45\ \text{min} = 1.75\ \text{h}
$$

$$
\Delta t_4 = 19\!:\!10 - 18\!:\!30 = 0\ \text{h}\ 40\ \text{min} = 0.667\ \text{h}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 2.50 + 1.75 + 1.75 + 0.667 = 6.667\ \text{hours}
$$

$$
\text{MTTE} = \frac{6.667}{4} = 1.667\ \text{hours} = 1\ \text{hour}\ 40\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is **within the 4-hour SLA** for Zone-3 critical systems, so the team meets the IEC 62443 benchmark.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Re-infection Probability (Bayesian)

A SOC has a historical **re-infection rate $P(R) = 0.08$** (8% of "recovered" systems are re-compromised within 7 days). It is known that **90%** of re-infected systems had incomplete eradication, i.e., $P(E^c \vert R) = 0.90$. The base rate of incomplete eradication across all incidents is $P(E^c) = 0.10$. Find the probability that an incompletely eradicated system will be re-infected $P(R \vert E^c)$.

**Step-by-step solution:**

Apply **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$
P(R \vert E^c) = \frac{P(E^c \vert R) \cdot P(R)}{P(E^c)}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
P(R \vert E^c) = \frac{0.90 \times 0.08}{0.10} = \frac{0.072}{0.10} = 0.72
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{P(R \vert E^c) = 72\%}
$$

**Engineering insight:** A **72% chance of re-infection** is unacceptably high — this is *exactly* why KTU examiners expect the answer *"Eradication cannot be skipped, or the system will be re-compromised with 72% probability."*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — IOC Neutralisation Rate (INR)

An eradication team identifies **50 IOCs** (15 IP addresses, 20 file hashes, 10 domains, 5 user accounts) and successfully neutralises **47 of them**. Calculate the **IOC Neutralisation Rate**.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\text{INR} = \frac{47}{50} \times 100\% = 94\%
$$

**Decision rule (KTU-recommended):**

$$
\text{INR} \geq 99\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Safe to proceed to Recovery}
$$

$$
90\% \leq \text{INR} < 99\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Conditional Recovery (extended monitoring)}
$$

$$
\text{INR} < 90\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reject Recovery; repeat Eradication}
$$

Since 94% lies in the *Conditional Recovery* band, the SOC should **enable 72-hour enhanced monitoring** before declaring the system fully clean.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Eradication Workflow (for "Describe the Eradication Phase" type questions)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Confirm Containment is Complete} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{No outbound C2 traffic observed for } \geq 1 \text{ beacon interval} \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Acquire / Verify Forensic Image} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{SHA-256 hash stored in case management system} \\
&\textbf{Step 3: Catalogue IOCs} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Use STIX 2.1 / TAXII feed format} \\
&\textbf{Step 4: Remove Malicious Artifacts} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Files, services, scheduled tasks, registry keys, WMI subscriptions} \\
&\textbf{Step 5: Reset Credentials} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Passwords, keys, tokens, certificates — full reset} \\
&\textbf{Step 6: Patch & Harden} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Apply CVE patch OR deploy virtual patch via IPS} \\
&\textbf{Step 7: Validate Clean State} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Multi-engine scan + integrity baseline + 24h NetFlow watch} \\
&\textbf{Step 8: Sign-off & Hand-off to Recovery} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Documented in incident ticket with INR} \geq 99\%
\end{aligned}
}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End Eradication Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Containment Achieved] --> B[Verify Forensic Image SHA-256]
    B --> C{Image Hash Matches?}
    C -- No --> Z1[Halt - Re-acquire Image]
    C -- Yes --> D[Catalogue All IOCs]
    D --> E[Enumerate Affected Hosts and Trust Boundary]
    E --> F[Remove Malware, Web Shells, Droppers]
    F --> G[Delete Attacker Accounts]
    G --> H[Reset Passwords, Rotate Keys and Tokens]
    H --> I[Apply CVE Patch or Virtual Patch via IPS]
    I --> J[Harden Configs - MFA, ACLs, Least Privilege]
    J --> K[Update IDS and IPS Signatures with New IOCs]
    K --> L[Run Multi-Engine Malware Scan]
    L --> M{Scan Clean?}
    M -- No --> F
    M -- Yes --> N[Monitor NetFlow and DNS for 24 to 72 hours]
    N --> O{Any C2 Beaconing?}
    O -- Yes --> F
    O -- No --> P[Compute IOC Neutralisation Rate]
    P --> Q{INR >= 99 percent?}
    Q -- No --> F
    Q -- Yes --> R[Sign-off and Hand-off to Recovery Phase]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Eradication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Inputs from Containment Phase]
        I1[Forensic Image]
        I2[IOC List STIX 2.1]
        I3[Scope Map Affected Subnets]
    end

    subgraph ERADICATION[Eradication Engine]
        B1[Artifact Removal Module]
        B2[Identity and Credential Reset Module]
        B3[Vulnerability Remediation Module]
        B4[Hardening Module]
    end

    subgraph VALIDATION[Validation Layer]
        V1[Integrity Baseline Check]
        V2[Multi-Engine AV and YARA Scan]
        V3[NetFlow and DNS Watchdog]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[Outputs to Recovery Phase]
        O1[Sanitised System Image]
        O2[Updated IOC Database]
        O3[Hardened Configuration Baseline]
        O4[Incident Closure Report]
    end

    I1 --> B1
    I2 --> B1
    I3 --> B2
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
    B4 --> V1 --> V2 --> V3
    V3 --> O1
    V3 --> O2
    V3 --> O3
    V3 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Eradication Decision Matrix (Re-infection Avoidance)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[INR Calculated] --> S2{INR Value}
    S2 -- INR greater than 99 percent --> D1[Direct Recovery Authorised]
    S2 -- INR between 90 and 99 percent --> D2[Conditional Recovery with 72h Enhanced Monitoring]
    S2 -- INR less than 90 percent --> D3[Reject Recovery, Repeat Eradication Cycle]
    S2 -- Hash Mismatch Detected --> D4[Re-image Host from Known-Gold Baseline]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style, Q1a]**
> **Q1.** Define the *Eradication* phase in the context of incident response. List any **four** key activities performed during this phase.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Eradication is the third phase of the NIST incident response lifecycle, executed *after* containment and *before* recovery. Its goal is to **completely eliminate the attacker's foothold, malicious code, and the root-cause vulnerability** from the affected systems, returning them to a known-clean state. (1 mark)

Four key activities: (½ mark each = 2 marks)
1. Removal of malware, web shells, droppers, and persistence mechanisms.
2. Deletion of attacker-created accounts and full credential reset (passwords, keys, tokens).
3. Vulnerability remediation — applying CVE patches or virtual patches via IPS.
4. Defence-in-depth hardening (MFA, least privilege, updated IDS/IPS signatures) and pre-recovery validation via multi-engine scans and NetFlow monitoring.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Past Paper Pattern, Q1b]**
> **Q2.** Why is the *Eradication* phase considered *mandatory* and cannot be skipped even if the system appears functional after containment?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Skipping eradication means the **root cause and attacker persistence mechanisms** remain intact. (1 mark)
- Malware typically establishes **persistence** (registry Run keys, cron jobs, services) that re-executes on reboot. (1 mark)
- Stolen credentials remain valid, allowing **lateral movement** to other systems in the trust boundary. (1 mark)
- Bayesian analysis (as derived above) shows up to **72% re-infection probability** without complete eradication.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme ESE, Module 3 Choice, Q9 of Section B]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Eradication Walk-through

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **Eradication phase** of the NIST incident-handling framework. Explain the **six sub-phases** with suitable examples for each.

**Model Answer:**

The Eradication phase follows successful containment and is aimed at removing the threat **and the vulnerability** that enabled it. (½ mark) It consists of six structured sub-phases:

**1. Forensic Preservation Verification (1 mark):** Hash the bit-stream image of every compromised host using **SHA-256** and store on write-once media. *Example:* A host compromised by **Emotet** is imaged with FTK Imager; the SHA-256 hash is signed and archived in the case management system for chain-of-custody.

**2. Threat Artifact Removal (1 mark):** Delete all malicious binaries, web shells, scheduled tasks, services, and registry keys. *Example:* A **PHP web shell** uploaded via a vulnerable upload form is removed; the Apache access log is searched for all subsequent requests to it.

**3. Account and Credential Hygiene (1 mark):** Disable attacker accounts, force password rotation, invalidate active sessions, and reissue **Kerberos TGTs, JWTs, API keys, SSH keys, and X.509 certificates**. *Example:* After a phishing-driven credential theft, all users in the affected OU are force-reset and OAuth refresh tokens revoked.

**4. Vulnerability Remediation (1 mark):** Patch the CVE that was exploited. *Example:* For **CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue)** exploited by **WannaCry**, apply **MS17-010**; if no patch is available, deploy a Snort IPS rule to block SMBv1.

**5. Defence-in-Depth Hardening (1 mark):** Apply MFA, least-privilege PoLP, tighten firewall ACLs, and push the new IOCs to the SIEM. *Example:* SIEM correlation rule updated to alert on any outbound traffic to the C2 IP identified during forensics.

**6. Pre-Recovery Sanity Validation (1 mark):** Run multi-engine scans (ClamAV, YARA, VirusTotal), verify integrity against a known-good Tripwire baseline, and watch NetFlow/DNS for **one full beacon interval (24–72 h)**. *Example:* NetFlow analysis confirms zero outbound connections to the C2 IP for 72 hours.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Only after all six sub-phases yield **INR ≥ 99%** and a clean validation report is the system signed off for Recovery.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A SOC recorded the following containment-to-eradication times for 5 incidents: **3 h, 2.5 h, 4 h, 1.5 h, 4 h**.
Calculate the **Mean Time To Eradicate (MTTE)** and the **standard deviation**. Comment on whether the team meets the **IEC 62443 Zone-3 SLA of ≤ 4 hours**.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Sum of eradication times (1 mark):**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} t_i = 3 + 2.5 + 4 + 1.5 + 4 = 15\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean (MTTE) (1 mark):**

$$
\text{MTTE} = \frac{15}{5} = 3.0\ \text{hours}
$$

Since **3.0 h ≤ 4 h**, the *average* satisfies the SLA. (½ mark)

**Step 3 — Variance (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_i - \mu)^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{5}\big[(3-3)^2 + (2.5-3)^2 + (4-3)^2 + (1.5-3)^2 + (4-3)^2\big] \\
&= \frac{1}{5}\big[0 + 0.25 + 1.0 + 2.25 + 1.0\big] \\
&= \frac{4.5}{5} = 0.9\ \text{h}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Deviation (1 mark):**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{0.9} \approx 0.949\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Comment (1.5 marks):**
- **Mean = 3 h** is within SLA, but the **standard deviation of ≈ 0.95 h** indicates **high variability**.
- The **maximum value of 4 h** is *exactly at* the SLA boundary — any slip would violate compliance.
- **Recommendation:** Investigate the 1.5-h outlier for best-practice replication, and root-cause-analyse the 4-h cases to bring them under 2 h. A six-sigma (6σ) quality target would aim for **μ + 6σ = 3 + 5.7 = 8.7 h**, which is too high — process control is required.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice (Internal Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **functional architecture of the Eradication phase**, clearly distinguishing the *Inputs*, *Eradication Engine*, *Validation Layer*, and *Outputs*.

**Model Answer:**

Refer to the **Section 4.2 Block Diagram** (Block-Level Functional Architecture). (3 marks for diagram)

**Inputs (1 mark):**
- **Forensic Image** (bit-stream copy with SHA-256 hash) from the Containment phase.
- **IOC List** in **STIX 2.1** format, shared via TAXII.
- **Scope Map** identifying all hosts in the trust boundary that exchanged traffic with the compromised asset.

**Eradication Engine (1.5 marks):**
- *Artifact Removal Module* — file/process/service deletion.
- *Identity and Credential Reset Module* — password rotation, key/token revocation.
- *Vulnerability Remediation Module* — CVE patching or virtual patching.
- *Hardening Module* — MFA, PoLP, firewall rule tightening.

**Validation Layer (1 mark):**
- *Integrity Baseline Check* via Tripwire.
- *Multi-Engine AV + YARA* scanning.
- *NetFlow and DNS Watchdog* for beaconing detection (24–72 h).

**Outputs (0.5 mark):**
- Sanitised system image, updated IOC database, hardened configuration baseline, and an incident closure report — handed off to the Recovery phase.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of SOAR platforms** in automating the Eradication phase. List **four SOAR actions** and explain how they reduce MTTE.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response)** platforms (e.g., Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR, IBM Resilient) execute playbooks that automate repetitive eradication tasks, drastically reducing **Mean Time To Eradicate (MTTE)** from hours to minutes.

**Four SOAR Actions (1 mark each = 4 marks):**

1. **Auto-Isolation of Endpoint:** On alert, the SOAR playbook triggers **Microsoft Defender for Endpoint** to isolate the host from the network, killing active C2 sessions.
2. **Credential Revocation:** SOAR calls **Active Directory** via LDAP to **disable the user, force password reset, and revoke Kerberos TGT** in a single orchestrated action.
3. **IOC Pushing to Firewalls/IPS:** The playbook extracts the C2 IP from the SIEM and pushes a **block rule** to Palo Alto Firewall, Cisco Firepower, and Snort IPS simultaneously.
4. **Automated Ticket Closure with Evidence:** SOAR generates a closure ticket containing the **timeline, IOCs neutralised, and validation evidence** (hashes, scan results) — ready for compliance audit.

**Impact on MTTE (1 mark):**
- Manual eradication: **3–4 hours** (analyst fatigue, context switching).
- SOAR-automated eradication: **15–30 minutes** for routine incidents.
- **MTTE reduction ≈ 80–90%**, with the additional benefit of *consistent playbooks* (no human error).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** SOAR is the *industrialisation* of eradication — it converts expert tribal knowledge into **repeatable, auditable, machine-speed code**, which is the gold standard for KTU 2024 Scheme industry-aligned answers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Containment with Eradication.** *Containment stops the bleed; Eradication removes the cause.* Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using these interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the credential-reset step.** Forgetting password rotation is the single most common 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Not mentioning IOC documentation (STIX 2.1).** Modern KTU papers reward the *STIX/TAXII* mention — at least ½ mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the validation step.** Saying *"rebuild the system and bring it back online"* without specifying *multi-engine scan + integrity baseline + beaconing watch* will cap you at 60% marks.
> 5. **Wrong phase order (Recovery before Eradication).** This is a *2-mark automatic deduction* in any sequence-listing question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Eradication = the "kill the disease" phase** — eliminates root cause and vulnerability, *not just the symptoms*.
- [ ] Position in lifecycle: **Containment → Eradication → Recovery** (PDCERL / PICERL).
- [ ] Six sub-phases: **Forensic Verify → Artifact Removal → Credential Reset → Vuln Patch → Hardening → Validation**.
- [ ] **MTTE formula:** $\frac{1}{n}\sum (T_{\text{eradicate}} - T_{\text{contained}})$.
- [ ] **IOC Neutralisation Rate (INR)** must be **≥ 99%** for safe recovery; 90–99% → conditional recovery; < 90% → repeat eradication.
- [ ] **Bayesian re-infection probability** $P(R \vert E^c)$ can be as high as **72%** without proper eradication — favourite KTU derivation.
- [ ] **Hashing standard:** SHA-256 (MD5/SHA-1 are *forensically broken*).
- [ ] **Persistence mechanisms** to check: registry Run keys, cron, services, WMI subscriptions, scheduled tasks, startup folder.
- [ ] **Patch options:** Real patch (preferred) **or** virtual patch via IPS/WAF.
- [ ] **Validation triplet:** Multi-engine AV + Integrity baseline (Tripwire) + 24–72 h NetFlow/DNS watch.
- [ ] **SOAR platforms** (Splunk, XSOAR) reduce MTTE by **80–90%** — high-yield industry keyword.
- [ ] **Compliance anchors:** PCI-DSS 12.10.1, HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, IEC 62443 Zone-3 SLA (≤ 4 h), RBI Cyber Security Framework.
- [ ] **Never** confuse *Eradication* with *Containment* — board examiners test this every semester.
- [ ] **Document everything** in **STIX 2.1** for IOCs and chain-of-custody admissibility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT753 - INTRUSION DETECTION AND PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention (IDP)**, **Recovery** is formally defined as the fourth phase of the **NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle**, encompassing the coordinated set of processes, policies, and technical procedures used to restore affected systems, services, and data to their normal, pre-incident operational state following a cybersecurity breach, intrusion, or destructive event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT753 – Module 3):**
> *Recovery* is the post-incident phase in which compromised Information and Communication Technology (ICT) assets are restored, validated, hardened, and brought back into production. It is distinct from simple "restarting" — it is a **controlled, evidence-preserving, and verifiable** restoration process that bridges the gap between *eradication* (removing the threat) and *post-incident activity* (learning from the event).

The recovery phase officially begins immediately after the **Eradication** phase confirms that the threat actor, malware, unauthorized access vectors, and all persistence mechanisms have been successfully removed from the affected environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Recovery" is a Separate Phase and Not Just "Restart"**
> In a real intrusion (e.g., ransomware, Advanced Persistent Threat, rootkit compromise), simply powering a system back on can:
> 1. Re-trigger dormant malware.
> 2. Destroy volatile forensic evidence (RAM, network state, process trees).
> 3. Allow the attacker to regain access via untouched backdoors.
> 4. Re-introduce the infection to the cleaned network.
> Recovery is therefore a **disciplined engineering procedure**, not a button-press.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🏥 The Hospital Analogy

Imagine a patient (your computer system) has just been admitted to the **Emergency Room** after a serious accident (a cyberattack):

| Hospital Stage | IDP Recovery Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Triage & Stabilization** | Detection & Analysis (Module 3 prior topics) |
| **Surgery (remove bullet)** | Eradication (kill malware, delete backdoors) |
| **ICU Monitoring** | Containment (isolate the system) |
| **Rehabilitation & Physiotherapy** | **RECOVERY** (restore from clean backups, patch, validate, return to service) |
| **Discharge & Follow-up** | Post-Incident Activity (lessons learned) |

Just as a patient is **not discharged** the moment the surgeon finishes — they must be **monitored, tested, and confirmed fit** before returning to normal life — a compromised system is **not returned to production** the moment the malware is deleted. It must be **cleanly rebuilt, patched, monitored, and validated** before being trusted again.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Hook for Exam:**
> *"Recovery = Restore + Validate + Harden + Monitor" — the four pillars of bringing a system back to life safely.*

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** The maximum acceptable duration within which a system **must** be restored after a disaster/incident.
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** The maximum acceptable age of data (in time) that can be lost — i.e., the frequency of backups.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Recover):** The average time taken to restore a system to functional state.
- **SLA (Service Level Agreement):** The contractual uptime/recovery commitments to stakeholders.

---

## 1.4 Scope of Recovery (KTU Module 3 Boundary)

The KTU syllabus frames Recovery as covering the following six technical sub-areas:

1. **Recovery Planning** — BCP and DRP frameworks.
2. **Backup & Restoration Strategies** — Full, Incremental, Differential, Synthetic.
3. **System Restoration Procedure** — Rebuild, patch, re-image.
4. **Recovery Testing & Validation** — Verification before reconnecting to the network.
5. **Forensic Preservation During Recovery** — Maintaining chain of custody while restoring.
6. **Post-Recovery Monitoring & Hardening** — Watching for attacker re-entry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping:**
> This topic primarily maps to **CO3:** *Design and evaluate incident response, recovery, and continuity strategies for enterprise security infrastructures.* It carries **direct 14-mark** weightage potential in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Recovery Phase — Detailed Operational Workflow

Recovery is **not a single action** but a **sequential engineering pipeline**. The KTU board expects students to describe this as a structured sequence of well-defined steps. Below is the canonical 8-step Recovery workflow.

### Step 1: Pre-Recovery Verification (Pre-flight Checks)
Before touching the compromised host, the IR team **must** confirm:
- The threat has been **fully eradicated** (all IOCs eliminated).
- No backdoors, rootkits, or scheduled tasks remain.
- The eradication report is signed off by the Incident Commander.

> **Why?** Returning an infected system to production before full eradication causes **re-infection** within minutes/hours.

### Step 2: Forensic Image Preservation
- Create a **bit-stream forensic image** of the compromised disk (e.g., using `dd`, EnCase, FTK Imager).
- Compute and record cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-256) of the image.
- Store the image in a **write-protected, access-controlled evidence vault**.

> **Why?** The image is needed for potential **legal proceedings**, regulatory compliance (e.g., IT Act 2000, GDPR), and post-incident forensic analysis.

### Step 3: Clean-State Restoration
Choose one of three clean-state options (selected based on incident severity):

| Restoration Strategy | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Re-imaging from Gold Image** | Wipe disk, install OS from a known-clean master image | Compromised by malware/rootkit |
| **Rebuild from Scratch** | Manual reinstallation + configuration | Critical infrastructure, no trusted image |
| **Restore from Clean Backup** | Restore the last known-good backup taken **before** the intrusion | Data-only loss, OS integrity intact |

### Step 4: Patching and Hardening
Apply the **latest security patches**, reconfigure firewall rules, disable unused services, and apply the **principle of least privilege**.

> **The exact vulnerability that was exploited MUST be patched**, otherwise the attacker can re-enter via the same vector.

### Step 5: Configuration Re-establishment
- Re-apply user accounts, group policies, application settings from **configuration baseline** documents (not from the compromised host).
- Rotate **all credentials** (passwords, API keys, SSH keys, certificates) that were stored on or accessible to the compromised system.

### Step 6: Validation & Testing
- Run vulnerability scans (Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys) to confirm no known vulnerabilities.
- Verify integrity using file integrity monitoring tools (Tripwire, OSSEC).
- Conduct functional testing to ensure business operations are restored.

### Step 7: Controlled Re-introduction to Production
- Place the system in a **quarantined VLAN** first, with enhanced logging.
- Monitor for 24–72 hours for any signs of attacker re-entry.
- If clean, gradually restore to the production network.

### Step 8: Post-Recovery Monitoring
- Increase log verbosity (Syslog, SIEM alerts).
- Deploy additional EDR/XDR sensors.
- Watch for known IOCs and TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) of the attacker.

---

## 2.2 Recovery Planning — BCP vs. DRP

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks students to **differentiate between BCP and DRP**. This is a 3-mark favourite.

| Aspect | **Business Continuity Plan (BCP)** | **Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Entire business operations (people, process, technology, facilities) | IT infrastructure (servers, networks, data) |
| **Focus** | Keep the **business running** during/after a crisis | Restore **IT systems** to working order |
| **Trigger** | Any disruption (natural, man-made, cyber) | Specific IT disasters (data center failure, cyberattack) |
| **Time Horizon** | Mid-to-long term continuity | Short-term, tactical recovery |
| **Subset Relationship** | BCP **encompasses** DRP | DRP is a **subset** of BCP |
| **Owners** | Senior management, business unit heads | IT operations, system administrators |
| **Output** | Business continuity strategy | Recovery runbooks, RTO/RPO targets |

> **Memory Hook:** *BCP = Business; DRP = Data center.*

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** for KTU ESE numericals. Memorize all entries.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Definition | Unit | Typical Value (Enterprise) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Recovery Time Objective** | $\text{RTO}$ | Max acceptable downtime after incident | Hours / Minutes | 4 hours (Tier-2 systems) |
| 2 | **Recovery Point Objective** | $\text{RPO}$ | Max acceptable data loss (age) | Hours / Minutes | 1 hour (transactional DB) |
| 3 | **Mean Time To Recover** | $\text{MTTR}$ | Average repair/restoration time | Hours | Varies |
| 4 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\text{MTBF}$ | Average time between two failures | Hours | Varies |
| 5 | **Mean Time To Detect** | $\text{MTTD}$ | Average time to identify an incident | Hours | Varies |
| 6 | **Availability** | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Percentage (0–1) or % | 99.9 % ("three nines") |
| 7 | **Annual Downtime** | $D_{\text{annual}}$ | $D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours}$ | Hours / Year | 8.76 h (for 99.9 %) |
| 8 | **Backup Window (Full)** | $T_{\text{FB}}$ | Time to complete a full backup | Hours | 6–12 h (overnight) |
| 9 | **Backup Window (Incremental)** | $T_{\text{IB}}$ | Time to back up only changed data since last backup | Hours | 1–2 h |
| 10 | **Total Recovery Cost** | $C_{\text{R}}$ | $C_{\text{R}} = C_{\text{down}} + C_{\text{restore}} + C_{\text{validate}}$ | Currency | Varies |
| 11 | **Cost of Downtime (per hour)** | $C_{\text{D}}$ | Revenue loss + productivity loss per hour | Currency / Hour | ₹ 50,000 – ₹ 10,00,000+ |
| 12 | **RTO Compliance Check** | — | Recovery Time $\leq$ RTO | Boolean | Pass / Fail |
| 13 | **RPO Compliance Check** | — | Last Backup Age $\leq$ RPO | Boolean | Pass / Fail |
| 14 | **Service Level Agreement** | $\text{SLA}$ | Contractual uptime commitment | % | 99.9 %, 99.99 %, 99.999 % |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars in the table above have been replaced with parentheses or written as "vertical bar" to comply with KTU Markdown Table Safety Rules. For example, $\vert x \vert$ has been avoided in table cells.

---

## 2.4 Backup Strategies — Engineering Trade-offs

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to know the **three classical backup strategies** and their trade-offs.

### 2.4.1 Full Backup
- **What:** Every file on the system is copied, regardless of whether it has changed.
- **Pros:** Simplest to restore (only one tape/image needed).
- **Cons:** Slowest, largest storage, highest network/CPU load.
- **Restore Time:** Very low.

### 2.4.2 Incremental Backup
- **What:** Only files changed **since the last backup of any type** are copied.
- **Pros:** Fastest, smallest storage footprint.
- **Cons:** Restoration requires the **last full backup + all subsequent incremental backups** in order.
- **Restore Time:** Longest (must reconstruct chain).

### 2.4.3 Differential Backup
- **What:** Only files changed **since the last full backup** are copied.
- **Pros:** Restoration requires **only the last full backup + the latest differential**.
- **Cons:** Grows in size each day (until next full backup).
- **Restore Time:** Moderate.

### 2.4.4 Comparative Storage & Recovery Table

| Strategy | Day 1 (Full) | Day 2 | Day 3 | Day 4 | Day 5 (Full) | Tapes to Restore |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Daily** | 100 GB | 100 GB | 100 GB | 100 GB | 100 GB | **1** |
| **Incremental** | 100 GB | 5 GB | 5 GB | 5 GB | 100 GB | **4** (Full + 3 inc) |
| **Differential** | 100 GB | 5 GB | 10 GB | 15 GB | 100 GB | **2** (Full + latest diff) |

> **Memory Hook:** *"Incremental grows linearly, Differential grows like a staircase, Full is the heavyweight."*

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recovery engineering is **not theoretical** — it is a **business-critical function**:

- **Banking & Finance:** RBI mandates RTO ≤ 4 hours and RPO ≤ 15 minutes for core banking systems.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA requires rapid recovery of Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems to ensure patient safety.
- **E-Commerce (e.g., Flipkart Big Billion Days):** Every minute of downtime translates to lakhs of rupees in lost revenue.
- **Critical Infrastructure (Power, Water):** Recovery is a **national security** concern — governed by CERT-In directives in India.
- **Cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Multi-region failover and automated recovery are standard offerings.

> **Career Hook:** Certified roles like *GIAC Certified Incident Handler (GCIH)*, *EC-Council Disaster Recovery Professional (EDRP)*, and *AWS Solutions Architect* all heavily test these recovery metrics.
