# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Define IPSec and list its four primary security services.**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**IPSec (Internet Protocol Security)** is a suite of IETF-defined protocols (RFC 4301, 4302, 4303) operating at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** that provides end-to-end security for IP datagrams traversing untrusted networks.

The **four primary security services** are:

1. **Confidentiality** — Protects payload from unauthorized disclosure using symmetric encryption (AES, 3DES).
2. **Integrity** — Ensures data has not been altered using HMAC-based checksums.
3. **Authentication** — Verifies the source/origin of packets using digital signatures or pre-shared keys.
4. **Anti-Replay** — Prevents replay attacks using sequence numbers and sliding windows.

IPSec uses two main protocols: **AH (Authentication Header — Protocol 51)** and **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload — Protocol 50)**, with key management handled by **IKEv2 (Internet Key Exchange version 2)**.

---

### **Question 2: What is a Security Association (SA) in IPSec? Why is it simplex?**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Question]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Security Association (SA)** is a **one-way logical (simplex) connection** between two IPSec peers that defines the complete set of security parameters required to protect communication in a single direction.

An SA is uniquely identified by a **triple**: (SPI, Destination IP Address, Protocol).

The SA is **simplex** (one-way) because it represents security applied in a single direction. For **bidirectional communication, two separate SAs are required** — one inbound and one outbound. This design allows asymmetric security policies, where the encryption algorithm, keys, or lifetime may differ in each direction.

Each SA contains: encryption algorithm, authentication algorithm, encryption keys, authentication keys, SA lifetime, anti-replay window size, and mode (transport or tunnel).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Modified Past Paper]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the architecture of IPSec with a neat diagram. Differentiate between AH and ESP.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain ESP in both Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode with packet format diagrams. List the algorithms supported by ESP for confidentiality and integrity.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — IPSec Architecture, AH vs ESP**

**IPSec Architecture (4 marks):**

The IPSec framework is defined in **RFC 4301** and consists of three primary components:

1. **Authentication Header (AH) — Protocol 51** — Provides integrity, authentication, and anti-replay. Defined in RFC 4302. Does **not** provide confidentiality.
2. **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) — Protocol 50** — Provides confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and anti-replay. Defined in RFC 4303.
3. **Internet Key Exchange (IKEv2) — UDP Port 500** — Automates SA negotiation and key management.

Supporting databases include the **SAD (Security Association Database)**, **SPD (Security Policy Database)**, and **PAD (Peer Authentication Database)**.

**AH vs ESP — Key Differences (3 marks):**

| Feature | **AH** | **ESP** |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | **No** | **Yes (AES, 3DES)** |
| Integrity | **Yes (covers IP header + payload)** | **Yes (covers ESP header + payload + trailer)** |
| Authentication | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Header Coverage | **Yes (except mutable fields)** | **No (new outer header not protected)** |
| Protocol Number | $51$ | $50$ |
| Modern Use | Rare | Dominant |

**Conclusion:** ESP is more commonly deployed because modern applications require encryption, not just authentication.

---

#### **Part (b) — ESP Transport Mode vs Tunnel Mode**

**ESP Transport Mode (3.5 marks):**

In transport mode, ESP encrypts **only the payload** of the IP packet. The **original IP header is preserved**, and an ESP header is inserted between the IP header and the upper-layer payload (e.g., TCP segment).

Packet structure:
$$
P_{\text{transport}} = H_{\text{orig}} \mid\mid \text{ESP\_Header} \mid\mid E_{K_e}(D) \mid\mid \text{ESP\_Trailer} \mid\mid \text{ICV}
$$

**Use case:** Host-to-host communication on a trusted network.

**ESP Tunnel Mode (3.5 marks):**

In tunnel mode, ESP **encrypts the entire original IP packet** (header + payload) and prepends a **new outer IP header**. The original IP header is hidden from intermediate routers.

Packet structure:
$$
P_{\text{tunnel}} = H_{\text{new}} \mid\mid \text{ESP\_Header} \mid\mid E_{K_e}(H_{\text{orig}} \mid\mid D) \mid\mid \text{ESP\_Trailer} \mid\mid \text{ICV}
$$

**Use case:** VPN gateways, site-to-site VPNs, hiding internal IP topology.

**Supported Algorithms by ESP:**

- **Confidentiality:** DES (56-bit), 3DES (168-bit), **AES-128/192/256**, ChaCha20
- **Integrity:** HMAC-MD5, HMAC-SHA-1, **HMAC-SHA-256 (recommended)**, AES-GCM (combined mode)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4, Model Paper]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** What is a Security Association (SA)? Explain the Security Association Database (SAD) and Security Policy Database (SPD) with their key fields.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the IKEv2 protocol with its 4-message exchange. How does Diffie-Hellman key exchange work in IKE?

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Security Association, SAD, SPD**

**Security Association (3 marks):**

A **Security Association (SA)** is a simplex (one-way) logical connection between two IPSec endpoints that defines the complete set of security parameters for protecting traffic. Each SA is identified by a unique triple:
$$\text{SA}_{\text{ID}} = (\text{SPI}, \text{Destination IP}, \text{Protocol})$$

Bidirectional traffic requires **two SAs** (one per direction).

**SAD — Security Association Database (2 marks):**

The SAD stores all active SAs. Each SAD entry contains:
- SPI (32 bits)
- Sequence Number Counter (for anti-replay)
- Anti-Replay Window
- AH/ESP algorithms (encryption + integrity)
- Encryption key $K_e$ and authentication key $K_a$
- SA lifetime
- Path MTU
- Mode (transport or tunnel)

**SPD — Security Policy Database (2 marks):**

The SPD stores security policies that determine what to do with each packet. For each packet, the policy is one of three actions:
- **PROTECT** — Apply IPSec (ESP or AH) processing
- **BYPASS** — Forward without any IPSec processing
- **DISCARD** — Drop the packet silently

SPD entries are matched using selectors: source IP, destination IP, protocol, source port, destination port.

---

#### **Part (b) — IKEv2 and Diffie-Hellman**

**IKEv2 Protocol (4 marks):**

IKEv2 (RFC 5996) is the modern key management protocol for IPSec. It uses a clean **4-message exchange** divided into two phases:

**Phase 1 — IKE_SA_INIT (Messages 1 & 2):**
- Message 1 (Initiator → Responder): SA proposals, Diffie-Hellman public value $g^a$, nonce
- Message 2 (Responder → Initiator): Selected SA, DH public value $g^b$, nonce
- After Message 2, both peers compute the shared secret $g^{ab} \pmod{p}$

**Phase 2 — IKE_AUTH (Messages 3 & 4):**
- Message 3 (Initiator → Responder): Authentication data, identity, traffic selectors
- Message 4 (Responder → Initiator): Authentication confirmation, identity, traffic selectors
- After Message 4, the IKE SA and first IPSec SA are both established

**Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange in IKE (3 marks):**

The DH algorithm allows two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel:

1. Both peers agree on public parameters: a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$.
2. Alice picks a private key $a$ and computes $A = g^a \pmod{p}$.
3. Bob picks a private key $b$ and computes $B = g^b \pmod{p}$.
4. Alice and Bob exchange $A$ and $B$ publicly.
5. Both compute the shared secret:
   $$K_{\text{shared}} = g^{ab} \pmod{p}$$
6. An eavesdropper seeing $A$, $B$, $g$, $p$ cannot feasibly compute $a$ or $b$ due to the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**.

The derived shared secret is used to generate the **SKEYID** and other session keys for encrypting IKE traffic and deriving ESP/AH keys.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU IPSec Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing AH and ESP roles (–2 marks):** Students often write "AH provides encryption" — this is **FALSE**. AH provides **only integrity and authentication**, never confidentiality. ESP is the protocol that encrypts.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that SA is simplex (–2 marks):** Writing "an SA is a bidirectional connection" loses marks. Always state that an SA is **one-way** and **bidirectional communication requires two SAs**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up protocol numbers (–1 mark):** AH is **51**, ESP is **50**. Reversing them is a common error. Tip: think "**E**SP = **E**ncapsulation = **5**0" (E and 5 are close in alphabet).
>
> 4. **Not distinguishing Transport vs Tunnel mode (–3 marks):** The examiner expects a clear comparison with **packet diagrams**. A text-only explanation without showing the **position of the new IP header** in tunnel mode loses full marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to mention IKE (–2 marks):** IPSec questions often ask about key management. Students who describe AH/ESP but **omit IKE/IKEv2** lose significant marks. Always mention IKEv2 as the **key management backbone**.
>
> 6. **NAT incompatibility of AH (–1 mark):** If asked about NAT traversal, students forget that **AH breaks with NAT** because it authenticates the original IP header, which NAT changes. **ESP with NAT-T (UDP 4500)** is the solution.
>
> 7. **Sequence number reset confusion (–1 mark):** After SA renegotiation, the sequence number **resets to 1**, not continues. This is a subtle but important KTU point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for IPSec (Module 4):**

- **IPSec** is a **Layer 3** security suite providing **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and anti-replay**.
- **AH (Protocol 51)** = Integrity + Authentication only. **No encryption.**
- **ESP (Protocol 50)** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Authentication. **The dominant protocol.**
- **SA (Security Association)** is **simplex (one-way)**. Bidirectional traffic needs **two SAs**.
- **SA triple identifier** = (SPI, Destination IP, Protocol).
- **SAD** stores active SAs; **SPD** stores policies (PROTECT / BYPASS / DISCARD).
- **Transport Mode** protects payload only — original IP header is preserved. Used for host-to-host.
- **Tunnel Mode** protects entire original packet — new outer IP header is added. Used for **VPNs**.
- **IKEv2** uses a **4-message exchange** (IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH) for SA negotiation.
- **Diffie-Hellman** key exchange provides **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** in IKE.
- **IKE port** = UDP 500; **NAT-T port** = UDP 4500.
- **AH is incompatible with NAT** because it authenticates the IP header.
- **Common encryption algorithms** = AES-128/256, 3DES.
- **Common integrity algorithms** = HMAC-SHA-256 (recommended), HMAC-SHA-1, HMAC-MD5.
- **HMAC definition** = $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H((K \oplus opad) \mid\mid H((K \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M))$.
- **DH shared secret** = $g^{ab} \pmod{p}$, secure due to the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**.
- **Anti-replay** uses **32-bit sequence numbers** and a **sliding window** (default size 32).
- **ESP in tunnel mode** = the most common IPSec VPN configuration in industry.
- **IPSec is natively built into IPv6** and supported via extension headers in IPv4.

> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When answering KTU questions on IPSec, **always draw a packet diagram** to illustrate Transport vs Tunnel mode. Examiners award full marks only when both the textual explanation and the visual representation are present. A diagram without explanation or vice versa will cost you at least 2-3 marks.
